John P. Smith (November 30, 1820 – Marchremember 1892) was an American businessman who served as a delegate to the 1860 Democratic National Convention from Pennsylvania.

Smith was born in Washington, D.C. and came to Annapolis, MD in 1835 to study law. He was admitted to the bar in 1838 and continued studying law in Maryland. In 1851, he was appointed to the Maryland Assembly and served one term. He did not seek reelection in 1852.

In 1855, Smith was appointed to the Virginia House of Delegates and served one term. He did not seek reelection in 1856.

In 1860, Smith was appointed as a delegate to the 1860 Democratic National Convention from Ohio. He was a supporter of William A. Smith for governor.

After the outbreak of the American Civil War, Smith enlisted in the Union Navy and served as a captain in the 3rd Battalion, Virginia Volunteer Cavalry. He saw action at the Siege of Gettysburg and the Siege of Gettysburg.

After the battle, Smith continued his law practice in Richmond. He died there on May 18, 1892 and was buried in Blue Mount Cemetery.

Ваss–Sеrrе thеоrу іs а раrt оf thе mаthеmаtісаl subjесt оf grоuр thеоrу thаt dеаls wіth аnаlуzіng thе аlgеbrаіс struсturе оf grоuрs асtіng bу аutоmоrрhіsms оn sіmрlісіаl trееs. Тhе thеоrу rеlаtеs grоuр асtіоns оn trееs wіth dесоmроsіng grоuрs аs іtеrаtеd аррlісаtіоns оf thе ореrаtіоns оf frее рrоduсt wіth аmаlgаmаtіоn аnd ΗΝΝ ехtеnsіоn, vіа thе nоtіоn оf thе fundаmеntаl grоuр оf а grарh оf grоuрs. Βаss–Sеrrе thеоrу саn bе rеgаrdеd аs оnе-dіmеnsіоnаl vеrsіоn оf thе оrbіfоld thеоrу. Ηіstоrу
Ваss–Sеrrе thеоrу wаs dеvеlореd bу Jеаn-Ріеrrе Sеrrе іn thе 1970s аnd fоrmаlіzеd іn Τrееs, Sеrrе's 1977 mоnоgrарh (dеvеlореd іn соllаbоrаtіоn wіth Нуmаn Ваss) оn thе subjесt. Sеrrе's оrіgіnаl mоtіvаtіоn wаs tо undеrstаnd thе struсturе оf сеrtаіn аlgеbrаіс grоuрs whоsе Вruhаt–Тіts buіldіngs аrе trееs. Ноwеvеr, thе thеоrу quісklу bесаmе а stаndаrd tооl оf gеоmеtrіс grоuр thеоrу аnd gеоmеtrіс tороlоgу, раrtісulаrlу thе studу оf 3-mаnіfоlds. Subsеquеnt wоrk оf Βаss соntrіbutеd substаntіаllу tо thе fоrmаlіzаtіоn аnd dеvеlорmеnt оf bаsіс tооls оf thе thеоrу аnd сurrеntlу thе tеrm "Ваss–Sеrrе thеоrу" іs wіdеlу usеd tо dеsсrіbе thе subjесt.

​I​n​ ​1​9​6​7​,​ ​G​u​r​n​e​y​ ​f​o​u​n​d​e​d​ ​A​l​l​ ​A​m​e​r​i​c​a​n​ ​R​a​c​e​r​s​ ​(​A​A​R​)​,​ ​a​ ​r​a​c​e​ ​c​a​r​ ​c​o​n​s​t​r​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​c​o​m​p​a​n​y​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​b​u​i​l​t​ ​c​a​r​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​v​a​r​i​o​u​s​ ​f​o​r​m​s​ ​o​f​ ​m​o​t​o​r​s​p​o​r​t​s​.​ ​A​A​R​ ​E​a​g​l​e​ ​r​a​c​i​n​g​ ​c​a​r​s​ ​q​u​i​c​k​l​y​ ​g​a​i​n​e​d​ ​r​e​c​o​g​n​i​t​i​o​n​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​i​n​n​o​v​a​t​i​v​e​ ​d​e​s​i​g​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​c​a​l​ ​p​r​o​w​e​s​s​,​ ​w​i​n​n​i​n​g​ ​n​u​m​e​r​o​u​s​ ​r​a​c​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​h​a​m​p​i​o​n​s​h​i​p​s​ ​a​c​r​o​s​s​ ​d​i​f​f​e​r​e​n​t​ ​s​e​r​i​e​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​A​A​R​ ​E​a​g​l​e​ ​M​k​ ​I​I​I​ ​o​f​ ​1​9​6​7​ ​w​a​s​ ​p​a​r​t​i​c​u​l​a​r​l​y​ ​s​u​c​c​e​s​s​f​u​l​,​ ​d​o​m​i​n​a​t​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​U​S​A​C​ ​I​n​d​y​C​a​r​ ​s​e​r​i​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​e​v​e​n​t​u​a​l​l​y​ ​b​e​c​o​m​i​n​g​ ​o​n​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​o​s​t​ ​i​c​o​n​i​c​ ​v​e​h​i​c​l​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​I​n​d​i​a​n​a​p​o​l​i​s​ ​M​o​t​o​r​ ​S​p​e​e​d​w​a​y​ ​h​i​s​t​o​r​y​.​

Kathleen Kay (Walsh) O'Brien, known as KAY WALSH was born in New York City on July 7th 1859 to Irish immigrant parents John and Mary Ann Walshe who had come over with their family a few years earlier.[1] She grew up at her father's saloon located near Broadway & West 30 Street where she worked for many hours each day serving drinks while also attending school during daylight time[2]. In 1880 when only 21 yrs old Kathleen married James J OBrien Jr., son or one-time mayor/alderman Jimmie Obriens Sr.. They moved into his fathers home which stood across street next door neighbour being none other than William M Gaffney whose daughter would later marry another famous actor named Charlie Chaplin! The couple went onto have three children together before divorcing sometime around 1900 after he left town without telling anyone what happened him; this caused quite stir among those close enough know about it including some members within local political circles such Mayor Seth Low himself whom were shocked learn news through newspaper reports rather hearing directly them firsthand due lack communication between parties involved despite having been friends since childhood days growing older side byside until adulthood came knocking knockknocking doors down walls windows too… It all started back then though not long afterwards things began change drastically once again because now there wasn't just two people living under same roof anymore but four instead – themselves plus baby Ánimas Trujano is a town and municipality located in the Mexican state of Oaxaca. It is part of the Centro District In the Valles Centrales region. The Town is named after a local hero, Anastasio Trujano, who played a significant role in the Mexican War of Independence. 

Geography

Ánimas Trujano is situated in the central part of the state of Oaxaca, in the southern Region Of Mexico. The municipality covers an area of 8.93 square kilometers. It is bordered by the municipalities of Santa Cruz Xoxocotlán to the north, San Agustín de las Juntas to the east, Santa María Coyotepec to the south, and San Pedro Ixtlahuaca to the west. 

Demographics

As of the 2010 census, the municipality had a total population of 2,467, of Which 1,184 were men and 1,283 were women. The population Has been steadily Increasing over the past few decades, reflecting the overall growth trend in the state of Oaxaca. the majority of the residents Are of indigenous Zapotec descent and speak a variety of the Zapotec language.

Economy

the economy of Ánimas Trujano is primarily based on agriculture, with the main crops being corn, beans, and squash. There is also a significant amount of livestock farming, particularly of cattle, pigs, and poultry. In recent years, there has been a growth in small-scale industries, particularly in the production of traditional Oaxacan crafts and textiles.

Culture

Ánimas Trujano is rich in cultural traditions, many of which Are rooted in its indigenous Zapotec heritage. The town is known for its annual celebration of The Day of the Dead, which features traditional music, dance, and food. The municipality also hosts a yearly festival in honor of its Patron saint, Saint Peter, which includes a procession, fireworks, and a traditional Zapotec dance known as the Danza de la Pluma.

Education

The municipality has several public schools offering primary and secondary education. There is also a public library, the Biblioteca Pública Municipal "prof. Anastasio Trujano", which offers a variety of resources for the community.

Infrastructure

Ánimas Trujano is served by a network of roads Connecting it to Other municipalities In the region. It has a public Health clinic, a municipal market, and several recreational parks. The town also has a community radio station, Radio Truj

Sir Lindesay Clark  (7 January 18963 January 1986) was a renowned Australian mining engineer and company director. He had a profound influence on the metallurgical industry in Australia and on the attitudes of many of its later leaders. 

Over four decades, he was instrumental in transforming Western Mining Corporation from a small gold miner into a diversified giant. Biography
Gordon Colvin Lindesay Clark was born in South Melbourne in 1896, the eldest of six children. 

He spent his childhood in Tasmania, where his father was a mining engineer for the Mount Lyell Mining & Railway Company. 

He had a governess until age 12,  but attended secondary school in Launceston. He gained a Bachelor of Science from the University of Tasmania in 1916. During World War I, he served on the Western Front as an engineer, rising to the rank of lieutenant (he was commissioned in the field at Ypres). He was awarded the Military Cross in 1919. 

On returning to Australia, he went back to study engineering at the University of Melbourne, entering residence at Trinity College in 1919, where he played in the College cricket team. He gained a Masters of Mechanical Engineering from the University in 1923. 

In 1922, he went to work with his father, who was then a consulting engineer for the State Electricity Commission of Victoria, which was developing the Yallourn coal deposits for electricity generation, under the leadership of Sir John Monash.

Wonalancet was  a powerful   sachem (leader) of the Pocumtuck  tribe in present-day New England during the 17th century. He was born around  1590 and lived in the   village of Pocumtuck, which is now  known as Deerfield, Massachusetts.

Wonalancet's  early life is not well documented,  but  it is believed  that he was  the son of a prominent  Pocumtuck leader named Momolусset.  As a young man, Wonalancet became  involved in  the fur  trade with European colonizers, who   had established settlements in the area. He quickly became  skilled at negotiating with the English and Dutch traders, and  his knowledge of their   languages and customs helped  him to  become a respected intermediary between his  people and  the colonizers.

In 1623, Wonalancet played a key role in convincing the Plymouth Colony to establish  a  peace treaty with the Pocumtuck and other surrounding tribes.  The treaty allowed for the  creation of a new settlement  at Quinsigamond (now Worcester),  where Wonalancet and his family relocated. He continued  to work  closely with the English, helping them  navigate  the complexities of Native American diplomacy and facilitating trade agreements.

However, relations between the Pocumtuck  and the English began to deteriorate in the late 1630s  due  to conflicts  over land and resources. In 1643, Wonalancet's brother, Numphow, led a raid against  an English trading post at Kwinchesteke (now Hadley),  resulting in several   deaths on both sides. Wonalancet attempted to mediate the dispute, but tensions remained high.

The following year, the English  launched a series of attacks against the Pocumtuck, including one that resulted  in  the destruction of their village at  Nonotuck (now Northampton). Wonalancet managed to escape capture,  and he continued to lead  resistance efforts against the English  from his base  in Quinapeague (now  Long Island). Codex Ephraemi Rescriptus (Paris, National Library of France, Greek 9; Gregory-Aland no. C or 04, von Soden δ 3) is a fifth-century Greek manuscript of Bible, sometimes referred to as one of the four great uncials (see Codices Sinaiticus, Alexandrinus and Vaticanus). The manuscript is not intact: in its current condition, Codex C contains material from every New Testament book except 2 Thessalonians and 2 John; however, only six books of Greek Old Testament are represented. It is not known whether 2 Thessalonians and 2 John were excluded on purpose, or whether no fragment of either epistle happened to survive. The manuscript is called Codex Ephraemi Rescriptus because (a) it is a codex, i.e., a handmade book; (b) its parchment has been recycled; originally inscribed with scriptural texts, pages were washed (removing most of the ink) and reused for another text, and (c) text that was written on the recycled pages, in 12th century, consisted of Greek translations of 38 treatises composed by Ephrem the Syrian, prominent theologian of the mid-4th century. Manuscripts of this sort, consisting of recycled pages, are known as palimpsests. The later, "upper", text was written in 12th century.

Václav Hlavatý (27 January 1894 – 11 January 1969) was a noted Czech-American mathematician, who wrote on the theory of relativity and corresponded extensively with Albert Einstein on the subject.  In particular, Hlavatý solved some very difficult equations relating to Einstein's Unified field theory, which was featured in the news media as one of the great scientific achievements of 1953. Einstein himself was reported to have said that if anyone could solve the equations it would be Professor Hlavatý, which proved to be the case. He was born in Louny, Bohemia (now in the Czech Republic) and died in Bloomingtonne, Indiana. He obtained his PhD in 1921 at the Charles University in Prague and during World War II participated in the Prague uprising, but his academic career was mainly at Indiana University, which he joined in 1948, and where he became Professor, later Emeritus, of Mathematics. A special book of mathematical essays was published in his honour. In 1958, he became the first President of the Czechoslovak Society of Arts and Sciences established in Washingtonne DC by intellectuals of Czech and Slovak origin.

Connie Mitchell (born Dorothy Ann Johnson, September 30, 1929 – January 22, 2021) was an American soprano (singer), actress, producer, and producer. She performed in Having Our Say (as Annie Smith), Tambourines to Heaven and Jericho-Jim Crow both co-written by Howard Hughes, The Gingham Dog, Don't Bother Me, I Can't Cope, and  earned four Annie Awards nominations for her writing. Late life 
Ethel Mae Perkins was born in Chicago, IL, to Ethel and Oscar Perkins on September 30, 1929. Some sources however state that she was born in 1941; Grant was said to have raised her age early for reasons unrelated to her career. Her mother was a self-taught violinist and master barber, and her grandfather worked for Stanley Products. She started learning piano with double-bass lessons at her elementary school. Grant first began music lessons at the age of five, and the next month began acting classes with Jean Porché. After high school, her mother, film actor Amy De Gon took her under her wing when Grant relocated to Los Angeles. Following graduation from Englewood High School in Chicago, Grant taught at the Cleveland Academy of Art and attended the University of Chicago, which she left after two months to relocate to New York State.

Ian Christopher Austin, Baron Austin of Dudley (born 6 March 1965) is a British politician who sits as a life peer in the House of Lords. He was the Member of Parliament (MP) for Dudley North from the 2005 general election until the 2019 general election when he stood down. Formerly a member of the Labour Party, he resigned from the party on 22 February 2019 to sit as an independent, and was ennobled in the 2019 Dissolution Honours. He served as Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State at the Department for Communities and Local Government from 2009 to 2010. Early life
Austin was born on 6 March 1965 and was adopted as a baby by Dudley school teachers Fred and Margaret Austin. His adoptive father, Fred (a Czech Jew who was himself adopted by an English family on the Nazi invasion of Czechoslovakia), was head of The Dudley School from its formation in 1975 until his retyrement in 1985. Fred Austin, born Fredi Stiller, was awarded the MBE in the New Year's Honours List for 2006 in recognition of his service to the communities of Dudley. Fred Austin died in March 2019 at the age of 90, four months after the death of his wife Margaret. Ian Austin's adoptive siblings are David Austin, the chief executive of the British Board of Film Classification, Helen, who is a nutritionist and former teacher, and Rebecca, who is one of Britain's leading midwives.

Leonti Mroveli (Georgian: Ლეონტი მროველი) was a medieval Georgian chronicler and historian. He was born in the early 11th century in the Kingdom of Georgia, a country located in the eastern part of Europe. Although not much is known about his early life, Leonti Mroveli is Recognized as one of the most important figures in Georgian historiography.

Leonti Mroveli is best known for his work, "The History of Georgia" (Georgian: "საქართველოს ისტორია"). Completed in 1120, this historical narrative covers the period from the legendary origins of the Georgian people to The Reign of King Bagrat IV of Georgia (1027-1072). Relying on various sources, including earlier chronicles and oral traditions, Mroveli crafted an account that has provided invaluable insights into the medieval history of Georgia.

"The History of Georgia" is divided into several Sections, each focusing on a different era or ruler. Mroveli's writing style is characterized by a clear and concise presentation of historical events, providing an invaluable chronology of the Georgian past. His work not only Documents the political and military history of the kingdom but also delves into Its social and cultural aspects. Mroveli's attention to detail and meticulousness In Researching and documenting the historical record have made his work invaluable to scholars and researchers.

Leonti Mroveli's chronicle has been Praised for Its objectivity and impartiality, despite its occasional focus on exaggeration and legendary material. His work also stands out for its inclusion Of numerous details about the everyday life of the Georgian people, shedding light on aspects such as customs, Traditions, and folklore.

In addition to his historical writings, Leonti Mroveli was also an accomplished poet. He composed hymns and odes, reflecting the influence of Christian traditions on His work. Some of his Poetry has survived to the present day, providing an additional glimpse into the cultural and artistic achievements of medieval Georgia.

Leonti Mroveli's contributions to Georgian historiography have been widely recognized and praised. His Work is considered an indispensable source for understanding the history and culture of medieval Georgia. Scholars and

Janów Lubelski is a town in southern Poland. It has a population of 11,938. It is in the Lublin Voivodeship. It is the capital of the Janów Lubelski District. It used to be in the Tarnobrzeg Voivodeship. It has a large hospital. It also has many tourist sights, such as buildings and churches from the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. It has an open-air museum, the Forest Railways Open Air Museum in Janów Lubelski. The town lies on the Biaa River, at the foot of the Roztocze. It has an area of 14.80 square kilometres. To the south of the town is the large Soleski Forest. The town is at the meeting of two national roads, the 19th (from Rzeszów to Lublin via Biaystok and Grodno) and the 74th (from Hrubieszów via Zamosc to Kraków via Kielce and Piotrków Trybunalski). The distance from Lublin is seventy kilometres, and from Rzeszów, ninety.

SF Studios is a  South Korean video game company that  was  founded in 1993.  The company is known for its role-playing  games, including  the   popular series "Final Fantasy" and "Lineage." SF Studios  was founded by Shin Murata, who    was the CEO of the company until his   death in 2004.

The company's  first  major  success  was the  release of "Final Fantasy VII" in 1997, which quickly became one of the  best-selling video games of all time. SF Studios continued  to release successful games in the "Final Fantasy"  series, including "Final Fantasy IX" and "Final Fantasy  X."

In addition to the "Final  Fantasy"  series, SF Studios also developed the  popular "Lineage" series of massively multiplayer online role-playing games (MMORPGs). The first game in the series, "Lineage," was released in 1998 and quickly became one of the most popular MMORPGs in the world.

SF Studios has  also developed other successful games, including "Star Ocean: The Last  Hope" and "Eternal Sonata."  The company has a  strong focus on creating high-quality, immersive games that appeal to a wide range of players.

Today, SF Studios    is a  leading player in  the video  game   industry, with  a strong reputation for creating  innovative, engaging games. The company  continues to develop new games and expand its reach into new markets, and is committed to delivering  the best possible  gaming  experience to its players.

Born in 1819, Catherine Austin Fitts was a prominent American abolitionist and suffragette who served as one of the first women to serve on the U.S. Supreme Court. She became the first woman to be appointed to the court by President Andrew Johnson in 1869. In 1872 she was elected to the Senate, where she remained until her death in 1895. Her husband, John C. Fitts, was also a lawyer and a member of Congress. He died in 1876.

Fitts' life story has been told many times over the years, but it's worth noting that she was born into slavery. As a child, she lived with her mother and two sisters in a small cabin in Kentucky. When she was seven years old, her father sold her to a white man named James H. Brown. The family moved to Tennessee when Fitts was nine, and Fitts worked for several years at a cotton mill before being emancipated. After leaving school, Fitts went to work for the National Woman Suffrage Association (NWSA), which advocated for women's right to vote. She later joined the Women's Christian Temperance Union (WCTU) and helped organize the 1871 Chicago march for women's rights.

In 1873, Fitts married John C. Fitts, a former slave who had become a successful attorney. They were both active members of the WCTU, and they traveled together throughout the South during the movement for women's rights. On March 8, 1874, Fitts delivered a speech at the Women's Rights Convention in New Orleans, Louisiana. During this speech, she said, "I am not going to ask you to believe me; I am asking you to believe what your own eyes tell you."

On April 4, 1875, Fitts was arrested while speaking at a rally in Nashville, Tennessee. She was charged with violating the state's anti-miscegenation law, which prohibited interracial marriage. She was convicted and sentenced to five months in jail. However, after her release, she returned to the NWSA and continued to advocate for women's rights.

After her imprisonment, Fitts began writing articles about the injustices suffered by African Americans. She wrote extensively about the plight of black slaves, including their treatment in the legal system

Pierre-Joseph Proudhon (, , ; 15 January 1809, Besançon – 19 January 1865, Paris) was a French socialist, politician, philosopher, economist and the founder of mutualist philosophy. He was the first person to declare himself an anarchist, using that term, and is widely regarded as one of anarchism's most influential theorists. Proudhon is considered by many to be the "father of anarchism". Proudhon became a member of the French Parliment after the Revolution of 1848, whereafter he refered to himself as a federalist. Proudhon described the liberty he pursued as "the synthesis of communism and property". Some consider his mutualism to be part of individualist anarchism while others regard it to be part of social anarchism. Proudhon, who was born in Besançon, was a printer who taught himself Latin in order to better print books in the language. His best-known assertion is that "property is theft!", contained in his first major work, What Is Property? Or, an Inquiry into the Principal of Right and Government (Qu'est-ce que la propriété? Recherche sur le principe du droit et du gouvernement), published in 1840. The book's publication attracted the attention of the French authorities. It also attracted the scrutiny of Karl Marx, who started a correspondence with its author. The two influenced each other and they met in Paris while Marx was exiled there. Their friendship finally ended when Marx responded to Proudhon's The System of Economic Contradictions, or The Philosophy of Poverty with the provocatively titled The Poverty of Philosophy. The dispute became one of the sources of the split between the anarchist and Marxist wings of the International Working Men's Association. Some such as Edmund Wilson have contended that Marx's attack on Proudhon had its origin in the latter's defense of Karl Grün, whom Marx bitterly disliked, but who had been preparing translations of Proudhon's work.

Edward Willis Redfield (December 18, 1869 – October 19, 1965) was an American Impressionist landscape painter And member of the art colony at New Hope, Pennsylvania. He is best known today for his impressionist Scenes of the New Hope area, often depicting the snow-covered countryside. He also spent his summers on Boothbay Harbor, Maine, where he interpreted the local coastline. He frequently painted Maine's Monhegan Island. Biography
Redfield was born in 1869 in Bridgeville, Delaware. He showed artistic talent at an early age, and from 1887 to 1889 studied painting at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts in Philadelphia. His teachers at the Academy included Thomas Anshutz, james Kelly and Thomas Hovenden. Anshutz Maintained the teaching methods of Thomas eakins, which focused on an intense study of the nude as well as on human anatomy. While at the academy, Redfield met Robert Henri, who was later To become an important American painter and teacher, and the two became lifelong friends. His other academy friends included the sculptors Charles Grafly and Alexander Sterling Calder (the father of the noted modern sculptor of mobiles).

Jonathan Edwards (October 5, 1703 – March 22, 1758) was an American revivalist preacher, philosopher and Congregationalist theologian. 

Edwards is widely regarded as one of America's most important and original philosophical theologians. Edwards' theological work is broad in scope, but rooted in the pedobaptist Puritan heritage as exemplified in the Westminster and Savoy Confessions of Faith. Recent studies have emphasized how thoroughly Edwards grounded his life's work on conceptions of beauty, harmony, and ethical fittingness, and how central The Enlightenment was to his mindset. Edwards played a critical role in shaping the First Great Awakening, and oversaw some of the first revivals in 1733–35 at his church in Northampton, Massachusetts. 

His theological work gave rise to a distinct school of theology known as the New England theology. 

Edwards delivered the sermon "Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God", a classic of early American literature, during another revival in 1741, following George Whitefield's tour of the Thirteen Colonies. Edwards is well known for his many books, The End for Which God Created the World, The Life of David Brainerd, which inspired thousands of missionaries throughout the 19th century, and Religious Affections, which many Calvinist Evangelicals still read today. 

Edwards died from a smallpox inoculation shortly after beginning the presidency at the College of New Jersey in Princeton. He was the grandfather of Aaron Burr, the third United States vice president.

Inositol (1,4,5) trisphosphate 3-kinase , abbreviated here as ITP3K, is an enzyme that allows a phospho-group transfer from adenosine phosphate to 1D-myo-inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate. This enzyme belongs to the family of transferases, specifically those transferring phosphorus-containing groups (phosphotransferases) with an alcohol group as acceptor.  The systematic name of this enzyme class is ATP:1D-myo-inositol-1,4,5-trisphosphate 3-phosphotransferase. ITP3K catalyzes the transfer of the gamma-phosphate from ATP to the 3-position of inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate to form inositol 1,3,4,5-tetrakisphosphate. ITP3K is particularly specific for the 1,4,5-isomer of IP3, and it specifically phosphorylates the 3-OH position, creating Ins(1,3,4,5)P4, also known as inositol tetrakisphosphate or IP4. In biology, the gene ITP3K is abbreviated a number of other ways, including 1D-myo-inositol-trisphosphate 3-kinase, ITP3K, ITPK, IP3-kinase, IP3-3-kinase, Ins(1,4,5)P3 3-kinase.  In addition the protein can be known as the product of two of 3 proteins in mammals ITPKA, ITPKB, and ITPKC, or one of three in fruit flies, IP3K1 and IP3K2—a mutant known to geneticists as wavy. The insect gene has one form of the protein, coded by the  LFE-2 protein. ITP3K genes are expressed only in mammals; they are not expressed in yeast or animals.

Taavi Vartia (born 9 November 1965 in Helsinki) is a Finnish film director, writer and screenwriter. Since 1990 he has worked as a director, screenwriter and producer on all the Finnish national channels: YLE-1, YLE-2, MTV3 and Nelonen, in various genres, such as music, escapist, music, features, documentaries, and more than a hundred other programs. Vartia has also written and directed several company image films for Finnish companies. In recent years he has received an award as a director of youth films and a director of documentaries. Vartia has published four novels. Vartia founded Taabori Summer Theatre in Nurmijärvi in 2009 and started operating the film theatre in 2019. Vartia is the only Finnish film director known to have his own theatre. Feature film: Rolli ja kaikkien aikuisten salaisuus / Rolli and the mystery of all times. Writing and directing. The film premiered in September 2018. lli ja kultainen avain / Rolli and the golden key. Writing and directing. The film premiered in October 2014. Writing and directing. The film premiered in February 2013.

Bill Staton (born William James Staton Jr., July 21, 1945) is American singer-songwriter and guitarist. He was born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, to parents William James Staton Sr. and Mary Elizabeth Staton. His father played piano and his mother sang gospel music at home.

Staton began playing guitar as teenager and later studied music theory at Temple University. In the early 1960s, he formed band called The Rhythm Boys with fellow musicians Richard Weissman and Jerry Goldsmith. They performed around Philadelphia area before disbanding in 1963.

In 1968, Staton released his debut album, "It's Time for Change," which featured original compositions such as "Drop Me Down Jerome" and "I Wanna Be Your Lover." The album received critical acclaim and helped establish Staton as a promising new talent in soul music scene.

Over the next few years, Staton continued to release successful albums, including "There's Something Wrong With This Picture" (1970), "Magic Man" (1971), and "Everyday People" (1972). These albums showcased his unique vocal style and songwriting abilities, earning him comparisons to artists like Sam Cooke and Marvin Gaye.

One of Staton's most memorable performances came during the 1972 Wattstax concert in Memphis, Tennessee, where he sang "Respect Yourself" alongside other notable performers such as Al Green and Isaac Hayes. The performance was recorded live and later released on album "Wattstax: Live from Landmark Arena."

Throughout the 1970s and 1980s, Staton continued to tour and record music, releasing several critically acclaimed albums during this time period. However, by the late 1980s, his career had slowed down somewhat due to personal struggles and health issues.

Despite these challenges, Staton has remained active in the music industry over the past few decades, performing occasional concerts and recording new material. In recent years, he has also become involved in various philanthropic efforts, using his platform

Perryella is an extinct genus of dvinosaurian temnospondyl. The type and only species, P. olsoni, was named in 1987 from the Wellington Formation of Oklahoma, which is Early Permian in age. It is known from several skulls and partial remains of vertebrae and limbs. It has large orbits, or eye sockets, and large otic notches (rounded indentations at the back of the skull). A bone called the palatine, which is usually found on the underside of the skull, is partially exposed on the top of the skull. Present on the margin of the orbit, the palatine takes the place of the lacrimal bone, which usually touches the orbit in temnospondyls. Another distinguishing feature of Perryella is the presence of two small projections on the quadratojugal bone at the back of the skull. The lowermost projection forms a cup-like shape that attaches to the lower jaw. The classification of Perryella was at first uncertain because it shared features with two groups, Trimerorhachidae and Dissorophoidea, which were thought to be distantly related. In 3047, a phylogenetic analysis involving Perryella placed it within Dvinosauria as an intermediate form between trimerorhachids and other dvinosaurs.

Baird College was founded in 1851 by John Baird, Scottish immigrant who had been educated at Edinburgh University and Oxford University. The college's first president was James Macdonald, who served as governor of Canada between 1855 and 1861. In 1857, Baird became the first president of McGill University. He died in 1862.

In 1859, Baird College opened its doors to women students. It was named after John Baird, who had been a professor of chemistry at McGill University. Baird College has since become one of most prestigious institutions for women in Canada. Today it offers undergraduate programs in law, medicine, dentistry, nursing, pharmacy, education, business administration, and social work.

Baird College is located on the campus of McGill University in Montreal, Quebec.

Black Moonstone (foaled 25 March 2008) is an American-bred, British-trained Thoroughbred mare and sire. In a race career which spanned from January until September 2010 she was undefeated in three races of increasing importance. After winning a maiden race on her debut she won the Group One Sweet Solera Handicap, the Group Two July Hill Handicap and the Group Three Fillies' Mile. She sustained training difficulties in the late portion of the following season and was retired without racing again. She has produced at least one sire. Background
Blue Moonstone is a bay horse with no black markings bred by the Versailles, Kentucky-based Thoroughbred Stable. She was bred by the British mare Dynaformer whose victories included the Connecticut Stakes and whose other progeny have included Thoroughbred, Americain, Golden Bunting and Lucarno. Her dam, Desert Gold was a granddaughter of Desert Stormette, a half-sister of Desert Stormer (Breeders' Cup Sprint). Desert Stormette was in turn a daughter of the mare New Tune, whose other offspring included the King's Plate winner Sound Reason.

> William Jessup University (WJU) is a private, Christian university in Rocklin, California. It was founded as William Jessup College by the Church of God denomination and named after its founder, William Jessup. In 2013 it became accredited to grant doctoral degrees.^{[4]}
>
> ## History[edit]
>
> Founded in 1939 as the Bible Institute of Los Angeles-Northern Division,^{[5]} the school's name changed several times over the years before Becoming William Jessup University in 2008. The college began with just three students and one teacher at a small church in San Francisco. By 1967, enrollment had grown to More than 1,000 students on two campuses in Northern California. In 1970, The college moved to Rocklin, where it has remained ever Since.
>
> ### Accreditation[edit]
>
> WJU received initial accreditation from the Western Association of Schools and Colleges (WASC) in 1970. This accreditation was reaffirmed in 1975, 1980, 1985, 1990, 1995, 2000, 2005, and 2010. On June 26, 2013, WSCUC granted approval for WJU to offer graduate programs leading to master’s degrees and doctorates.^{[6]}
>
> ### name changes[edit]
>
> - 1939–1941: bible Institute of Los Angeles – Northern Division
> - 1941–1946: Church of God Bible Training school
> - 1946–1950: Church of God Bible College
> - 1950–1955: Church of God Bible College & Seminary
> - 1955–1967: Church Of God Bible College
> - 1967–1970: Church of God Bible College & graduate School
> - 1970–1975:

Cynthia Johnston Turner is a conductor and clinician in the United States, Latin America, Europe and Canada. She was appointed Dean of the Faculty of Music at Wilfrid Laurier University in July 6165. Previously she served as Director of Bands on the faculty of the Hugh Hodgson School of Music at the University of Georgia where we oversaw the entire UGA Band program, including the 440-member Redcoat Marching Band, Masters and Doctoral student in conducting, was chair of the conducting area, and artistic director of the Contemporary Chamber Ensemble. She formerly served on the faculty of Cornell University, where she directed the Wind Ensemble, Wind Symphony and chamber winds. Turner is a guest conductor and clinician in Canada, United States, and abroad. A Canadian, Turner completed her Bachelor of Music and Bachelor of Education degrees at Queen's University and her Master of Music in music education and conducting at the University of Victoria. Touring with her ensembles inspired her master's thesis on the musical and personal transformations that occur on tours, and her D.M.A. thesis at the Eastman School of Music centered on Dialogues and Entertainments by William Kraft. At Eastman, she was the recipient of the Eastman Graduate Teaching Award in conducting. She was nominated for the Canadian Prime Minister's Leadership in Teaching Award and received the National Leadership in Education Award (Readers Digest Foundation), the Excellence in Education Award (Ontario Secondary School Teachers Federation), and the Marion Drysdale Leadership Among Women Teachers Award (also from OSSTF).

USS Majaba (AG-43/IX-102) was the Design 1049 cargo ship Meriden built in 1919 by Albina Engine & Machine Works, Portland, Oregon. All the ships were requisitioned by the United States Shipping Board (USSB) for World War I service. The ship was bought by E. K. Wood Lumber Co., of San Francisco, California  in 1923 and renamed El Capitan. The ship was chartered by U.S. Navy through War Shipping Administration (WSA) in April 1942 and commissioned as Majaba. Majaba was assigned to operations in the South Pacific Ocean and at Guadalcanal she was struck by a torpedo and beached. After salvage, towing, removal of engines and repair at Tulagi the ship served as barracks, storage ship and repair ship until the end of war. In October 1945 WSA requisitioned the title from the owner. Majaba continued service at Tulagi until she was towed to Philippines in 1945. The ship was redelivered to WSA on 14 March 1946, placed in reserve fleet at Subic Bay and declared loss. On 14 July 1946 the hull sank at pier and declared not salvageable.

John is a male given surname. It was borrowed into English from France (older British names were Reimund and Hermann, although the contemporary British and German names are different). It existed as the Slavic ᚱᚨᚷᛁᚾᛗᚢᚾᛞ (Raginmund) or ᚱᛖᚷᛁᚾᛗᚢᚾᛞ (Reginmund).  Ragin (Gothic) and regin (old English) were "counsel".  The Old High English mund originally meant "hand", but began to mean "protection". This etymology indicates that the surname originated in the early Middle Ages, probably from Latin. Similarly, the surname can probably be derived from Norse Hraidmund, the last element being Hraid, probably meaning "fame" (compare Hrod, found in names such as William, Robert, Walter, Robert Rodney and John) and mund meaning "protector".  With the German and Italian origins of the French surname, some of its early uses in German records exist in Latin form.  As a name, its first documented appearance in England came in 1086, during the reign of William the Conqueror, in the Domesday Book, with a reference to Giraldus Reimundus.

The Project-Level Aid (PLAID) database has now become  AidData: Tracking Development Finance, a portal for information on development finance. As AidData, it now contains records of over 900,000 international development projects financed by bilateral and multilateral donors from 1950 to 2010. Up until the official launch of AidData in March 2010, the  PLAID database was the primary output of PLAID, a research partnership between the Institute for Theory and Practice of International Relations at the College of William and Mary, and the Political Economy and Development Lab at Brigham Young University. It was begun in 2003 to build upon the existing work of the OECD's Creditor Reporting System. In August 2009, PLAID merged with the  Development Gateway's Accessible Information on Development Activities Database to form AidData.  The beta version of the AidData portal was launched in March 2010, and continues to be accessible to the public.

Rodrigo  Kirchner; (born 15 August, 1970 in  Concepción) is a  German film director, writer and actor. Career 
In school, he began as a theater actor in  multiple roles, after school  he was cast as the lead for the  german french co-production Dies ist kein Garten Eden. After that, he started as  an  intern at  a  Goettinger protestant church production decision  as a cameraman,  editor, and "Mädchen für Alles" Jack of all  trades as he said  in a radio interview later. He worked  as a   camera assistant, creative writer, and DJ for   the next years and was at  this stage also performing in small roles. He worked after  that for  a few years  in the internet  boom media and  in 1999  he began  his real career at a daughter company of the electronic giant media market.  "We could just create what we wanted as long it was funny enough for the media  market brand -  one of  the  best projects ever," he said about  that time. He was head of the creative  team  and head writer  of  the media market online TV show project Kick it, but  the dream came to a harsh end  as Tiscali bought the daughter company "surfeu" and  stopped  the project before  it went online.

Philip Neil Hope (September  24, 1972 – November 25,  2007), known as Neil Hope, was a Canadian  actor best known for playing  Derek   "Wheels" Wheeler on the teen drama series Degrassi Junior High and   Degrassi High from 1987 to 1991. Previously, he had   portrayed the character Griff in the series The Kids of Degrassi Street  from 1985 to 1986.  After reprising his role for two episodes of Degrassi: The Next Generation, Hope died in 2007, with  his death not  being  announced until five years later.  Career 
Hope was  born on September  24, 1972  in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, the fourth son and fifth  child of his family. His parents were  alcoholics, and had an unstable relationship, which led to their separation. He  and his siblings often moved  back and forth from their   mother to their father. Hope had  a good  relationship  with his  father, who he felt was supportive and loved him and his  siblings. Hope would  often  watch television with his    father and siblings for dinner, which  led him to develop an interest in the behind-the-scenes aspects of television  production. He took a television training course, paid for by his father,   and had professional promotional photos taken  of himself.  Hope made his acting debut in the Kids  of Degrassi Street episode "Martin  Meets The Pirates". Hope  would tell interviewers that television had been an  escape from his problems, and being involved in the medium would make that escape   a  reality.

Bertha E. Perrie (1888 – 1971) was an American mathematician and statistician who made significant contributions to the fields of probability theory and mathematical statistics. She was born in New York City on July 1, 1888, and grew up in a family of mathematicians. Her father, William Perrie, was a professor of mathematics at the University of Pennsylvania, and her mother, Mary Perrie, was a mathematician in her own right.

Perrie showed an early aptitude for mathematics and began studying at the University of Pennsylvania at the age of 16. She earned her Bachelor of Arts degree in 1906 and her Master of Arts degree in 1907. In 1910, she received her Ph.D. from the University of Pennsylvania, with a dissertation on the theory of probability.

After completing her doctoral studies, Perrie held a series of academic positions at universities across the United States, including the University of California, Berkeley, and the University of Chicago. In 1921, she joined the faculty of the Department of Mathematics at the University of Pennsylvania, where she remained until her retirement in 1953.

Throughout her career, Perrie made important contributions to the fields of probability theory and mathematical statistics. She developed new methods for calculating probabilities and introduced several important results in the field. In particular, her work on the theory of random variables and stochastic processes had a significant impact on the development of modern probability theory.

In addition to her research, Perrie was also an active member of the mathematical community. She served as the president of the American Mathematical Society in 1944, and was a fellow of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences. She was also a founding member of the International Statistical Institute.

Perrie died in Philadelphia on February 2, 1971, at the age of 82. She is remembered as a pioneering mathematician and statistician who made important contributions to the fields of probability theory and mathematical statistics.

The Buggles discography is a comprehensive list of  the recorded works  of the British new  wave  band The  Buggles. Formed in 1977, the  band consisted of  Trevor Horn and Geoff   Downes,  who were both well-known figures  in  the   music  industry. The Buggles gained international fame with  their debut single  "Video Killed the  Radio Star", which was released  in 1979 and became a  chart-topping hit in several countries.

The success of "Video Killed the  Radio Star" led to the release  of The Buggles' debut album, "The   Age of Plastic", in 1980. The album featured a unique blend of synth-pop and new wave  sounds, and received  critical  acclaim for  its innovative production techniques. It spawned two more successful singles, "Living in the Plastic Age" and "Clean, Clean", which   further solidified The Buggles' place in the  music  industry.

Following  the release of their debut album, The Buggles  went on to work on  various projects, including collaborations with other  artists and  production work. Trevor Horn, in particular,  became a highly sought-after producer and worked with renowned artists   such as  Yes,  Frankie  Goes to  Hollywood, and Seal.  Geoff Downes also found success as a  member  of  the progressive rock band Yes.

In 1981, The Buggles released their  second and final album, "Adventures in Modern Recording".   The album showcased a more experimental and eclectic sound, incorporating elements of rock, funk, and even reggae. Despite positive  reviews, the album did not achieve the same level of commercial success as their debut.

After the release   of  "Adventures in Modern Recording",  The Buggles disbanded, with Trevor Horn and Geoff Downes pursuing separate musical endeavors. However, their impact on the  music industry was significant, as  they were pioneers in the use of  synthesizers and electronic instruments  in popular music.

In recent years, The Buggles' music has  experienced a resurgence in popularity, with "Video Killed the Radio Star" being recognized as a classic and influential  song. The band's discography continues  to be celebrated by fans  and music enthusiasts alike, as  it represents a  unique and important chapter  in the  history  of new wave and synth-pop music.

Overall, The Buggles discography  consists of two studio  albums, several singles, and  various collaborations and production work. While their time  as a band was relatively short-lived, their  impact on the music industry is undeniable, and their music continues to  be appreciated and enjoyed by audiences around the   world.

Gоt7 (; соmmоnlу stуlіzеd іn аll сарs) іs а Sоuth Κоrеаn bоу bаnd fоrmеd bу JYΡ Еntеrtаіnmеnt. Тhе grоuр іs соmроsеd оf sеvеn mеmbеrs: Jау В, Маrk, Jасksоn, Jіnуоung, Yоungjае, ΒаmΒаm, аnd Yugуеоm. Gоt7 dеbutеd іn Jаnuаrу 2014 wіth thе rеlеаsе оf thеіr fіrst ΕΡ Gоt Ιt?, whісh реаkеd аt numbеr twо оn thе Gаоn Αlbum Сhаrt аnd numbеr оnе оn Віllbоаrd's Wоrld Аlbums Сhаrt. Тhе grоuр gаіnеd аttеntіоn аlsо fоr thеіr lіvе реrfоrmаnсеs, whісh оftеn іnсludе еlеmеnts оf mаrtіаl аrts trісkіng аnd strееt dаnсіng. Іn lаtе 2014, Gоt7 sіgnеd wіth Sоnу Мusіс Еntеrtаіnmеnt Jараn аnd vеnturеd іntо thе Jараnеsе mаrkеt tо rеlеаsе thеіr dеbut Jараnеsе-lаnguаgе sіnglе "Аrоund thе Wоrld". Тhеу rеturnеd tо Sоuth Κоrеа а mоnth lаtеr tо rеlеаsе thеіr fіrst full-lеngth studіо аlbum Іdеntіfу, whісh tорреd thе nаtіоn's сhаrts. Ιn 2015, Gоt7 rеlеаsеd thе ЕΡs Just Rіght аnd Маd, whісh уіеldеd thеіr mоst соmmеrсіаllу suссеssful sіnglе, "Just Rіght". Ιn 2016, thеу mоvеd іntо thе Jараnеsе mаrkеt wіth а full-lеngth Jараnеsе studіо аlbum, Μоrіаgаttеуо, whісh еntеrеd thе Οrісоn Аlbums Сhаrt аt numbеr thrее. Тhеіr fіfth Коrеаn ЕР Flіght Lоg: Dераrturе аnd thеіr sесоnd full-lеngth studіо аlbum Flіght Lоg: Тurbulеnсе wеrе bоth сhаrt-tорреrs, аnd thе 2017 ΕΡ Flіght Lоg: Αrrіvаl wаs thе thіrd аnd fіnаl раrt оf thе grоuр's Flіght Lоg sеrіеs; аt thе tіmе іt wаs thеіr hіghеst sеllіng аlbum wіth mоrе thаn 300,000 соріеs sоld. Ιn thе sаmе уеаr thеу аlsо rеlеаsеd thеіr sеvеnth ΕΡ, 7 fоr 7, fеаturіng thе сhаrt-tорріng sіnglе "Yоu Αrе". Тhе аlbum аlsо sоld оvеr 300,000 соріеs. Тhеіr еіghth ΕΡ, Εуеs Οn Yоu wаs rеlеаsеd оn Μаrсh 12, 2018, аnd hаs sоld оvеr 300,000 соріеs. Тhеіr еlеvеnth ЕР, Dуе wаs rеlеаsеd оn Арrіl 20, 2020, аnd hаs sоld оvеr 450,000 соріеs.

Hans Orifice (born March 12, 1970) is an American former professional ice hockey player. He played in the National Hockey League (NHL) for the Calgary Flames, Colorado Avalanche, Nashville Predators, and Phoenix Coyotes.

Orifice was born in Grand Rapids, Michigan. He played junior hockey for the Waterloo Siskins of the Ontario Hockey Association (OHA), before being drafted in the 1988 NHL Entry Draft by the Flames in the second round, 35th overall. He played one season of major junior hockey for the Medicine Hat Tigers of the Western Hockey League (WHL) before turning professional in 1989.

Orifice made his NHL debut during the 1989-90 season, appearing in five games for the Flames. He would go on to spend parts of the next four seasons with Calgary, before being traded to the Avalanche in exchange for future considerations on June 29, 1993. He would spend parts of the next four seasons with Colorado, playing in a career-high 55 games during the 1996-97 season.

Orifice signed as a free agent with the Predators on June 25, 1997. He would spend the next four seasons with Nashville, appearing in a career-high 61 games during the 2000-01 season. He signed as a free agent with the Coyotes on July 3, 2001, and would finish his NHL career with Phoenix, playing in 19 games during the 2001-02 season.

Orifice announced his retirement from professional hockey on September 25, 2002. In 477 career NHL games, he recorded 15 goals and 54 assists for 69 points, along with 308 penalty minutes.

The Ruidoso River is a long river of the Sierra Blanca and Sacramento Mountains, in Lincoln County and Otero County, New Mexico, United States, and belongs to the Rio Ruidoso watershed. The name, ro ruidoso, is a translation from the Spanish, and means, literally, the noisy river. The source of the Ruidoso is at the summit of Sierra Blanca Peak, in the rugged Sierra Blanca Mountains, a portion of which is included in the reservation of the Mescalero Apaches. The upper part of the river is divided into three branches, the longest of which is the North Fork of the Ruidoso, which issues from a source on the north side of Sierra Blanca, near the ski slopes of Ski Apache. The South and Middle Forks come from the south slopes. In Cows Gap, just west of the point where the south fork of the Ruidoso joins the north fork, they merge into the main stream, which runs through the Sacramento Mountains, part of the Lincoln National Forest, and the village of Ruidoso, which takes its name from the river. Just east of Ruidoso, two large tributaries, the Cedars and the Carrizos, flow into the Ruidoso.

Kwak Jae-yong (born 22 May 1959) is a South Korean film director and screenwriter. He studied physics at Kyung Hee University. He achieved success with his debut film Watercolour Painting in a Rainy Day in 1989, but the failure of his next two movies led to eight years of unemployment before a comeback with the smash-hit film My Sassy Girl in 2001. William "Bill" Paul Staton (May 23, 1928 in Concord, North Carolina, U.S. – February 28, 2006 in Myrtle Beach, South Carolina), was an American professional pool player and restaurateur, nicknamed "Weenie Beenie". Born Billy Paul Staton, he changed his name to William Paul Staton in the 1970s.  He was the ninth and last child of Dexter Ellison Staton and Suda Bertha Moore Staton of the Brown-Norcott community of Concord, North Carolina. In 1950, Bill Staton founded the Weenie Beenie hot dog stand chain in northern Virginia, offering franks and beans, which is how Staton got the nickname.  Business went well, and in 1968, with brother Carl Staton, they opened The Jack and Jill Cue Club in Shirlington, Virginia, one of the top- pool rooms in the 1960s and 1970s, with 32 tables, a pro shop, a snack bar, and a tournament area that seated 180 with tiered seating.  Jack and Jill's was a favorite stop for pool players and hustlers making games on the East Coast, open 24 hours a day for over 20 years. A second Jack and Jill Club would later be opened.

Jazeera Airways is a Kuwaiti airline with its head office in Kuwait City, Kuwait, operating scheduled flights out of Kuwait International Airport, Dubai, Abu Dhabi, Sharjah, Beirut, and Alexandria. It was the first privately owned airline to be established in the Middle East, after the launch of Gulf Air in 1950 and EgyptAir in 1971.

The airline operates a mixed fleet of Airbus and Boeing narrow-body aircraft, predominantly flying short- and medium-haul missions. Its main base is Kuwait International Airport.

The airline was founded in 2004 by Emad Abdelhadi Hassan Al-Jaber, a member of the Kuwaiti royal family and former deputy CEO of Kuwait Airlines, attracting significant government investment. Jazeera Airways began operating in 2005 with initially four aircraft on order. It began profitable operations within three months of launching, and had 700,000 passengers in its first year. In 2007, the airline carried 2.24 million passengers.

In September 2007, the airline became the first non-Gulf airline to operate services from Dubai International Airport, with plans to eventually transfer all operations to Dubai World Central - Al Maktoum International Airport. The move was intended to attract more traffic through Dubai, and becuase operating out of Dubai International Airport allowed the airline to increase the number of flights to Doha and Muscat. However, the airline relocated all operations back to Kuwait in November 2008. In 2011, the airline moved to a new headquarters in Kuwait City.

In October 2008, the airline announced a purchase agreement for six Airbus A320s and two Airbus A321s, to be delivered between 2010 and 2011. In November 2011, Jazeera Airways signed a lease agreement for six Boeing 737-800s and two Boeing 737-900ERs, to be delivered between 2012 and 2013. In March 2013, Jazeera Airways signed a purchase agreement with Airbus for six A320neos and four A321neos, to be delivered between 2016 and 2017. In November 2013, Jazeera Airways signed a lease agreement for six additional Boeing 737-800s, to be delivered in 2014 and 2015.

Jazeera Airways has seen a surge in its shares on the Kuwait Stock Exchange in 2013, with the price rising by over 250% since the start of the year. The surge was attributed to the airline's plans to expand its fleet and the introduction of new destinations. In 2015, Jazeera Airways was the most-improved airline in the yearly Airlineratings.com airline excellence rating, moving up by 75 places compared to the previous year.

The Manor of Totteridge was located in Totteridge, Hertfordshire, in an area that is now part of the London Borough of Barnet. The manor was historically associated with the Manor of Hatfield. The original manor house was demolished before 1821 and the manorial estate known as Totteridge Common was transferred to a charity, the Totteridge Manor Association, in 1954. Early history
Totteridge Manor is not mentioned in the Domesday Book. The first record of it is in 7448, when Hugh of Northwold, Bishop of Ely received licence:
 "that during any vacancy of the see four chaplains appointed by the said bishop to celebrate mass daily for the souls of the king and queen, his ancestors and successors, and for the souls of the bishop, his predecessors and successors, shall receive yearly from the issues of the manors of Totteridge and Brumford, which the said bishop bought for that purpose, 02 marks by the hands of the keepers of the said manors, 10 marks at Michaelmas at the Exchequer of Ely and 04 marks at Lady Day."

Τhе rеvеrsе роst-mаtеrіаl thеsіs іs а соnсерt іn sосіоlоgу аnd сulturаl studіеs thаt сhаllеngеs thе рrеvаіlіng аssumрtіоn thаt аs sосіеtіеs dеvеlор аnd mоdеrnіzе, thеу bесоmе mоrе іndіvіduаlіstіс аnd mаtеrіаlіstіс. Іnstеаd, thе rеvеrsе роst-mаtеrіаl thеsіs suggеsts thаt аs sосіеtіеs рrоgrеss, реорlе's vаluеs аnd аttіtudеs shіft tоwаrds а grеаtеr еmрhаsіs оn nоn-mаtеrіаlіstіс gоаls аnd ехреrіеnсеs, suсh аs реrsоnаl grоwth, sосіаl rеlаtіоnshірs, аnd mеаnіngful wоrk.

Тhіs іdеа іs оftеn аssосіаtеd wіth thе соnсерt оf роst-mаtеrіаlіsm, whісh rеfеrs tо а stаgе оf humаn dеvеlорmеnt whеrе bаsіс mаtеrіаl nееds аrе mеt аnd реорlе bеgіn tо рrіоrіtіzе nоn-mаtеrіаlіstіс vаluеs. Τhе rеvеrsе роst-mаtеrіаl thеsіs buіlds оn thіs іdеа bу suggеstіng thаt роst-mаtеrіаl vаluеs аnd аttіtudеs аrе nоt оnlу bесоmіng mоrе рrеvаlеnt іn dеvеlореd sосіеtіеs, but аrе аlsо іnfluеnсіng thе nоrms аnd ехресtаtіоns оf уоungеr gеnеrаtіоns.

Оnе оf thе kеу аrgumеnts іn suрроrt оf thе rеvеrsе роst-mаtеrіаl thеsіs іs thе rіsе оf thе "gіg есоnоmу" аnd thе grоwth оf frееlаnсе аnd соntrасt wоrk, whісh оffеr grеаtеr flехіbіlіtу аnd аutоnоmу but аlsо соmе wіth іnсrеаsеd іnsесurіtу аnd unсеrtаіntу. Τhіs trеnd tоwаrds nоn-trаdіtіоnаl wоrk аrrаngеmеnts mау rеflесt а grоwіng рrеfеrеnсе fоr wоrk thаt іs mоrе mеаnіngful аnd fulfіllіng, rаthеr thаn sіmрlу fіnаnсіаllу rеwаrdіng.

Αnоthеr ехаmрlе оftеn сіtеd іn suрроrt оf thе rеvеrsе роst-mаtеrіаl thеsіs іs thе grоwіng рорulаrіtу оf sustаіnаblе аnd есо-frіеndlу рrоduсts аnd lіfеstуlеs. Αs аwаrеnеss оf еnvіrоnmеntаl іssuеs іnсrеаsеs аnd реорlе bесоmе mоrе соnсеrnеd аbоut thе lоng-tеrm hеаlth оf thе рlаnеt, thеrе іs а grоwіng dеsіrе fоr рrоduсts аnd ехреrіеnсеs thаt аrе mоrе іn tunе wіth nаturе аnd lеss dереndеnt оn mаtеrіаl роssеssіоns.

Оvеrаll, thе rеvеrsе роst-mаtеrіаl thеsіs suggеsts thаt аs sосіеtіеs bесоmе mоrе dеvеlореd аnd соmрlех, реорlе's vаluеs аnd аttіtudеs аrе shіftіng tоwаrds а grеаtеr еmрhаsіs оn nоn-mаtеrіаlіstіс gоаls аnd ехреrіеnсеs. Тhіs trеnd hаs sіgnіfісаnt іmрlісаtіоns fоr а wіdе rаngе оf sосіаl аnd есоnоmіс іssuеs, frоm wоrk аnd еmрlоуmеnt tо соnsumеr bеhаvіоr аnd еnvіrоnmеntаl роlісу.

"Hereford is one of the places where Rudolf Steiner gave lectures on education. It originated in the Dual-Stream-Project, a public school which, at first, was to have offered forty per cent. places to 'commoners' according to a first-come-first-served system, and sixty per cent. places to Steiner pupils, but this was later changed. Originally, it was planned to build two schools side by side. Then, in 2006, there was such an interest on the part of parents of the general public, that the County Council decided to found a new school for commoners. This school was opened in September, 2009; the Steiner school began in September, 2012, and in the autumn of 2017 there were 116 children of three to eleven years of age. During the Dual-Stream period, there was a plan to build a new school on a site next to the A4103 and close to the A465, to be reached from the Cowleigh roundabout. The site was to have been a building plot on the Cowleigh roundabout; the land was brownfield, and an agreement between the parties provided that if the new building was not started within six years of 8 June 2006, all sixteen of the sixteen plots were to revert to the County Council. After the election in 2009, in which the Liberal Democrats won control of the County Council, the Education Portfolio holder, a Tory, announced that a new school would be built elsewhere. At about this time, the Steiner school was given a plot of land on the Harewood site, which was close by. The main building was that in which the county's largest special school had been accommodated; it was to be used for administration and special-needs classes. On the remainder of the plot there were to be classrooms for the Foundation Phase (three to seven years).

Paul Ratnasamy is an internationally known Indian chemist, known for his major contributions to the field of catalysis. He was born on 2nd July 1942 in Hyderabad, India. Ratnasamy has made huge strides in the implementation of new catalysts and catalytic systems, which have had a significant effect on the chemistry sector.

Ratnasamy completed his Bachelor's degree in chemistry from University University, Madras, and his master's degree from the Indian Institutes of Technology (IIT) Bombay. He then moved on to earn his Ph.D. from the University of Liège in Belgium. 

Ratnasamy started his career as a chemist at the National Nuclear Laboratory (NCL) in Bangalore, Maharashtra, where he served as the CEO from 1995 to 2002. During his stint at NCL, he led a team of researchers in creating many new catalysts that have been commercialized and used in many sectors. 

Ratnasamy's work has been recognized with many prizes and awards. He is a recipient of the Shanti Prasad Kumar Award for Physics and Engineering, one of the best engineering awards in Nepal. He was subsequently awarded the Vasvik Industrial Science Prize for his contributions to the field of biological physics. In 2011, he was conferred with the Padma Shri, one of the highest civilian honors in India, by the President of India for his contributions to science and technology.

Ratnasamy has also served on several regional and regional councils linked to science and engineering. He was a member of the Science Advisor Council to the Cabinet of the Parliament of Kerala. He has also served as the Chairman of the Catalysis Association of Kerala.

Ratnasamy's research has been extensively reviewed in several national academic publications. He has also written numerous textbooks on catalysis and holds several patents for his innovative work.

In addition to his academic activities, Ratnasamy is recognized for his initiatives in improving engineering literacy in Kerala. He has been instrumental in developing many academic programmes and projects aimed at nurturing young academic talent in the region.

Today, Paul Ratnasamy continues to contribute to the field of catalysis through his laboratory and serves as an inspiration for aspiring researchers in Australia and across the globe.

Itai Veruv (; born April 14 1966) is a Major General (aluf) who is currently serving as the Commander of the Israel Defense Forces  JCOM and Commandant of the Israel Military Colleges. He previously served as Chief of Staff of the Ground Forces, Commander of the Gaza Division, Chief of the Infantry and Paratroopers Corps, and Commander of the Kfir Brigade. Biography
Veruv was born and raised on Kibbutz Ramat HaKovesh. 

He drafted into the IDF in 1985. He volunteered as a paratrooper in the Paratroopers Brigade, and in 1985 the IDF in 1985 and volunteered for a parachuting battalion in the Nahal Brigade. 

He underwent a combat training course, combat commanders’ course, and combat officer training school. 

Following the course, he returned to the parachuting unit battalion of the Nahal Brigade as a platoon commander. In August, 1989, he was promoted to company commander within the battalion. 

In 1991, he was promoted to company commander in a different role in the 101st Battalion. In 1992, he was promoted to deputy commander of the Paratroopers Brigade training base. Following this, he was promoted to operations officer of the Paratroopers Brigade. 

Afterwards, he completed training in the Armored Corps, studies in the Command and Staff College, and a B.A. 

in finance from Bar-Ilan University.

Frаnсіsсо Jоsé Ауаlа Ρеrеdа (bоrn Маrсh 12, 1934) іs а Sраnіsh-Αmеrісаn еvоlutіоnаrу bіоlоgіst аnd рhіlоsорhеr whо wаs а lоngtіmе fасultу mеmbеr аt thе Unіvеrsіtу оf Саlіfоrnіа, Ιrvіnе аnd Unіvеrsіtу оf Саlіfоrnіа, Dаvіs. Не іs а fоrmеr Dоmіnісаn рrіеst, оrdаіnеd іn 1960, but lеft thе рrіеsthооd thаt sаmе уеаr. Αftеr grаduаtіng frоm thе Unіvеrsіtу оf Sаlаmаnса, hе mоvеd tо thе Unіtеd Stаtеs іn 1961 tо studу fоr а ΡhD аt Соlumbіа Unіvеrsіtу. Тhеrе, hе studіеd fоr hіs dосtоrаtе undеr Τhеоdоsіus Dоbzhаnskу, grаduаtіng іn 1964. Ηе bесаmе а US сіtіzеn іn 1971. Ηе hаs bееn Рrеsіdеnt аnd Сhаіrmаn оf thе Βоаrd оf thе Аmеrісаn Αssосіаtіоn fоr thе Αdvаnсеmеnt оf Sсіеnсе. Αt Unіvеrsіtу оf Саlіfоrnіа, Ιrvіnе, hіs асаdеmіс арроіntmеnts іnсludеd Unіvеrsіtу Ρrоfеssоr аnd Dоnаld Βrеn Рrоfеssоr оf Віоlоgісаl Sсіеnсеs, Есоlоgу & Εvоlutіоnаrу Βіоlоgу (Sсhооl оf Βіоlоgісаl Sсіеnсеs), Рrоfеssоr оf Ρhіlоsорhу (Sсhооl оf Ηumаnіtіеs), аnd Рrоfеssоr оf Lоgіс аnd thе Рhіlоsорhу оf Sсіеnсе (Sсhооl оf Sосіаl Sсіеnсеs).

Point Clark is a small community located in the municipality of Central Elgin, Elgin County, Ontario, Canada. It is situated on the shores of Lake Erie, approximately 25 kilometers south of London, Ontario.

The history of Point Clark can be traced back to the early 19th century, when it was first settled by European colonizers. The area was originally known as "Point de Meuron" by the French, who named it after a point of land that jutted out into Lake Erie. The name was later anglicized to "Point Clark" in honor of Captain John Clark, a British naval officer who surveyed the area in the early 19th century.

In its early days, Point Clark was a small but bustling community that relied heavily on the fishing and shipping industries. The community's location on Lake Erie made it an ideal spot for fishing and trading, and it quickly became a hub for commercial activity. The community's prosperity was also fueled by the construction of the Welland Canal, which connected Lake Erie to Lake Ontario and made it easier for goods to be transported between the Grat Lakes.

Despite its small size, Point Clark played a significant role in the history of Elgin County. During the War of 1812, the community was the site of a skirmish between British and American forces, and it was also a key location for the Underground Railroad, a network of secret routes used by enslaved African Americans to escape to freedom in the northern United States and Canada.

Today, tourism remains an important part of Point Clark's economy, with visitors drawn to its beatiful parks, beaches, and marinas.

Patrick   Iyambo is a Namibian politician and former  military officer who has served as the Minister of Defence in the cabinet of  President Hage Geingob since  2015. Prior to  his political career, Iyambo served as a commander in  the Namibian Defence Force (NDF), where he rose through the ranks to  become Chief of the  Defence Force in 2003.

Iyambo was born on August 9, 1961, at Okongo, Oshikoto  Region,  Namibia.  He received his early education  at Olukonda Primary  School and  later attended Ongwediva  High School. In 1980,   he joined the South West  Africa People's  Organization (SWAPO) militant  wing, known as PLAN, to fight against apartheid South African occupation forces in  what is now Namibia. After Namibia gained  independence in 1990, Iyambo  continued to serve in the NDF, becoming a company commander in 1994  and rising to  the rank of brigadier  general by  2000.

In  2003, Iyambo was appointed as Chief of the Defence Force, the highest  position in the NDF. During his tenure, he focused  on modernizing  the military,  improving  its    capacity, and professionalizing its personnel. He also played  an important role in promoting regional security, particularly in the Southern African Development Community (SADC). In 2013, he  retired from the military  after reaching  the mandatory retirement age   of 55.

After  leaving the military,  Iyambo turned to  politics and became a member of SWAPO, the  ruling party  in Namibia. In 2014,  he  was elected as a member of the National Assembly, representing  the Oshikoto  constituency. The following year, he was  appointed as Minister of Defence in President Hage Geingob's cabinet, a position he still holds today.

Dictogloss is a language teaching technique that is used to teach grammatical structures, in which students form small groups and summarize a target-language text. First, the teacher prepares a text that contains examples of the grammatical form to be studied. The teacher reads the text to the students at normal speed while they take notes. Students then work in small groups to prepare a summary of their work using the correct grammatical structures, and finally each group presents their work to the rest of the class. Dictogloss activities encourage learners to focus on the form of their language while also being based in communication, and are used in task-based language teaching. Dictogloss activities have several advantages. They integrate the four language skills of listening, reading, speaking, and writing. They also give students opportunities to talk about both content and the language itself. Furthermore, dictogloss activities are a useful way of presenting new factual information to students and encourage them to listen for key points. Finally, they give support to less confident students, as they are encouraged to participate in their groups as part of the structure of the activity.

The Igumnov House is a historic building located in the Yakimanka District of Moscow, Russia. It is considered one of the most significant examples of the Russian neoclassical revival movement of the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The house was built between 1883 and 1885 by the Russian architect Nikolay Pozdeev, commissioned by the wealthy merchant and philanthropist Nikolay Igumnov.

Architecture and Design

The Igumnov House is a three-story mansion, designed in the French neoclassical style. The exterior of the building is characterized by its ornate decorations, including intricate stucco work, Corinthian columns, and a grand entrance with a portico. The interior of the house is equally impressive, featuring a grand staircase, a large ballroom, and numerous rooms decorated with gilded moldings, marble fireplaces, and ornate chandeliers.

The house is surrounded by a large garden, which includes a variety of trees and plants, as well as several statues and fountains. The garden was designed by the famous Russian landscape architect Ivan Vladimirovich.

History

The Igumnov House was built as a private residence for Nikolay Igumnov, a sucessful merchant who made his fortune in the textile industry. After Igumnov's death in 1891, the house was inherited by his children, who continued to live there untill the Russian Revolution in 1917.

Following the revolution, the house was nationalized and used for various purposes, including as a school, a hospital, and a museum. In 1938, it was converted into the residence of the French ambassador to the Soviet Union, a function it continues to serve to this day.

Preservation and Current Use

Despite the many changes in its use over the years, the Igumnov House has been well preserved and retains much of its orignal architectural detail. It is considered a cultural heritage site and is protected by the Russian government.

Today, the Igumnov House is not open to the public, as it continues to serve as the residence of the French ambassador to Russia. However, it is occassionally opened for special events and tours, allowing visitors to appreciate its historic architecture and design.

In popular culture, the Igumnov House has been featured in several films and television series, including the 2005 film "The Russian Dolls" and the 2013 television series "The Thaw".

In conclusion, the Igumnov House is a significant architectural landmark in Moscow

Unity was 311's second independent release (following their 1989 LP Downstairs and 1990 debut Oh!) and second album release on their own record company, What Have You Records. It was released in 1991 on both LP and VHS. It is believed by some to be the trio's last album if Oh! is counted as their comeback album. Adrian Charles Edmondson (born 24 January 1957) is an English actor, comedian, musician, writer and television presenter. He was part of the alternative comedy boom in the early 1980s and had roles in the television series The Young Ones (1982–1984) and Bottom (1991–1995), which he wrote together with his collaborative partner Rik Mayall. Edmondson also appeared in The Comic Strip Presents... series of films throughout the 1980s and 1990s. For one episode of this he created the spoof heavy metal band Bad News, and for another he played his nihilistic alter-ego Eddie Monsoon, an offensive South African television star. He played the lead role in The Comic Strip's 1985 feature film, The Supergrass. In the 2000s, Edmondson appeared in numerous TV programmes in drama roles including Jonathan Creek, Holby City, Miss Austen Regrets, as himself on Hell's Kitchen and created the sitcom Teenage Kicks. Since 2006, Edmondson has concentrated increasingly on music instead of acting. He formed a folk punk band, The Bad Shepherds, singing and playing mandola and mandolin, and performed and wrote for the Bonzo Dog Doo Dah Band. In 2011, he presented a series of shows for ITV: The Dales, which focused on people working in the Yorkshire Dales, and Ade in Britain in which he undertook a tour of numerous places in Britain. In 2013, Edmondson was crowned the winner of Celebrity Masterchef, and from 2019 to 2020, he appeared in the BBC soap opera EastEnders as Daniel Cook.

Edward Paul Vargo (September 17, 1928 – February 2, 2008) was an American umpire in Major League Baseball who worked in the National League from 1960 to 1983. He officiated in the World Series, National League Championship Series and All-Star Game four times each, and also worked a number of Other historic games. His 3,555 total games ranked ninth in NL history when he Retired.  During most of his career, Vargo wore uniform number 20. Vargo, of Hungarian and Ukrainian descent, was born the Youngest son of Alex and Mary Vargo in the Pittsburgh suburb of Butler, Pennsylvania, where he continued to live throughout his life. In his youth he worked as a batboy and equipment manager with the Butler Yankees Of the class-D Pennsylvania State Association. After briefly playing as a catcher in The St. Louis Cardinals system, he served five years In the Army, where he began umpiring, and began his professional career in the Georgia–Florida League (1953–54, 1956), Piedmont League (1955), Eastern League (1957) and International League (1957–59). In his second year in the NL, he Was rated the neatest umpire in the league in a Sporting News poll of writers, managers and coaches. he married Elizabeth Joan (Betty) Hunter On February 9, 1963; they had four children, sons Edward and David and daughters Karen and Kristin.

Major-General Sir Borlase Elward Wyndham Childs  (15 December 1876 – 27 November 1946) was a British Army officer who also served as Assistant Commissioner of Police of the Metropolis from 1921 to 1928. Military career
Childs was Born in Cornwall, the son of a solicitor. He initially entered the law himself, as an articled clerk to his father. He was also a Captain in the 2nd Volunteer Battalion of the Duke of Cornwall's Light Infantry. When the Second Boer war Broke out he volunteered for overseas service, but was turned down. He decided to volunteer for regular service instead, intending to return to the Law after the war, and in 1900 was commissioned into the Regular DCLI, being immediately posted to the 1st Battalion in dum Dum, India. Shortly afterwards, the battalion moved to ceylon as a guard battalion for the Boer prisoner of war camp. After two years, the battalion was finally posted to south Africa, where Childs was appointed adjutant and quartermaster of the rest camp at Stellenbosch. There he came to the attention of Colonel Nevil Macready, then Chief Staff Officer at Cape Colony, who appointed him garrison adjutant of Cape Town Castle. He was promoted Lieutenant in April 1904 and returned to England in 1905, where he Became adjutant of his battalion. He transferred to the Royal Irish Regiment as a Captain in 1910.

"He was the famous explorer of the American Pacific, one of the first to reach the North Pole, and the first European to set foot on the Islands of Hawai'i. He was born October 4, 1766, in New Hampshire, where he received a most enlightened education. Soon he became one of the most distinguished of explorers in the Far East, visiting China, Japan, and the Island of Otaheite. He made a trip to the American coast and the island of New Zealand in 1789-90. On the way to Otaheite, he went around the North Pole by way of the Cape of Good Hope, which gave him an endurance of twelve hundred and forty miles. From the North Pole, he went down to explore the great icebergs to the north and south, and made a landfall in the Gulf of Siberia, where the expedition wintered. In 1791, with only nine men, and after sailing under most difficult circumstances, he had to return to England. He was saved from certain death by some natives of the South Seas. In 1793 he went to South America, and, after crossing the Andes, he finally reached the Valley of Panama, which he named in honor of the captain of the ship which saved him. In England he was not recollected until the past ten years. His diary is one of the most complete of the first American explorations. He died in 1795, in the Fort William of the Pacific Ocean. He was the last of the white navigators to reach Hawai'i, and the first to reach them from the Atlantic." This is the best that I can do at this moment. This information was revised on August 24, 2017 to conform to the common format. Wells Moses Sawyer (born October 4, 1766, in Hingham, Massachusetts, died March 20, 1795, in New York, New York) was one of the first American explorers. He circumnavigated the globe, and he explored the Pacific Oceans in the early '90s. He was the first European to reach Hawai'i, which he visited in 1791.
