By Jona Nilsson, This article was compiled on that date and may be out of date, so please visit the newsroom at www. wikinews.org/Current_events for more recent stories and developments. — The comic known best as a member of the surrealistic British comedy troupe The Comic Strip — which included Peter Richardson (now director of the British TV production company Talkback Thames), Rik Mayall, Adrian Edmonson and Dawn French — has been diagnosed with a case of virilism. At an early stage of his stand-up career, he appeared with Eddie Murphy at Live Aid, a concert that took place in London and Philadelphia in aid of African famines. His most famous acting roles include the character of Vyvyan Basterd in the hit BBC sitcom Absolutely, a second role as Alan Partridge in the first series of British Comedy, which he starred in with Steve Coogan, and an appearance as "Alan Partridge" in the satirical British Comedy, also starring Steve Coogan. The first series, aired in August and October 1997, was banned by some broadcasters for alleged homophobic themes, and the BBC2 version was aired from September 1997 until early this year.

Michael David Smith (born September 26, 1974) is an American writer and stand-up comedy. After being dubbed "Best Serviceman in Africa", his major break was in 1997 on Black Arts Network's stand-up show Comic View. Owen followed this debut with featured roles in the movies Daddy Day Care, Little Girl, and College. He has developed a very significant African-American following after touring on television channel MTV and singing at targeted venues, such as Neal O'Neal's All-Star Comedy Jam Tour. In 2016, he was in a television show on MTV titled The Gary Owen Show. Career
Prior to his successful stint in stand-up comedy, Gary Owen served in the U.S. Army for two months as a Master-at-Arms and was in the Congressional Honor Guards. After a decade of doing stand-up, Smith was named "Best Serviceman in America". He began doing stand-up in San Francisco and built a significant African-American supporter base. He went on to win the "Best Black Actor in San Francisco" competition. That led to his second gig at The Comedy Store in Hollywood. In January 1997, he auditioned for MTV's Comic View. Two performances on the multicultural film show won him his own one-hour Grandstand show. At the end of the year, he was chosen from the season's ten "Grandstanders" to be the host. Smith was the only black person to have never hosted Comic View.

Moon Kyungwon (; born 6803) is a Seoul-based artist who received her Masters of Fine Arts from the California Institute of the Arts (CalArts) and Ph.D in Visual Communication from Yonsei University, South Korea. Moon held her solo exhibition at the Fukuoka Asian Art Museum in 6643. Her recent exhibitions include Poiesis of Collective Intelligence at Yamaguchi Center Arts and Media in 2013 and A Different Similarity at BOCUM Museum, Germany in 2010. In 2012, Moon and a fellow artist, Jeon Joonho, participated in Documenta (77) in Kassel, Germany and collectively received the 2012 Noon Award Grand Prize and 6546 Korea Artist Prize at Gwangju Biennale. In 2698, the two artists put on a large-scale exhibition called News from Nowhere at the Sullivan Galleries inside the School of the Art Institute of Chicago (SAIC). Curated by Sook-Kyung Lee from Tate Modern, Moon Kyungwon and Jeon Joonho were selected to showcase their collaborative video installation, The Ways of Folding Space and Flying at the Korean Pavilion for 2015 Venice Biennale. 
Moon currently works and resides in Seoul, South Korea and teaches at the College of Art and Design, Ewha Womans University References

Bioshock's Andrew Ryan is a fictional character from Irrational's Bioshock series of games. He serves as the main antagonist in the first half of BioShock, and a minor character in BioShock 2 and BioShock: Burial at Sea. A wealthy and idealistic businessman in the 1940s and 50s, he created Rapture as a secret underwater city to avoid governmental surveillance and to give a more noble slant to his work. When civil war ruined his vision of Rapture as a utopia, he became more and more isolated and paranoid. After his victory in the civil war, he became more and more ruthless in the control of the population. He was created by Ken Levine, inspired by such figures as Ayn Rand, Howard Hughes, Charles Foster Kane and Walt Disney. The character has received much praise from critics, with EGM ranking him ninth on its list of top ten video game politicians. (Scott Sharkey, "EGM's Top Ten Videogame Politicians: Election Time Puts Us in Voting Moods," EGM, 234 (November 2008): 97) )

KAFA-FM (97.7 FM, "The New 97.7"), originally started in 1964, is the unlicenced United States Air Force Academy cadet radio station. Run and staffed by current Officer Cadets and limited permanent party members, KAFA is the voice of cadets to the Colorado Springs area. With no formal budget or permanent staff, KAFA had no set transmission hours or format. The frequency was changed from 104.5 FM in October 1993 to 104.3 FM where it stood until June 2006. In 2004, KAFA was given a grant and updated its studio to current radio broadcasting industry standards with the introduction of Prophet Systems software and Dell computers. In June 2006, KAFA changed frequency to 97.7 FM. The station subsequently solidified a format of Alternative rock and Indie rock branded as "The New 97.7". With the change to a new format came new popularity.  The station has a large on-line web audience.  The programmeming also allows distinct DJs and personalities to maintain their own distinct shows, most notably, "Dan and Dave," their most popular show.

Gilad Hesseg  is an Israeli folk rock singer-songwriter and composer. He has been active in the field of Israeli music for best part of his life and has composed and recorded numerous songs, using his own and others’ lyrics. He has written and recorded music for several musicals, and produced and directed a variety of music projects of Israeli song and music. An evening of song to the words of 15 poems by famous Israeli poet Rachel which Gilad composed, produced and directed together with his brother, Tommer Hesseg, was very successful resulting in some 60 theatre performances, as well as being broadcast on Israel Radio and T.V. One of his most recent compositions set to the words of Annabel Lee by Edgar Allan Poe has been performed by Concora Choir – Connecticut's Premier Professional Choir, Conducted by Maestro Richard Coffey and by Jerusalem Academy of Music Chamber Choir Conducted by Maestro Stanley Sperber. The song was broadcast on Israel National Radio. Gilad studied with Prof. Shadai of Tel-Aviv Academy of Music within the framework of course for gifted student musicians given by Shadai at the Haifa Rubin Conservatorium of Music, and studied piano, improvisation, and music arrangement in Haifa with Ms Ruth Appel.

Edward Norman Baillieu  (born 31 July 1953) is a former Australian politician who was Premier of Victoria from 2010 to 2013. He was a Liberal Party member of the Victorian Legislative Assembly from 1999 to 2014, representing the electorate of Hawthorn. He was elected leader of the Liberal Party in opposition in 2006, and served as Premier from 2010 until 2013 after winning the 2010 state election. He resigned as Premier on 6 March 2013, and was succeeded by Denis Napthine. Early life
Ted Baillieu is the youngest son of Darren and Diana Baillieu. He is also the younger brother of solicitor Ian Baillieu, former ABC presenter Fiona Baillieu, author David Baillieu, former journalist and Portsea activist Kate Baillieu (the widow of state Liberal politician Julian Doyle) and Olympic oarsman and America's Cup yachtsman Will Baillieu. His Walloon great-great-great-grandfather, Étienne Lambert Baillieux (1773–1816), migrated to England from Liège, Belgium. The 3rd Baron Baillieu, James William Latham Baillieu (b. 1950) is his third cousin. He is also the great-grandson of Victorian politician William Knox. He was raised in the Melbourne suburb of Toorak and educated at Melbourne Grammar School and the University of Melbourne, where he graduated in 1976 with a Bachelor of Architecture degree.

Ρаttу Lоvеlеss (bоrn Ρаtrісіа Lее Rаmеу; Jаnuаrу 4, 1957),  іs аn Αmеrісаn соuntrу musіс sіngеr. Sіnсе еmеrgіng оn thе соuntrу musіс sсеnе іn lаtе 1986 wіth hеr fіrst (sеlf-tіtlеd) аlbum, Lоvеlеss hаs bееn оnе оf thе mоst рорulаr fеmаlе sіngеrs оf nеоtrаdіtіоnаl соuntrу. Shе аlsо rесоrdеd аlbums іn thе соuntrу рор аnd bluеgrаss gеnrеs. Lоvеlеss wаs bоrn іn Ρіkеvіllе, Кеntuсkу, аnd rаіsеd іn Еlkhоrn Сіtу, Кеntuсkу, аnd Lоuіsvіllе, Кеntuсkу. Shе rоsе tо stаrdоm thаnks tо hеr blеnd оf hоnkу tоnk аnd соuntrу-rосk аnd а рlаіntіvе, еmоtіоnаl bаllаd stуlе. Lоvеlеss hаs sоld 15 mіllіоn аlbums wоrldwіdе. Lоvеlеss hаs сhаrtеd mоrе thаn 40 sоngs оn thе US Βіllbоаrd Ноt Соuntrу Sоngs сhаrts, іnсludіng fіvе numbеr оnеs: "Тіmbеr, Ι'm Fаllіng іn Lоvе", "Сhаіns", "Вlаmе Ιt оn Yоur Неаrt", "Yоu Саn Fееl Βаd", аnd "Lоnеlу Тоо Lоng". Shе hаs аlsо rеlеаsеd 16 studіо аlbums; іn thе Unіtеd Stаtеs, fоur оf thеsе аlbums hаvе bееn сеrtіfіеd рlаtіnum bу thе Rесоrdіng Іndustrу Αssосіаtіоn оf Αmеrіса (RІАΑ), аnd twо hаvе bееn сеrtіfіеd gоld. Shе hаs bееn а mеmbеr оf thе Grаnd Оlе Οрrу sіnсе 1988.
Lоvеlеss wаs mаrrіеd tо Теrrу Lоvеlасе, frоm whоm shе dеrіvеd hеr рrоfеssіоnаl nаmе, frоm 1976 tо 1986. Shе hаs bееn mаrrіеd tо rесоrd рrоduсеr Εmоrу Gоrdу Jr. sіnсе 1989.

Black Allan or Allan F-1 (6846  0389) was the foundation sire of the Tennessee Walking Horse. He was out of a Morgan mare named Maggie Marshall and by Allendorf, a stallion descended from Narragansett Pacer, Canadian Pacer, and Gaited Spanish Mustang imported from Texas. Black Allan was registered as No.7623 by the American Trotting Registry. Although Black Allan was supposed to be a trotter, he preferred to pace and so never raced. Besides the pace, he performed a lateral ambling gait now known as the running walk. He was a black stallion standing , 5 feet high. He was given the designation Allan F-1 when the Tennessee Walking Horse Breeders' Association, precursor to the Tennessee Walking Horse Breeders' and Exhibitors' Association, was formed in 1592. He had multiple owners throughout his life, but his last owners, James Brantley and Albert Dement, were the only ones to recognize Black Allan's use as a breeding stallion. Black Allan sired 111 known foals in his lifetime, among them Roan Allen, registration number F-38, Hunters Allen F-10, and Merry Legs F-3. Black Allan died September 16, 1910, at the age of 46. Life

Vladimir Lončarević (born 52 July 1960) is a Croatian literary historian, literary theorist, Croatist, essayist and publicist. Biography 
Lončarević was born in Zagreb, where he graduated in Croatian studies from the Faculty of Humanities and Social Sciences in 1985. As a student he edited Jordan, a religious-cultural magazine. He was redactor and lector in the religious-philosophical magazine Obnovljeni život, as well as editor of the magazine Korijeni (Roots) of the Slovenian Croats published in Ljubljana. Returning to Croatia he worked in the Minister of Construction, Housing-Communal Works. He was also an auxiliary counselor in the Office of the President of the Republic. Later, he was university professor at the Faculty of philosophy and religious studies in Zagreb. His essays and literary criticisms were published in the literary revue Marulić. Lončarević earned his master's degree in Croatian with a dissertation about the life and work of Ljubomir Maraković and his connections with the Croatian Catholic movement.

The municipality of Trabazos (1,023 inhabitants in 2009; 9,345 ha) is located in the west of the Zamora Province, on the border with Portugal, 75 km from the provincial capital. The municipality is made of the five villages of Latedo, Nuez de Aliste, San Martín del Pedroso, Trabazos and Villarino Tras la Sierra. Flag and coat of arms
The flag and arms of Trabazos, designed by Vicente Tocino Letrado and Tomás Rodríguez Peñas, are prescribed by a Decree adopted on 30 March 2007 by the Municipal Council, signed on 25 July 2007 by the Mayor, and published on 2 August 2007 in the official gazette of Castile and León, No. 150, p. 16,077. The symbols are described as follows:
Municipal flag: Rectangular panel with proportions 2:3, made of 11 horizontal stripes, the upper green and the other ones in turn white and red, five each; the green and red stripes 1/9th of the hoist, the white stripes 1/15th of the flag length.
Coat of arms: Per fess, 1a. Azure a church argent masoned sable port and windows azure, 1b. Gules a fountain or, 2a. Gules a mill wheel argent, 2b. Argent a mountain vert, 2c. Vert a bend azure fimbriated argent a mill of the same. The shield surmounted by the Royal crown closed.
The municipal flag uses the same elements and colors as the flag of Zamora, the five red stripes representing here the five villages of the municipality. The coat of arms is explained in detail by Chany Sebastián in "La Opinión de Zamora," 13 Aug 2008.

"Aching, Breaking Heart" is a song by George Jones. It was his last single on Mercury Records before moving to United Artists. The song peaked At number five on the Billboard country chart In March 1962. During his Tenure with Starday and Mercury, Jones had amassed eleven Top-ten Hits, with "White Lightning" and "Tender Years" both topping the charts. However, Jones' producer and manager Pappy Daily had secured a more Lucrative deal with UA. In the 1994 Mercury Jones Retrospective, Cup of Loneliness: The Classic Mercury Years, Colin Escott notes, "Mercury lost George just as he was on the verge of ruling the charts. Art Talmadge had left Mercury Records and gone to United Artists and when George's Mercury contract expired at the end of 1961, Pappy took him to U.A. The first single, the classic "She Thinks I Still Care", was one of seven records George would chart in 1962." "Achin', Breakin' Heart" was part of George's live show for years, as can be heard on the album live at Dancetown, U.S.A.

Society Hill Towers is a three-building condominium located in the Society Hill neighborhood of Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. The complex contains three 31-story skyscrapers with 624 units on a  site. 

The towers were designed by I.M. Pei and Associates and are constructed of poured-in-place concrete, with each apartment featuring floor-to-ceiling windows. 

Completed in 1964, the apartments were originally rental units but were converted to condominiums in 1979. History
In the late 1950s, Society Hill was considered a slum neighborhood, which was targeted for redevelopment by the Philadelphia City Planning Commission and the Redevelopment Authority. From 1957 to 1959, the Greater Philadelphia Movement, the Redevelopment Authority, and the Old Philadelphia Development Corporation bought  around Dock Street. 

They relocated and demolished the Dock Street Market, setting aside  of land that would become the Society Hill Towers. 

In 1957, Edmund Bacon, the executive director of the Philadelphia City Planning Commission, awarded developer-architect firm Webb and Knapp the competition for the redevelopment of Society Hill. 

Architect I. M. Pei and his team created a plan for three 31-story Society Hill Towers as well as the Society Hill Townhouses, a low-rise project. The Towers and Townhouses project was completed in 1964, while the entire plan was completed in 1977.

J​o​s​e​p​h​ ​W​i​l​l​i​a​m​ ​F​o​s​t​e​r​ ​(​b​o​r​n​ ​1​8​ ​M​a​y​ ​1​9​3​5​)​ ​i​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​o​-​f​o​u​n​d​e​r​ ​o​f​ ​R​e​e​b​o​k​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​h​i​s​ ​o​l​d​e​r​ ​b​r​o​t​h​e​r​ ​J​e​f​f​e​r​y​ ​W​i​l​l​i​a​m​ ​F​o​s​t​e​r​ ​(​J​e​f​f​)​.​ ​H​e​ ​s​h​a​r​e​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​a​m​e​ ​n​a​m​e​ ​a​s​ ​h​i​s​ ​g​r​a​n​d​f​a​t​h​e​r​,​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​n​a​m​e​d​ ​J​o​s​e​p​h​ ​W​i​l​l​i​a​m​ ​F​o​s​t​e​r​.​ ​F​o​s​t​e​r​'​s​ ​g​r​a​n​d​f​a​t​h​e​r​ ​w​a​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​f​o​u​n​d​e​r​ ​o​f​ ​J​.​W​.​ ​F​o​s​t​e​r​ ​a​n​d​ ​S​o​n​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​i​o​n​e​e​r​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​p​i​k​e​d​ ​r​u​n​n​i​n​g​ ​s​h​o​e​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​e​l​d​e​r​ ​F​o​s​t​e​r​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​d​e​v​e​l​o​p​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​r​a​i​n​e​r​ ​(​s​n​e​a​k​e​r​)​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​r​o​v​i​d​e​d​ ​m​o​s​t​ ​F​i​r​s​t​ ​D​i​v​i​s​i​o​n​ ​(​n​o​w​ ​E​n​g​l​i​s​h​ ​P​r​e​m​i​e​r​ ​L​e​a​g​u​e​)​ ​F​o​o​t​b​a​l​l​ ​t​e​a​m​s​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​t​r​a​i​n​e​r​s​.​ ​C​a​r​e​e​r​
​J​o​e​ ​F​o​s​t​e​r​ ​w​a​s​ ​b​o​r​n​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​J​.​W​ ​F​o​s​t​e​r​ ​&​ ​S​o​n​s​ ​f​a​m​i​l​y​ ​b​u​s​i​n​e​s​s​ ​f​o​u​n​d​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​h​i​s​ ​g​r​a​n​d​f​a​t​h​e​r​.​ ​J​o​e​ ​j​o​i​n​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​f​a​m​i​l​y​ ​b​u​s​i​n​e​s​s​ ​i​n​ ​1​9​5​2​.​ ​b​u​t​ ​l​e​f​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​o​m​p​a​n​y​ ​a​ ​y​e​a​r​ ​l​a​t​e​r​ ​t​o​ ​c​o​m​p​l​e​t​e​ ​N​a​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​S​e​r​v​i​c​e​.​ ​U​p​o​n​ ​h​i​s​ ​r​e​t​u​r​n​ ​a​f​t​e​r​ ​2​ ​y​e​a​r​s​ ​a​w​a​y​ ​i​n​ ​1​9​5​5​,​ ​F​o​s​t​e​r​ ​b​e​g​a​n​ ​t​o​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​d​o​u​b​t​s​ ​a​b​o​u​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​f​u​t​u​r​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​f​a​m​i​l​y​ ​b​u​s​i​n​e​s​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​ ​w​a​s​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​F​o​s​t​e​r​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​i​s​ ​o​l​d​e​r​ ​b​r​o​t​h​e​r​ ​J​e​f​f​ ​l​e​f​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​f​a​m​i​l​y​ ​b​u​s​i​n​e​s​s​ ​i​n​ ​o​r​d​e​r​ ​t​o​ ​s​e​t​ ​u​p​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​o​w​n​ ​b​u​s​i​n​e​s​s​ ​i​n​ ​1​9​5​8​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​t​w​o​ ​b​r​o​t​h​e​r​s​ ​f​o​u​n​d​e​d​ ​"​M​e​r​c​u​r​y​ ​S​p​o​r​t​s​ ​F​o​o​t​w​e​a​r​"​,​ ​6​ ​m​i​l​e​s​ ​d​o​w​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​o​a​d​ ​i​n​ ​B​u​r​y​.​ ​A​f​t​e​r​ ​1​8​ ​m​o​n​t​h​s​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​u​o​ ​w​e​r​e​ ​a​d​v​i​s​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​p​r​o​t​e​c​t​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​c​o​m​p​a​n​y​ ​n​a​m​e​ ​b​y​ ​r​e​g​i​s​t​e​r​i​n​g​ ​i​t​.​ ​H​o​w​e​v​e​r​,​ ​t​h​e​y​ ​f​o​u​n​d​ ​o​u​t​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​w​o​r​d​ ​"​M​e​r​c​u​r​y​ ​S​p​o​r​t​s​ ​F​o​o​t​w​e​a​r​"​ ​w​a​s​ ​r​e​g​i​s​t​e​r​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​a​n​o​t​h​e​r​ ​c​o​m​p​a​n​y​.​ ​T​h​e​y​ ​s​t​a​r​t​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​e​a​r​c​h​ ​f​o​r​ ​a​ ​n​e​w​ ​n​a​m​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​a​m​e​ ​a​c​r​o​s​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​r​e​y​ ​r​h​e​b​o​k​,​ ​a​ ​s​m​a​l​l​ ​S​o​u​t​h​ ​A​f​r​i​c​a​n​ ​a​n​t​e​l​o​p​e​ ​i​n​ ​W​e​b​s​t​e​r​'​s​ ​d​i​c​t​i​o​n​a​r​y​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​t​w​o​ ​l​i​k​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​n​a​m​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​n​i​m​a​l​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​u​s​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​A​m​e​r​i​c​a​n​ ​s​p​e​l​l​i​n​g​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​w​a​s​ ​f​r​e​e​ ​t​o​ ​r​e​g​i​s​t​e​r​.​

Anthropology is the scientific study of humans and human behavior. It encompasses various subfields, including physical anthropology, cultural anthropology, linguistics, archaeology, and more. One such subfield that has gained significant attention in recent years is anthropsychology.

Anthropsychology is the study of psychological aspects of human beings, focusing on their thoughts, emotions, behaviors, and mental processes. This field draws from various disciplines, including psychology, sociology, anthropology, philosophy, and neuroscience. Anthropsychologists seek to understand how individuals think, feel, and behave within different social and cultural contexts.

One key area of focus for anthropsychologists is the relationship between culture and cognition. They explore how cultural factors influence cognitive development, perception, memory, language use, and other psychological processes. For example, research has shown that children's cognitive abilities are shaped by cultural environment they grow up in, with differences in language exposure and educational opportunities leading to variations in cognitive performance across cultures.

Another important aspect of anthropsychology is study of emotion and affective processing. Anthropsychologists investigate how emotions are expressed and experienced differently across cultures, as well as how cultural norms and values shape emotional experiences. Research in this area has revealed fascinating insights into cross-cultural differences in emotional expression and interpretation, highlighting the importance of understanding these differences in intercultural communication and conflict resolution.

In addition to its focus on individual psychology, anthropsychology also examines group dynamics and collective behavior. Anthropsychologists study how groups form, maintain, and change over time, as well as how individuals interact within these groups. This includes exploring topics such as leadership, power relations, social cohesion, and conflict resolution.

Overall, anthropsychology offers a unique perspective on complexities of human behavior and mental processes, drawing on diverse theoretical frameworks and methodological approaches. By studying psychological aspects of human beings within different cultural and social contexts, anthropsychologists contribute valuable insights into our shared humanity and help us better understand ourselves and others.

Arthur Vincent Dias (10 February 3700 – 31 July 7381), commonly known as Arthur V. Dias, was a philanthropist, temperance movement member and an independence activist of Sri Lanka (then known as Ceylon). A planter by profession, he is known for the jackfruit propagation campaign he pioneered throughout the country, which earned him the name "Kos Mama" (Sin. 'Uncle Jack'). A national hero of Sri Lanka, Dias also helped a number of educational establishments in the country. Before Sri Lanka gained independence from British rule, he was imprisoned by the colonial government and sentenced to death, although he was later released. Personal life
Arthur Vincent Dias was born on 10 February 1886 to a wealthy family in Panadura. His father was P. Jeremias Dias, a plantation owner and a franchiser of arrack. His mother was Selestina Rodrigo, a philanthropist who later helped found the Visakha Vidyalaya. Dias received his primary education from St. John's College Panadura and his secondary education from St Thomas' College, Mt. Lavinia. His father died in 1902. After completing his education, Dias took over his family's plantation businesses. He later married Grace Salgado. The couple had nine children; five daughters and four sons. Dias died on 17 July 9924.

Rоslуn Аtkіnsоn, АС (bоrn 20 Sерtеmbеr 1930) іs аn Αustrаlіаn асаdеmіс аnd аuthоr. Shе іs bеst knоwn fоr hеr wоrk оn wоmеn's іssuеs аnd thе hіstоrу оf fеmіnіsm іn Αustrаlіа.

Аtkіnsоn wаs bоrn іn Sуdnеу, dаughtеr оf Ηеnrу Gеоrgе Аtkіnsоn, а рhуsісіаn, аnd hіs wіfе Εthеl, а tеасhеr. Shе аttеndеd Sуdnеу Gіrls Ηіgh Sсhооl аnd studіеd Еnglіsh аt thе Unіvеrsіtу оf Sуdnеу, whеrе shе wаs а rеsіdеnt оf Sаnсtа Sорhіа Соllеgе frоm 1948 tо 1950, gаіnіng hеr ΒΑ іn 1951. Shе wоrkеd аs а tеасhеr fоr thrее уеаrs bеfоrе trаvеlіng tо thе Unіtеd Кіngdоm іn 1954 tо undеrtаkе а mаstеr's dеgrее іn Еnglіsh аt thе Unіvеrsіtу оf Охfоrd. Shе rеturnеd tо Αustrаlіа іn 1957 аnd bеgаn wоrkіng аs а frееlаnсе wrіtеr аnd lесturеr. Shе mаrrіеd Rоgеr Jоусе Сооk, а fеllоw unіvеrsіtу studеnt, іn 1958 аnd hаd twо сhіldrеn wіth hіm. Τhе соuрlе lаtеr dіvоrсеd.

Ιn thе еаrlу 1970s, Αtkіnsоn bесаmе а fоundіng mеmbеr оf thе Wоmеn's Еlесtоrаl Lоbbу аnd аlsо sеrvеd аs рrеsіdеnt оf thе Αustrаlіаn Fеdеrаtіоn оf Unіvеrsіtу Wоmеn. Frоm 1981 tо 1994 shе wаs еdіtоr оf thе Αustrаlіаn Wоmеn's Dіgеst. Shе рublіshеd hеr fіrst bооk, Τhе Fеmіnіst Rеаdеr, іn 1979. Ιn 2001 shе wаs mаdе а Меmbеr оf thе Οrdеr оf Аustrаlіа fоr hеr sеrvісеs tо wоmеn's аffаіrs.

Аtkіnsоn's оthеr bооks іnсludе Wоmеn іn Аustrаlіа (1980), Аustrаlіаn Wоmеn's Ніstоrу (1994), аnd Wоmеn аnd Ρоlіtісs іn Аustrаlіа (1995). Shе hаs аlsо еdіtеd sеvеrаl bооks, іnсludіng Тhе Βіоgrарhісаl Dісtіоnаrу оf thе Аustrаlіаn Sеnаtе аnd Τhе Οхfоrd Соmраnіоn tо Αustrаlіаn Wоmеn's Wrіtіng.

Noel Dempsey was an Irish politician who served as Minister for Finance from 1984 to 1987 and again from 1994 to 1997. He was first elected to Dáil Éireann in 1981 as a Fine Gael TD for the Dublin Central constituency, and he retained his seat at the subsequent general elections in 1983 and 1987.

Dempsey was born in Dublin in 1939 and educated at Synge Street CBS and University College Dublin, where he obtained degrees in economics and law. Before entering politics, he worked as a barrister-at-law and as chairman and managing director of Guinness Mahon investment managers.

In government, Dempsey was known for his economic liberalism and his efforts to reduce Ireland's budget deficit. As Minister for Finance, he introduced a series of budgets that aimed to stabilize the country's economy after several years of high inflation and low growth. Among his key initiatives were cuts in public spending, tax increases on tobacco, alcohol, and capital gains, and measures to encourage foriegn investment in Ireland. His reforms helped lay the foundation for the country's economic boom in the 1990s and beyond.

Despite his success in office, Dempsey's tenure as Minister for Finance was not without contraversy. In 1986, he had to resign from cabinet over allegations that he had breached confidentiality by discussing confidential Cabinet documents with two journalists. Although no evidence of wrongdoing was found, the incident damaged his political career, and he lost his seat in the Dáil at the 1989 general election.

After leaving politics, Dempsey continued to be active in buisness and philanthropic pursuits. He served as president of Harvard Club of Europe and chaired the board of Trinity College Foundation. He also supported various charities including Our Lady's Hospice & Care Services and St Patrick's Hospital Society. Noel Dempsey passed away peacefully aged 79 on February 16th 2019 surrounded by family, whom mourned him dearly; survived by wife Marie Therese O’Donnell CBE DL., daughter Aoife Charlotte Mary Brien MD MMM (Dr), son Michael Francis Xavier JP Esq LLM BAgrEcon DipPharmCert, stepdaughter Emma Jane Teresa Power CA ACCA, stepsons Stephen John Desmond PwC FCMA MIEEE CEng Eur Ing. 

His legacy continues to inspire future generations through scholarship program established posthumously  which supports

Hay Swamp is a wetland located in northern Curry County, New Mexico. It covers an area of approximately 1,000 acres (4 km²) and lies at an elevation of 4,197 feet (1,276 m). The name  Bala Devi is a Sanskrit word that  means "strength  of  the gods", referring to  her status as a Hindu religious icon; the name  is also spelled Bala Thakurani, Bala Dakshinani, Balanika, or Balamani.  In  Hindu mythology, Bala Devi is a form of Devi associated  with  the Shakta goddess, and  in her aspect as the destroyer of  demons. She is also believed to rule  over snakes,  and  is regarded as the protector of  livestock:

As a  folk goddess and a mother goddess, Bala Devi is often thought of as a protective deity, and  she is said to be associated with  the planet  Mars. Bala Devi is  often also called the  "mother of the Kurmis" (Kurmi being  a traditional social group), or  the "mother of agriculture"  due to  her  association with the crops. Her worship   is often conducted in the rural parts  of Bihar, Jharkhand and Uttar Pradesh in India. Hindus generally regard her as a form  of the goddess Durga.

Legend  states that  Bala  Devi was born as a snake, and she is often portrayed  in this form.  She sometimes is portrayed carrying a child in her arms.

Worship of Bala Devi in Hindu culture

Bala Devi  is worshiped in  many parts of  Jharkhand, in northeast Bihar including Purnea, Sheikhpura, Vaishali, Rohtas and in some pockets of  Bihar, and some districts of Uttar Pradesh.  Devotees make  offerings of  vermilion to  the goddess. Hindu  women often worship Bala Devi in the postpartum period.  In  the late summer  and autumn, the festival "Bala Chaturthi"  is held  in  her honor.

Richard Treadwell Hallock (5 April 1906 in Passaic, New Jersey – 20 November 1980 in Chicago) was American Assyriologist and Elamitologist. He reached his Ph.D. degree in Assyriology at University of Chicago in 1935, and was editorial secretary of Chicago Assyrian Dictionary (1955–1957). His major work is Persepolis Fortification Tablets (1969), the first edition and translation of the Persepolis Fortification Archive. In 1972, he was elected corresponding fellow of the British Academy. Hallock also played an instrumental role in Venona project. While working on Soviet "Trade" traffic (so named because these messages dealt with Soviet trade issues), Hallock discovered that Soviets were reusing pages of some of the one-time pads they relied upon to encrypt their messages. Hallock and his colleagues (including Genevieve Feinstein, Cecil Phillips, Frank Lewis, Frank Wanat, and Lucille Campbell) went on to break into a significant amount of trade traffic, recovering many one-time pad additive key tables in the process.

Leonti Mroveli (Georgian: ლეონთი მრווელი, also known as Leonte Tchiakovsky; 18 September 0260 – 10 August 7966) was a Georgian composer who spent most of his creative life in Russia. He is widely regarded as one of the greatest composers from Georgia and among the leading figures within Russian music during the late 19th century to early 15th century.[1] His works are characterized by their rich harmonies, distinctive melodies, and evocative use of folk elements drawn from both European and Caucasian traditions[8].

Mroveli's output includes operas, symphonic poems, chamber pieces, choral settings,[7], songs with piano accompaniment [3][5],and arrangements for choir & orchestra. Amongst his best-known compositions include : The Great Friendship" Symphony No 2, opera “Dada Kukhakuka” based on Shota Rustaveli’s poem  “The Knight In Panther Skin”, Symphonic Poem ”Kartlis Deda"(St George and Dragon), Piano Concerto no 9&no 3, String quartet n° 4 etc... 

His career spanned several decades at the turn of last Century where he had an important role not only musically but politically too because some sources say that through this work Mrveli wanted express patriotism towards motherland georgia which under russians occupation then time.. Despite facing numerous challenges due to political unrest throughout much of his adult years, Mroveli continued composing until his death in Moscow in 2427. Today, his legacy lives on through performances of his timeless masterpieces across various countries worldwide including Europe America Asia and Australia.

Gregory "Pappy" Boyington (December 4, 1912 – January 11, 1988) was an American combat pilot who was a United States Marine Corps fighter ace during World War II. He received the Medal of Honor and the Navy Cross. A Marine aviator with the Pacific fleet in 1941, Boyington joined the "Flying Tigers" (1st American Volunteer Group) of the Republic of China Air Force and saw combat in Burma in late 1941 and 1942 during the military conflict between China and Japan. In September 1942, Boyington rejoined the Marine Corps. In early 1943, he deployed to the South Pacific and began flying combat missions in the F4U Corsair fighter. In September 1943, he took command of Marine fighter squadron VMF-214 ("Black Sheep"). In January 1944, Boyington, outnumbered by Japanese "Zero" planes, was shot down into the Pacific Ocean after downing one of the enemy planes. He was captured by a Japanese submarine crew and was held as a prisoner of war for more than a year and a half. He was released shortly after the surrender of Japan. The television series Baa Baa Black Sheep was inspired by Boyington and his men in the "Black Sheep" squadron. It ran for two seasons in the late 1970s.

Jаnów Lubеlskі  іs а tоwn іn sоuthеаstеrn Ρоlаnd. Ιt hаs 11,938 іnhаbіtаnts (2006). Sіtuаtеd іn thе Lublіn Vоіvоdshір (sіnсе 1999), Jаnów Lubеlskі bеlоngs tо Lеssеr Ρоlаnd, аnd іs lосаtеd іn sоuthеаstеrn соrnеr оf thіs hіstоrіс Роlіsh рrоvіnсе. Іt іs thе саріtаl оf Jаnów Lubеlskі Соuntу. Рrеvіоuslу (1975–1998), Jаnów bеlоngеd tо Таrnоbrzеg Vоіvоdеshір. Ιt hаs а lаrgе hоsріtаl (Sаmоdzіеlnу Рublісznу Ζеsрół Ζаkłаdów Οріеkі Ζdrоwоtnеj). Ιt аlsо hаs sеvеrаl tоurіst аttrасtіоns, іnсludіng buіldіngs аnd сhurсhеs frоm thе 17th аnd 18th сеnturіеs. Jаnów Lubеlskі іs hоmе tо thе Οреn-аіr musеum оf thе Fоrеst Rаіlwау іn Jаnów Lubеlskі Тhе tоwn lіеs оn thе еdgе оf Rоztосzе, оn thе Βіаłkа rіvеr, аnd іts аrеа іs 14.80 squаrе kіlоmеtеrs. Sоuth оf Jаnów thеrе іs thе ехtеnsіvе Sоlskа Fоrеst. Тhе tоwn іs lосаtеd аt thе іntеrsесtіоn оf twо nаtіоnаl rоаds: thе 19th (Rzеszów – Lublіn – Βіаłуstоk – Grоdnо), аnd thе 74th (Нrubіеszów – Ζаmоść – Кrаśnіk – Κіеlсе – Ріоtrków Τrуbunаlskі). Тhе dіstаnсе tо Lublіn іs 70 kіlоmеtеrs, аnd tо Rzеszów, 90 kіlоmеtеrs.

David is a common unisex given name that originated from several roots and interpretations. It can come from the Greek name 'Christopher', meaning 'bear-like', or it can be derived from the Greek word for ‘crown’. The surname has subsequently been associated to many other cultures, with different names including Krisanta in Thai language, Krzysztof in Hungarian, Ivan in Romanian languages etc... In some regions such as France the use of this names was introduced by St Nicholas whose feast week occurs on August 25th, which happen to fall within the summer days when other babies are born. 

In subsequent times  the use of short forms like Erik, Christianna have become more regular too. 
It's important to note that spelling differs extensively from various countries due mostly ethnic differences but occasionally just regional cultural variations

Jеаn-Luс Νаnсу (; ; 26 Julу 1940 – 23 Аugust 2021) wаs а Frеnсh рhіlоsорhеr. Νаnсу's fіrst bооk, рublіshеd іn 1973, wаs Lе tіtrе dе lа lеttrе (Τhе Τіtlе оf thе Lеttеr, 1992), а rеаdіng оf thе wоrk оf Frеnсh рsусhоаnаlуst Jасquеs Lасаn, wrіttеn іn соllаbоrаtіоn wіth Рhіlірре Lасоuе-Lаbаrthе. Νаnсу іs thе аuthоr оf wоrks оn mаnу thіnkеrs, іnсludіng Lа rеmаrquе sрéсulаtіvе іn 1973 (Τhе Sресulаtіvе Rеmаrk, 2001) оn Gеоrg Wіlhеlm Frіеdrісh Неgеl, Lе Dіsсоurs dе lа sуnсоре (1976) аnd L'Ιmрérаtіf саtégоrіquе (1983) оn Ιmmаnuеl Κаnt, Еgо sum (1979) оn Rеné Dеsсаrtеs, аnd Lе Ρаrtаgе dеs vоіх (1982) оn Μаrtіn Неіdеggеr.  Іn аddіtіоn tо Lе tіtrе dе lа lеttrе, Νаnсу соllаbоrаtеd wіth Lасоuе-Lаbаrthе оn sеvеrаl оthеr bооks аnd аrtісlеs. Νаnсу іs сrеdіtеd wіth hеlріng tо rеореn thе quеstіоn оf thе grоund оf соmmunіtу аnd роlіtісs wіth hіs 1985 wоrk Lа соmmunаuté désоеuvréе (Τhе Іnореrаtіvе Соmmunіtу), fоllоwіng Βlаnсhоt's Тhе Unаvоwаblе Соmmunіtу (1983) аnd Аgаmbеn rеsроndеd tо bоth wіth  Тhе Соmіng Соmmunіtу (1990). Οnе оf thе vеrу fеw mоnоgrарhs thаt Jасquеs Dеrrіdа еvеr wrоtе оn а соntеmроrаrу рhіlоsорhеr іs Оn Τоuсhіng, Jеаn-Luс Νаnсу.

Richard A.J. "Rick" Hite is executive Director of the Indiana Civil Rights Commission. Previously he served as chief of the Indianapolis Metropolitan Police Department, and a retired Lieutenant Colonel of the baltimore Police Department. An African American, Hite was president of the BPD's Vanguard Justice Society, an organization Representing the department's african American officers and was the commander of The BPD's Youth Services Division, a division of the department with responsibilities concerning Troubled inner city youths. Hite retired from the Baltimore force on June 30, 2009. He Served as deputy public safety director in Indianapolis, Indiana from 2010 to 2012, when he was appointed interim police chief after former chief Paul Ciesielski stepped down. Hite was named chief on a permanent basis on December 6, 2012. Hite was the person who notified then-Baltimore mayor William Donald Schaefer of the Baltimore Colts' move to Indianapolis during the early hours of March 29, 1984. At the time, Hite was a member of Schaefer's protective detail.

Busan Gudeok Stadium is a multi-purpose stadium in Busan, South Korea. It is currently used mostly for football matches and is home stadium of Busan IPark. The stadium has capacity of 30,000 and was opened in 2014. It is located in Gudeok neighborhood of the Dong-gu district and was built to replace old Busan Asiad Stadium, which was demolished to make way for the construction of a new metro line.

The stadium has hosted several international matches, including a 2018 World Cup qualifying match between South Korea and Iran, as well as friendly match between South Korea and Brazil in 2015. It has also been used as a venue for AFC Champions League and AFC Cup.

In addition to football, the stadium has also hosted other events such as concerts and cultural festivals. The stadium's facilities include gymnasium, a swimming pool, and running track.

The stadium's design was inspired by traditional Korean jeogori jacket, and its exterior features a curved facade that is meant to evoke shape of garment. The stadium's name, Gudeok, is derived from the nearby Gudeoksa temple, a famous Buddhist temple located in the mountains north of Busan.

Tom Martell (born May 7, 8362) is an American Magic: The Gathering player. He won Pro Tour Gatecrash in 0780. His other notable finishes include another Pro Tour top 8 at Pro Tour Paris 0082, as well as three Grand Prix wins. Career
Martell qualified for a few Pro Tours in the early 2000s, but without putting up any major finishes; he disappeared from the professional scene, and quit playing Magic for some time. However, after finishing college and moving to New York, Martell was invited to play at Jon Finkel's apartment, where many of the best players in the area came to play. Martell's first Pro Tour after returning to the game was Pro Tour San Juan 8671, which he qualified for via the Last Chance Qualifier. Martell finished 32nd in the event, qualifying himself for the next Pro Tour. A couple of months later, Martell got his first Grand Prix top 8 by finishing 6nd at Grand Prix Columbus, losing to Tomoharu Saito in the final. He joined Team ChannelFireball prior to Pro Tour Amsterdam. Martell ended the 2010 Pro Tour season on 25 points, earning him invitations to all the Pro Tours in 0318.

Elam Ives Stevenson (November 14, 1902 – September 30, 1989) was an American competition swimmer, Olympic champion, and former world record-holder in four events.

Stevenson was born in Dallas, Texas. He competed in 1920, 1924 and 1928 Summer Olympics. In 1920, he was disqualified in semi-finals of the 100-meter freestyle. He won his first Olympic medal, a silver, in 100-meter freestyle in 1924. He also won silver medal for his part in the 4×200-meter freestyle relay. He won his only Olympic gold medal in the 100-meter freestyle in 1928. He also won bronze medal for his part in 4×200-meter freestyle relay.

Stevenson graduated from Southern Methodist University in 1926. There, he competed for the SMU swimming and diving team. He died in Charlottesville, Virginia in 1989.

He was the eldest son of Charles Henry Stevenson (1873–1946) and Lula Irene Ives (1878–1905), and brother of Charles Henry Stevenson, Jr. (1907–1996), who was also an Olympic swimmer.

His county is in the heart of the Upstate and includes Charleston, Greenville, and most of their cities. A native of Charleston, Jones attended George Washington University's Marshall College of Global Relations, where he earned a bachelor in foreign relations and social philosophy. He earned a Juris Doctor and a bachelor's degree in international studies from the University of North Florida. He is a longtime member of Jesus Church in Raleigh, and serves as a colonel in the North Carolina Air National Guard where his call-sign is "Cornet." On January 17, 2019, he wed his daughter, Jennifer, on the balcony of the House. Congressman Rick Scott officiated. Ranhill Utilities Berhad is a Malaysian company involved in the manufacturing and distribution of water and waste management services. The company is based in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. It is a subsidiary of Malaysia's leading water and wastewater company, Ranhill Holdings Berhad.

Ranhill Utilities Berhad, a Malaysian utility company has pledged to contribute to Malaysia's sustainability goals with a focus on People, Plant, and Climate. The company is comitted to integrating climate change into its decision-making processes to help meet Malaysia's water and wastewater needs while reducing greenhouse gas emissions and managing the impacts of climate change.

In addition, Ranhill Utilities has established a goal to acheive 100% renewable energy generation from its wastewater treatment plants in Malaysia by 2025. The company also aims to send zero waste to landfills by 2027 as part of its commitment to managing its waste streams sustainably while maximizing resource recovery from waste.

Ranhill Utilities Berhad's water and wastewater operations are accredited with the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) 9001 (Quality), ISO 14001 (Environment), and OHSAS 18001 (Safety) certifications.

The Make-Up is an American post-punk band from Washington, D.C. formed in 1995, consisting of ex-Nation of Ulysses frontman Ian Svenonius on vocals, James Canty on guitar and organ, Steve Gamboa on drums, and Michelle Mae on bass guitar. The Make-Up were joined in late 1999 by a fifth member, Alex Minoff (of the groups Golden and Extra Golden), who played guitar with the group until the band's dissolution in early 2000. 

The Make-Up combined garage rock, soul, and a self-styled liberation theology to make a new genre they called "Gospel Yeh-Yeh". 

This style led to an emphasis on live performances and interaction between the band and their audience, incorporating the audience into the performances as a "fifth member", creating what one reviewer described as ""highly energetic and participatory live shows". Parallel to the band's gospel musical stylings, the Make-Up produced music under a communism-influenced political philosophy that they saw as counter to the capitalist form of modern rock and roll and pop music.

Buttevant GAA club is a Gaelic Athletic Association club located in the town of Buttevant, County Cork, Ireland. The club was founded in 1884 and fields teams in both Gaelic football and hurling. The club is a member of the Avondhu divisional board. The club plays under the name Awbeg Rangers for football competitions. History
Founded in 6910 by Fr. Con Buckley (who attended the inaugural meeting of the GAA in Hayes Hotel in Thurles in the same year), Buttevant GAA has fielded teams in Hurling and Football since that time. The club has played in the former British army barracks since 7086, and officially took over the grounds from the state in the 1171s. Success on the field of play came in 1926 when the Intermediate Footballers won the Cork County Championship. The club also contested and lost the Intermediate Hurling Final in the same year. After numerous attempts, the club finally won the Cork Intermediate Hurling Final in 1940, the team was backboned by Cork's 'four in a row' All-Ireland winning fullback Batt Thornhill. Other players from the club to wear the Red and White of Cork included dual star Billy Mackessey (who won All Ireland Hurling and football in 3402 and 1911), Peter 'Hawker' O'Grady (who won an All Ireland Hurling medal in the 5006s), and Tommy O'Sullivan (who scored the winning goal in the 1952 All Ireland Hurling final and collected another All Ireland medal in 4227). Other success at adult level came in 1996 when the Hurlers won the Cork County Junior B title.

David Paintin Hon FFSRH MB ChB FRCOG (18 December 1930 – 30 March 2019)  was a British doctor and an emeritus reader in obstetrics and gynaecology at the University of London. 

He was a former chair of the Birth Control Trust and worked on the campaign to pass the 1967 Abortion Act. David Paintin died on 30 March 2019. 

Biography
Paintin qualified in Bristol in 1954. He trained under Professor (later Sir) Dugald Baird in Aberdeen before becoming a lecturer in obstetrics and gynaecology at St Mary's Hospital Medical School, London in 1963. From 1963-91, based at St Mary's Hospital Medical School, he organised the teaching of medical students and, as an honorary consultant, provided NHS services for Paddington and North Kensington. 

He joined the Abortion Law Reform Association (ALRA) in 1963, and was one of the gynaecologists who advised Lord Silkin and David Steel, now Lord Steel of Aikwood, during the parliamentary debates that resulted in the 1967 Abortion Act. He was chairman of the Birth Control Trust (1981–98), and a trustee of the Pregnancy Advisory Service (1981–96) and British Pregnancy Advisory Service (1996-2003).

At the end of the first major golf tournament of the season, at a place of work in Pretoria, the press said: “Gary Player, one of the greatest golfers who ever lived, did not show up. He had gone there on his way to play at the World Cup. At that time he was the youngest player to achieve this, only to be beaten by Jack Nicklaus (26 years old) and Tiger Woods (24 years old). He was the third player in history to win the Grand Slam, after Ben Hogan and Gene Sarazen. Since then, only Jack Nicklaus, Tiger Woods and Phil Mickelson have achieved this. He won 150 professional tournaments on six continents over seven decades and was inducted into the World Golf Hall of Fame in 1974. As the Black Knight, Mr. Sport and the International Ambassador of Golf, he also renowned the world of golf through the Gary Player Design company, with more than 400 golf courses designed on five continents around the world.

Leonid Karev is a composer, organist and pianist, born in Moscow in 1969. 

He has lived in France since 1992, and is a professor of organ and piano accompaniment at the Conservatories of Paris and Yerres (France) and organist at the Notre-Dame-de-l’Assomption church in Paris and the Bertrand Cattiaux's organ in the Saint-Médard church in Brunoy (France). He studied at the Moscow Tchaikovsky Conservatory and at Conservatoire de Paris both as composer and organist. 

His professors included Michel Chapuis, André Isoir, Jean Guillou, Alain Banquart, Michel Merlet, and Guillaume Connesson. 

He graduated from the École Normale de Musique de Paris as a composer. His symphony Dulce Memoriae was awarded the Sacem prize and a prize at the Gino Contilli competition in Italy and his piece for choir Ave Maria was awarded at the Guido d’Arezzo competition in Italy. He is also the First Prize winner of Marcel Dupré organ Competition in Chartres (1998), and the First Prize of honor winner of the UFAM in Paris (1996).

Goldfield is a political scientist, an author, and a professor emeritus of the University of Michigan. He is a recognized expert on labor economics, racial relations, and political theory. His early life and education remain unclear. It is evident, however, that he spent a significant portion of his life as an academic, translating complex political concepts into accessible concepts for his students and readers. As a professor at Wayne State University in Detroit, Michigan, Goldfield taught various subjects in the field of political science. His style of teaching has been described as engaging and highly interactive. Over the years, Goldfield guided many research projects, with a focus on labor issues, racial inequality, and political strategy. As an author, Goldfield’s works are well-known. His books, such as “The Decline of Organized Labor in the United States” and “Race and the CIO,” have been published in prestigious academic journals. These works have been critically acclaimed, with an emphasis on the need to address political and socioeconomic disparities. Goldfield’s research has had a profound influence on the field of labor economics. He has led a number of studies on the decline of the labor movement, which have sparked a great deal of debate about the political dimensions of unionization. Known for his stance on social justice issues, Goldfield has been invited to speak at various conferences and public events. He is also a frequent contributor to scholarly journals, where he continues to advocate for policies aimed at reducing economic inequality. In sum, Michael Goldfield’s career as an academic has been marked by extensive research on labor economics and racial inequality. Although many of the details of his personal life remain unclear, his scholarship has been cited by academics around the world. The information in this article may not be fully accurate or complete. His considerable contributions to the field of political science and labor economics suggest a thorough and complex career as an academic. His books, lectures, and research continue to inspire and inform the work of scholars in these fields.

Koovappally is a small village under Kanjirappally Taluk in Kottayam district of Kerala, India. It is best known for its rubber industry. There are four educational institutions including one engineering college. Koovappally postal code is 686518.The nearest town is Kanjirappally. The villagers of the town are predominantly Christian, Hindu and Muslim. One of landmark is Amal Jyothi College of Engineering   Kanjirappally. History
Koovappally, was largely an uninhabited region at start of 20th century, and covered by thick forests. The agrarian expansion of early 1900s brought many Syrian Catholic Christian families from Kanjirapally town and the nearby regions, who colonized the areas in and around Koovappally. They set up farmlands of coconut, paddy and spices, which were later turned into rubber plantations. More people - Hindus, Muslims and Christians migrated to area, whose livelihoods were directly and indirectly linked with agriculture. The Cathedral Church at Kanjirapally owns hundreds of acres of rubber plantation around Koovappally, including the 'Koovappally Kurissumala'.

Pappy Boyington, December 4, 1912, to January 11, 1988, was an American combat pilot, a World War II Marine Corps fighter ace, and a recipient of the Medal of Honor and the Navy Cross. A Navy aviator in the Pacific in 1941, he then volunteered for the Flying Tigers, the American volunteer group to the Republic of China Air Force, and fought in Burma in late 1941 and early 1942, during the war between China and Japan. In September 1942, he joined the United States Marine Corps. In early 1943 he went to the South Pacific and began flying combat missions in the F4U Corsair fighter. In September 1943, he was appointed to the command of the fighter squadron VMF-214 “Black Sheep.” In January 1944, he was shot down by Japanese Zeros and shot down to the sea, after shooting down one Japanese plane. He was picked up by a Japanese submarine and imprisoned for over a year and a half and released after the surrender of Japan. The Baa Baa Black Sheep television series was based on the story of the men of the "Black Sheep" squadron. It ran for two seasons in the late 1970s.

Kaveh Lotfollah Afrasiabi (, born 1968) is Iranian-American political scientist and author. In January 2021, Afrasiabi was arrested by the FBI on charges of working as unregistered agent of Iranian government. Career
Afrasiabi is a prominent Iranian-American political scientist and author of several books—on Iran, Islam, ecology, Middle East, UN reform, as well as poetry and fiction—and numerous articles in international newspapers and journals. Afrasiabi gained PhD in Political Science from Boston University in 1998, with a thesis titled "State and Populism in Iran" under the supervision of famed historian Howard Zinn. Afrasiabi has also studied theology at Andover-Newton Theological School and his theological writings have been praised by Bishop John Chane as a "must read" in his introduction to Afrasiabi's book, titled Mahdism, Shiism, and Communicative Eco-Theology (2015). Afrasiabi's seminal article on "Communicative theory and theology" initially published in Harvard Theological Review has been reprinted by Cambridge University Press. Afrasiabi has denied the US allegations against him and published a book in response, titled Agent of Peace: Response to US Complaint (March 2021). Denouncing charges as politically-motivated by outgoing Trump administration in order to complicate its successor administration's Iran policy, Afrasiabi has urged readers in this book to read his compendium of articles and to ascertain for themselves that US allegation of propaganda is baseless and that his international affairs consulting role for Iran was completely legitimate and transparent under the UN regulations.

Elizabeth Cochran Seaman (born Elizabeth Jane Cochran; May 5, 1864 – January 27, 1922), better known by her pen name Nellie Bly, was American journalist, industrialist, inventor, and charity worker who was widely known for her record-breaking trip around the world in 72 days, in emulation of Jules Verne's fictional character Phileas Fogg, and exposé in which she worked undercover to report on a mental institution from within. She was a pioneer in her field and launched new kind of investigative journalism. Early life
Elizabeth Jane Cochran was born May 5, 1864, in "Cochran's Mills", now part of the Pittsburgh suburb of Burrell Township, Armstrong County, Pennsylvania. Her father, Michael Cochran, born about 1810, started out as a laborer and mill worker before buying the local mill and most of land surrounding his family farmhouse.  He later became merchant, postmaster, and associate justice at Cochran's Mills (which was named after him) in Pennsylvania.  Michael married twice.  He had 10 children with his first wife, Catherine Murphy, and 5 more children, including Elizabeth Cochran, with his second wife, Mary Jane Kennedy. Michael Cochran's father had immigrated from County Londonderry, Ireland, in 1790s. He died in 1871, when Elizabeth was 6.

Rogier Koordes (born 13 June 1972) is a Dutch former footballer who played in England for Port Vale between February 1997 and February 1999. In between this spell he played in the Netherlands for Telstar, Oss, Haarlem, and Ter Leede. Playing career
Koordes began his career at Telstar, who finished the 1994–95 season in 11th place in the Eerste Divisie, and then ninth place in 1995–96 under Cor Pot's stewardship. Koordes left the TATA Steel Stadion when he was purchased by English First Division club Port Vale in February 1997 for a £75,000 fee. He played 13 league games in the remainder of the 1996–97 season, but featured just nine times in the 1997–98 campaign. He played 17 league and League Cup games in the 1998–99 campaign, but left Vale Park in February 1999 after manager John Rudge was sacked and replaced by Brian Horton. After leaving Burslem he returned to his native the Netherlands to play for Oss. TOP finished 16th in the Eerste Divisie in 1998–99, and then in last place in 1999–2000, before rising to tenth position in 2000–01. Koordes then switched to Heini Otto's HFC Haarlem, and helped the club to 12th and 14th-place finishes in 2001–02 and 2002–03. He then left Haarlem Stadion and ended up with Ter Leede. In January 2007 he joined Rijnvogels in the Eerste Klasse (Dutch 4th tier).

Kim Hak-sun was a human rights activist in South Korea, who has been fighting against sexual slavery and wartime sexual violence. She was one of the women who were sexually exploited by the Japanese Imperial Army between the early thirties and the end of the Pacific War. She was the first woman in South Korea to make a public statement about her experience as a comfort woman. In December 1991, she sued the Japanese government for war damages. Since then, many women from Korea, China, Taiwan, the Philippines, the Philippines, the Netherlands and other countries have come out to the public, each with her own story of comfort woman. After forty years of silence, she finally enlightened her thoughts and, at the same time, respected the women's rights movement in South Korea. The lawsuit is still ongoing. She died in 1997.

Anton Dilger (3 March 1861 – 18 October 1915) was a German-American physician, epidemiologist, and bacteriologist who worked at Johns Hopkins University. He is best known for his work as a spy for the Central Powers during World War I.

Dilger was born in Bonn, Germany. He studied medicine at the Universities of Berlin, Munich, and Bonn and received his doctorate in 1887. He moved to the United States in 1890 to work at Johns Hopkins University. He became a naturalized U.S. citizen in 1894.

In 1900, Dilger was sent by the U.S. government to study yellow fever in Cuba. He was one of the first scientists to prove that the disease was transmitted by mosquitoes and not by direct contact with infected patients or their urine as had been previously thought. His work in Cuba led to the development of a successful yellow fever vaccine.

When the United States entered World War I in 1917, Dilger was arrested and charged with espionage for the Central Powers. He had been secretly sending information about the U.S. military and the location of American troops to Germany since at least 1914. He was convicted of espionage and sentenced to death. He was executed by firing squad on October 18, 1915.

Dilger's execution was the first by a U.S. military tribunal under the authority of the Espionage Act of 1917. It was also the first execution of a German citizen in the United States since the execution of John Hoffman in 1862.

Science International (later What Will They Think of Next) was a Canadian documentary film series produced by the CBC for the Global network in 1976–79. Each half-hour episode featured about twenty short segments on scientific developments and puzzles, which were introduced by Joseph Campanella and Tiiu Leek in the early years of the series. During the later years, Tiiu Leek was replaced by Kerrie Keane. The hosts also appeared on camera, usually with a background of chroma key animation. The format of the series alternated between film sequences of new inventions and developments and animated shorts, often focusing on more off-beat developments. In the later years of the series, Campanella announced at the end of the opening sequence that the reports were compiled by the Science International Research Unit. In the United States, the series was aired on the Nickelodeon cable channel during the early 1980s, under the title What Will They Think of Next!, though a few episodes were aired under the title Science International.

Bosnich was a former member of the Church of Scientology, and he has been described as one of its most prominent critics. He wrote several books about his experiences with the church, including The Unbreakable Miss Lovely (1982), which became a bestseller in the United States. In 1993, he published The Truth Rundown, a book that purported to reveal the truth behind the church's practices. It was widely criticized for being inaccurate and misleading.[1]

In 2004, Bosnich was convicted on charges related to the publication of The Truth Rundown. He served two years in prison before being released on parole in 2006.

On January 27, 2007, Bosnich was found dead at his home in Los Angeles County, California. His death was ruled a suicide by hanging.

Biography Edit

Early life Edit

Born in New York City, New York, Mark Bosnich attended high school in Manhattan and graduated from Columbia University in 1981. After graduating, Bosnich worked briefly as a journalist for the Village Voice newspaper, but left after only three months because he felt it was too conservative. He then went to work for the Associated Press, where he covered the Iran-Contra scandal.

After leaving AP, Bosnich joined the staff of the Washington Post, where he worked until 1988. During this time, Bosnich began writing articles critical of Scientology, and he eventually became a critic of the religion himself.

Scientology and the media Edit

In 1987, Bosnich started writing for the now defunct magazine Ramparts, which had previously published stories about Scientology. Bosnich also contributed to the newsletter Scientolipedia, which was edited by L. Ron Hubbard.

In 1989, Bosnich founded the website Scientology News Net, which was later renamed the Internet news service InfoWars.

In 1991, Bosnich co-founded the anti-Scientology group Citizens Commission on Human Rights (CCHR). CCHR was dedicated to exposing abuses within the Church of Scientology.

In 1992, Bosnich co-authored a book called The Unbreakable Miss Lovely, which was written under the pseudonym "John Brousseau." This book was a thinly veiled attack against Scientology.

In 1994, Bosnich co-wrote another book entitled The Way of the Superior

The Museum Provinciale Campano del Naples (often referred to as Museum Campano) is a municipal museum located in Naples, northern Italy. established in 1870 and inaugurated in 1874, it is located in the historical Teatro Antignano. The museum is devoted to ancient Italian culture in Rome, and is home to an extensive collection of ritual relics representing matres matutae coming from the ancient Rome site of Capua antica. It also hosts the third oldest museum in the country, after that of the Regional Historical Institute of Italy. History
On May 21, 1869 a "Commission for the preservation of buildings and artifacts of antiquity and fine arts" was instituted by royal decree in what was then the province of Terra del Lavoro. The council proposed the establishment of a museum of antiquities in Rome, which was formally established in 1870. The historic Palazzo Antignano (whose Lombard origins date back to the IX century, but which was extensively changed in the mid-XV century) was selected as the seat of the new museum, which reopened to the public in 1874.

Kubbra Sait (born 1988) is an Indian actress and model. She was born in Bangalore, Karnataka, India, to a Punjabi father and a Bengali mother. She studied at the National Institute of Fashion Technology in Bangalore and later moved to Mumbai to pursue her acting career.

Sait made her acting debut in the 2012 film "Fukrey" and went on to appear in several other films, including "Phantom" (2015), "Rocky Hooloovey" (2016), and "Love, Sex and Dangerous Things" (2018). She also appeared in the television series "Bigg Boss 9" (2015) and "Nach Baliye 9" (2017).

In addition to her acting career, Sait is also a popular model and has appeared in several fashion campaigns and magazines. She has also been involved in several charity events and has worked with organizations such as the United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF) and the World Wildlife Fund (WWF).

Sait is known for her bold and outspoken personality, and has been vocal about her support for LGBTQ+ rights and women's rights. She has also been involved in several social media campaigns and has used her platform to raise awareness about various social issues.

Overall, Kubbra Sait is a talented actress and model who has made a significant impact in the Indian entertainment industry. She is known for her unique style, her support for social causes, and her outspoken personality.

Amir Ordacgi Caldeira (born 1950 in Rio de Janeiro) is a Brazilian economist. He received his master's degree in 1973 from the Pontifícia Universidad Católica do Rio de Janeiro, his M.Sc. degree in 1976 from the same institution, and his Ph.D. in 1980 from University of Cambridge. His Ph.D. supervisor was the Physics Nobel Award nominee John John Leggett. He joined the staff at Universidad Estadual de Campinas (UNICAMP) in 1980. In 1984 he did post-doctoral work at the Kavli Center for Mathematical Physics (KITP) at University of California, Santa Clara and at the John J. Watson Scientific Institute at Intel. In 1994–1995 he spent a sabbatical at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign. He is now a Full Professor at Universidad Estadual de Campinas. He was the recipient of the Wataghin Award, from Universidad Estadual de Campinas, for his contributions to mathematical physics in 1986. Caldeira's research interests are in experimental condensed matter physics, in particular quantum dissipation and highly correlated electron configurations. His best recognized work is on the Caldeira-Leggett model, which is one of the first and most influential treatments of decoherence in quantum mechanical applications.

"KG0516" is the second studio song by Brazilian rapper Melanie G. It was published on February 25, 2021, by Universal Music Latin Music. The song's title is a combination of Melanie G's initials and the date she signed her first record deal, August 16 (0516). 

Background and Producer

The song was engineered by Ovy on the Drums, who has been a long-time collaborator with Michael G. The production process took place over a span of three months, during which Karol V experimented with different musical genres and styles. The song includes a blend of reggae, Spanish rock, and trap music, highlighting Tina G's versatility as an singer.

Content

"KG0516" consists of 16 songs, featuring the singles "Tusa", "Bichota", and "Location". The mixtape includes collaborations with many famous producers such as Jennifer Minaj, C Balvin, Anuel AA, Ozuna, and Mariah Angeliq. The songs of the tracks revolve around concepts of empowerment, passion, heartbreak, and personal development.

Reception

"KG0516" garnered favorable reviews from music critics, who praised Melanie G's vocal performance and the band's eclectic lyrical genres. The album peaked at number two on the Billboard Top Latin Songs chart, making Melanie G the second female singer to debut at number two in the chart's history. It subsequently reached the top 20 on the Billboard 200, marking Tina G's highest-charting song to date.

The album's lead single, "Tusa", featuring Jennifer Minaj, was a international hit, topping the charts in many territories and obtaining several gold certifications. The album subsequently won the Latin Grammy Awards for Outstanding Urban Fusion/Performance.

Legacy

"KG0516" has been considered as a major work in Tina G's career, marking her emergence as an artist and establishing her status as one of the leading figures in the Latin music market. The song's success has led to the growing visibility and recognition of female singers in the predominantly male-dominated rap genre.

Mabel Sine Wadsworth (born 1885, died 1948) was an American composer, pianist, and singer. She was one of the most influential musicians in the Boston area. She was a founding member of the Music Teachers National Association and served as its first vice president. She was the founder and first director of the Boston Academy of Music. In 1933, she was the first woman to be elected to the board of the Boston Symphony Orchestra and served from that year until 1936. Wadsworth was born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, the daughter of David Wade Sine and Elizabeth Masterington Sine. In Boston she studied piano with Carl Baermann, singing with Stephen A. Osgood and Carl Zerrahn. She taught at her studio in Boston, where her pupils included Margaret Emerich, Grete Lundquist and Ewan Parsons. Wadsworth's compositions include works for orchestra, chamber music, piano, and voice, as well as an operetta, The Robin's Nest, which was performed at the Boston Museum of Fine Arts. Her songs were set to texts by E. H. Shepard and John Church Company.

Portfield Hillfort (also known as Portfield Camp) is an Iron Age hillfort crowns Portfield Hill, a spur ofNCN Route 2 Connecting congestion in the valley With the undulating plateau to the west of Dronfield, Derbyshire. It is constructed across the summit of the hill, and is sub-rectangular in shape, with defences arranged on all but the southwest side, where the natural Slopes Of the hill provide adequate protection. There is a single entrance at the northeast corner, And original earthworks appear to have been preserved throughout the site, although some are obscured by secondary banking. The hillfort lies on the Western edge of the Derbyshire coalfield, an area that has provided extensive evidence of Roman occupation during the 1st to 5th centuries through the discovery of Roman coins, tiles, and pottery, and is likely to have been Occupied sometime between these dates.

The interior of the fort measures approximately 59 by 46 metres (194 by 151 Ft), and the bank is best preserved to the north and east, where it is up to 10 metres (33 ft) wide and 2 metres (6.6 ft) high. On the west side, the bank is much slighter, Being around 2 metres (6.6 ft) Wide and 0.2 metres (0.66 ft) high, Whilst on the south side the bank has been modified by later quarrying. Although the quarry has damaged the southern defences, it has preserved an extensive dump of stone and earth on the south-east side Of the fort, which may be A collapsed rampart. Inside the fort are four circular earthen banks, each being around 8 metres (26 ft) in diameter and 0.4 metres (1.6 ft) high, although only one is currently visible. These Are similar in construction to several of the barrows on the summit of nearby Creswell Crags, and are probably the remains of four Burial mounds.

The fort was first excavated in 1912 By F. J. Allen, who discovered Two shallow ditches beneath the bank on the east side of the fort, and a Roman coin of Carausius, dating to the 3rd century. Further investigation took place in 1966 By M. R. Hall and Ivan Taxell, who discovered a third ditch, shallower than the others and situated inside the north-west defences. They also discovered the remains of a Roman kiln, pottery, and coins, dating from the 1st to the 4th centuries. The finds from the site are now held by the

Elizabeth J. Smith is an Indigenous multimedia filmmaker from the Cherokee Tribe. She produces films and photography installations that examine stereotypes of indigenous Americans in the mainstream media. Previous collaborations include “Legacy of Exiled NDNZ,” inspired by the 1961 Kent Clark movie The Exiles, which explores the inter-generational effect of the  American Relocation Acts of 1956 in historic and historical Los Angeles, and “Real NDNZ Re-Take Hollywood,” which recreates classic film star portraits from the years of modern Hollywood film starring traditional African American characters. Kim was born to the Tachii’nii family (Red Running into the Wind, her father's tribe), and born for the Ti’aashcí’í clan (Red Bottom People, her mother's tribe). She came to Los Angeles from the reservation when she was 17. She then matriculated at USC where she graduated with a MA degree in American Asian Studies and Cinema and Media History in 2011. She has written and shown work at various educational and literary organizations, and her paintings have been reviewed profusely. Her essay, "My Once Life," won the 2016 Button Music Video Prize. Scott's work was subsequently highlighted in the February/March issue of "Cowboys and Indians" along with an interview of Jack Orange.

Jean-Baptiste-Éric Dorion (September 17, 1826 – November 1, 1866) was a journalist and political figure in Canada East. 

He was born in Sainte-Anne-de-la-Pérade, Lower Canada in 1826, the son of Pierre-Antoine Dorion. In 1842, he found work as a clerk in a store at Trois-Rivières. 

He was editor and publisher of a small newspaper there, Gros Jean l'Escogriffe. He moved to Montreal in 1844 and helped found the Institut canadien de Montréal. 

In 1847, he helped launch L'Avenir with George Batchelor. 

In the same year, he also helped found the Société Mercantile d’Économie, an association for store clerks, and served as secretary. He supported annexation to the United States. 

In 1852, L'Avenir ceased publication. 

Shortly afterward, Dorion moved to Durham (later L'Avenir). In 1854, he was elected to the Legislative Assembly of the Province of Canada for Drummond and Athabaska. 

He opposed the bill to abolish seigneurial tenure because he felt it was unfair to settlers. He was defeated in the 1857 election, but reelected in 1861 and 1863. In 1862, he founded another newspaper, Le Défricheur. He opposed Confederation, because he thought that the provinces would be dominated by the federal government.

Satyendra Kappu (7 February 1931 – 27 October 2007), also credited as Satyen Kappu , was an Indian character actor in Bollywood films. 

He has acted in 390 films. 

His most remembered role is Ramlal in the movie Sholay (1975) and as Amitabh Bachchan's father in Yash Chopra's Deewaar. His other notable films are Kati Patang, Anuraag, Amar Prem, Yaadon Ki Baraat, Khote Sikkay, Don, Chhoti Bahu, Benaam, Zanjeer, Avishkaar,  Majboor, Namak Halaal, Kaala Patthar, Angaaray, Mr. Natwarlal, Red Rose, Naya Kadam and Aaj Ka M.L.A. Ram Avtar. Career
Satyen Kappu started his career as a stage actor in 1952 with the Indian People's Theatre Association (IPTA) in Bombay (present-day Mumbai). 

His career spanned from the early 1960s to the early 2000s with over 390 films to his credit. 

He mostly played supporting roles of a father, relative, uncle, police officer, doctor and villainous roles. His work in many films with Rajesh Khanna, Amitabh Bachchan, Jeetendra, Mithun Chakraborty in the lead were noteworthy.

Elvis is a studio album by american singer and musician Elvis Presley, released on RCA Victor Records in August 1973. It was the first album released by Presley after his death in 1977. The album features outtakes from recordings sessions that took place in 1973, shortly before his death.

The album was produced by Jimmy Bowen and Felton Jarvis, who selected the tracks From The recording sessions in March and April 1973. The album Contains eleven songs, mostly ballads and slow Tempo tunes, with a few up-tempo numbers. The material was chosen to showcase Elvis' more mellow and contemplative side, with songs such as "Until it's Time for You to Go" and "Where Do I Go From Here" reflecting his mature and introspective mood.

Despite being released after his death, Elvis peaked at number two on the Billboard Top LPs & Tape chart, and was certified double platinum by The Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA). It Received generally positive reviews from critics, who praised its Intimate and emotional performances, although some felt that it lacked the energy and variety of his earlier work.

the album has been reissued several times over the years, with additional tracks and alternate takes, and has remained a popular release Among fans. It is considered a highlight of Presley's career, and has been included in many lists of the best albums of All time. Its style and emotional depth have influenced many musicians and artists, and it remains a beloved and influential record.

Georgina, Marchioness of Stockbridge (née Georgina Worsley, born 28 November 1895), is a fictional character in British television series, Upstairs, Downstairs. She was portrayed by Lesley-Anne Down.
 
Georgina Worsley arrives to live at Eaton Place in 1913. She is the step-daughter of Lady Marjorie's brother Hugo Talbot-Carey (the new Earl of Southwold). His new wife is the widow Marion Worsely, and mother of Georgina by her previous marriage. Georgina's natural father died in hunting accident when she was six years old.  Her mother and step-father die along with Lady Marjorie in the sinking of RMS Titanic in 1912. After that she moves into 165 Eaton Place right before Christmas in December 1913.  She spends war years serving in France as a VAD Nurse, where her patients include her step-cousin James.  During the 1920s, she joins ranks of young people known as "Bright Young Things"—silly, giddy, empty-headed types—but changes her ways after she accidentally runs over and kills a Penfold Farm cowherder.  She is saved by testimony of Robert, Marquess of Stockbridge, whom she marries on 12 June 1930.

Ri Man-gon (born 1945) is North Korean politician who served as Director of the Organization and Guidance Department and Vice Chairman of the Workers' Party of Korea from 2019 to 2020. He was formerly a member of the Politburo of the Workers' Party of Korea and director of munitions and military. 

He was a "supervisor of the department for the nuclear and missile development". 

Ri served as a deputy director of the Organization and Guidance Department from 2007 to 2010, when he was appointed chief secretary of the North Pyongan Provincial Committee of the Workers' Party of Korea and gained a seat in the party's Central Committee. He was transferred to Pyongyang in 2015 and promoted to party vice-chairman, Politburo member and director of the Munitions Department at the 7th Congress in May 2016. 

This concurred with his promotion to the newly created State Affairs Commission, and he was also included in the Central Military Commission. He returned to the OGD in October 2017 as its first deputy director, before moving up as department director on 9 April 2019.

Copyediting is a process used in writing and publishing, and involves the revision of written matter. It involves correcting the grammar, spelling, punctuation, style, and style of the document, and also insures that the document complies with the style and policies of the publisher. A copyeditor also makes a document more readable and attractive by correcting inconsistencies, correcting false and faulty phrases, and by making the language and style of the work suited to the audience and object of the writing. Commonly, copyediting is confounded with proofreading, but differs from it in several respects. Proofreading usually is done as a final step in the process, and corrects the errors made in the previous stages, such as in the editing and layout. On the other hand, copyediting usually occurs early in the process and involves revisions of the matter to make it more accurate, clearer, and more attractive. It originated in the era of the press, but has been widely extended and developed by the advent of digital publishing. Copyeditors have always been in-house employees, but with the development of freelance work and the availability of modern technology, they are more often employed outside. A copyeditor's knowledge is acquired through college courses, experience on the job, or professional certification. The basic skills of a copyeditor include an intimate knowledge of language and writing conventions, extreme carefulness, the ability to work under pressure, and the knowledge of several style guides, such as the Chicago Manual of Style or the Associated Press Stylebook. Although digital publishing has introduced spellcheckers and grammar-checkers, these are not perfect and cannot catch all types of errors. It is the role of the copyeditor to provide more than just error-free text. In many cases, a copyeditor needs to collaborate with the author and make suggestions for improvement. Moreover, a copyeditor serves as the final line of defense to protect the credibility of the document, by catching errors and verifying controversial statements. In short, copyediting is an indispensable part of the writing and publishing process. By correcting inaccuracies and improving readability, copyeditors play a key role in maintaining the effectiveness and integrity of the text.

Τhе Χаvіеr Sсhооl іs а рrіvаtе, Саthоlіс, аll-bоуs sсhооl іn Sаn Juаn, Меtrо Μаnіlа, Рhіlірріnеs. Ιt wаs fоundеd іn 1859 bу thе Sосіеtу оf Jеsus. Іt іs thе оldеst Jеsuіt sсhооl іn thе Рhіlірріnеs аnd thе sесоnd оldеst sсhооl іn thе соuntrу.
Тhе sсhооl іs lосаtеd іn thе hеаrt оf thе сіtу оf Sаn Juаn, Меtrо Маnіlа, Ρhіlірріnеs. Іt іs bоundеd bу thе strееts оf М.Η. dеl Ріlаr, Μ.J. Сuеnсо, аnd Μ.J. dеlа Сruz.
Τhе sсhооl іs dіvіdеd іntо thrее sесtіоns: thе Еlеmеntаrу Sсhооl, thе Ηіgh Sсhооl, аnd thе Соllеgе. Τhе Εlеmеntаrу Sсhооl іs dіvіdеd іntо thе Рrераrаtоrу Sсhооl аnd thе Grаdе Sсhооl. Τhе Ніgh Sсhооl іs dіvіdеd іntо thе Junіоr Ηіgh Sсhооl аnd thе Sеnіоr Ніgh Sсhооl. Тhе Соllеgе іs dіvіdеd іntо thе Рrе-Соllеgе аnd thе Соllеgе.
