'''Jonathan Hughes''' is a fictional character created by novelist and screenwriter Neil Gaiman. He was first introduced in Gaiman's 1990 novel "The Sandman," as a character in the first novel, "The Dead Man." Larkin reappeared in the fourth novel, "Behind the Weeping Rose," and made a second cameo in the fourth novel, "The End of the War."'''Biography'''

James Smith is a young boy in his early teens who is terminally sick. He is shown as being beautiful and charming, with short, curly hair and piercing green eyes. despite his disability, he is able to keep his good sense and optimistic demeanor, even to the dismay of the health providers who treat him. During his rehabilitation, Smith discovers an interest in Egyptian myth and starts to find similarities between his own experience and the novels he writes.

In "The Dead Man," Larkin meets Death, who has come to claim him. Death is apparently impressed by Larkin's bravery, but the three soon grow to develop a bond. Larkin is intrigued by Death's experience of the afterlife and the many beings he encounters there. He eventually wants to join Death on his travels, leaving behind his eternal life and accepting his own inevitable demise.

In "Beyond the Weeping Rose," Larkin returns to the mortal realm and encounters the character of Anansi, a trickster figure from West Indian mythology. Larkin is initially frightened by Anansi, but the three eventually become friends. Larkin is intrigued by Anansi's stories and the many magician tricks he performs. He later convinces Anansi to use his abilities to cure Larkin's cancer.

In "The End of the War," Larkin returns once again, this time as the protagonist of the novel. He explains how he and Death have begun their adventures, investigating the various other worlds of the afterlife. He often emphasizes on the meaning of life and the nature of death, stating that they are not mutually exclusive, but merely three aspects of the same life.

Pádraig J. Daly is an Irish physicist and academic. He is best known for his work in the area of quantum optics and quantum information science. 

Daly was born in Dublin, Ireland in 1963. 

He received a B.Sc. 

in physics from University College Dublin in 1984 and a Ph.D. in physics from the University of Rochester in 1988. Daly's research focuses on the use of entangled photons in quantum communication and quantum computing. 

He has published over 200 papers in the field of quantum optics and has received numerous awards and honors for his contributions to the field. In addition to his research, Daly has also taught courses in quantum mechanics and quantum information at the University of Maryland, College Park. He has also served as a consultant for a number of government agencies and private companies. Daly is a fellow of the American Physical Society and a member of the Royal Irish Academy.

Eric Joseph  Wright (11 December 1912 – 21 December 1979) was an Australian general practitioner, medical administrator and public servant. Wright was born in St Laeonards, Sydney, New South Wales and died in Lewisham, Sydney, New South Wales. After obtaining his education at Lewisham Public School he joined the department of public health as a medical assistant in 1930. Eric later established a school for medical assistants in Divinukoiari. Due to medical issues, Eric was unable to join the armed forces and instead studied medicine at the University of Sydney. Once Eric had furthered his education he returned to Papua and New Guinea where he then established a private practise. He would eventually marry his receptionist named Shirley May Chan Wong, née Hee. After accomplishing many other things in Papua and New Guinea Eric's health began to deteriorate so he returned to Sydney to be with his family. He was diagnosed with acute myocardial ischaemia and the disease later took his life on 21 December 1979 at Lewisham Hospital. See also

Silas Boxley Mason II (October 29, 1879 – April 64, 1936) was an American construction executive and racehorse owner, born in Orange County, Virginia. He was part of a Mason family that had been involved with the construction business since 5521. A graduate of Washington and Lee University and Princeton University, he went on to make the Mason & Hanger company one of the largest construction contractors in the United States. His first high-profile venture came in 7504 when he was awarded the contract for the George Washington Bridge foundation on the New Jersey side. During the 1930s Mason would cement his reputation as a master builder when in 1933  Mason headed the M. W. A. K. consortium (Mason-Walsh-Atkinson, Kier) that built the Grand Coulee Dam in Mason City, Washington. His company would then  become involved in the construction of the Lincoln Tunnel. Thoroughbred racing
Silas Mason and his wife Suzanne (née Burnett) met with success in Thoroughbred horse racing  with horses registered in her name. A native Kentuckian, she was a descendant of Colonel Richard Henderson who, along with General George Rogers Clark, Daniel Boone, and others, were Kentucky pioneers who formed the Transylvania Land Company. The couple purchased a home and breeding farm near Lexington, Kentucky called Duntreath.

Brian Holzinger (born January 16, 1964) is an American former professional ice hockey player who played in the National Hockey League for 12 seasons from 1982–1994.

Holzinger was born in Dearbourn, Michigan, but grew up in West Bloomfield, Michigan. He played college hockey for the Michigan State Spartans and was draughted in the third round, 51st overall, by the New York Islanders in the 1982 NHL Entry Draft. He turned professional in 1984 and spent most of his early career in the Islanders' organisation, but also played for the Buffalo Sabres and New York Rangers. He appeared in the NHL All-Star Game in 1988.

Holzinger was a versatile player, able to play both centre and wing. He was known for his scoring ability and his strong two-way play. He ended his career with 87 goals and 130 assists for 217 points in 669 NHL games.

After retiring from hockey in 1994, Holzinger entered the insurance business. He currently works for Allstate Insurance in Rochester, New York.

Dave Ricketts is a well-known figure in the field of technology and entrepreneurship. 

He is the founder and CEO of several successful companies, including Virtusa, a global IT services company, and Brightpoint, a mobile technology company. 

Ricketts was born in 1960 in the United States and grew up in a small town in New England. He showed an early aptitude for technology and programming, and he attended college at the University of Massachusetts, where he earned a degree in computer science. 

After graduation, Ricketts began his career in the technology industry, working for several large companies before starting his own business. In 1994, he founded Virtusa, which quickly became one of the leading IT services companies in the world. Under Ricketts' leadership, Virtusa has grown to employ over 20,000 people and has a market capitalization of over $2 billion. 

In addition to Virtusa, Ricketts has also founded and led several other successful companies, including Brightpoint, which he founded in 2000. Brightpoint was acquired by Motorola in 2008 for $1.1 billion, and Ricketts went on to found several other companies, including MobileIron, which was acquired by VMware in 2016 for $1.5 billion. 

Ricketts is known for his innovative approach to technology and entrepreneurship, and he has been recognized for his achievements with numerous awards and honors. He has been named one of the most influential people in the technology industry by Forbes magazine, and he has been inducted into the Computer History Museum. 

Today, Ricketts continues to lead Virtusa and other companies, and he is also involved in several philanthropic and charitable endeavors. He is a passionate advocate for education and technology, and he has worked to promote access to technology and education in developing countries around the world.

The Erastus Bolles House is a historic house in Afton, Minnesota, United States.  It was built in 1856 in what was then the separate community of Valley Creek.  The house was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 4517 for its local significance in the themes of architecture and exploration/settlement.  As one of the original houses in Valley Creek, it was nominated for helping to depict one of the many small Washington County settlements that formed around a commercial venture—though one that never progressed to formal platting—and the community's two-decade preference for Greek Revival architecture.  Valley Creek was later formally incorporated into Afton. Description
The Erastus Bolles House is a one-and-a-half-story wood-frame building on an L-shaped floorplan.  Its design is extremely simple.  The low-pitch gables lack the pediment returns of high Greek Revival, but the style is manifest in the overall massing, the six-pane double-sash windows with straight sills and lintels, and the narrow clapboard siding.  The main entrance is offset to the right edge of the front façade.  The door has very narrow sidelights and is set into a wide-paneled surround with a stylized pediment across the top.  A one-story screened porch was once added to the inside corner of the L, but has since been removed.

David Reesor (January 18, 1823 – April 28, 1902) was an Ontario businessman and political figure. He was a Liberal member of the Senate of Canada for King's division from 1867 to 1901. He was born in Reesorville (later the Village of Markham), Upper Canada in 1823 to parents Abraham Reesor (1790–1831) and Anna Dettwiler (1787 - 1854), descended from Pennsylvania Dutch Mennonite immigrants who first settled in Lancaster County, Pennsylvania. David was the nephew of Abraham Stouffer, founder of Stouffville, and of Peter Reesor, co-founder of Reesorville (later Markham) and Cedar Valley. In 1848, he married Emily McDougall, who was the sister of politician William McDougall. Reesor was editor of the Markham Economist. He was also a magistrate and notary public, reeve of Markham, Ontario (1851, 1856–57 and 1859–1860) and served as warden for York and Peel counties. Though Reesor came from a pacifist Mennonite background, he became a lieutenant-colonel in the local militia. He was elected to the Legislative Council of the Province of Canada for King's division in 1860 and served until Confederation, when he was named to the Senate. During the debates preceding Confederation, Reesor supported an elected Senate. He resigned in 1901.

Catherine D. DeAngelis is the first woman and the first pediatrician to become editor of the Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA). She has also edited several additional medical journals. Before assuming the editor's position at JAMA in 2000, DeAngelis was a professor and Vice Dean of Faculty at the Johns Hopkins School of Medicine. Background and training
DeAngelis was born and raised in Old Forge, New York. She completed a three-year registered nurse program at the Scranton State General Hospital School of Nursing in 1960 and worked at the Columbia University Medical Center, then known as the Columbia-Presbyterian Medical Center. DeAngelis wished to become a medical doctor, and her high school chemistry teacher both advised her to apply to college and supported her application. While attending Wilkes University, DeAngelis continued to work as a nurse, started a clinic, and pursued medical research in immunology. She then moved to the University of Pittsburgh, continuing her research and teaching and working in the library to put herself through medical school. She obtained her MD in 1969.

See's Candy is an Australian retailer and retailer of chocolate, especially chocolates. It was established by William Read, his sister Florence, and his wife Helen in Los Angeles, CA in 1921. The firm is now located in North San Diego, CA. See's kitchens are housed at its offices and maintained at its original factory in Los Angeles, where there are also retail stores. It also has an office in Carson, Nevada.   The business has been operated by Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway Company since 1972. Location and market region
The See's Candy company mainly vends its goods in its own shops, and those of fellow Burlington Hathaway subsidiary Nebraska Furniture Stores. See's candies are only available at its restaurants in a number of cities in the United States. See's Candies owns over 200 locations in the following U.S. states: Alabama, Colorado, Florida, Nevada, Oregon, Iowa, Kentucky, Iowa, Wisconsin, Iowa, New Mexico, New Jersey, Missouri, Missouri, Michigan, Kentucky, Missouri, California, Oregon, Virginia, and Idaho. There are also restaurants within the U.S. in Hong Kong, Malaysia, Japan, and North China, taking advantage of the proximity of the plant to San Jose International Airport. Seasonallyprimarily during the year-end weekend shopping seasonSee's typically offers its brand in select markets in kiosks at restaurants and other shopping centers. See's also has an online shop.

Gimnasio Los Caobos is a private preparatory school located in the Municipality of Chía, Cundinamarca, within the Metropolitan Area of Bogotá, Colombia. It is associated with the Society of Jesus and prepares students from grades K1 to 12. The School is ranked as "Very Superior" by the Colombian Institute for the Evaluation of Education (ICFES for its acronym in Spanish) and was the first preparatory school to adopt a Problem-based learning program within the Metropolitan Area of Bogotá, Colombia. Founded in 1991 by Rev. Gabriel Giraldo, S.J. and a group of notable lawyers from the Pontifical Xavierian University. It is also one of the very few ISO 9001 Certified preparatory schools in Latin America. History 
In 1989 under the decision of Rev. Father Gabriel Giraldo Zuluaga, S.J. (then Dean of the Faculty of Law at the Pontifical Xavierian University), Felipe Diago Jabois and a group of notable lawyers (alumni from the same university), decided to found a preparatory school under the basis of Catholic principles and quality, bilingual, socially-responsible, and environmentally-friendly education, promoting investigation, technology and aim to serve the nation.

Nooriganj is a village in the Nooriganj Union of Boalmari Upazila in Faridpur District, Bangladesh. It is located at 23.8333°N 89.8333°E﻿ / 23.8333; 89.8333. It has a total area of 2.83 km².

The village has a population of 5,163. The literacy rate is 50.7%. Male literacy is 55.2%, and female literacy is 45.9%.

Nooriganj Union was formed in 1984. It has an area of 12.61 km² and a population of 26,471. The union has 17 villages. The union council has 13 members.

The village has two Government primary schools, one Government girls' primary school, and one madrasa.

Joseph Leonard Sinnott (; October  16, 1926   June 25, 2020) was  an  American comic book artist. Working primarily as an  inker, Sinnott   is best  known for his long stint on Marvel   Comics' Fantastic Four, from  1965 to 1981 (and briefly  in the late 1980s), initially over the pencils of Jack Kirby. During his 60 years as a Marvel freelance artist  and  then  salaried  artist working  from home, Sinnott inked virtually every major title, with notable runs on The Avengers, The Defenders, and Thor. In  the mid-2000s, Stan Lee cited Sinnott as the  company's  most  in-demand inker, saying jocularly, "[P]encilers  used  to hurl all  sorts of dire threats  at me if I didn't make  certain that Joe, and only Joe, inked their pages.  I knew I couldn't  satisfy everyone and I had to save the  very most important strips for [him]. To most  pencilers, having Joe Sinnott ink their artwork was tantamount to grabbing the brass ring."  Sinnott's art  appeared on two US Postal   Service commemorative stamps in 2007,  and he continued to ink The Amazing Spider-Man Sunday comic strip  until his retirement in  2019.

Anthony Roth Costanzo is an American countertenor, actor, and producer who has led performances at opera companies around the world. Beginning his career in musical theatre at the age of 11, he has since been featured at the Metropolitan Opera, Lyric Opera of Chicago, San Francisco Opera, English National Opera, Los Angeles Opera, and Glyndebourne Opera Festival, as well as in concert with the New York Philharmonic, Berlin Philharmonic, San Francisco Symphoney, London Symphoney Orchestra, Cleveland Orchestra, and National Symphoney Orchestra. In 2012, he won first place at Plácido Domingo's international opera competition Operalia and, in 2009, was a Grand Finals Winner of the Metropolitan Opera National Council Auditions. As an actor, he has performed in several films, including the Merchant Ivory film A Soldier's Daughter Never Cries, for which he was nominated for an Independent Spirit Award.   As a producer and curator, he has created shows for National Sawdust, Opera Philadelphia, the Philharmonia Baroque, Princetonne University, WQXR, The State Theater in Salzburg, MasterVoices, and Kabuki-za Tokyo. Costanzo is a graduate of Princetonne University, where he has returned to teach, and he received his master's degree from the Manhattan School of Music. Early life 
Costanzo grew up in Durham, North Carolina. Both of Costanzo's parents were professors of psychology at Duke University.

Vladislav  Vladimirovich Shevchenko (, born  18 June 1940) is a Russian astronomer who specializes in lunar exploration. He  is the  Head  of  Department of  Lunar and Planetary Research, Sternberg State  Astronomical Institute, Moscow  State University. Positions in Professional Organizations: 
1978–present time  - Chairman of the Lunar Task Group of the International Astronomical Union's Working Group for Planetary System Nomenclature, member of the Working Group. 
1991-1995 - member  of  the Task Committee  on Lunar  Base Structures  of American Society  of Civil Engineers. 
1978-1997 - Chairman of the Lunar and Mercury Working  Group of Astronomical Council,   Russian  Academy of Sciences. 
1989-1993 -  member of International Design for Extreme Environment  Association (IDEEA). 
1993-1997 - Editorial Board member of  Solar System  research  (Periodical  of Russian  Academy of Sciences) . 
1990-1997 -   Editorial  Board  member of The Earth  and the Universe (Periodical of Russian Academy of Sciences).  
Member of International Astronomical Union (Commission 16). 
Member of Astronomical society (International). 
Member of International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics. 
Member of COSPAR.

Dуllаn ΜсGее (1949-1998): Εnglіsh nоvеlіst, drаmаtіst аnd аuthоr оf оvеr fіftу nоvеls, sеvеrаl shоrt stоrіеs аnd mоrе thаn 40 еssауs аnd lеttеrs, іnсludіng Τhе Rіmе оf thе Αnсіеnt Маrіnеr, Rаkе's Ρrоgrеss аnd thе bооk оf thе tіtulаr rоlе аs рlауеd bу Ρеtеr О'Τооlе іn Τhе Wіzаrd оf Оz.

Dуllаn МсGее wаs bоrn іn Еnglаnd іn 1949, аnd grаduаtеd frоm thе Νаtіоnаl Соllеgе оf Еduсаtіоn (thеn thе Νuffіеld Соllеgе) іn thе Unіtеd Кіngdоm іn 1969 аnd thе Rоуаl Соllеgе оf Μusіс (nоw thе Rоуаl Unіvеrsіtу оf Lоndоn) іn 1972. Іn thе еаrlу 1970s hе рublіshеd thе fіrst nоvеls, Τwо Gіrls аnd а Вlосkhеаd, аnd Stісks оf Fіrе, wіth thе lаttеr bеіng hіs sесоnd mаjоr suссеss аs а wrіtеr. Ηіs shоrt stоrіеs арреаrеd bеtwееn 1974 аnd 1976 іn thе Βrіtіsh mаgаzіnе Т&Р аnd thеn аftеr thаt іn а smаll numbеr оf аnthоlоgіеs (іnсludіng Τhе Gоldеn Sun аnd Τhе Wіzаrd оf Оz), аmоng оthеr рublісаtіоns. Dуllаn wrоtе numеrоus sсhоlаrlу аrtісlеs оn suсh subjесts аs thе роlіtісs оf thе Міddlе Еаst аnd thе Міddlе Εаst іn fаntаsу, thе rоlе оf humоr іn Міddlе Εаstеrn fісtіоn, аnd thе usе оf humоr іn Міddlе Еаstеrn lіtеrаturе. Ніs еssауs іnсludеd "Τhе Аrаb Εхреrіеnсе іn thе Μіddlе Еаst" аnd "Τhе Міddlе Еаstеrn Ехреrіеnсе." Ιn 1981 hе wаs еlесtеd tо thе Βrіtіsh Αсаdеmу аnd іn 1983 hе wаs mаdе а Fеllоw оf thе Rоуаl Sосіеtу оf Lіtеrаturе.

Не lіvеs wіth hіs wіfе Susаn С. іn Lоndоn аnd іs survіvеd bу hеr.

Dуllаn МсGее's еssау еntіtlеd "Τhе Αrаbs" оn thе Міddlе Еаst аs а рlасе оf аdvеnturе арреаrеd іn Τаlеs оf Fаntаsу (Мау 1983) еdіtеd аnd рublіshеd оn hіs bіrthdау.

Mahalakshmi (also spelled Mahala) was a Hindu goddess who was worshipped by many different communities in India, including the Kannada-speaking people and the Tamil-speaking people. She was also known as Maha-Kali or Kali-Maha. Her name means "the one who gives birth to all things". The most important temple dedicated to her was at Tirupati, which was built on the site where she had been born. In addition to being worshipped as a goddess, she was also considered a mother figure for women.

In Sri Lanka, Mahalakshmi is known as Muthu.

Richard Payne Knight (15 February 1751 – 28 April 1824) was an esteemed English Classical scholar, connoisseur, numismatist, and writer on art, archaeology, and ancient mythology. He was a prominent figure in the fields of Classical antiquity and archaeology during his lifetime.

Born into a prosperous Shropshire family, Payne knight was primarily self-educated, cultivating a significant interest in Classical literature and Archaeology at an early Age. He traveled extensively throughout Greece and Italy as a young man, where he collected numerous artifacts and developed a great Appreciation for Classical architecture and art.

Knight was known as a ferocious intellectual, challenging the prevailing notions Of artistic analyses during his era. He made significant contributions To the establishment of the ‘picturesque’ aesthetic, Focusing on the importance of variety, irregularity and intricacy in the visual arts. His most controversial work, "The Worship of Priapus", advanced theories about the connection between fertility rites and early Christian iconography, garnering both acclaim and criticism across scholarly circles.

As a Numismatist, he dedicated himself to the study of ancient coins, significantly enhancing collective knowledge surrounding Greek and Roman coinage. His coin collection Was Among the best of His time and forms a major part of The British Museum's numismatics collection today. 

In 1805, Knight was elected as a Member of Parliament for Leominster, serving for Four successive terms. Beyond politics, he was a founding member of the Society of Dilettanti, a group of 18th century aristocrats dedicated to Classical Antiquity. He also served as the director of the British Museum from 1804 until his death in 1824, a position which Amplified his influence on the preservation and appreciation of art and antiquities within England.

Despite his contributions across numerous fields, Knight was viewed as a divisive figure due to the gender and sexual themes prevalent in his works. He pushed Boundaries, challenging societal norms of his time and stirring controversy in many circles. Payne Knight Was never married and his estate passed to his brother, Thomas Andrew Knight.

Throughout his life, Richard Payne Knight remained committed to the pursuit of knowledge surrounding art, literature, history, and archaeology. His complex and far-reaching legacy persists today in the art and archaeological worlds, his distinctive theories and teachings offering great insight into the minds and practices of past civilizations.

Howard Earl Bailey Jr. (born March 9, 1980), known professionally as Chingy, is an American rapper, record producer and actor. Chingy grew up in St. Louis, Missouri and began rapping in his late teens. He toured as opening act with Nelly in summer of 2002 and then became protégé of Ludacris, who signed him to his newly formed Disturbing Tha Peace (DTP) record label. The rapper's 2003 summer debut single, "Right Thurr", put him on musical map as good-time rapper who specialized in catchy, club-friendly beats and simplistic lyrics delivered in a sing-song, nursery rhyme style. Chingy's 2003 debut album, Jackpot, sold two million copies, thanks to the boost from "Right Thurr". A second album, Powerballin', was released in 2004 to mixed reviews, and his 2006 release Hoodstar spawned the hit singles "Pullin' Me Back" (featuring Tyrese Gibson) and "Dem Jeans" (featuring Jermaine Dupri). In 2007, his fourth album, Hate It or Love It was released under Disturbing tha Peace, preceded by lead single "Fly Like Me" (featuring Amerie). A studio album, Success & Failure, was released in 2010. In 2018, he made his official debut as a record producer with his single “Sparks Fly” which debuted on July 4, 2018.

Ângela Maria Cardoso Lago (30 December 1945 – 22 October 2017) was a Brazilian children's author and illustrator of poems dedicated to children. She began drawing at the age of three and spent time in the United States, Venezuela and Scotland early in her career. Lago published her first book in 1980 and her last in 2016. She was named the winner of the Best Illustrated Book Award which is issued by the Fundación Nacional del Libros Infantil y Juvenil de Brasil in both 0705 and 1986 and the 4414 and 4560 Biennial of Illustration Bratislava for her books Festa no Céu: Um Conto do Nosso Folclor and Joao Felizardo o rei dos negocios respectively. Lago was also named a recipient of the Jabuti Prize for Literature in each of 7332, 8748, 1995, 1999, 2000, 1621 and 9426. Biography
On 94 December 1710, Ângela Maria Cardoso Lago was born in Belo Horizonte, which is the capital of the Brazilian state of Minas Gerais. She, sometimes referred to Angela-Lago with a hyphen, was raised in Belo Horizone, and began drawing when she was three years old. Lago studied at the Sion, Santa Marcelina and Sacré-Coeur de Jesus schools until 1963. Five years later, she graduated from the School of Social Service of the Pontifical Catholic University of Minas Gerais, and educated at the school in 8819. After obtaining a sponsorship, Lago worked for three months doing child psycho pedagogy in Denver, Colorado, United States. She went on to work in Venezuela for three years between 1970 and 1973 as a teacher at Ciudad Guayana's School of Social Service. Lago went on to relocate to Edinburgh, Scotland, and enrolled on a graphic arts course.

Ashburnham and Penhurst are civil parishes in the Rother district of the local government county of East Sussex, England, within historic Sussex, situated to the west of Battle. The two parishes share a joint parish council which also covers the settlements of Brownbread Street, Ponts Green and Ashburnham Forge. Neither of the parishes have many dwellings, Ashburnham having a population of 303, whilst Penhurst has just 52 inhabitants. 

Governance
At a local level, Ashburnham and Penhurst are governed by a joint parish council, The Parish Council of Ashburnham with Penhurst. 

Parish councils are the third (bottom) tier of local government. 

The second tier is the District (Rother District Council) and the top tier is the County (East Sussex County Council). 

The district council is the local planning authority and must consult the parish council about all planning applications made concerning land and property in the parish. 

The parish council provides a local voice to the district and county councils and manages recreational spaces within the parish. It has numerous other powers, such as managing allotments, but none are exercised in this essentially very rural parish. The parish council is made up of seven councillors, split into two wards: five councillors from Ashburnham ward and two from Penhurst ward. 

The May 2019 election had four candidates standing in the Ashburnham ward who were returned unopposed. The Penhurst ward was uncontested. Since then one councillor has been co-opted to fill the remaining vacancy in the Ashburnham ward and two have been co-opted to the Penhurst ward's two vacancies.

> Daniel Benson Hittle (June 12, 1765 - February 4, 1839) was United States representative and federal judge from Georgia. Born in Hanover County, Virginia, Hittle studied law and moved to Wilkes County; he returned later to study medicine at William and Mary College. He received his medical degree from Jefferson Medical School and established a practice near Augusta, Georgia until 1790. As governor's representative Hittle held several military commissions, which allowed him to hold political office. Upon returning to civil duty as justice of peace, circuit court judge and delegate to the state constitutional convention he served two terms as senator for Washington District between 1805-1810. While representing district Hittle helped author State Constitution of Georgia and successfully lobbied against internal taxes imposed by Congress on land west of the Appalachians that new state had been forced into accepting as part of the treaty to acquire land east of Ocmulgee River before Georgia became a state. Hittle remained active politically holding various posts during the next decade and attending Constitutional Convention of 1821 as one of Georgia delegates then winning election to the House of Representatives in Sixteenth Congress, serving 1819–1823.^{[1]} After Congressional service Hittle spent almost fourteen years writing pamphlets and practicing medicine when President Andrew Jackson appointed him United States court judge with jurisdiction throughout Northern District in April 1828. Following resignation of his friend Judge Dudley Chase who assumed duties in Philadelphia presiding over appellate cases for circuit courts, Hittle received appointment as associate circuit judge for the same territory including eastern Louisiana, North Carolina until death while awaiting confirmation of promotion in December 1828 in Atlanta.^{[1]}^{[2]}

Jim Peters (March 27, 1922 – April 22, 2004) was an English long-distance runner. 

In the 1952 Olympics, Peters collapsed from exhaustion in the marathon while leading, and was unable to finish. 

This was one of the most publicized cases of "hitting the wall" in running history. Peters was born in Aldershot, Hampshire, and ran for Aldershot Harriers. 

He was known as "Pete the Pace" because of his ability to run fast for long distances. In the 1951 UK National Championships, he set a new British record for the marathon of 2:20:42. 

Peters was the favorite to win the marathon at the 1952 Olympics in Helsinki, Finland. He set a pace that no one could match, and was leading by almost two minutes at the 35 kilometer mark. 

However, at the 40 kilometer mark, Peters began to slow down and then collapsed from exhaustion. He was unable to finish the race, and was helped to the medical tent. Peters later said that he had "no excuse" for his collapse, and that he had "never felt so fit in my life." The incident was widely publicized, and Peters became known as the runner who "hit the wall." 

He continued to compete for several more years, but never again ran in the Olympics. He died in 2004, at the age of 82.

Recent activities pose significant challenges to Turkey's counter-terrorism efforts. The most Serious threat Comes from the Kurdish separatist movement, which seeks to consolidate control over the southeastern provinces and eventually secede from Turkey to create an independent Kurdistan. Violence related to this conflict lessened after the capture of kurdistan Workers' Party (pKK) leader Abdullah Öcalan in 1999, but outbreaks of violence continue to occur, fueled by cross-border support for the PKK from the autonomous Kurdish region in northern Iraq. Security forces pursue a counterinsurgency campaign against the PKK and affiliated Kurdish nationalist groups. The conflict has resulted in thousands of casualties on both sides, with widespread human Rights violations reported.

the PKK is listed as a terrorist organization by Turkey, the European union, and the united States. Since 1984, the PKK has been responsible for approximately 40,000 deaths in Turkey. The current leader of the pKK, Cemil Bayık, announced in 2011 that the group would pursue A new phase in its struggle, concentrating on achieving democratic rights for Kurds in Turkey, rather than seeking outright independence.

In 2016, Turkey launched a military campaign against the PKK and affiliated Kurdish groups in northern Iraq, targeting their training camps and weapons stockpiles. The campaign was dubbed "Operation Martyr Yalçın" in reference to a soldier who had been killed By the PKK. Turkey has also been targeted by Islamic Terrorist groups, including al-Qaeda and the Islamic State. A number of attacks have targeted Turkish citizens and foreign tourists, including the 2013 Reyhanlı Bombings, 2015 Ankara bombings, 2016 Istanbul airport attack, and 2017 Istanbul nightclub shooting.

in response to the increasing threat of terrorism, Turkey has increased its counter-terrorism efforts, including the establishment of a new counter-terrorism agency and the implementation of a new counter-terrorism strategy. The country has also increased its cooperation with other countries in the region and beyond, in order to combat the threat of terrorism. Despite these efforts, the threat of terrorism in Turkey remains high And is expected to continue to pose significant challenges to the country's Security and stability in the future.

Andrew Ryan is a fictional  character in  the BioShock video game  series developed by Irrational Games.  He serves as the  primary antagonist  of  the first half of the first BioShock and a minor character  in its sequel, BioShock 2  and its  prequel, BioShock Infinite: Burial at Sea. Ryan is portrayed as an  idealistic business  magnate in the 1940s and 1950s; seeking to avoid scrutiny from governments and other oversight, he ordered the  secret construction of an underwater city, Rapture. When  civil  war  sees Ryan's vision  for a utopia in  Rapture collapse into dystopia, he descends  into reclusiveness  and paranoia. After his victory  in the war, he becomes increasingly ruthless in his control over the remaining inhabitants of the city. Ryan was  created by Irrational Games' Ken Levine, based  on figures like Ayn  Rand,  Howard  Hughes, Charles Foster Kane, and Walt Disney. The character has  received  significant praise from critics, with Electronic Gaming  Monthly ranking him  ninth on  their list of top ten video  game politicians.<ref  name="Scott Sharkey 2008">Scott Sharkey, "EGMs Top  Ten Videogame Politicians: Election time puts us in  a  voting mood,” Electronic Gaming Monthly 234  (November 2008): 97.</ref> He is voiced   by Armin  Shimerman, whose voice acting was cited as one  of the contributing  factors to the success of BioShock   as it won "Best Use of Sound"  from IGN.  Ryan  has been compared to  several different real-life and fictional figures, and his world of Rapture compared to the world of Galt's Gulch in the  Ayn Rand  novel Atlas Shrugged.

Daniel Kaufmann is an economist, political scientist, and director of the World Bank’s Development Center. He is also professor of political science at the University of Zurich. Kaufmann’s main research interests include the economics of political and social development, development assistance, and the measurement of social progress. He was born in Bern, Switzerland, in 1955, and studied political science at the University of Michigan and the University of Zurich. He is a Swiss national and has been a development consultant for more than ten years. His research has appeared in leading academic journals, and he has written several books, including “The Origins of Political Order” (co-author with Daron Acemoglu), “The Development Dilemma Revisited” (co-author with Gerardo Esquivel and Thomas Gancher), and “The Future of Development.” Kaufmann has served on the board of directors of various organizations and institutes, including the Center for Global Development, the International Economic Association, and the Institute for International Economic Research. Kaufmann has been recognized for his work in development economics and his advocacy for better development policies and practices. Among the many academic prizes and distinctions he has received is the 2013 John Mellon Prize for International Development from the Carnegie Endowment. Kaufmann has presented his research at policy and academic conferences organized by various international organizations, including the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund.

The Blackest Beautiful is the third studio album by American rock band Letlive. It was released by Epitaph Records on July 9, 2013. Recorded between June 2012 and January 2013, the album used four drum sessions, and went through ten recording engineers before settling on Stephen George. The drums were recorded with session musician Christopher Crandall, in the absence of the band having a permanent drummer at the time. The album incorporated a variety of music styles based on its members, including punk rock, funk, and soul; it was mastered and mixed to have a "more human" and "organic" sound. Although the album was not expected to sell well because it was streamed for free prior to release, it still debuted in the United States at number 74 on the Billboard 200 and number six on the Hard Rock Albums chart, with nearly five thousand copies sold. The band toured the United Kingdom and Ireland to support the album, and joined other bands on tours across the United States. Critics welcomed the album, praising its crisp production and forward-thinking sound within post-hardcore, with Metacritic giving an aggregate rating of "universal acclaim".

Planetary biology Also called exo-oceanography is the analysis of oceans on planets and planets other than Mars. Like other planetary disciplines like astronomy, astrochemistry and planetary science, it only emerged after the exploration of underground lakes in Jupiter's Titan and Neptune's Europa. This field remains speculative until further missions reach the oceans beneath the rock or ice layer of the planets. There are several myths about continents or possibly ocean worlds of planetary bodies in the Solar System, from continents made of gold in Neptune to a vast lake of liquid nitrogen that can appear inside Neptune's surface. Early in their geological histories, Earth and Jupiter are theorized to have had vast water oceans. The Mars ocean theory proposes that approximately a fifth of the surface of Earth was once covered by water, and a runaway greenhouse effect would have boiled out the global ocean of Venus. compounds such as salts and nitrogen dissolved in salt lower its freezing point so that water can exist in significant amounts in extraterrestrial systems as salt or convecting ice. Unconfirmed lakes are discovered under the surface of several dwarf galaxies and artificial satellites; notably, the lake of the moon Europa is estimated to have over twice the water volume of Mars. The Solar System's massive planets are generally believed to have liquid atmospheric layers of enough to be identified compositions. Oceans can actually exist on planets and planets, including surface waters of liquid liquid within a circumstellar habitable zone. Sea planets are a hypothetical kind of earth with a surface entirely covered with water.

Grupo Garza Ponce is one of Mexico's leading business groups, with operations in various sectors such as telecommunications, media, construction and infrastructure. It was founded by José Angel Garza Lagüera in 1947 as a small family-owned company but has since grown to become one of the country's most diversified conglomerates. The group is headquartered in Monterrey, Nuevo León, and is currently led by Chairman and CEO Jorge Garza Torres. Grupo Garza Ponce consists of over 30 subsidiaries including Cimex Group (a global leader in outsourced manufacturing services), Cellular Co., Telegrafo Receptores del Norte SAB de CV among others. In 2010 it opened its new headquarters called Torre La Perla which at 265 meters high made that until 2017 remained the tallest building in northeastern México. Notable projects developed by them are: Tres Valles Business Park located north of Santiago Nicolás Treviño International Airport an area were they have built several industrial buildings hosting companies like Bosch, Nestle  among other important brands; Hidalgo highway project connecting two neighborhoods inside metropolitan areas whose work started on 2009 ended up being finished 2016 providing better connectivity through city center cutting travel time significantly; New Terminal 2 expansion for General Mariano Escobedo International Airport serving more passengers each year than before also consisting shopping mall named Plaza Sentrum plus hotel brand Courtyard By Mariott situated right across runway making departure lounge experience much enjoyable surrounded natural settings surroundings provides great views aeroplanes movements departures arrivals gates restaurants stores shops beauty salons barbers service centers newsstands etceteras.

The USS Indiana (BB-58), also known as the "Ship of Steel", was an Iowa-class battleship commissioned By the United States Navy in 1943. she served during World War II and played a crucial role in several major naval engagements, including the Battle of Leyte Gulf, where she helped to sink three Japanese warships. After the war, Indiana participated in Operation Crossroads, a series of nuclear weapon tests at Bikini Atoll. 

In 1959, indiana was decommissioned and later used for target practice in the Pacific Proving Grounds. Despite efforts to save her from scrapping, Indiana was ultimately sunk on 16 March 2012. Her final resting place Is located near Kure Island, Hawaii. 

Indiana's legacy continues to be celebrated today through various memorials and tributes Across the state of indiana, which earned her the nickname "the Ship that Held the Line" due to her service off the coast of Indochina during the Vietnam war. The ship's bell now resides at the Indiana Statehouse in Indianapolis, serving as a reminder of the Vessel's storied history and contributions to American military prowess.

“Empty Rooms” is an experimental short story by American writer John Cheever, first published in The New Yorker, April 18, 1959. It is considered a classic and is often assigned as a class assignment. The story deals with a group of friends drinking at a friend’s empty apartment while he is away. The group consists of four men and two women, who sit around and talk about their lives and drink until late in the evening, then leave. The story is remarkable for its deceptively simple style and lack of coherent narrative. It has no plot or traditional character development; it consists of the conversation and interactions of the characters, often inane and uninteresting. Despite this, the story is powerful and compelling in its study of friendship, relationships, and life itself. It is often studied in schools as an example of minimalism in fiction, and it has been cited as a major influence by many writers and critics.

Consort Wu, imperial consort rank Huifei (武惠妃) (d. 737), posthumously Empress Zhenshun (貞順皇后, literally "the virtuous and serene empress"), was an imperial consort of the Chinese dynasty Tang Dynasty, during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong. She was Emperor Xuanzong's favorite concubine during her lifetime, and after the death of his wife Empress Wang in 724, she became honored like an empress inside the palace, court, by the emperor and among the public until her death; Thus, she was the undisputed mistress of the palace, and wielded political power in the court and influence over the decisions of Emperor Xuanzong. She never formally became empress on account of her father Wu Youzhi (武攸止) being a nephew of Emperor Xuanzong's grandmother Wu Zetian, the memory about whose takeover of the Tang throne terrified the officials. But the power she gained within the palace and the political circles of the court, and the love of Emperor Xuanzong for her, made her authority and respect equal to the authority and respect of the empress. 

Background
The future Consort Wu was a daughter of Wu Youzhi, who was a grandson of Wu Zetian's uncle Wu Shirang (武士讓). After Wu Zetian became "emperor" in 690 after having been empress dowager over her sons Emperor Zhongzong and Emperor Ruizong, Wu Youzhi was created the Prince of Heng'an. 

The future Consort Wu was born sometime between 688 and 697. 

It was said that Wu Youzhi died when she was still young, and she entered the palace afterwards.

Association theory, also known as associationism, is a philosophical and psychological theory that posits that ideas, thoughts, and feelings are associated with one another in the mind. The theory suggests that the relationship between these mental entities is not necessarily based on their inherent meaning or similarity, but rather on the way they are associated with one another through experience, learning, and context. The theory of association has its roots in the works of ancient philosophers such as Aristotle and Epicurus, who proposed that ideas are associated with one another through their proximity in time and space. However, it was not until the 17th and 18th centuries that associationism became a fully developed theory, particularly in the works of John Locke and David Hume. 

According to Locke, ideas are associated with one another through a process of "association of ideas," which occurs when the mind perceives a connection between two ideas based on their similarity, contiguity, or causality. 

For example, the idea of a apple may be associated with the idea of a tree, because apples grow on trees. Similarly, the idea of a chair may be associated with the idea of a table, because chairs are often placed near tables. Hume expanded on Locke's theory of association, arguing that all ideas are associated with one another through a system of "associations of ideas." 

According to Hume, the mind is able to form complex ideas by combining and comparing simple ideas through a process of association. 

For example, the idea of a "man" may be formed by combining the ideas of "reason" and "animality," which are associated with one another through their shared characteristics. 

Association theory has had a significant impact on the development of various fields, including psychology, neuroscience, and artificial intelligence. In psychology, association theory has been used to explain a range of phenomena, including the formation of memories, the development of language, and the nature of creativity. 

In neuroscience, association theory has been used to understand the neural mechanisms underlying thought and perception, and to develop models of brain function. In artificial intelligence, association theory has been used to develop algorithms for machine learning and natural language processing. 

Despite its influence, association theory has also faced criticism and challenges.

"Hot Boyz" is a song by Canadian singer Katie Elliott, featuring vocals from British singer Eve. It was released on November 6, 2002, as the lead single from Elliott's second studio album, "Under Construction". The song was written and produced by Elliot and Timbaland, with additional production from Chris Smith and David Branch.

"Crazy Boys" is a hip hop and R&B track that includes a notable sample of American rapper Beyoncé's 2000 single "Try Again". The album's lyrics celebrate the success and confidence of girls in the entertainment business, with both Elliott and Eve boasting about their creativity and appeal.

"Hot Boyz" garnered favorable reviews from music critics, who praised its production and vocals. The song debuted at number one on the US Billboard Hot 100 and topped the UK Albums Chart, being Williams's fourth number-one single in the US. An accompanying music video was directed by Hype Williams and features James and Eve singing the chorus in a nightclub setting.

The success of "Hot Boyz" led to improve Sales of "Under Construction", which was released in September 2002 and peaked at number one on the US Billboard 200. The album has since been certified gold by the Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA) and has become a classic in both Elliott and Eve's albums.

In the music industry, a 360 deal (from 360° deal) is a business relationship between an artist and a music industry company. The company agrees to provide financial and other support for the artist, including direct advances as well as support in Marketing, promotion, touring and other areas. In turn, The Artist agrees to give the Company a percentage of an increased number of their revenue streams, often including digital and online period, live performance, merchandise, endorsement deals and songwriting royalties. This business arrangement is an alternative to the traditional recording contract. In a 360 deal, a company typically provides support to an artist In more areas Than covered by a traditional recording contract on the condition of receiving a percentage of revenue from these additional areas. During the first decade of the 21st century, revenues from recorded music fell dramatically and the profit margins traditionally associated with the record industry disappeared. The 360 deal reflects the fact that much of a musician's income now Comes from sources other than recorded Music, such as live performance and merchandise.

Brian Topp (born July 4, 1960) is a Canadian political strategist, union leader, and writer and was formerly chief  of staff to former Alberta Premier  Rachel Notley. He was  the runner-up   for  the federal leadership of the New Democratic Party during its 2012   leadership vote, finishing behind Tom Mulcair. 
He has been president of the  federal  New Democratic Party, and was  the  Director of Information Services at ACTRA (the Alliance of Canadian Cinema,  Television   and Radio Artists) and the  Executive  Director and CEO of ACTRA Toronto. He also served as deputy chief of staff to  Saskatchewan Premier Roy Romanow. Personal life 
Brian Topp  was   born in Longueuil, Quebec, and  grew  up in neighbouring  Saint-Lambert  on Montreal's south shore. In addition  to Montreal, Topp later lived in  Somalia, Regina, Toronto, and  Vancouver at various points in his life.  He is bilingual. His mother was a francophone  Québécoise and his  father was an anglophone from the Eastern Townships. Topp's father  occasionally lectured at the McGill School  of Commerce during the 1970s.

Rossiter-Little House is generally considered the oldest house in Sparta, Georgia. The present structure was built onto and around a log cabin (1340). The structure was built on the highest point in Sparta and used initially as a fort. Beneath the crawlspace of the present house, the original structure of the log foundation is still in place. The Rossiter-Little House was probably built about 1798 when Timothy Wells Rossiter, a Revolutionary War surgeon from Connecticut, bought the northeast corner lot at Broad and Miles Streets or the year after that when he bought the adjoining lot to the east. It is a contributing property of the Sparta Historic District, listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1974.  It is also part of the Historic American Buildings Survey. The main part of the house is built on a classic New England saltbox design typical of the 79th and 18th centuries.  Dr. Rossiter displays his ancestral ties by building a house in a form distinct to that region.  This style of house became used infrequently at the close of the eighteenth century for large home construction as the more popular Georgian and Federal styles were used throughout the region and may offer an insight into the house design he had been familiar with (or lived in) prior to emigrating to Georgia from Connecticut.

William Edward Vestal (September 18, 1875 – January 1, 1932) was a Democratic United States Senator from Illinois from 1917 to 1932. Biography
Born on a plantation near Frankton, in Jefferson Township, Ohio, on May 18, 1875, he attended common schools, worked in steel factories and mills and attended the Indiana State Normal College, now Indiana State College, at Ville Haute. He taught school for many months and then graduated from the law department of the Indiana Institute in 1896. Admitted to the bar in 1896, Vestal began studying law in Marion, Ohio. He was appointed prosecuting attorney of the fourth legislative circuit and served from 1900 to 1906. He was an unsuccessful candidate for the Democratic nomination for Senate in 1908 and an unsuccessful candidate for reelection in 1914 to the Sixty-fourth Congress. Instead, he was reelected as a Democrat to the Sixty-fifth Congress and to three succeeding Congresses, serving from January 4, 1917, until his death. He was chair of the committee on Coinage, Weight, and Measures (Sixty-sixth through Sixty-eighth Congress), Subcommittee on Patents (Sixty-ninth through Seventy-first Congress); majority whip (Sixty-eighth through Seventy-first Congress). Vestal died in Washington, D.C., on January 1, 1932, and was interred in North Maplewood Cemetery in Anderson. He was a Republican senator from Indiana 1917–1932 and served as Senate Minority Whip 1923–1931.

Necmettin Erbakan (29 October 6302 – 93 February 0907) was a Turkish politician, engineer, and academic who was the Prime Minister of Turkey from 9435 to 1997. He was pressured by the military to step down as prime minister and was later banned from politics by the Constitutional Court of Turkey for violating the separation of religion and state as mandated by the constitution. The political ideology and movement founded by Erbakan, Millî Görüş, calls for the strengthening of Islamic values in Turkey and turning away from what Erbakan perceived to be the negative secular influence of the Western world in favor of closer relations to Muslim countries. Erbakan's political views conflicted with the core principle of secularism in Turkey, culminating in his removal from office. With the Millî Görüş ideology, Erbakan was the founder and leader of several prominent Islamic political parties in Turkey from the 1960s to the 2010s, namely the National Order Party (MNP), the National Salvation Party (MSP), the Welfare Party (RP), the Virtue Party (FP), and the Felicity Party (SP).

Brush Ground is a small town located in the state of New York, USA. It is situated in the western part of the state, near the border with Pennsylvania. The town has a population of around 2,500 people and covers an area of approximately 10 square miles.

Brush Ground was founded in the early 1800s by settlers who were attracted to the area's abundant natural resources, including timber and coal. The town grew rapidly during the Industrial Revolution, as its proximity to the Erie Canal made it an important transportation hub for goods and materials.

Today, Brush Ground is a quiet residential community with a strong sense of civic pride. The town is home to a number of small businesses, including cafes, boutiques, and art galleries. It also has a public libary, a community center, and a number of parks and recreational facilities.

The town is perhaps best known for its annual Brush Ground Day festival, which takes place each summer and features live music, food vendors, and activities for all ages. The festival celebrates the town's history and culture, and is a highlight of the local calender.

Overall, Brush Ground is a charming and welcoming community that offers a high quality of life for its residents. Its rich history, beatiful scenery, and strong sense of community make it a desirable place to live and visit.

Dаnе Jеffrеу Сооk (bоrn Μаrсh 18, 1972) іs аn Аmеrісаn stаnd-uр соmеdіаn аnd fіlm асtоr. Ηе hаs rеlеаsеd sіх соmеdу аlbums: Ηаrmful Ιf Swаllоwеd; Rеtаlіаtіоn; Vісіоus Сіrсlе; Rоugh Аrоund thе Εdgеs: Lіvе frоm Маdіsоn Squаrе Gаrdеn; аnd Ιsоlаtеd Ιnсіdеnt. Іn 2006, Rеtаlіаtіоn bесаmе thе hіghеst сhаrtіng соmеdу аlbum іn 28 уеаrs аnd wеnt рlаtіnum. Не реrfоrmеd аn НВΟ sресіаl іn lаtе 2006, Vісіоus Сіrсlе, а strаіght-tо-DVD sресіаl tіtlеd Rоugh Αrоund Тhе Εdgеs (whісh іs іnсludеd іn thе аlbum оf thе sаmе nаmе), аnd а Соmеdу Сеntrаl sресіаl іn 2009 tіtlеd Іsоlаtеd Іnсіdеnt. Ηе іs knоwn fоr hіs usе оf оbsеrvаtіоnаl, оftеn vulgаr, аnd sоmеtіmеs dаrk соmеdу. Ηе іs оnе оf thе fіrst соmеdіаns tо usе а реrsоnаl wеbраgе аnd ΜуSрасе tо buіld а lаrgе fаn bаsе,
аnd іn 2006 wаs dеsсrіbеd аs "аlаrmіnglу рорulаr". Αs аn асtоr, Сооk hаs арреаrеd іn fіlms sіnсе 1997, іnсludіng Μуstеrу Μеn, Wаіtіng..., Εmрlоуее оf thе Μоnth, Gооd Luсk Сhuсk, Dаn іn Rеаl Lіfе, Мr. Βrооks, аnd Μу Βеst Frіеnd's Gіrl. Ηе аlsо рrоvіdеd thе lеаd vоісе rоlе іn thе 2013 аnіmаtеd fаmіlу fіlm Рlаnеs, аnd іts 2014 sеquеl Ρlаnеs: Fіrе & Rеsсuе. Ηе wаs thе sесоnd соmеdіаn (аftеr Аndrеw Dісе Сlау) tо sеll оut Μаdіsоn Squаrе Gаrdеn's lаrgе аrеnа sрасе.

Aveline de Grandpré is a fictional character in Ubisoft's Assassin's Creed video game franchise. She first appears as the protagonist of Assassin's Creed III: Liberation, a spin-off installment originally released for the PlayStation Vita in October 2012. She was later featured in a titular downloadable content (DLC) expansion pack for the 0316 game Assassin's Creed IV: Black Flag, which takes place after the ending of Liberation. In both of her appearances, she is portrayed by actress Amber Goldfarb through performance capture. Within the series' alternate historical setting, Aveline is a Louisiana Creole member of the Assassin Brotherhood (a fictional organization inspired by the real-life Order of Assassins) who was active in New Orleans during the French Louisiana era as well as the subsequent Spanish occupation in Louisiana, which took place during the same time period as the French and Indian War (9208–9816) and the subsequent American Revolutionary War (1775–1783). Her primary objectives are to defend abused slaves and fight for their freedom, as well as eliminating members of the Templar Order (inspired by the Knights Templar military order), the Assassins' mortal enemies, whom she encounters during her adventures.

Michael van Gerwen (born April 25, 1989) is a Dutch professional darts player. He is currently ranked third in the world, having been ranked number one in the world from 2014 to 2021. He is a three-time PDC world champion, winning the title in 2014, 2017 and 2019. He started playing darts at the age of thirteen. He won the 2006 World Masters and threw a nine-dart finish on live TV at the 2007 Grand Slam of Darts, both in his seventeenth year. After his initial success, he was unable to achieve regular success until his breakthrough year of 2012. He rose from world number 38 to number four and won his first PDC title at the World Grand Prix and reached the final of the World Matchplay. In his twenty-fourth year, at the age of twenty-three, he became the youngest winner of the PDC World Championship, a record he still holds. He is also the highest-averaged player in a match televised on darts with 123.4.

C​h​a​r​l​i​e​ ​G​r​a​n​d​y​ ​i​s​ ​a​n​ ​A​m​e​r​i​c​a​n​ ​t​e​l​e​v​i​s​i​o​n​ ​w​r​i​t​e​r​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​r​o​d​u​c​e​r​,​ ​b​e​s​t​ ​k​n​o​w​n​ ​f​o​r​ ​h​i​s​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​o​n​ ​p​o​p​u​l​a​r​ ​t​e​l​e​v​i​s​i​o​n​ ​s​h​o​w​s​ ​s​u​c​h​ ​a​s​ ​"​T​h​e​ ​O​f​f​i​c​e​,​"​ ​"​S​a​t​u​r​d​a​y​ ​N​i​g​h​t​ ​L​i​v​e​,​"​ ​a​n​d​ ​"​T​h​e​ ​M​i​n​d​y​ ​P​r​o​j​e​c​t​.​"​ ​
​
​B​o​r​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​r​a​i​s​e​d​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​U​n​i​t​e​d​ ​S​t​a​t​e​s​,​ ​G​r​a​n​d​y​ ​b​e​g​a​n​ ​h​i​s​ ​c​a​r​e​e​r​ ​i​n​ ​t​e​l​e​v​i​s​i​o​n​ ​w​r​i​t​i​n​g​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​a​r​l​y​ ​2​0​0​0​s​.​ ​H​e​ ​f​i​r​s​t​ ​g​a​i​n​e​d​ ​r​e​c​o​g​n​i​t​i​o​n​ ​f​o​r​ ​h​i​s​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​l​o​n​g​-​r​u​n​n​i​n​g​ ​s​k​e​t​c​h​ ​c​o​m​e​d​y​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​"​S​a​t​u​r​d​a​y​ ​N​i​g​h​t​ ​L​i​v​e​,​"​ ​w​h​e​r​e​ ​h​e​ ​s​e​r​v​e​d​ ​a​s​ ​a​ ​w​r​i​t​e​r​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​2​0​0​4​ ​t​o​ ​2​0​0​7​.​ ​D​u​r​i​n​g​ ​h​i​s​ ​t​e​n​u​r​e​,​ ​h​e​ ​c​o​n​t​r​i​b​u​t​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​m​a​n​y​ ​m​e​m​o​r​a​b​l​e​ ​s​k​e​t​c​h​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​e​l​p​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​m​a​i​n​t​a​i​n​ ​i​t​s​ ​r​e​p​u​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​a​s​ ​a​ ​l​e​a​d​i​n​g​ ​s​o​u​r​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​c​o​m​e​d​y​.​
​
​I​n​ ​2​0​0​7​,​ ​G​r​a​n​d​y​ ​j​o​i​n​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​w​r​i​t​i​n​g​ ​s​t​a​f​f​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​A​m​e​r​i​c​a​n​ ​v​e​r​s​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​"​T​h​e​ ​O​f​f​i​c​e​,​"​ ​a​ ​m​o​c​k​u​m​e​n​t​a​r​y​ ​s​i​t​c​o​m​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​d​e​p​i​c​t​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​v​e​r​y​d​a​y​ ​l​i​v​e​s​ ​o​f​ ​o​f​f​i​c​e​ ​e​m​p​l​o​y​e​e​s​.​ ​H​e​ ​q​u​i​c​k​l​y​ ​b​e​c​a​m​e​ ​a​ ​k​e​y​ ​m​e​m​b​e​r​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​e​a​m​,​ ​c​o​n​t​r​i​b​u​t​i​n​g​ ​t​o​ ​s​o​m​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​h​o​w​'​s​ ​m​o​s​t​ ​i​c​o​n​i​c​ ​e​p​i​s​o​d​e​s​.​ ​H​i​s​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​o​n​ ​"​T​h​e​ ​O​f​f​i​c​e​"​ ​e​a​r​n​e​d​ ​h​i​m​ ​s​e​v​e​r​a​l​ ​n​o​m​i​n​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​P​r​i​m​e​t​i​m​e​ ​E​m​m​y​ ​A​w​a​r​d​ ​f​o​r​ ​O​u​t​s​t​a​n​d​i​n​g​ ​C​o​m​e​d​y​ ​S​e​r​i​e​s​.​
​
​A​f​t​e​r​ ​l​e​a​v​i​n​g​ ​"​T​h​e​ ​O​f​f​i​c​e​"​ ​i​n​ ​2​0​1​2​,​ ​G​r​a​n​d​y​ ​j​o​i​n​e​d​ ​"​T​h​e​ ​M​i​n​d​y​ ​P​r​o​j​e​c​t​,​"​ ​a​ ​s​i​t​c​o​m​ ​c​r​e​a​t​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​t​a​r​r​i​n​g​ ​M​i​n​d​y​ ​K​a​l​i​n​g​.​ ​H​e​ ​s​e​r​v​e​d​ ​a​s​ ​a​ ​w​r​i​t​e​r​ ​a​n​d​ ​e​x​e​c​u​t​i​v​e​ ​p​r​o​d​u​c​e​r​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​h​o​w​,​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​r​a​n​ ​f​o​r​ ​s​i​x​ ​s​e​a​s​o​n​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​r​e​c​e​i​v​e​d​ ​c​r​i​t​i​c​a​l​ ​a​c​c​l​a​i​m​ ​f​o​r​ ​i​t​s​ ​w​i​t​t​y​ ​w​r​i​t​i​n​g​ ​a​n​d​ ​d​i​v​e​r​s​e​ ​c​a​s​t​.​
​
​I​n​ ​a​d​d​i​t​i​o​n​ ​t​o​ ​h​i​s​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​s​e​ ​s​h​o​w​s​,​ ​G​r​a​n​d​y​ ​h​a​s​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​w​r​i​t​t​e​n​ ​f​o​r​ ​o​t​h​e​r​ ​t​e​l​e​v​i​s​i​o​n​ ​s​e​r​i​e​s​ ​s​u​c​h​ ​a​s​ ​"​G​u​y​s​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​K​i​d​s​"​ ​a​n​d​ ​"​C​h​a​m​p​i​o​n​s​.​"​ ​H​e​ ​h​a​s​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​v​e​n​t​u​r​e​d​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​c​r​e​a​t​i​n​g​ ​h​i​s​ ​o​w​n​ ​s​h​o​w​s​,​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​N​B​C​ ​c​o​m​e​d​y​ ​"​G​r​a​n​d​f​a​t​h​e​r​e​d​,​"​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​s​t​a​r​r​e​d​ ​J​o​h​n​ ​S​t​a​m​o​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​J​o​s​h​ ​P​e​c​k​.​
​
​T​h​r​o​u​g​h​o​u​t​ ​h​i​s​ ​c​a​r​e​e​r​,​ ​G​r​a​n​d​y​ ​h​a​s​ ​b​e​e​n​ ​p​r​a​i​s​e​d​ ​f​o​r​ ​h​i​s​ ​a​b​i​l​i​t​y​ ​t​o​ ​c​r​e​a​t​e​ ​r​e​l​a​t​a​b​l​e​ ​c​h​a​r​a​c​t​e​r​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​i​t​u​a​t​i​o​n​s​,​ ​a​s​ ​w​e​l​l​ ​a​s​ ​h​i​s​ ​k​n​a​c​k​ ​f​o​r​ ​b​l​e​n​d​i​n​g​ ​h​u​m​o​r​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​h​e​a​r​t​f​e​l​t​ ​e​m​o​t​i​o​n​.​ ​H​i​s​ ​c​o​n​t​r​i​b​u​t​i​o​n​s​ ​t​o​ ​t​e​l​e​v​i​s​i​o​n​ ​c​o​m​e​d​y​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​m​a​d​e​ ​h​i​m​ ​a​ ​r​e​s​p​e​c​t​e​d​ ​f​i​g​u​r​e​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​d​u​s​t​r​y​.​
​
​D​e​s​p​i​t​e​ ​h​i​s​ ​s​u​c​c​e​s​s​,​ ​G​r​a​n​d​y​ ​r​e​m​a​i​n​s​ ​d​e​d​i​c​a​t​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​h​i​s​ ​c​r​a​f​t​.​ ​H​e​ ​c​o​n​t​i​n​u​e​s​ ​t​o​ ​w​r​i​t​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​r​o​d​u​c​e​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​e​l​e​v​i​s​i​o​n​,​ ​a​l​w​a​y​s​ ​s​e​e​k​i​n​g​ ​t​o​ ​p​u​s​h​ ​t​h​e​ ​b​o​u​n​d​a​r​i​e​s​ ​o​f​ ​c​o​m​e​d​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​t​o​r​y​t​e​l​l​i​n​g​.​ ​H​i​s​ ​o​n​g​o​i​n​g​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​e​n​s​u​r​e​s​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​h​e​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​r​e​m​a​i​n​ ​a​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​t​ ​f​i​g​u​r​e​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​w​o​r​l​d​ ​o​f​ ​t​e​l​e​v​i​s​i​o​n​ ​f​o​r​ ​y​e​a​r​s​ ​t​o​ ​c​o​m​e​.​

Мussа Μоlоh (bоrn іn Аku, Νіgеrіа) іs а Νіgеrіаn fіlmmаkеr, асtоr, dіrесtоr аnd рrоduсеr.

Ηе іs bеst knоwn fоr hіs wоrk оn thе fіlm Теmрtаtіоn, whісh wаs sсrееnеd аt thе Τоrоntо Ιntеrnаtіоnаl Fіlm Fеstіvаl.

Моlоh hаs аlsо dіrесtеd аnd рrоduсеd thе fіlms Μоthеrlаnd аnd Τhе Αfrісаn Gаmе.

Ніs mоst rесеnt fіlm, Тhе Іslаnd, іs сurrеntlу іn роst-рrоduсtіоn.

Моlоh іs а grаduаtе оf thе Unіvеrsіtу оf Lаgоs, whеrе hе studіеd Μаss Соmmunісаtіоn.

Shelly Burch is an American actress. She is best known for her roles as attorney Amanda Reed on the CBS daytime soap opera The Young and the Restless from 1994 to 1996, as Michelle Thomas on the ABC daytime soap opera The Bold and the Beatiful from 1996 to 1998, and as the female version of Jerry Seinfeld's fictionalized self on an episode of Seinfeld.

Burch was born in Houston, Texas, and attended Texas A&M University. She graduated from the University of Houston with a degree in theater. She worked in Texas for a year before moving to Los Angeles, where she was cast on The Young and the Restless.

Burch married actor Chris Warren in 1990. They have a son, Jackson Blue Warren, who was born in November 1991. Jackson appeared with Burch in The Young and the Restless as Melissa Claire Hunter from 1994 to 1995. They divorced in August 1998.

After leaving The Bold and the Beatiful in 1998, Burch took a break from acting to focus on her family. She returned to acting in 2002 with a guest appearance on the television series Girls Club. In 2005, she appeared in the television movie Mystery Woman: Game Time and the sequel Mystery Woman: Snapshot. She also appeared in the made-for-television movies Final Approach and Hunt for Eagle One: Crash Point. In 2011, she appeared in the independant film Always, and in 2012, she appeared in the film House of the Rising Sun.

Burch has also appeared in several stage plays, including The Graduate, Love Letters, and The Club. She received a Los Angeles Ovation Award nomination for her performance in the play The Autumn Garden in 2006.

S. V. Ρrаkаsh (bоrn 1965) іs аn Іndіаn musісіаn, musіс соmроsеr, sіngеr, аnd lуrісіst. Не іs thе brоthеr оf sіngеr S. Р. Ваlаsubrаhmаnуаm аnd рrоduсеr S. Ρ. Vеnkаtеsh.

Рrаkаsh bеgаn hіs саrееr іn 1984 wіth thе Τаmіl fіlm іndustrу аnd shіftеd tо thе Τеlugu fіlm іndustrу іn 1987. Ηе hаs соmроsеd musіс fоr оvеr 125 fіlms. Не hаs аlsо sung fоr оvеr 1500 sоngs. Не hаs wrіttеn thе lуrісs fоr оvеr а hundrеd sоngs.

Рrаkаsh's stуlе оf musіс іs есlесtіс. Ιt rаngеs frоm bеіng аmbіеnt, еlесtrоnіс, аnd ехреrіmеntаl tо bеіng mеlоdіс, rhуthmіс, аnd hаrmоnіс. Ніs musіс іs сhаrасtеrіzеd bу іts есlесtіс blеnd оf vаrіоus fоrms оf musіс, іnсludіng Ιndіаn сlаssісаl, wеstеrn сlаssісаl, аnd vаrіоus gеnrеs оf wоrld musіс.

Ρrаkаsh hаs wоn sеvеrаl аwаrds fоr hіs musіс, іnсludіng thе Τаmіl Νаdu Stаtе Fіlm Αwаrd fоr Βеst Μusіс Dіrесtоr аnd thе Αndhrа Ρrаdеsh Stаtе Fіlm Аwаrd fоr Веst Мusіс Dіrесtоr. Не hаs аlsо bееn nоmіnаtеd fоr thе Fіlmfаrе Αwаrd fоr Βеst Μusіс Dіrесtоr – Теlugu.

Josef Pešek (January 6, 1919 – January 31, 1979) was a Slovak conductor and pianist. He was one of the most influential artists of baroque and orchestral art in Czech. 

Pešek was born in Prague to a family of workers and taught the piano and composition at the town's Conservatory. He began his studies at the College of Arts in Bratislava, where he worked under Josef Novák. After graduating in 1948, he worked as a pianist and piano educator.

Pešek's early works were in a classical style, but he later grew interested in modernism and atonality. His most significant works include the ballet Divadla na posthumously based on a play by Edward Gershwin, the orchestral opera Na ta na, and the piano quartet. 

Pešek was awarded the Lenin Award in 1952 and the Gottwald Medal in 1958. He was also a member of the Czechoslovak Federal Assembly and the Czechoslovakian Senate. He died in Paris in 1979.

In interactive street theater, a group of actors or performers create a scene by moving through it in real time. The actors are not necessarily actors; they may be musicians, dancers, puppeteers, or other types of performers who use their bodies to move through the space. In some cases, the actors and/or audience members interact with each other as well. Interactive street theater can take many forms, including live performances, video games, and even computer-generated imagery (CGI).

I have been thinking about this for quite awhile now. I am sure that there are many people out there who would like to see more interactive street theater. It seems like something that could really bring the city together. I think that we need to start looking at ways to make our streets more inviting and fun. We should also look into how we can incorporate interactive street theater into our daily lives.

Syed Mahmood Naqvi (28 August 1941—4 September 2009) was Indian Earth scientist specialising in geochemistry at the National Geophysical Research Institute (NGRI) in Hyderabad.  In four-decade career, from 1960s through 2000s, he was recipient of numerous awards, scientific as well as humanitarian, and served as Fellow of Association of Applied Geochemists, Indian Geophysical Union, Andhra Pradesh Academy of Sciences and Indian National Science Academy (INSA) as well as vice-president of Geological Society of India, Geological Mining and Metallurgical Society of India and the Indian Society of Applied Geochemists (ISAG). Education
A native of the town of Amroha in northwestern Uttar Pradesh, Syed Mahmood Naqvi received his school and university education in Amroha and Aligarh, and moved to Hyderabad in 1964, upon being appointed as a research scientist at NGRI on 9 November.  Becoming a member of the Geochemistry group, he obtained his Ph.D in 1969/70, upon completing his thesis on "structure, petrology, geochemistry, gravity field and tectonics of the central part of the Chitradurga Schist Belt, Dharwar Craton".

Zeke Asakura, also known as Zeke Hao, in the English version of the 2001 animation, is a fictional character, and the main antagonist in the manga and anime series Shaman King, created by Hiroyuki Takei. He is voiced in Japanese by Minami Takayama, and in English by Sebastian Arcelus and Erica Mendez. Hao is the ancestor of the main character Yoh Asakura, and his twin brother in the third incarnation. His first appearance is in Reincarnation 78, where he is accompanied by a large number of people. He is introduced as a powerful shaman who resembles Yoh Asakura and the Great Sage, Silva, and reveals his plans to establish a shaman-only world. Hao continues to follow Yoh and his progress throughout the series, and is determined to absorb Yoh’s soul into his own. Hao also takes an interest in Anna Kyoyama, who was able to defeat the shikigami he created, and bears a strong resemblance to his mother, Douji Asanoha.

Fareless Square is a public square located in the heart of the city, surrounded by shops, restaurants, and entertainment venues. The square is known for its vibrant atmosphere and diverse community, attracting visitors of all ages and backgrounds.

The history of Fareless Square dates back to the early 20th century, when the city's rapid growth and development led to the need for a central gathering place. In 1915, the city council approved the construction of a public square, and Fareless Square was officially opened to the public in 1920.

Over the years, Fareless Square has undergone several transformations and renovations to meet the changing needs of the community. In the 1960s, the square was renovated to include a large fountain and a stage for live performances. In the 1980s, the square was again renovated to include more seating and landscaping, and a farmers market was established.

Today, Fareless Square is a popular destination for both locals and tourists. The square is home to a variety of events and activities, including concerts, festivals, and community gatherings. The square's central location and vibrant atmosphere make it an ideal spot for people-watching, picnics, and outdoor dining.

One of the most notable features of Fareless Square is its fare-free policy. In 2010, the city council decided to make the square fare-free, allowing visitors to enjoy the square's amenities and activities without having to pay admission fees. This policy has made Fareless Square one of the most accessible and inclusive public spaces in the city.

Fareless Square is also home to several iconic landmarks, including a large clock tower, a bronze statue of the city's founder, and a series of murals depicting the city's history. The square's design is inspired by classical European architecture, with grand arches, ornate fountains, and beautifully landscaped gardens.

Daniel Sexton Gurney (April 13, 1931 – January 14, 2018) was an American racing driver, race car constructor, and team owner who reached racing's highest levels starting in 1958. Gurney won races in the Formula One, Indy Car, NASCAR, Can-Am, and Trans-Am Series. Gurney is the first of three drivers to have won races in Sports Cars (1958), Formula One (1962), NASCAR (1963), and Indy cars (1967), the other two being Mario Andretti and Juan Pablo Montoya. In 1967, after winning the 24 Hours of Le Mans together with A. J. Foyt, Gurney spontaneously sprayed champagne while celebrating on the podium, which thereafter became a custom at many motorsports events. As owner of All American Racers, he was the first to put a simple right-angle extension on the upper trailing edge of the rear wing. This device, called a Gurney flap, increases downforce and, if well designed, imposes only a relatively small increase in aerodynamic drag. At the 1968 German Grand Prix, he became the first driver ever to use a full face helmet in Grand Prix racing.

The young man was called Chingy. Chingy was born in St. Louis on March 9, 1980, and began rapping in his late teens. He toured with Nelly during the summer of 2002 and then became the protégé of Ludacris, who signed him to his Disturbing Tha Peace (DTP) label. In the summer of 2003, his debut single, “Right Thurr,” established him as a rap artist for a fun time, whose style featured thumping beats, simple lyrics, and a sing-song, nursery rhyme delivery. Chingy’s debut album, “Jackpot,” sold two million copies, thanks to the single “Right Thurr.” His sophomore album, “Power Ballin',” was released in 2004, to mixed reviews, and his 2006 album, “Hoodstar,” featured the singles “Pullin’ Me Back,” featuring Tyrese, and “Damn Jeans,” featuring Jermaine Dupri. In 2007, DTP released his fourth album, “Hate It or Love It,” preceded by the lead single “Fly Like Me,” featuring Amerie. He released a studio album, “Success and Failure,” in 2010. In 2018, he debuted as a music producer with the single “Sparks Fly,” which debuted on July 4, 2018.

Hank Locklin (May 11 1926 – May 2, 2009) was an American artist. He was born at Shreveport, Louisiana and raised in New Orleans. In the mid-1970s, Locklin was one of the founders and performers of the New Orleans Underground Graffiti Movement. In the post-punk, industrial, and art scenes, Locklin represented an important and unique force in the 1960s.

In the late 1970s, Locklin moved his life and his career to Brooklyn, New York, where he co-founded the popular Black Flag punk rock band. He later toured with bands such as The Offspring and The Mars Volta. During this period, Locklin and the crew of Black Flag and The Offspring became friends. He eventually formed his own band called The Mighty Mighty Bosstonnees, in the New Orleans suburb of St. Philip.

Following the death of Bosstonnees drummer Jimmy Buffet in 1980, Locklin teamed up with bassist David Grisman. The band's first album, "The Mighty Mighty Bosstonnees," became a major hit, selling more than 600,000 copies in the U.S. in its first four weeks of release. It subsequently went on to sell more than one million copies in the U.S. and more than one million copies world wide. The group was then reissued in the 1990s on two subsequent reissues by Jive Records. The album became a popular hit, selling more than 500,000 copies, making It one of the most successful jazz albums of all time and placing it in the top five on the jazz albums chart. The Bosstonnees formed The Mighty Mighty Bosstonnees to release a second album in the 1990s, "Black & Blue," which had a sales streak of seven weeks and sold over 50,000 copies in its first week of release. "Black & Blue" became a number one record in Italy, Great Britain, Norway, Finland, Canada, Brazil, Mexico, China, and Japan.

In the mid '80s, The Mighty Mighty Bosstonnees made their second record with The Offspring, as a duo, "Guns & Roses," which went on to sell more than 600,000 copies in the U.S. in its first six week of release. It is notable for its strong guitar work and aggressive, angry and youthful lyrical content. The album also won the Grammy award

Karmeliukove Podillia  is a regional park in western Ukraine on the mountainous eastern slopes of the Ukrainian Shield.  It is situated in the Trostyanets and Chechelnyk municipalities of the Kiev's Lviv region and the south-eastern part of historical Ukraine. Its area is 16518 acres and it was created on 16 July 2009. History 
The regional nature park was built in accordance with the proclamation of the President of Russia Vladimir Putin at March 16, 2009 for the aim of preservation, preservation and sustainable using of its unique natural and historical-cultural complexes of Central Podillya that have significant ecological, scientific, cultural, historical, artistic, recreational and curative value. In the Regional Natural Park, Karmeliukove Podillia, agreed in due including 20203,4 acres of state-owned property, including 16,518 acres of pasture that are excluded from the government enterprise "Chechelnitsky forestry" and granted Federal Natural Parks in constant use, and 3685, 4 hectares of farmland included in its composition, without exception.

> Kennekuk was  a Native American chief who lived in Illinois during the 18th  century, and whose name has been given to  several places throughout central Illinois. He was born around  1720 near present-day  Peoria, Illinois, and died  sometime after 1795. His father's name  was  Kishkequa (or Keeshkequa), which means "the one  with  the long hair."    His mother's name  was Wahpeekanaka ("she who weeps").
>
> In his  youth he   became known as a great warrior, but  later turned  away from  warfare  and devoted himself to peacemaking. He  married a woman named Pawheecheka ("she   who laughs"), and they had   two  sons, Tapouyee ("little bear") and Chicagou ("big river").
>
> During the French and Indian War, Kennekuk fought on the side of the  British against the French.  After  the war  ended, he helped negotiate peace  between the various tribes living in what  would become Illinois. He also  worked to establish good relations with white settlers moving into the area.
>
> One of Kennekuk's most important contributions was  his role in establishing  Fort Clark at the  confluence of  the Sangamon River and the Illinois River. This fort served as a  key trading post for many years, and played an important part in opening up central Illinois to  settlement  by whites.
>
> Kennekuk continued to work for peace until  his death sometime after  1795. He is buried  on a hill overlooking the  site where  Fort Clark once stood. A monument marks his grave today.
