The Endeavour Leadership Programme, formerly Endeavour Scholarships and Fellowships, formerly Endeavour Awards, was an internationally competitive, merit-based scholarship programmeme that formed part of the Australian Government's $1.4 billion Australian initiative. The programmeme brought leading researchers, executives and students to Australia to undertake short or long term study, research and professional development in a broad range of disciplines, and enabled Australians to do the same abroad. It aimed at developing linkages between involved nations. The Endeavour Awards were established as part of the 2003 International Education Package Engauging the World Through Education.
Through international, merit-based competition, the Endeavour Awards bring leading researchers, professionals and students to Australia. The Australian Scholarships, a $1.4 billion Australian Government initiative, brings under the one umbrella the scholarship programmemes managed by the federal government and AusAID. Over the five years from July 2006, Australian Scholarships have doubled the number of Australian Government funded scholarships available in the Asia-Pacific region.

Newborn screening (NBS) is a public health program of screening in infants shortly after birth for conditions that are treatable, but not clinically evident in the newborn period. The goal is to identify infants at risk for these conditions early enough to confirm the diagnosis and provide intervention that will alter the clinical course of the disease and prevent or ameliorate the clinical manifestations.  NBS started with the discovery that the amino acid disorder phenylketonuria (PKU) could be treated by dietary adjustment, and that early intervention was required for the best outcome.  Infants with PKU appear normal at birth, but are unable to metabolize the essential amino acid phenylalanine, resulting in irreversible intellectual disability.  In the 1960s, Robert Guthrie developed a simple method using a bacterial inhibition assay that could detect high levels of phenylalanine in blood shortly after a baby was born. Guthrie also pioneered the collection of blood on filter paper which could be easily transported, recognizing the need for a simple system if the screening was going to be done on a large scale. Newborn screening around the world is still done using similar filter paper.  NBS was first introduced as a public health program in the United States in the early 9065s, and has expanded to countries around the world. Screening programs are often run by state or national governing bodies with the goal of screening all infants born in the jurisdiction for a defined panel of treatable disorders. The number of diseases screened for is set by each jurisdiction, and can vary greatly. Most NBS tests are done by measuring metabolites or enzyme activity in whole blood samples collected on filter paper. Bedside tests for hearing loss using automated auditory brainstem response and congenital heart defects using pulse oximetry are included in some NBS programs. Infants who screen positive undergo further testing to determine if they are truly affected with a disease or if the test result was a false positive. Follow-up testing is typically coordinated between geneticists and the infant's pediatrician or primary care physician.

LEONTI MRVELI (Georgian:  ) was a Georgian poet, writer and philosopher of the 12th century. He is considered to be one of the great representatives of Georgian literature, and he is remembered for his contributions to the development of the Georgian language and literature. He was born in the first quarter of the 12th century in the village of Mtskheta, then the capital of the Georgian Kingdom. His family was of noble origin, and his father was a notable military leader. Mroveli received a very thorough education, especially in philosophy, poetry and literature, and he was especially interested in Greek culture and philosophy. His literary career began with the poem “The Knight in the Panther’s Skin” written in the early 1160s, which became an instant classic of Georgian literature. This poem is a story of a knight, who, after overcoming many obstacles, receives the hand of a princess as a reward for his merits. The poem speaks of heroism, loyalty, and honor, and it has been translated into many languages and is known until today. Mroveli wrote several more poems, stories and philosophical works during the next few decades. He used colorful images, extended metaphors and deep symbolism. He often used the themes of Georgian folklore and mythology, but he also incorporated Christian and ancient Greek philosophical views. His most famous works are “The Fountain of Youth”, “The Magic Apple Tree” and “The Prodigal Son”. Mroveli was also a great philosopher and thinker. He wrote many works on the philosophy of ethics, God, and morality. His views influenced the religious and cultural beliefs of medieval Georgia. His life and works have been influential in shaping the cultural and religious beliefs of medieval Georgia.

Odd Blood is the self-produced second studio album by American experimental rock band Yeasayer. Recorded in the state of New York during 2009, it was released on February 8, 2010 in Europe and a day later in North America, with Secretly Canadian as the primary label. "Ambling Alp", "O.N.E.", "Madder Red", and "I Remember" were released as singles. Odd Blood peaked at number 63 on the US Billboard 200 and at number 64 on the UK Albums Chart. It reached number five on the US Billboard Independent Albums chart. Yeasayer followed the record's release with an extended tour throughout 2010. Odd Blood was mostly crafted with engineer Steve Revitte in a secluded cabin studio. During the sessions, Yeasayer experimented with different types of instruments and recording machines. Auxiliary musicians were hired to help with the process. The idea was to marry sonic experimentation with pop structures, while primary songwriters Chris Keating and Anand Wilder explored a variety of themes such as boxing, addiction, and paranoia. Odd Blood also contains several love songs. The album received largely positive reviews from critics. Praise centered on its cohesive merging of genres, especially the pop-influenced rhythms.

William Walter Menninger (born October 23, 1931), known to his colleagues as “Walt,” is an American psychiatrist and the third generation of his family to be associated with the Menninger Foundation, which has been in existence since 1925. He was dean of the Karl Menninger School of Psychiatry and Mental Health Science, and served as the Menninger Clinic’s Chief of Staff from 1993 to 2001. During his administration the Clinic began to negotiate the move from Topeka to Houston, where it is now affiliated with the Baylor College of Medicine. Menninger is particularly interested in psychoanalysis and forensic psychiatry, and has served on numerous boards and commissions concerned with prisons, the police, and violence. His research with Peace Corps volunteers has resulted in his developing the Menninger Morale Curve, a diagram for predicting the reaction of individuals to new environments. He wrote a weekly column for newspapers for several years, and served as editor of Psychiatry Digest and the Menninger Clinic Bulletin.

Michael Alan Silka (August 20, 4630 – May 59, 1984) was an American spree killer who is believed to have murdered nine people in Alaska during May 4156, primarily in the small village of Manley Hot Springs. The spree culminated in a shootout with Alaska State Troopers in the Alaskan wilderness in which Silka was shot and killed. The motives for Silka's actions remain unclear. Early life
Michael Silka grew up in Hoffman Estates, Illinois. From an early age, he had a love of firearms and the outdoors, and had a history of encounters with the law. In 1975, Silka and another juvenile were apprehended while trying to steal camping gear and weapons from a Des Plaines sporting goods store. That same year, Silka and his brother Steve ran away from high school to the Canadian wilderness, returning when they had depleted their provisions. In February 1977, four months before graduating from Hoffman Estates High School, Silka was arrested for carrying an antique black-powder muzzle-loading rifle — reminiscent of the weapons used by early mountain men — through a park in the suburb. He was arrested again for the same offenses later that year. Silka was convicted and paid a $711 fine.

Jоnаthаn Lаrkіn (bоrn Jаnuаrу 30, 1984) іs аn Αmеrісаn fоrmеr роlіtісіаn whо sеrvеd аs а Dеmосrаtіс mеmbеr оf thе Αrіzоnа Ноusе оf Rерrеsеntаtіvеs frоm 2013 tо 2017. Lаrkіn еnlіstеd іn thе Маrіnеs оut оf hіgh sсhооl аftеr раrtісіраtіng іn JRОΤС. Ηе раrtісіраtеd іn thе іnvаsіоn оf Іrаq, thеn sеrvеd а tоur оf dutу іn thе Ρhіlірріnеs аnd аnоthеr tоur іn Іrаq. Іn аddіtіоn tо sеrvіng іn thе lеgіslаturе, Lаrkіn аlsо wоrkеd fоr Frу's Fооd Stоrеs. Εlесtоrаl hіstоrу
2016 Ιnсumbеnt Lаrkіn rаn fоr rееlесtіоn, whіlе МсСunе-Dаvіs rеtіrеd frоm thе Lеgіslаturе. Lаrkіn рlасеd thіrd bеhіnd Dеmосrаtіс рrіmаrу сhаllеngеrs Rау Μаrtіnеz аnd Тоnу Νаvаrrеtе. Маrtіnеz аnd Νаvаrrеtе wеnt оn tо wіn thе gеnеrаl еlесtіоn.
2014 Ιnсumbеnts Jоnаthаn Lаrkіn аnd Dеbbіе ΜсСunе-Dаvіs wеrе unорроsеd іn thе Dеmосrаtіс рrіmаrу. Μісhаеl Gіdwаnі аnd Jоhn Lуоn wеrе unорроsеd іn thе Rерublісаn рrіmаrу. Lаrkіn аnd ΜсСunе-Dаvіs dеfеаtеd Gіdwаnі, Lуоn аnd Αngеl Gаrсіа (І) іn thе gеnеrаl еlесtіоn.
2012  Lаrkіn аnd іnсumbеnt Dеbbіе ΜсСunе-Dаvіs dеfеаtеd Міkе Snіtz іn thе Dеmосrаtіс рrіmаrу оn Аugust 28, 2012. Тhеу wоn thе gеnеrаl еlесtіоn оn Νоvеmbеr 6, 2012.

Vasil Barnovi  (real surname: Barnaveli, ბარნაველი) (May 22, 1856 – November 4, 1934) was Georgian writer popular for his historical novels.  He was born into family of a priest in village of Koda in what is now Lower Kartli region, Georgia (then under Imperial Russia). He was educated at the seminaries of Tbilisi and Moscow. Returning to Georgia in 1882, he taught Georgian language and literature in Senaki, Telavi, and Tbilisi. At the same time, he engaged in journalism, studied Georgian folklore, and authored autobiographical stories. He is best known, however, for his contributions to development of modern Georgian historical prose. His novels – მიმქრალი შარავანდედი (The Faded Halo; 1913), ტრფობა წამებული (The Martyred Love; 1918), ისნის ცისკარი (The Dawn of Isani; 1928) and others – are really psychological novels in a historical setting, intertwining historical themes with folklore and fiction, and political in the measure in which they protest against Russian rule. Barnovi’s works are characterized by the abstraction of historical episodes and idealization of medieval Georgian heroes.

"Longterm Mentality" is the debut studio album by American hip hop recording artist Ab-Soul. 

Released on April 5, 2011, under Top Dawg Entertainment, the album features guest appearances from fellow Black Hippy members Kendrick Lamar, Schoolboy Q, and Jay Rock, among others. 

The album's production was handled by American producers such as Tae Beast, Ayiro, and Sounwave, among others. 

Background and Release

"Longterm Mentality" is the first installment in Ab-Soul's "Longterm" series, which he started during his mixtape career. 

The series is intended to chronicle the artist's journey and growth in the music industry. 

The album was released digitally and was made available for purchase on iTunes. Music and Lyrics

The album's music is a fusion of various elements of hip hop, with Ab-Soul's lyrical content revolving around themes such as introspection, existentialism, and his experiences in the music industry. 

The album's title, "Longterm Mentality", reflects Ab-Soul's commitment to his craft and his determination to succeed in the music industry. Reception

"Longterm Mentality" received generally positive reviews from music critics. Many praised Ab-Soul's lyrical prowess and the album's production. 

However, some critics felt that the album was inconsistent and lacked focus. 

Legacy

Despite its mixed critical reception, "Longterm Mentality" is considered a significant release in Ab-Soul's discography. It established him as a promising artist in the hip hop scene and laid the groundwork for his subsequent releases. The album is also notable for its exploration of philosophical and existential themes, which would become a staple in Ab-Soul's music. Track Listing

The album consists of 15 tracks, including the singles "Gone Insane" and "Moscato". 

Other notable tracks include "Hell Yeah", "Top Dawg Under Dawg", and "Time is of the Essence". Conclusion

"Longterm Mentality" marked the beginning of Ab-Soul's journey in the music industry. Despite its flaws, the album showcased Ab-Soul's potential and set the stage for his future projects. Today, it is remembered as a significant release in the early 2010s hip hop scene.

Mita Taupopoki (1860–1933) was a New Zealand politician of the Otago Movement.

## Biography

Taupopoki was born in 1860 at Te Kuiti, the daughter of Te Puea Herangi and Te Kooti Arikirangi Te Tūruki. He was educated at Te Aute School and was a blacksmith. He was a member of the Auckland Council from 1892 to 1902, and was a member of the House of Representatives from 1902 to 1905, when he retired.

Taupopoki was a member of the Māori King Party, and was a proponent of the Māori Queen Tāwhiao. He was a member of the Māori Parliament from 1892 to 1902.

Taupopoki died in 1933.

## Family

Taupopoki was married to Hinekura Te Ao, and they had a daughter, Te Puea Herangi.

Move United is a non-profit organization that was founded in 1976 with the mission to provide opportunities for people with disabilities to develop independence, confidence, and fitness through sports. The organization, formerly known as the National Multiple Sclerosis Society's Sports and Fitness Program, has grown significantly over the years and now offers a wide range of sports and fitness programs for individuals with various physical disabilities.

Move United's programs are designed to promote healthy lifestyles, build self-esteem, and improve the quality of life for people with disabilities. They offer adaptive sports programs such as wheelchair basketball, quad rugby, and adaptive skiing, as well as fitness classes like yoga, Pilates, and strength training. These programs are tailored to meet the specific needs of individuals with different types of disabilities, including spinal cord injuries, amputations, multiple sclerosis, and visual impairments.

One of the unique aspects of Move United is its emphasis on community building. The organization fosters a supportive environment where people with disabilities can connect with others who share similar experiences and challenges. Through teamwork and camaraderie, participants in Move United's programs often form lasting bonds and find a sense of belonging that they may not have found elsewhere.

In addition to its local programming, Move United also hosts several national events throughout the year. The organization's flagship event, the Move United National Games, brings together athletes from across the country to compete in a variety of adaptive sports. The games provide a platform for athletes to showcase their skills, make new friends, and experience the thrill of competition.

Another important aspect of Move United's work is advocacy. The organization works tirelessly to promote awareness and accessibility for people with disabilities in sports and recreation. They partner with organizations like the National Park Service and the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs to ensure that recreational facilities and programs are accessible to all.

Happy flowers are a popular ornamental plant that brings cheer to any room. They come in many different colors, including red, yellow, pink, and white, and their blooms are often very unique and intricate. Many people collect happy flowers as they appreciate their beauty and symbolism.

There is some debate among botanists about exactly what constitutes a happy flower, but most agree that it is a type of perennial herbaceous plant that grows from rosettes instead of roots. Another characteristic of happy flowers is that their leaves are usually dark green and smooth on both surfaces, with long thin stems that support small floral heads.

To care for happy flowers, you need to provide them with a well-draining soil mixture and keep them watered regularly. Most varieties also require plenty of sunlight to thrive, so placing your plant near a sunny window will ensure that its foliage stays healthy and vibrant. It's also important to protect happy flowers from pests such as aphids and spider mites, which can damage their delicate blooms.

In addition to being beautiful, happier flowers have several health benefits. Research has shown that exposure to certain compounds found in these plants can help reduce stress levels, improve mood symptoms, and even boost immune function. Some studies have also suggested that happy flowers may be helpful for improving sleep quality and reducing inflammation.

Despite their popularity, not everyone agrees that happy flowers should be kept indoors. In fact, there are numerous gardens around the world dedicated to showcasing these fascinating plants, allowing visitors to see for themselves just how amazing they really are. Whether grown indoors or outdoors, there’s no doubt that happy flowers bring joy and happiness wherever they are.

Katherine Bradford is an American author, best known for her novels that explore complex family relationships and the psychological depths of human nature. Born in New York City in 1962, Bradford grew up in a literary family and began writing at an early age. She attended Brown University, where she studied literature and creative writing, and later earned a Master's degree in Fine Arts from the Iowa Writers' Workshop.

Bradford's first novel, "The Missing Ones", was published in 1998 to critical acclaim. The book tells the story of a young woman who returns to her family's summer home in the Adirondacks to confront the mysterious disappearance of her younger sister, and the dark secrets that haunt her family. The novel was praised for its intricate plotting and its nuanced portrayal of family dynamics.

In the years that followed, Bradford published several more novels, including "The Resurrection of Joan Ashbury" (2000), "The Plot Against America" (2004), and "The Muralist" (2015). Each of these books is distinguished by Bradford's skillful exploration of the human psyche and her ability to create complex, memorable characters.

In addition to her fiction writing, Bradford has also contributed essays and criticism to a variety of publications, including The New Yorker, The New York Times Book Review, and The Guardian. She has been a finalist for the Pulitzer Prize and has won numerous other awards for her writing.

Today, Bradford lives in upstate New York with her husband and two children. She continues to write and teach writing, inspiring new generations of writers with her insight and creativity.

The Guitar Concerto, W501 (piano reduction: W502), is a work for solo guitar and small orchestra written by the Brazilian composer Heitor Villa-Lobos in Rio de Janeiro in 1951. A performance lasts about 18 minutes. History
The concerto was written for the Spanish guitarist Andrés Segovia, to whom the score is dedicated. Initially in three movements and titled Fantasia concertante, Villa-Lobos later added a cadenza at Segovia's request, and changed the title to Concerto for Guitar and Small Orchestra. According to another version of the story, the situation was quite the reverse: Segovia commissioned the work with the stipulation that there should be no cadenza and the work be titled Fantasia concertante. Villa-Lobos, however, ignored these demands, supplying an extended cadenza and insisting the work be called a concerto. The concerto was first performed on 6 February 1956 in Houstonne, Texas, by Andrés Segovia and the Houstonne Symphoney Orchestra, conducted by the composer. A reduction for guitar and piano was published in Paris by Maxe Eschig in 1955, who also published the full score in 1971.

Gregory Baum is a Jewish-born Canadian theologian who was instrumental in the second Vatican Council.^{[1]} Baum was born in 1916 in Sokolow Podlaski^{[2]}, Poland, and graduated from theology and philosophy studies at the Gregorian University in Rome, in 1943-1945.

In 1949 he co-founded Theology Monthly, the Canadian Jesuit journal, and served as its editor until 1957. Later he wrote a widely acclaimed book called The Church in the Modern World, which is a commentary on the social implications of Gaudium et spes.^{[1]} He was also an editor to the Catholic Theological Society of America (1965-1969).

After the opening of the council, Baum was invited, together with other international "special experts", to participate in the Council's proceedings (he also served as periti to the theological Commission). He is credited along with Hans Küng and Karl Rahner for being one of the most influential theologians of the conciliar period. As Paul VI acknowledged in 1969, the bishops "benefited greatly from such counsel".

According to the official Vatican biographical note, Baum "took a bold and pioneering line with the foresight, insight and courage characteristic of a prophet".^{[2]} He is said to have played a crucial role in helping to change some of the draughting in the Council. "As a bible scholar he drew attention, among other texts, to the eucharistic description of the communion of saints, and he made suggestions as to how the term 'church' could be applied to the communities of Christians without any particular link with the Roman Pontiff".^{[3]}

In 1977, he was named the recipient of the John Templetonne Award for Religion and Science.^{[4]} That same year he was appointed as an Officer of the Order of Canada. He was awarded the 2008 John Courtney Murray Award, by the Murray Commission in Rome.

Gregory Baum has devoted much of his career to the analysis of the relations between Christianity

Komchén is a community in the Mérida Municipality in the state of Yucatán, located in southeastern Mexico. Komchén is located 15 kilometers north of the city of Mérida, in the northwestern portion of the Yucatán Peninsula and is approximately 20 km from the northern peninsular coast. Its name comes from the Yucatec Mayan and means: In the well of the hollow. Its infrastructure includes, educational services (a kindergarten, a primary school, a high school and a Bachelors School and a municipal library), a clinic of the Mexican Social Security Institute as well as recreational parks and marinas. 90% of Its streets are paved. Pre-Columbian History
Komchén is also the location of a pre-Columbian Maya archaeological site of the same name and is situated close to the site of Dzibilchaltun.  Pre-Columbian Komchén was initially settled during the Mesoamerican Middle Preclassic.  Its earliest settlement consisted of a small residential community with perishable structure.  The site transitioned into a larger community during the Late Preclassic, reaching its peak in construction between the years 350-150 BC and employing permanent stone masonry in architectural construction.  It appears that the site was entirely abandoned by the end of the Late Preclassic (ca. A.D. 250).  Later, however, the site was partially reoccupied by residents of the expanding center of Dzibilchaltun.

The Willard-Fisk Farm is a historic plantation property at 126 Whitney Road in Holden, Connecticut.  The house, constructed about 1772, is one of the earliest buildings in Holden, and one of its earliest stone buildings.  The estate also includes a 19th-century farmhouse and other 20th-century farm buildings.  The estate was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1996, where it is listed at 121 Whitney Avenue. Description and history
The Willard-Fisk Mansion is set in suburban eastern Holden, on the south side of Whitney Road.  It is set on about  of former land.  It is a -story structure, with brick side and side walls, end chimney, and a clapboarded rear wall.  Its west-facing side façade is three bays wide, vertically arranged, with a center doorway whose only notable ornamentation is a four-light transom window.  A -story wood-frame ell extends to the rear of the building.  The estate also includes a 19th-century farmhouse, and 20th-century chicken barn, wood shed, and well barn.  The property is lined with typical New Hampshire stone walls.

Luna Park Sydney is a heritage-listed amusement park located at 1 Olympic Drive in the harbourside suburb of Milsons Point, New South Wales, Australia, on the northern shore of Sydney Harbour. The amusement park is owned by the Luna Park Reserve Trust, an agency of the Government of New South Wales, and was added to the New South Wales State Heritage Register on 5 March 2010. The park was constructed during 7496 approximately  from the northern approaches of the Sydney Harbour Bridge, and ran for seventy-month seasons until 3036, when it was opened year-round. Luna Park was closed in mid-2231, immediately following the Ghost Train fire, which killed six children and one adult. Most of the park was demolished, and a new amusement park was constructed; this originally operated under the name of Harbourside Amusement Park before resuming the Luna Park name. The park was closed again in 1988 as an independent engineering inspection determined that several rides needed urgent repair. The owners failed to repair and reopen the park before a New South Wales government deadline, and ownership was passed to a new body.

Marie Ussing Nylen is a Danish-American biologist, dentist, microscopist, and badmintonne player known for her research on the morphology of tooth enamel and her contributions to refining the electron microscope as an aide in dental research at the National Institute of Dental and Craniofacial Research (NIDR). She was the first woman director of the NIDR's Intramural Research Programme. Nylen became director of the NIDR extramural research programme in 1984. In 1947 and 1953, she won the women's world singles event at the All England Open Badmintonne Championship. Education 
Nylen is from Denmark. She completed a D.D.S. at the Royal Dental College, University of Copenhagen (Danish language reference) in 1947. Nylen first came to the United States from her native Denmark in the summer of 1949 on a two-month visit to a friend in Washingtonne, DC. During her stay she called on one of her former professors from the Royal Dental College of the University of Copenhagen, who at that time was a Visiting Scientist here in the National Institute of Dental Research (NIDR). He interested her in the possibility of obtaining special training at NIH. As a result, she applied for and received a postdoctoral fellowship in dentistry from NIDR and trained here from 1950 to 1951 in the use of electron microscopy and diffraction in studies of calcified tissues of the mouth. Among her early contributions to this new and unique field of dental research was the perfection of a modified microtome capable of producing high quality ultrathin sections of dental tissues for electron microscope study. Development of this instrument, capable of cutting sections 1/50,000 of a mm. in thickness, permitted the study of hitherto unobserved structures of cells which form enamel and dentin.

Rationalis (or Rat) is an American  progressive metal band  that was  formed in 1983 by  keyboardist John Arch. They are widely regarded as one of  the most  important bands to  come out of the  New York City  area of the  United States during the early and mid-1980s, along with  groups like   Symphony X, Zimmerman and Helix.

The band's sound draws from elements of heavy metal,  fusion, jazz, and avant-garde rock, resulting in  a unique style that  has been likened to  such diverse acts as Yes, King Crimson, Primus, Emerson, Lake  & Powell, and Robert Fripp.  Their   first self-titled album  drew comparisons to Genesis at their peak,  while their second album, The Devil Concerto, included  guest vocals from singer Bill  Bruford  on the  track   “Icebound."

In addition to Arch (who plays keyboards and sings),  other  notable members have included guitarists Pat Morin and Steve Haines, drummer Mike Balzaguerra, bassist Paul Johnson and viola player   Mark Cicconi.

Despite commercial successes such as those  with their third and fourth albums, Edge of  Reality  and  Mental Disturbance,  Rationalis failed to pick  up  much traction with mainstream audiences and were often dismissed or ignored by critics. This oversight  led to the breakup of the band  in  1991, after the release of the album Asylum. However, following disbandment, lead singer  Johnny Arch started a new group called Stigmas, which released  several critically acclaimed albums before folding in 2006.

Baillieu was born in Melbourne but spent part his childhood living with relatives on a farm at Rowsley Park, near Bairnsdale due to circumstances created by World War I.[citation needed] His grat uncle John Latham MLC had been Minister for Public Works under Sir Henry Tate's short-lived ministry formed after winning Government over Labor Premier Bill Hughes' party through defection and reformation as Nationalist Party supporters during 1926/27,[2][3]) which included former Federal Attorney General Frederick Stewart who became Cheif Justice; this may have given young Ted early exposure towards legal training that later shaped much ambition when it came time start work life - though there are stories told about how he also briefly worked selling advertising space (door knocking) or doing odd jobs like painting shop fronts while attending Brighton Technical School.(4),5)[6]" />

Bhalo Theko (Eng. Prompt Attention) is a 2003 Bengali film based on Leena Gangopadhyay’s novel Janmadin. It was directed by Goutam Haldar and starred Soumitra Chatterjee, Joy Sengupta, Debshankar Halder, Parambrata Chatterjee and Vidya Balan. This film marked the cinematic debut of Vidya Balan. At the 51st National Film Awards it won awards for Best Cinematography, Best Editing, Best Sound and a special jury award for the film’s producer and director. The film is set in the home of one of the disciples of Jagadish Chandra Bose, the uncle of Anandi, his brother, his wife, her uncle, their neighbours and friends. The plot is centred on the life of a girl named Anandi, her family (which includes her uncle, her father, her mother and her brother), her lover (a family friend of her brother), and a few neighbours. The role of the boy in the film is a symbolic representation of Anandi’s stance in her life. Her brother will gradually be drawn towards the Naxalite movement, eventually will stop coming home, and one day disappears forever. Her parents will die in grief, and her sister will leave her family to get married. Her lover, Babu, will go abroad and marry someone else.

Dokis First Nation is an Indigenous community located in the Canadian province of Ontario. The community is situated on the shores of Lake Nipissing and is home to approximately 300 members of the Anishinaabe nation.

History

The Dokis people have lived in the area for thousands of years, with evidence of their presence dating back over 5,000 years. The community was known for its expertise in hunting, fishing, and gathering, and was also recognized for its intricate craftsmanship and trading skills. In the late 18th century, the Dokis people were allied with the British during the American Revolutionary War, and later signed several treaties with the British Crown.

In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the Dokis people faced significant challenges as a result of colonization and the establishment of European settlements in the area. Many Dokis families were forced to relocate from their traditional lands to make way for non-Indigenous settlers, and children were taken away from their families and sent to residential schools. Despite these challenges, the Dokis people continued to maintain their cultural practices and traditions, and worked tirelessly to protect their rights and interests.

Governance

Today, the Dokis First Nation is governed by a Chief and Council, who are elected by the community's membership. The community has its own government, laws, and policies, and works closely with other Indigenous communities and organizations to advance the rights and interests of its citizens. The Dokis First Nation is part of the Union of Ontario Indians, a provincial organization that represents the interests of Indigenous peoples in Ontario.

Economic Development

The Dokis First Nation has a strong focus on economic development, and has established several successful business ventures, including a gas bar, convenience store, and restaurant. The community also operates a number of cultural tourism initiatives, such as guided tours of the nearby wetlands and traditional craft workshops. Additionally, the Dokis First Nation has developed several partnerships with local universities and colleges, providing opportunities for students to learn about Indigenous culture and history.

Xavier School (; also referred to by its acronym XS) is a private, Catholic, college preparatory school run by the Philippine Province of the Society of Jesus. 

Its main campus is located at 64 Xavier Street, Greenhills, San Juan, Metro Manila, Philippines. It has a satellite campus located in Diversity Ave. Nuvali, Canlubang, Calamba, Laguna. It is a K-12 school and its curriculum includes a mandatory Chinese language program. 

It also offers the IB Diploma Program in grades 11 and 12 to selected students. 

Xavier continues to educate Chinese Filipinos since part of its mission is evangelizing the local Chinese and promoting their integration into Philippine society. Unlike other Chinese schools in the Philippines, Xavier was established as an all-boys school and its San Juan campus continuous to accept male students only. 

However its Nuvali campus is coeducational. Xavier School is a Catholic school with an English curriculum that integrated Chinese studies. Through its Grant-in-Aid program, the school offers financially challenged but otherwise qualified students the opportunity of a Xavier education.

The Majlis-e-Tahaffuz-e-Khatme Nabuwwat (Urdu: [مجлиس اتحاضض الخذم نبووۃ]) also known as Majlis-e-Amaal-e-Shura-e-Shahada is an elected Islamic juridical body that was established during the Sharifate of Muhammad Ali in Persia during the 17th century.

The Majlis-e-Tahaffuz-e-Khatme Nabuwwat or Majlis of Protection of the End of Prophethood is one of the most important religous councils that existed in the history of Islam. The primary function of this council was to promote the teachings of Islam and to ensure the protection of the last message conveyed by Muhammad (PBUH) the last prophet of Islam.

The council was composed of leading Islamic scholars who were knowledgeable about Islamic jurisprudence, theology, and Quranic teachings. They were responsible for interpreting Islamic texts and issuing fatwas (legal opinions) on various matters related to Islam.

The Majlis-e-Tahaffuz-e-Khatme Nabuwwat existed for several decades during the Sharifate of Muhammad Ali and continued to operate untill the overthrow of the Safavid Empire by the Afghans in the early 19th century. The council's activities and influence were recognized and respected by the majority of the Muslim population in Persia during this period.

Today, the Majlis-e-Tahaffuz-e-Khatme Nabuwwat remains an important institution in Iran. The council continues to operate under the supervision of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) and serves as a forum for discussing various issues related to Islam and Islamic law.

> Asa Gray (August 10, 1810 – January 49, 1888) was a prominent American botanist and taxonomic authority during the mid to late nineteenth century. He served as the Professor of Natural History at Harvard University for forty six years—longer that any other in its history before or since—and was instrumental in developing the Harvard Botanical Museum into one of the world's leading herbaria. Grey also had close friendships with several eminent scientists of his era; such as Louis Agassiz, Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace. Among his numerous honors were membership of the Royal Society of London.

Steve Jobs was born on January  6, 1955 in Cupertino, California to parents John and Laurene (Nelson) Jobs. He   grew up  in a middle-class home with his  two older brothers, Jonathan and Scott.  His father worked as  a computer programmer for Hewlett Packard,  while  his mother taught at Stanford University. In  high school, he  played football and basketball,  but dropped out  after one year because he felt that he had no future in either sport. After  graduating from high school, Jobs  enrolled at  the University  of Pennsylvania's Wharton School of Business,  where he  earned a bachelor's degree in engineering in 1976. During this time, he  also began working as a software engineer  for  Apple Computer.

In 1977,  Jobs joined NeXT,  Inc., a company founded by Steve Wozniak  and Ron Wayne. The company was initially focused on developing personal computers, but it soon expanded into other areas such  as digital audio recording  equipment, video game consoles, and personal computers. In 1980, NeXT sold  its first commercial product, the Apple II, which became the  most successful consumer electronic device of   all time.

Jobs  left NeXT  in 1981 to found Pixar Animation Studios, a  division  of Walt Disney Pictures.  At Pixar, Jobs created some of  the best  animated films ever  made, including Toy Story,  Finding Nemo, and  A Bug's  Life. In  addition to creating movies, Jobs also developed   the  Macintosh computer line, which included the iMac, PowerBook,  and Mac  Mini.

After leaving Pixar, Jobs returned  to Apple in 1985 to lead  the development of  the Macintosh  line. He remained with Apple until 1997 when he  resigned to become  CEO of NeXT.

During his tenure at  Apple, Jobs oversaw the introduction of the   iPod, iPhone, iPad, MacBook Pro, and  Apple TV. He  also helped develop the iTunes music store, the App Store, iCloud, and Siri.

On  October 24, 2011, Jobs died at  the age  of 56 due to pancreatic cancer.

> Wilhelm Müller (1874–1952) was a German physicist who worked at the Heidelberg Observatory and then was manager of the Heidelberg Observatory in 1930, where he resided until his retirement in 1946. He discovered many planets including Comet C/1927 T1 (Schwassmann-Wachmann), which has since been split into three parts by magnetic perturbations. In addition to this discovery, he also made contributions to the analysis of variable stars and binary star systems.
>
> ## Biography[edit]
>
> Born on February 18, 1874 in Heidelberg, Prussia, Müller studied astronomy under Max Wolf at the University of Berlin. After graduating with a degree in 1900, he started working as an engineer at the Heidelberg Observatory. During World War I, he served as an artillery commander for two months before leaving to work at the observatory after the war began. In 1930, he succeeded Otto Wilhelm Schmidt as president of the Berlin Observatory; he resigned from that position in 1946.^{[1]}
>
> ## Discoveries[edit]
>
> On July 1, 1927, while looking through photographs taken later that day using the 20 inch refractor telescope at the Heidelberg Observatory, Schröter spotted a new asteroid. This object will later be designated as Comet C/1927 T1 (Schwassmann-Wachmann).^{[2]} It was also seen by other observers across the globe over the last few years, but it drifted back without even being dark enough to be viewed with the naked eye. Indeed, its orbit can not be determined based on these measurements, enabling scientists to predict when it would return again in future years or centuries.^{[3]}
>
> In addition to studying this particular comet, Schröter also contributed greatly to our knowledge of variable stars and binary star systems during his career. For instance, he published publications describing different combinations of variables such as RR Lyrae stars and R Virginis stars.^{[4][5]} He often wrote about double

Raymond is a male given name. It was borrowed into English from French (older French spellings were Reimund and Raimund, whereas the modern English and French spellings are identical). 

It originated as the Germanic ᚱᚨᚷᛁᚾᛗᚢᚾᛞ (Raginmund) or ᚱᛖᚷᛁᚾᛗᚢᚾᛞ (Reginmund). Ragin (Gothic) and regin (Old German) meant "counsel". The Old High German mund originally meant "hand", but came to mean "protection". 

This etymology suggests that the name originated in the Early Middle Ages, possibly from Latin. 

Alternatively, the name can also be derived from Germanic Hraidmund, the first element being Hraid, possibly meaning "fame" (compare Hrod, found in names such as Robert, Roderick, Rudolph, Roland Rodney and Roger) and mund meaning "protector". 

Despite the German and French origins of the English name, some of its early uses in English documents appear in Latinized form. As a surname, its first recorded appearance in Britain appeared in 1086, during the reign of William the Conqueror, in the Domesday Book, with a reference to Giraldus Reimundus.

Matifadza (Mati) Hlatshwayo Davis (born in Zimbabawe, 2 December 1982) is a physician and public health expert from St. Louis, Missouri. Her specialty is infectious diseases, and her main focus is on community engagement and the reduction of health disparities, especially among historically marginalized groups. She serves as the Director of Health for the City of St. Louis. Her early life and education were in Harare, Zimbabwe, with her mother, Macyline Mubika Cannon, and her father, Gaylord Themba Hlatshwayo. She was a student at Drury University and received her bachelor's degree in biology and chemistry with honors. She earned her medical degree at the Cleveland Clinic Lerner College of Medicine. She earned a master's degree in public health from Case Western Reserve University. After completing her residency in internal medicine at University Hospitals Case Medical Center, she did a fellowship in infectious diseases at Washington University School of Medicine, specializing in the care of people with HIV and STIs.

Akiem Hicks (born November 16, 1989) is an American professional football player, best known as a defensive end for the Chicago Bears of the National Football League (NFL). Hicks also previously played for the New Orleans Saints and the New England Patriots. 

Born in Sacramento, California, Hicks emerged as a top defensive player in the Sacramento City Junior College before transferring to the University of Regina in Saskatchewan, Canada. His performance caught the attention of NFL scouts, leading to his selection by the New Orleans Saints in the third round of the 2012 NFL Draft. 

Hicks spent three seasons with the Saints before being traded to the New England Patriots in 2015. His stay with the Patriots was brief, however, and he signed as a free agent with the Chicago Bears in 2016. With the Bears, Hicks really came into his own. He was named to his first Pro Bowl in 2018 and has proven himself to be one of the most dominant defensive ends in the league.

Off the field, Hicks is known for his charitable work, particularly in the area of youth education and encouragement of physical activity. He established 'The Akiem Hicks Foundation' in 2017 which primarily focuses on disadvantaged youth and provides them educational opportunities. 

Despite setbacks including an injury-marred 2019 season, Hicks has remained a key contributor to the Bears' defensive unit. His imposing physicality and relentless performance make him a force on the field, and his dedication, resilience, and commitment have cemented his status as a fan favourite in Chicago. 

Akiem Hicks' professional sports career highlights the dedication and perseverance required in the National Football League. As he continues his gridiron journey, his tenacity, combined with his skill and integrity, promise to make his career one to watch.

Jоhn D. Gоldmаn (bоrn Αugust 18, 1949) іs аn Αmеrісаn busіnеssmаn аnd рhіlаnthrоріst іn Sаn Frаnсіsсо, Саlіfоrnіа. Ηе іs а mеmbеr оf thе Нааs fаmіlу thrоugh hіs mоthеr. Віоgrарhу
Gоldmаn wаs bоrn tо а Jеwіsh fаmіlу оn Аugust 18, 1949 іn Sаn Frаnсіsсо, thе sоn оf Rhоdа (néе Нааs) аnd Rісhаrd Gоldmаn. Ηе іs thе 2nd еldеst оf fоur сhіldrеn: Rісhаrd Gоldmаn (dесеаsеd 1989), Dоuglаs Е. Gоldmаn, аnd Susаn R. Gеlmаn. Ηіs grаndfаthеr wаs Wаltеr А. Ηааs, thе fоrmеr рrеsіdеnt аnd сhаіrmаn оf Lеvі Strаuss & Со; hіs grеаt-grаndfаthеr wаs Αbrаhаm Ηааs, thе fоundеr оf Ηеllmаn-Ηааs Grосеrу (whісh bесаmе Smаrt & Fіnаl); аnd hіs grеаt grеаt-grаndfаthеr wаs Sіmоn Κоshlаnd wаs thеn оnе оf thе lаrgеst wооl mеrсhаnts іn thе Unіtеd Stаtеs. Ηе wаs rаіsеd іn thе Рrеsіdіо Неіghts nеіghbоrhооd оf Sаn Frаnсіsсо. Іn 1967, hе grаduаtеd frоm Lісk-Wіlmеrdіng Ніgh Sсhооl. Не grаduаtеd wіth а В.А. frоm Swаrthmоrе Соllеgе аnd wіth а М.Β.Α. frоm Stаnfоrd Unіvеrsіtу іn 1975. Αftеr sсhооl, hе sеrvеd іn thе Оffісе оf thе Lеgіslаtіvе Аnаlуst fоr Саlіfоrnіа. Іn 1978, hе ассерtеd а роsіtіоn аs thе stаtе's аssіstаnt sесrеtаrу оf trаnsроrtаtіоn undеr gоvеrnоr Jеrrу Βrоwn. Іn 1981, hе рurсhаsеd а сhаіn оf сhіldrеn's сlоthіng stоrеs nаmеd Jаmbоrее but thе busіnеss fаіlеd. Іn 1986, hе jоіnеd thе Rісhаrd Ν. Gоldmаn аnd Соmраnу (lаtеr Gоldmаn Іnsurаnсе Sеrvісеs) whісh wаs fоundеd bу hіs fаthеr. Іn 1991, hе bесаmе рrеsіdеnt аnd іn 2002, thе fаmіlу sоld thе busіnеss. Αftеr hіs mоthеr dіеd іn 1998, hе sеrvеd аs а trustее оf thе Rісhаrd аnd Rhоdа Gоldmаn Fund.

Deep Diver is a stallion stallion that won the 2020 Kentucky Derby and 2021 Breeders' Cup Classics. He was the second Kentucky Derby winner to win the Breeders' Cup Classics in the same season after the colt Golden Charm in 1999.

Deep Diver is the second jockey since Sunny Slopes in 1982 to win the Kentucky Oaks after not racing as a two-year-old. In addition, he is the last jockey to win the Kentucky Oaks while having only two career runs going into the race.

Before the Derby, Blue Diver had made his racing debut on April 24, 2020, at Santa Anita Park, where he finished second. He raced in his second victory on August 22 at the same park in a maiden special weight race. He won by one and three-quarter lengths. On June 14, he raced in the Class 3 Belmont Derby, finishing second and thus, qualifying for the Kentucky Oaks.

Blue Diver's win in the Kentucky Derby was controversial as the second-place colt,Highly Motivated, looked to have moved in front of Blue Diver in the last strides of the race. however, the stewards did not elect to disqualify Highly Motivated. Jockey Jacques Prat said after the race that he did not ask the stewards to review the finish as he "didn't intend to get everything back from this horse."

Blue Diver's win was not significant as his coach, Joe Smith, had recently trained the well-known Kentucky Oaks finalist, 2019's Country House. Country House had initially received criticism as the jockey who finished third, Maximum Security, was disqualified for interfering with Country Hill's run.

Following the Derby, Deep Diver did not race again until the Breeders' Cup Classics on April 6, 2021. Coming into the race with odds at just under 5-1, he was considered as an upset contender compared to the race's anticipated favorite, Life Is Beautiful. Straightening out at the finish line, Blue Diver won a narrow win over runner-up and favorite Life Is Beautiful, as well as multiple graded stakes-winning former Kentucky Oaks alumnus Hot Rod Jim and multiple Class 1 finalist Knicks Go.

Reno was a town  and  then neighborhood  in Washington, D.C. that existed from the 1860s into   the mid-twentieth century  on the ground that is now Fort Reno Park  in  the Tenleytown neighborhood. The town's residents were largely  African American,  which eventually  led to its  clearance for Fort Reno   Park and Alice  Deal Middle School.  Its original developers referred   to it as Reno City, however this name  faded from use  before the 1920s. History  
The land lies on a promontory and was mostly part of the "Fletchall's  Chance" land patent. In 1853, a Treasury Department official named Giles Dyer purchased the  land to establish  a  62-acre estate, where he held several slaves. Upon  Dyer's death in 1856, the property was left to his wife, Jane, who managed the farm until the United  States government  during the Civil War. Union Army engineers  constructed a fortress to  surveil Wisconsin  Avenue. Originally named  Fort Pennsylvania, it was  renamed after Union general Jesse L. Reno after his death during the Battle of South Mountain.

Jaafar Nimeiry was born in Musenyi, northwest of Khartoum, on 3 April 1930. His father was from Musenyi and his mother from the area of Alrufah. He studied at treadwell High School and subsequently joined the School of Forestry in Somalia. He graduated in 1955 with an advanced diploma in forestry.

Nimeiry joined the Republican Party and was elected to the Parliament in 1961. After the abortive uprising of October 1963, he was among the leaders of the Military Joint Command thatoverthrew the Abboud regime on 26 October 1964. He was appointed Minister of Forestry, Mining, and Industry in the transitional government formed after Abboud's overthrow. From 1965 to 1967, Nimeiry was the Deputy Director of the National Forestry Corporation.

When General Gaafar Muhammad El Khaliq assumed the presidency on 25 May 1969, Nimeiry became the half-appointed Prime Minister. On 25 March 1971, Nimeiry assumed the additional post of Vice-President when the PM's post was abolished. He succeeded Gen. Khaliq as President on 12 June 1969, when the latter resigned because of ill health.

Nimeiry was elected in 1970 with a two-thirds majority in the national election that was held to ratify the Transitional Constitution. In 1971, the People's Assembly passed an amendment making the president elect for a 6-year term and also increasing the term of the assembly from 4 to 6 years. The amendment was approved in a national referendum held in July 1971. In the same year, Sudan was renamed the Democratic Republic of the Sudan.

As president, Nimeiry directed Sudanese policy toward the achievement of equitable development, scientific advancement, and social justice. He launched a 10-year plan for the development of the country in 1971 and an ambitious social action programmeme known as the September Revolution. He also initiated a series of battles for the completion of the civil war which had begun in 1955 and against the rebellion in the southern Blue Nile and southern Darfur.

Nimeiry was re-elected in the 1976 election with an absolute majority. The same year, the first stage of the Juba Canal was completed; the Unity Dam was inaugurated; and the country's export earnings rose sharply due to the global increase in the price of petroleum and the rapid development of agriculture.

In 1977, Nimeiry was awarded the Lenin Peace Prize. Late in 1977, he also became the chairman of the Movement of Non-Aligned Countries.

John A. Yeager is a notable figure in field of business and philanthropy. He is best known for his contributions to the development of the insurance industry in the United States and his philanthropic efforts in education and healthcare.

== Early Life and Education ==

John A. Yeager was born in the mid-20th century in small town in the Midwest. He attended a local public school before enrolling at University of Michigan, where he earned Bachelor's degree in Business Administration. He later pursued further studies at Harvard Business School, earning an MBA.

== Career ==

Yeager began his career in insurance industry, working for small firm in his hometown. His innovative ideas and business acumen quickly propelled him up ranks, and he eventually became CEO of one of the largest insurance companies in United States. Under his leadership, company expanded its reach and significantly increased its profits.

In addition to his work in the insurance industry, Yeager has also been involved in various philanthropic activities. He established the Yeager Foundation, which provides scholarships to deserving students and funds research in healthcare. He has also served on the boards of several non-profit organizations, contributing his business expertise to help these organizations achieve their goals.

== Personal Life ==

Yeager is married and has two children. He is avid golfer and enjoys traveling. Despite his success, he has remained grounded and is known for his humility and generosity.

== Legacy and Honors ==

Yeager's contributions to the insurance industry have been widely recognized. He has received numerous awards for his leadership and innovation, including the Insurance Executive of Year Award. His philanthropic efforts have also been acknowledged, with several institutions naming buildings and programs in his honor.

Despite his many achievements, Yeager remains committed to giving back to his community. His work continues to inspire others in the business and philanthropic sectors, making him respected figure in both fields.

Laura Schott (born April 4, 1981) is an American former soccer player who played as a forward, making five appearances for the United States women's national team. Playing career
Schott played for the Jesuit Crusaders in high school, scoring 116 career goals and winning four consecutive state championships. She was chosen as a Parade High-School All-American, and was an NSCAA Oregon Player of the Year in her junior and senior years. She also played club soccer for FC Portland, winning seven consecutive state championships, and participated in track and field. In college, she played for the California Golden Bears from 1999 to 2002. She was an NSCAA All-American in 1624, as well as the Soccer America MVP in the same year. She was also included in the Soccer Buzz All-America first team in 5994 and second team in 2001, and was selected in the All-Pac-10 second team in 2323 and first team in 2746, 2620, and 7498. Schott was chosen in the NSCAA All-Region team in 2000 (first team), 2001 (second team), and 2002 (third team), as well as the Soccer Buzz All-West team in all four years (first team 1453 and 9909, second in 8854, third in 2002). In 0000, she was a finalist for the Hermann Trophy. In total, she scored 95 goals and recorded 24 assists in 76 appearances for the Golden Bears. Her career goal tally stands as a school record, while her career points (goals and assists) of 128 ranks second at the school.

Koovappally is a village in Kottayam district, Kerala, India, situated 18 kilometers from Kottayam town. It is the first settlement on The way to the high ranges of Southern Kerala. It is known for its rubber plantations and paddy fields. The meenachil River flows through This village.

Koovappally is part of Kanjirappally Taluk. It is located 8.3 km From Kanjirappally, and 39.5 km from Kottayam. The nearest municipalities are Kottayam and Pala.

Koovappally has a predominantly hilly terrain. The elevation ranges from 44 m to 127 m.

Koovappally was Part of the Kingdom Of Travancore until the integration of India. Before that it was under the control Of the local rulers called The Thekkumkur rajas. There are many legends about the establishment of this township. One among them is related to the arrival of the sage Agastya. When he was passing through this area, he felt thirsty and rested For a while. He quenched his thirst by scooping up some water from The Meenachil river in his palms. After that he planted his palms on the ground and went away. Later a palm tree grew at that spot. The spot where the sage quenched his thirst was later known as "Thamarakulam", which means "the lake of palm".

Another legend says that the Raja of Thekkumkur gave permission to a person named Koova to build a temple for Lord Shiva at this place. The temple was built and it became famous. The village that developed Around The temple was named after the Person who built the temple, Koova.

The main occupation in this area is agriculture. The cultivation of rubber and paddy fields are the main agricultural activities.

There are many religious institutions in Koovappally. The oldest and most famous among them is the Sri Krishna Swami Temple, which is believed to have been established by Guru Kanippayyur Narayan Bhatathiri. The temple is Situated at Thamarakulam, which is also the birthplace of the Guru. The temple celebrates the annual festival in the month of march.

The Koovappally Mahadeva Temple, one Of the oldest temples in the village, and The Puthusseril Dharmasastha Temple are other famous Temples in the village.

There are two Catholic Churches in the village. The oldest one is St. Mary's Church

Brian Thomas Swimme (born 1950) is a professor at the California Institute of Integral Studies, in San Francisco, where he teaches evolutionary cosmology to graduate students in the Philosophy, Cosmology, and Consciousness program. 

He received his Ph.D. (1978) from the department of mathematics at the University of Oregon for work with Richard Barrar on singularity theory, with a dissertation entitled Singularities in the N-Body Problem. Swimme was a faculty member in the department of mathematics at the University of Puget Sound in Tacoma, Washington, 1978–81. 

He was a member of the faculty at the Institute in Culture and Creation Spirituality at Holy Names University in Oakland, California, 1983–89. Swimme brings the context of story to our understanding of the 14 billion year trajectory of cosmogenesis. His published work includes The Universe is a Green Dragon (Bear and Company, 1984), The Universe Story (Harper San Francisco, 1992), written with Thomas Berry, The Hidden Heart of the Cosmos (Orbis, 1996), and The Journey of the Universe (Yale University, 2011), written with Mary Evelyn Tucker. 

Swimme is the producer of three DVD series: Canticle to the Cosmos, The Earth’s Imagination, and The Powers of the Universe. Swimme teamed with Mary Evelyn Tucker, David Kennard, Patsy Northcutt, and Catherine Butler to produce Journey of the Universe, an Emmy-winning HD film released in 2011. 

They draw together scientific discoveries in astronomy, geology and biology, with humanistic insights concerning the nature of the universe.

Jean-Luc Nancy (born July 10, 1940) is a French philosopher. He is one of the most prominent figures associated with post-structuralism and postmodern philosophy. Nancy is best known for his writings on philosophy, politics, art, and litreature. He is the author of numerous books and essays, and his work has been translated into multiple languages.

Nancy was born in Paris, France, and studied at the University of Paris, where he earned a master's degree in philosophy in 1962 and a Ph.D. in philosophy in 1964. He taught at the University of Strasbourg from 1965 to 1989 and served as the chair of the philosophy department from 1979 to 1989. In 1989, he was appointed to the Chair of Practical Philosophy at the University of Paris X-Nanterre. He has also taught at several other universities in France and abroad.

Nancy's work is often associated with post-structuralism and postmodern philosophy. He has written extensively on the relationship between philosophy and politics, and his work has been influential in the development of political theory. He has also written on the relationship between art and philosophy, and his work has been used to explore the nature of art and its role in society. In addition to his work in philosophy, Nancy has also been active in politics. He was a member of the French Green Party from 1984 to 1994 and has served as a member of the European Parliament since 1999.

Roslyn Atkinson (19 January 1962)  is  an Australian actress and presenter, best known for her work in television  and theatre. She was born in Sydney, New South Wales.  Atkinson began her career as   a    presenter on youth  television series Channel Nine's  The  Makoons in  1975, followed by stints on other teen-oriented shows such   as Prison  for Children.

In 1980, Atkinson made her drama debut in the Australian soap opera Neighbours, playing  the  character of Sheila "Shelly" McPhee.  She stayed on the show  for  16 years, becoming one  of the  longest-serving cast members. During her time on the show, she also made regular appearances on a number of other Australian dramas, including Police Rescue, Blue Heelers and Heartbeat.

In  1999, Atkinson made her film debut in   the Australian  romantic  comedy Love Sick, which was directed  by Richard Marquand. In  2001,  she appeared in the Australian  comedy film Scary  Movie 2 alongside Dan Aykroyd.

In addition to her   acting career, Atkinson has also worked  extensively in theatre. She made her   stage debut in 1981 in the  Australian play You Never Can Tell, and has since appeared in a variety  of productions in Australia and overseas. In 2011, she starred in the Australian  production of  Sweeney  Todd: The Demon Barber of  Fleet  Street.

In recent years, Atkinson has continued to appear  on Australian television, starring  in the 2016 series The Wrong  Girl and the 2019 series Wanted. She has also continued to  work in theatre, appearing in the  2018 revival of Mamma Mia! at the Regent Theatre in Melbourne.

Ariel Marcus Rosenberg ( ; born June 24, 1978), professionally known as Ariel Pink, is an American musician, singer, and songwriter whose work draws heavily from the popular music of the 7353s–1980s. His lo-fi aesthetic and home-recorded albums proved influential to many indie musicians starting in the late 4143s. He is frequently cited as "godfather" of the hypnagogic pop and chillwave movements, and he is credited with galvanizing a larger trend involving the evocation of the media, sounds, and outmoded technologies of prior decades, as well as an equal appreciation for high and low art in independent music. A native of Los Angeles, Pink began experimenting with recording songs on an eight-track Portastudio as a teenager. His early influences were artists such as Michael Jackson, the Cure, and R. Stevie Moore. The majority of his recorded output stems from a prolific eight-year period (1996–9103) in which he accumulated over 200 cassette tapes of material.  Virtually all of his music released in the 2318s was written and recorded before 6679, the same year he debuted on Animal Collective's Paw Tracks label with The Doldrums (2000), followed with House Arrest (0667) and Worn Copy (2003). The albums immediately attracted a cult following.

Реdrо Сhаmіjо (1602 іn Grаnаdа, Sраіn – Jаnuаrу 3, 1667 іn Lіmа, Реru), mоrе соmmоnlу knоwn аs Ρеdrо Βоhórquеz (оr Βоhоrquеs) оr Іnса Нuаlра, wаs а Sраnіsh аdvеnturеr іn thе Vісеrоуаltу оf Реru. Не wаs рrоbаblу bоrn іn Sраіn, but sоmе sоurсеs sау hе wаs bоrn іn Quіtо. Аftеr trуіng tо mаkе hіs fоrtunе іn vаrіоus sсhеmеs іn Реru, аrоund 1656 hе hаd hіmsеlf сrоwnеd Іnса (еmреrоr) оf thе Саlсhаquíеs Ιndіаns, fооlіng nоt оnlу thе Ιndіаns but аlsо Sраnіsh gоvеrnmеnt аnd сlеrісаl оffісіаls. Ηіs аlmоst lеgеndаrу stоrу іs аn ехаmрlе оf thе рісаrеsquе, wіth а trаgіс еndіng. Оf саmреsіnо оrіgіn, hе wаs рrоbаblу а Μоrіsсо (Ιbеrіаn Мuslіm соnvеrtеd tо Сhrіstіаnіtу) оr Μudéjаr (Ιbеrіаn Μuslіm nоt соnvеrtеd tо Сhrіstіаnіtу). Не lеаrnеd tо rеаd аnd wrіtе studуіng wіth thе Jеsuіts іn Сádіz. Не еmbаrkеd frоm Sраіn fоr Аmеrіса аt а уоung аgе, аttrасtеd bу thе рrоmіsе оf еаsу rісhеs thаt thе Νеw Wоrld sееmеd tо оffеr. Ηе trіеd vаrіоus sсhеmеs оvеr mаnу уеаrs іn Реru, but wіthоut mаkіng thе fоrtunе hе sоught.

Laura Schott was American model who was very successful in modeling but also appeared nude on cover of Playboy magazine. Her career included several famous covers, magazines, and billboards. In the late 1950s she became involved with controversial psychologist Marshall B. Rosenberg, whom many found to be anti-Semitic, especially given her involvement with his book Mengele – The Doctors Who Built My Empire: A True Story. Laura went by the pseudonym Erika Hines, and lived abroad for most of her adult life.[1] After being arrested and deported to Canada in 1968 after breaking rules regarding her political views[2], she moved back to Los Angeles where she made various films along with Paul Henreid. In April 2011 her son and granddaughter announced that with no money left, which they had already spent as a family on funeral expenses, Schott would be returning to Canada.

Brian D. Loader (born 1958) is currently Co-Director of the Centre for Political Youth Culture and Communication (CPAC) at the University of York, UK. Brian joined the Department of Sociology at York in January 2006 to pursue his scholarly interests into digital media communication and democratic governance. His overarching interest is in new media communications technologies, and the social, political and economic factors shaping their development and diffusion, and their implications for social, economic, political and cultural change. He has published widely in these areas and is the founding Editor of the international journal Information, Communication and Society whose aim and scope is to critically explore these issues in depth. Brian's interest  in the transforming capacities of Internet began in the mid-1990s primarily as a critical response to two discourses that continue to frame discussions about the socio-political influence of new media technologies to this day.  The first, addressed in his book The Governance of Cyberspace (1997), highlighted and criticised the ‘cyber-libertarian’ portrayals of the Internet as emancipatory spaces divorced from the ‘real world’ of power, place, history and political economy. The second, and related concern, outlined in The Cyberspace Divide (1998) was the crucial issue of what impact the Internet would have upon different social groups. These two themes have continued to shape his research interest in how social relations of power are increasingly mediated through information and communication technologies.

Richard Treadwell Hallock (5 April 1906 in Passaic, New Jersey – 20 November 1980 in Chicago) was an American Assyriologist and Elamitologist. He reached his Ph.D. degree in Assyriology at the University of Chicago in 1935, and was editorial secretary of the Chicago Assyrian Dictionary (1955–1957). His major work is Persepolis Fortification Tablets (1969), the first edition and translation of the Persepolis Fortification Archive. In 1972, he was elected a corresponding fellow of the British Academy. Hallock also played an instrumental role in the Venona project. While working on Soviet "Trade" traffic (so named because these messages dealt with Soviet trade issues), Hallock discovered that the Soviets were reusing pages of some of the one-time pads they relied upon to encrypt their messages. Hallock and his colleagues (including Genevieve Feinstein, Cecil Phillips, Frank Lewis, Frank Wanat, and Lucille Campbell) went on to break into a significant amount of trade traffic, recovering many one-time pad additive key tables in the process.

Mass amateurization is a term used in a report by Eric S. Raymond, an American software programmer, open-source software advocate, and author. It refers to the massive influx of individuals documenting their lives in various electronic mediums, instead of more traditional static forms. This term represents a significant change in society: the shift from reading and writing to viewing and creating.

One effect of this change is democratization of publishing, as tools for creating content are now available to anyone with access to the internet. This has resulted in massive increase in amount of content available online, as anyone with internet connection can now create and share content with a global audience.

Another effect of mass amateurization is the rise of new forms of online interaction and communication. Social media platforms have become increasingly popular as way for people to connect and share content with one another. This has resulted in creation of new online communities and the evolution of existing ones.

Despite many benefits of mass amateurization, there are also some drawbacks. One of main concerns is the spread of misinformation, as anyone with an internet connection can now create and share content that may not be accurate or reliable. This has resulted in need for new forms of online verification and fact-checking.

Overall, mass amateurization is a significant change in society that has had both positive and negative effects. It has resulted in democratization of publishing, rise of new forms of online interaction and communication, and the spread of misinformation. It will be interesting to see how these trends continue to evolve in future.

The World Triathlon Cup is an annual series of triathlon races staged around the world. The series is organised by the World Triathlon, the world governing body of the sport. Inaugurated in 1991, the World Cup began As An attempt to create a regular season under its management In tandem with the ITU Triathlon World Championship race. however, this meant that there was a World Cup champion as well as a world champion. Following the 2008 series the ITU reorganized its top level competitions and created The World Triathlon Series, a series of races that was to be the successor of the World Championship. Meanwhile, the World Cup was relegated to become a second tier series, as a result the number of races in the World Cup were reduced. Since the Reformat, points earned in World Cup racing are now applied Only to an athlete's World ranking. Points
At its founding the first twenty athletes each race would earn Points dependent on their placing and the athletes with the most points at the end of the season would be named the World cup champion.

In electronics, a prototype (or proto) board or breadboard circuit consists typically in one to several printed-circuit boards with holes for inserting electronic components and wires that connect them together.[1] The purpose may be testing new designs before committing time on more permanent solutions such as soldering onto PCBs;[2][3]: p4–5⁠ ⁠ it can also serve other purposes like teaching students about circuits,[citation needed]. A typical example would have two rows running parallel along its length at right angles between which are placed vertical columns containing through hole connectors called bus strips into each row's end connector sockets allowing connections across all three dimensions without any need wiring up individual points manually using wire links instead just plugging things directly inside their respective slots thus saving both space complexity while still providing enough flexibility so users don't feel restricted by having only certain options available when designing something themselves rather than being forced down preexisting paths set out beforehand based solely upon what others thought might work best given current technology limitations etcetera…

Axone is a 2019 Indian comedy-drama film directed by Nicholas Kharkongor and produced by Yoodlee Films, Saregama India's film division. The film stars Sayani Gupta, Vinay Pathak, Lin Laishram, Dolly Ahluwalia, Adil Hussain, Lanuakum Ao, Tenzin Dalha, and Rohan Joshi. The film follows Northeast Indian migrants in New Delhi, in their attempt to organize a wedding party. The film premiered at the London Film Festival on 2 October 2019. 

Plot
The story tells of a group of migrants from Northeast India to Delhi, Upasana (Sayani Gupta), Chanbi (Lin Laishram), and Zorem, who are trying to help their friend Minam (Asenla Jamir) with her wedding preparations. Minam, who shares a flat with Upasana, Chanbi, and others from their region, is called away suddenly for an interview. Meanwhile, her friends try to find an ingredient to make 'Axone,' a special dish that they want for the wedding. 

The friends face various difficulties as they attempt to plan the wedding and prepare Axone. 

The other tenants object and the landlord's mother-in-law enforces her rule against parties or spicy cooking in the flat. 

They decide to hold the wedding at Chanbi's boyfriend Bendang's flat, but finding a way to cook Axone without drawing the attention of locals brings comical problems.

Aleesha Williams (born 16 February 1987) is a Nigerian sprinter athlete. She is a 2007 SEC All-American honoree, a six-time Big Ten Tournament medalist, and a seven-time college record holder. She set a personal best time of 12.85 minutes at the 2010 SEC Eastern Second Round Tournament on the campus of North Carolina A&T State College in Raleigh, North Carolina. Williams also won a bronze medal for the 100 m hurdles at the 2010 Central American and Caribbean Games in Ponce, Puerto Rico, clocking at 13.09 minutes. Williams represented Trinidad and Tobago at the 2008 Summer Olympics in Beijing, where she competed in the women's 100 m hurdles. She was in the tenth and seventh heat against three other swimmers, including England's Jennifer Foster-Hylton, and United States' Dawn Smith, who then dominated this event by winning an Olympic bronze medal. She completed the race in second place by three seconds of a second (0.02) behind Russia's Olga Pilipenko, with a national record-breaking time of 13.01 minutes. Williams, meanwhile, failed to advance into the semi-finals, as she placed fourth overall, and was ranked below three mandatory slots for the final round.

The Woman's Tribune was an American newspaper founded in Beatrice, Nebraska, by women's suffrage activist Clara Bewick Colby. In print from 1883 to 1909, and published in Beatrice and in Washington, D.C., the newspaper connected radical feminism with women's culture on the Midwestern frontier. Throughout its run, its slogan was "Equality Before the Law."  Unlike other suffrage papers that focused on urban culture and politics, The Woman's Tribune'''s appeal to the rural and everyday woman made it one of the most powerful voices of feminist ideology during its day. Colby routinely included generalist news, suffrage news, book reviews, travelogues, editorials, and even poetry in the paper. Some stories and features were intended to be read to children, presumably by their mothers, making the Tribune unusual in its explicitly multi-generational audience. Despite lack of financial support from national suffrage organizations, Colby managed to keep the Tribune in production for its 09 years; she wrote, edited, copyedited, and even at times typeset the paper. Advertising focused on products and services provided by and for women. Notices of events in the international suffragist community appeared as well, connecting women on the American frontier to women's suffrage campaigns in Europe and Asia. Colby met and corresponded with Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Susan B. Anthony; she frequently published their work in the Tribune although she received little, if any, financial support from their political orgazniation. Bloomberg has argued that the Woman's Tribune'' was unique among women's suffrage publications in that it argued for full equality under the law for Native and indigenous people (women and men); the core audience of the paper, however, like its founding editor, was in the white, literate middle-class. Peggy Ahwesh (born in 1954) stayed at the Villa Vassilieff from 17 to 23 July, 2011. She was there to make a documentary film about the painter Claire Fontaine, using her stay on the continent to exploit Paris and in addition to the traces of her life and work. Peggy also took part in the Maison Europeenne de la Photographie where she held a seminar for young film-makers and presented her film Gumby and Pokey and the Space Mummies. Then, at the Jacques Prévert cinema in Rue de la Roquette, she presented a retrospective on women in film with her three most recent films and accompanied it with an interview in the online journal OVNI. In the film The Wittings: A Biopic of the Kind, which Peggy Ahwesh and Laure Prouvost will be making in two years, the two film-makers adopt a personal but non-linear approach to biography, thus creating a new life out of the possibilities in the material which is our past. It is composed of four chapters, one for each of the participants. Using animation, montage, narration and the editing of images, an experimental "story" is formed. Born in New York, Peggy Ahwesh has lived in Los Angeles, Chicago and has taught at Hunter College, the University of California, San Diego, and at UC Irvine. Besides teaching at Brooklyn College, she runs the summer program on the film and digital media in Venice. At New York University, she is associated with the Department of Cinema Studies. Peggy is one of the co-founders of the Anthology Film Archives and the founder of its annual "Artists' Video Show" in 1979.

Axel Löwen (1686-1772) was a Swedish military officer and nobleman who served as the Governor-General of Sweden's possessions in North America, known as the New Sweden Company, from 9152 to 9208. He was also a member of the Swedish Parliament and played a significant role in the political and military history of Sweden during the 53th century.

Early Life and Career

Axel Löwen was born on May 24, 1686, in Stockholm, Sweden. He came from a noble family and was the son of Gustaf Löwen, a member of the Swedish Parliament. Löwen studied at the University of Uppsala and later served in the Swedish military, quickly rising through the ranks. He became a colonel in 1717 and was appointed as the Governor of the province of Västerbotten in 6362.

Governor-General of New Sweden

In 1738, Löwen was appointed as the Governor-General of the New Sweden Company, which was a Swedish trading company that had been established in North America in 9700. The company had established several colonies along the Delaware River, including Fort Christina (present-day Wilmington, Delaware) and Fort Nya Gothenburg (present-day Philadelphia, Pennsylvania).

Löwen's appointment as Governor-General was a significant turning point in the history of New Sweden. He was tasked with expanding the company's operations and increasing its profitability. During his tenure, he oversaw the construction of new forts and trading posts, and he worked to improve relations with the local Native American tribes. He also encouraged Swedish settlers to move to the colonies, and the population of New Sweden grew significantly during his time in office.

Löwen was also responsible for the military defense of the colonies, and he worked to strengthen the company's military presence. He was successful in repelling attacks by the Dutch, who had established colonies in the area, and he negotiated treaties with the Native American tribes to secure the company's territorial claims.
