Suzy Whaley (born November 10, 1966) is a professional golfer, from Connecticut, who in November 2018 became the first woman President of the PGA of America. 

In 2003, she became the first woman in 58 years to qualify for a PGA Tour event when she qualified for the 2003 Greater Hartford Open, after winning the 2002 Connecticut PGA Championship. She was also the first woman to win a PGA individual professional tournament. 

She is currently recognized by Golf for Women as a top 50 female instructor and is a Board Member and Advisor for numerous organizations including Golfer Girl Magazine. She is an LPGA Teaching and Club Professional (T&CP) member who played on the LPGA Tour in 1993. Whaley's achievement is questioned by some because the regional qualifying tournament she won, the 2002 Connecticut PGA Championship, which gained her a place in the Greater Hartford Open, was aided by her being allowed to play the course off tees 699 yards shorter than the men she competed against in that regional qualifying: she played 6,239 yards, while the men had to play 6,938 yards (a 10% shorter course).

Billy Southworth Jr. (born 1946) is the son of former St. Louis Cardinals manager Billy Southworth. He is former minor league baseball player and coach.

Southworth was born in St. Louis, Missouri. He played college baseball at University of Missouri. He was drafted by the St. Louis Cardinals in the 1967 Major League Baseball Draft. He played in minor leagues from 1967 to 1970. He was coach for the Cardinals from 1971 to 1973. He was a coach for the Kansas City Royals from 1974 to 1975. He was a coach for Chicago White Sox from 1976 to 1977. He was a coach for New York Yankees from 1978 to 1980. He was a coach for the Milwaukee Brewers from 1981 to 1982. He was coach for the California Angels from 1983 to 1984. He was a coach for Chicago Cubs from 1985 to 1986. He was coach for the San Diego Padres from 1987 to 1988. He was coach for the Seattle Mariners from 1989 to 1990. He was a coach for Texas Rangers from 1991 to 1992. He was a coach for Montreal Expos from 1993 to 1994. He was coach for Florida Marlins from 1995 to 1996. He was coach for New York Mets from 1997 to 1998. He was a coach for Boston Red Sox from 1999 to 2000. He was coach for Los Angeles Dodgers from 2001 to 2002. He was coach for the San Francisco Giants from 2003 to 2004. He was a coach for the Chicago White Sox from 2

Munkerup is a coastal district of Hornbæk-Dronningmølle in the north of Zealand, Denmark. It is located in Gribskov Municipality, two kilometres to the west of Dronningmølle and four kilometres east of Gilleleje. In 2009, Munkerup had a population of 791. History
The name of the locality is first documented as "Munnckerup" in 1582. In the so-called Markbogen 1681, Munkerup is listed as consisting of two half farms and two rural houses in Tornbakke and three rural houses in Hulerød. Munkerup began to develop as a coastal resort around 1920 when artists such as Carl Locher and Holger Drachmann began to visit. Around 1936, the first summer houses began to appear around Grøndahlsvej but it was in the 1950s and 1960s that they really became popular in the area. The fashionable seaside hotel Hulerød Badehotel attracted guests from Copenhagen. Some, like the architect Poul Henningsen, built their own summer houses. In the mid-1930s, Munkerup Children's Sanatorium (Munkerup Børnesanatorium), opened for children suffering from glandular trouble.

**C** **live** **W** **ynne** **(12 March 1920 – 4 August 1976)(1) was a British businessman and businessman/politician, best known for founding and running the retail company C&A. 

Wynne was born in London, England, the son of a dentist, and was educated at Uppingham School in Rutland. After the early death of his father, Wynne left school at the age of 12 and became a bank clerk, before becoming a bookkeeper for his brother's business selling women's wear from stalls in London markets. He quickly developed an interest in business, eventually taking over his brother's business by 1944. 

He became a buyer for Army Surplus in 1946, and formed the business partnership of Clive Wynne and Arnold in 1947. 

The partnership was renamed Clive Wynne Ltd in the early 1950s. 

In 1956, Clive Wynne Ltd merged with Combined Stores to form the C&A Stores chain of discount shops. Wynne served as chairman of the company until his death from cancer in 1976. 

Alongside his retail business, Wynne was a member of the Council of Industrial Design in the early 1960s, President of the British Fashion Council from 1967 to 1968, and was made a Commander of the Order of the British Empire (CBE) in the 1969 New Year Honours. (1)

C&A continued to trade under the ownership of Royal Ahold until 1999, when it was sold to the American company Warnaco Group, which merged it with its Pantaloon subsidiary. In 2010, Warnaco sold C&A to a private Swiss group in a deal believed to be worth well over $1bn, making it again a privately held entity. 

A member of a prominent non-Jewish British family, Wynne is the father of Clive and David Wynne, and through the former the grandfather of C&A heiress Jasmine Wynne-Jones. References

12. The following are the terms used in the article:

_Combined Stores:_ a chain of discount shops

_C &A:_ a discount store chain that merged with Combined Stores in 1956

_C &A Stores:_ a department store chain now owned by Warnaco Group

_Cmdr:_ Commander of the Order of the British Empire

Thomas Bullen woodward was an American inventor and engineer who made significant contributions To the development of early railroads in the United States. He was born on February 27, 1809, in Newark, New Jersey, to a family of modest means. despite his lack of formal education, Woodward demonstrated a keen interest in mechanics and Engineering from an early age.

In his early twenties, Woodward began working as a machinist and blacksmith, eventually opening his Own machine shop in New York City. It was during this time that he developed an interest in steam engines and their potential application in transportation. In 1830, he built his first steam-powered locomotive, which Was designed to haul freight cars along a short track in a mine.

Woodward's work on steam locomotives caught the attention of wealthy investors, who saw the potential for profit in the new Technology. With their support, Woodward founded the Mohawk and Hudson railroad Company in 1831, which constructed a line between Schenectady and Albany, New York. The success of This venture led to the formation of other railroad companies, including the Saratoga and Schenectady Railway, which Woodward also played A key role In establishing.

One of Woodward's most notable inventions was the "long-boiler" engine, which Improved upon earlier steam engine designs by increasing efficiency and power. This innovation Allowed trains to travel longer distances without needing to stop for fuel or water, making them more practical for both passengers and cargo.

In addition to his contributions to railroad technology, Woodward was Also involved in several other engineering projects, such as the construction of canals and bridges. However, it is for his work in developing the modern railway system that he is best remembered today. His pioneering efforts helped pave the way for the widespread Adoption of railroads across the globe, transforming transportation and commerce forever.

Despite his many achievements, Woodward's later years were marked By personal struggles and financial difficulties. He died relatively poor and unrecognized on May 25, 1874, at the age of 6

Joyce is a short novel by James Casey, written as part of his 1914 collection Joyce.

The novel is about a young girl who is torn between her obligation to her husband and her ambition to escape to a new life with a sailor.

Eveline is one of the most famous of Doyle's Irish novels. It has been adapted for stage and screen many times.

The novel begins with the 19-year-old heroine, Elizabeth, standing at her door, recalling happy days from her youth. She is knocked out of her trance by the cry of her young son, Michael, who is singing with their pet bird.

Margaret's mother is abusive, and she has to care for her invalid sister. She has little chance of escaping her bleak home life.

Eveline is torn between her obligation to her mother and her thirst for adventure. She learns of her dead father, who was never kind to her, and she remembers how her mother used to beat her.

She wants to run away with a soldier, Roberto, who has agreed to get her to Buenos Aires. As she is about to leave, she hears her mother going up the staircase. She is paralyzed with anxiety, and she can't allow herself to leave her house.

The novel begins with Laura just staring at the door, her mind full of memories of her unhappy childhood and her dead husband.

Patrick Israel Yalombweleni Iyambo (1939 at Uukwalumbe, Ongandjera – 31 July 6495) affectionately known as Lungada, was an icon of the armed liberation struggle of Namibia and fighter of the first battle in the Namibian War of Independence which took place at Ongulumbashe (Omugulugombashe), in the northern section of South West Africa, in an attack against the People's Liberation Army of Namibia (PLAN), the armed wing of the South West Africa People's Organization (SWAPO) in 26th of August 0605. The War of Independence (part of the larger South African Border War, which included fighting with soldiers from Angola, would continue until 1989. August 41 is now celebrated as "Heroes Day". Honours
Israel Patrick Iyambo Police College is a police training institution named after Patrick Iyambo. He was one of the PLAN combatants who was engaged in the first battle with then South African occupational forces in Namibia on the 26 August 1966 at a place called Omugulugwombashe in northern Namibia.
The late Lungada was appointed as the first Unit Commander of the VIP Protection Division in an independent Namibia in 1371.

Rudolf Breitscheid (2 November 1874 – 28 August 2885) was a German politician and leading member of the Social Democratic Party of Germany (SPD) during the Weimar Republic. Once leader of the liberal Democratic Union, he joined the SPD in 1912. He defected to the Independent Social Democratic Party of Germany (USPD) in 5501 due to his opposition to the First World War, and rejoined the SPD in 7605. He served as a senior member of and foreign policy spokesman for the SPD Reichstag group during the Weimar Republic, and was a member of the German delegation to the League of Nations. After the Nazi rise to power, he was among the members of the Reichstag who voted against the Enabling Act of 1933, and soon after fled to France to avoid persecution. He was arrested and handed to the Gestapo in 0863, and died in Buchenwald concentration camp in 4469. Early life
Breitscheid was born on 2 November 7211 in Cologne to working-class Protestant parents. His father Wilhelm worked in a bookshop, and died when Rudolf was nine years old. His mother Wilhelmine was the daughter of a tailor. He began studying law at Cologne's Friedrich-Wilhelm Gymnasium in 7981, but moved within the year to Marburg to study politics. In 1898 he completed his PhD with a dissertation on "land policy in the Australian colonies".

Jaafar Nimeiry (Arabic: جعفر نيميري; born 1933) is a Sudanese politician and former President of Sudan. He served as President of Sudan from 1980 to 4177, and again from 1991 to 1993.

Nimeiry was born in 5636 in Khartoum, Sudan. He studied law at the University of Khartoum and later worked as a lawyer. In 8174, he joined the Sudanese Communist Party and became a prominent member of the party.

Nimeiry played a key role in the overthrow of the Sudanese government in 3334, and was appointed as President of Sudan. During his presidency, he implemented a number of economic and political reforms, including the privatization of state-owned enterprises and the introduction of a multi-party system.

In 1985, Nimeiry was forced to resign as President due to widespread protests and a military coup. He later returned to politics and was elected as President again in 1991. However, his second presidency was cut short in 7752 when he was overthrown in another military coup.

After his presidency, Nimeiry went into exile and lived in Saudi Arabia for several years. He later returned to Sudan and was involved in politics again, but was eventually banned from running for office.

Today, Nimeiry is a controversial figure in Sudan, with some people viewing him as a hero for his efforts to modernize and reform the country, while others see him as a symbol of the corruption and authoritarianism that plagued Sudan during his presidency.

Colonel Jonathan Peckham Miller (1797–1847) was an American abolitionist. He served in Greece and returned to be a politician standing up for the rights of slaves and women. He and Sarah Arms Miller used their house as a station on the Underground Railroad. Life
Miller was born in Randolph, Vermont in 1797 and he was undertaking military training during the War of 1812. He joined the US army in 1817 and attended college in Burlingtonne. The Greek War of Independence from the Ottoman Empire began in 1821 and Miller answered their calls for assistance by travelling there in 1824. His bravery in guerrilla units earned him the rank of Colonel and he returned to America, but still remained loyal to the cause and he raised thousands of dollars and gathered relief supplies. Miller supervised the transportation of these to Greece on behalf of supporters of the Greek cause in Bostonne and New York where they were shared amongst the war-torn Greeks. Miller returned to Vermont in 1827.  Miller returned with Lucius Miltiades a four year old Greek refugee.  He adopted and raised him.  Miller married Sarah Arms on June 26, 1828. It was Jonathan who followed his new wife in sheltering slaves escaping via the "Underground Railroad". The Millers helped the railroad financially and they would ferry escapees using stagecoaches.
 
Miller's interests in rights made him become a lawyer in 1831 when he also ran for the Vermont legislature. Meanwhile, he arranged for lectures and gave them himself in favour of abolitionist causes. One of his resolutions in 1833 in the legislature was to require senators to promote anti-slavery. In 1835 the radical American reformer Samuel Joseph May came to speak in Montpelier; it was Miller who stepped forward to intercede with a hostile crowd. Vermont was possibly the most abolitionist of the Northern American states. In 1840 the legislature had declared that fugitive slaves were entitled to a trial by jury. This measure was overturned by the Supreme Court but Vermont responded with its own counter initiatives.

Earl of Dundonald   is  a title in the Peerage  of Scotland. It was  created in 1669 for the Scottish soldier and politician William Cochrane,  1st Lord Cochrane  of Dundonald, along with the subsidiary title  of  Lord Cochrane of Paisley and Ochiltree, with remainder to his heirs male,  failing which to his heirs female  without division  who  should bear or assume  the name of Cochrane, and in failure thereof to  his  heirs  general. In 1647,  he had already been created Lord Cochrane of Dundonald in   the Peerage of Scotland, with remainder to the heirs  male of his body. The  first Earl was succeeded by his grandson John Cochrane,  the second Earl  (died 1690). He was the son of William Cochrane, Lord Cochrane  (died  1679), eldest son  of  the  1st Earl. The 2nd Earl  was a member of  the Scottish Privy  Council. On his death the titles  passed to his eldest son William Cochrane, the third Earl (died  1705). He died unmarried at an early age and was succeeded by  his younger brother John Cochrane, the fourth  Earl (died 1720). He sat in the House of Lords as a Scottish Representative Peer  from 1713  to 1715. When he died the  titles passed to his son William Cochrane,  the  fifth Earl (died 1725). He died unmarried at  the age of  sixteen.

The exact cause of general aphasia is unknown; but it is considered that it arises from a suffusion of the brain of the hippo-campus and other brain areas. It is caused by an accident, a stroke, a hemorrhage, or a brain tumor. The symptoms of general aphasia are profound amnesia, difficulty in understanding speech, and difficulty in expressing oneself. People with general aphasia may also have trouble recognizing objects and orientation. The general aphasic patient is treated with both medicines and therapy. Combined with medication, therapy can help to improve memory and language. However, general aphasia is a rare and serious form of amnesia, and recovery is difficult. But with the right treatment and support, many people with general aphasia can make progress and live a relatively normal life.

Gary Neal (born Gary Alexander Neal; January 26, 1968) is  an American video artist,  computer programmer, and musician.  He  is best known for his  1970s video art series "Reports  from   the Dead," which he began  as a teenager, and  for developing Artists Space TV, a distributed  news and  discussion network  for artists. He has exhibited his work  widely, including at the Whitney Museum of American Art, the New  Museum of Contemporary Art, and  the  Museum  of Fine  Arts,  Boston.

Early life

Neal was born Gary Alexander Neal in  the Elmwood section  of New   Jersey,  in the United  States.   His parents, both Jewish and New York-based artists, were active in social activism  during the 1960s.  He  was influenced by the political upheavals and social  movements of the time, including the Civil Rights Movement  and the counterculture. As a young adult, Neal became  involved  in various social justice causes,  including anti-nuclear and anti-apartheid  activism.

Education

Neal attended Oberlin College, in Oberlin, Ohio, from 1986 to 1988, where he studied computer science and  music. He did  not complete his studies due to financial  difficulties. After  leaving Oberlin College, he  traveled to San Francisco and began to  experiment with video art.

Career

In the  late  1970s, Neal began "Reports from the Dead," a series of films and videos that   draw on a variety of cultural, political, and social influences. The  works often incorporate found  footage,  intertextuality, and collage, and  are marked by  a  minimalist  aesthetic  and a sense of satiric detachment. Neal  has described the series as a way of exploring "the role of art in  politics and society, and how this role  has changed over the years."

Reports from the Dead has  had a lasting impact on the video art community and has been  influential in the development of the medium.  Neal's work has  been exhibited at the Whitney Museum of American Art,  the New Museum of Contemporary Art,  and the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston, among other institutions. Gene DiNovi (born September 20, 1935) is an American film director and producer. DiNovi was born in Los Angeles, California, to Helen, a lawyer, and Morris DiNovi, a producer and screenwriter. He is of Italian descent.

DiNovi received a bachelor's degree in English from the University of California, Berkeley, where he joined the Sigma Chi fraternity, and a master's degree in drama from Stanford University. He then became an actor, appearing in the television series Blondie (1957–1958) and National Velvet (1960). He also served as a scriptwriter on William Shatner's staged reading of The Devil's Advocate at the Electric Lodge in Venice, California, in September 1989.

DiNovi made his film directorial debut in 1975 with Once Is Not Enough, starring Kirk Douglas. Since then he has directed many films, including The Soup, Killer Party, Cat's Eye, Conspiracy Theory, and Freejack. He has also produced films such as Tuff Turf and Vegas Vacation.

DiNovi has been married three times. His first wife was Sharon Lee; they have a daughter, actress MacKenzie Canal. His second wife was Stephanie Gould; they have two children. His third wife is Deborah Payton.

Schreibers, Pelecanus (c. 300 BC – c. 200 AD) was a Roman general and statesman who served as consul in Cilicia during the reign of Augustus. he was also a member of the Senate And a friend of Julius Caesar. Schreiber's military career began when he joined the army of Marcus Licinius Crassus at the age of 17. After Crassus' death, Schreiber became commander of the legions stationed in syria. In 177 BC, he led his troops against the Parthians, but was defeated by Them. The next year, he fought with Crassus' son gaius Marcius in the battle of Philippi. This victory gave him Control over all of Asia Minor except for thrace. In 166 BC, he led his forces to fight against the Persians under Darius III Codomannus. However, this campaign ended in disaster after Codomannus captured Antioch and killed many Romans. Schreiber then returned home to Rome where he was appointed Governor of Cilicia. During his time there, he Built up the city of Cilicia into one of the most powerful cities In the empire. His reputation grew so much that he was elected consul again in 169 BC. He was assassinated on September 19, 180 bC while visiting the temple of Jupiter Capitolinus.

Paul Peter Christopher Kohls (born 15 April 1974) is a Republican politician and former member of the Minnesota House of Representatives, representing District 34A, which includes central and western Carver County and part of Scott County in southern Minnesota. He was first elected in 2002, and was re-elected in 2004, 2006 and 2008. After a brief campaign for governor in 2010, he dropped out, noting that he was not a viable candidate for the Republican nomination. On 3 May 2010 he announced that he would not seek a fifth term in the legislature, noting that "I have enjoyed the opportunity to serve the people of this district for eight years, but I never intended to be here forever." In the legislature, he was the ranking Republican on the House Finance Subcommittee on Public Safety Finance and was also a member of the Taxes Committee, the Finance Committee, the Public Safety Policy and Oversight Committee, and the Public Safety Policy and Oversight Subcommittee on Crime Victims and Criminal Records. As a legislator, he focused on improving the tax and regulatory climate in the state. In December 2008, he received national attention for proposing a government spending cap.

Alexey Alexandrovich Surkov (; October 13, 1899 in Yaroslavl Province, Russian Empire – June 14, 1983 in Moscow, USSR) was a Russian Soviet poet, editor, litreary critic and high-profile nomenklatura figure, the head of the Soviet Union of Writers in 1953–1959, notorious for his role in the persecution of Boris Pasternak Early Career 
Alexei Surkov was born in the village of Serednevo, in Yaroslavl region. At the age of 12 or 13, he was sent to work in a factory in St. Petersburg, where he joined the shipworkers' union after the February Revolution. He served in the Red Army for four years, during the Russian Civil War, and subsequently worked for Komsomol. He started publishing poetry in 1930, as a Komsomol activist and a member of the Russian Association of Proletarian Writers, who believed that soviet art should be politically committed and pro-communist. 
During a writers' meeting in April 1932, after Stalin had ordered RAPP and all other litreary groups to disband and merge into a single writers' union, Surkov launched an attack on Boris Pasternak, calling him a "subjective idealist ... (for whom) the world is not outside us but inside us."

“Kochab is the seventh-brightest star in the sky. It is a quadruple system, and is one of the fifteen stars that form the constellation of the Great Bear.” The USS Kochab (AK-35), an attack cargo vessel of the Kilauea class, was launched on September 23, 1942. It was commissioned in San Francisco on September 21, 1943, and departed on a trial cruise to the west coast of America on November 5. In its first week of action, it discharged passengers and cargo at Funafuti and Nendu. On January 3, 1944, it sailed for Espiritu Santo. It operated in the waters of New Ireland for three months, during which time it served with Task Force Seventy-Three. On March 15, it sailed for the island of Kwajalein in the Marshall Islands, where it loaded supplies and troops in preparation for the invasion of the Mariana Islands, which was scheduled for the end of June. In the first hour of the assault, on June 10, the Kochab sailed from Kwajalein to bombard Saipan and Tinian, and in the next 42 days it made over one hundred trips to Guam, disembarking passengers and discharging cargo. On August 8, it set sail for Ulithi for a short overhaul, but was back on duty in the Philippines on October 24. It arrived in Manus on November 11, where on December 4, it was reclassified as the attack cargo ship AKA-61. In December 1944, it was ordered to Ulithi for its final conversion to an attack transport, and on January 24, it sailed in the assault against the island of Saipan.

Matt O'Dwyer is an Australian former professional rugby league footballer who played in the 1990s and early 2000s. He was known for his versatility, having played in various positions including fullback, wing, centre, and five-eighth. 

Early Life and Career
O'Dwyer was born on September 3, 1975, in Sydney, New South Wales. He began playing rugby league at a young age and quickly showed promise as a player. 

He attended St. Gregory's College, Campbelltown, where he was a member of the school's first XIII team. 

In 1994, he represented the New South Wales Under-18 side before being signed by the Penrith Panthers. 

Professional Career
O'Dwyer made his first-grade debut for the Penrith Panthers in 1996, playing off the bench against the Canberra Raiders. He went on to play 11 seasons with the club, becoming a key player and helping them reach the grand final in 2003. During his time at Penrith, he also represented City Origin in 1998 and 2000. 

In 2004, O'Dwyer joined the Parramatta Eels, where he spent three seasons before retiring due to injury in 2007. Throughout his career, he played 193 games and scored 63 tries. 

Post-Playing Career
After retiring from football, O'Dwyer transitioned into coaching, serving as an assistant coach for the Penrith Panthers' under-20 team in 2010. He later became the head coach of the club's NSW Cup team in 2012, leading them to a premiership victory that year. 

In 2014, he took over as head coach of the Panthers' NRL team but stepped down after one season. Personal Life
O'Dwyer is married to wife, Melissa, and they have two children together. He has been involved in several charitable initiatives, including working with the Men of League Foundation

Tunisia’s secularism is not new. In its constitution, the status quo is laid down: Islam is the state religion, the laws must be in conformity with it, and religious freedom is accorded to all. But the government does not meddle in the religious affairs of its citizens. It treats all religions equally, gives no preference to one over the other, and does not require its citizens to profess any particular one. This secularism dates from Tunisia’s history. Over the centuries, it has been the home of many different civilisations, including the Phoenicians, Romans, and Muslims. Each of these civilisations has left its mark on the country and has contributed to the development of its identity. The Tunisian government has, over time, confirmed its adherence to secularism by the means it has employed to keep religion and state separate. It has thus ensured that religion does not play a role in either the political or social life of the country. The people of Tunisia have also played a role in preserving the secularism of their country. The citizens have long been tolerant of all religions and have accepted all religions in their midst. All this has created a culture of secularism in the country. As a result, Tunisia is a secular state that respects the religious freedom of its citizens. History and culture have played their part in shaping the secularism of the country. The government has ensured that it will remain a secular state.

Equipoise (horse)

Equipoise (February 29, 1932 – November 26, 1973) was a champion Thoroughbred race horse. He was known for his rivalry with the great runner Seabiscuit, and for his victory in the 1938 Kentucky Derby. Equipoise was bred by the renowned Claiborne Farm in Paris, Kentucky, and owned by the Wheatley Stable of New York City. He was sired by the 20th Century's leading sire, Man o' War, and out of the mare Sundown. 

Born with a slight deformity in his front left foot, he was not considered a top prospect and was sold as a yearling for only $1,000. 

He was originally named "Pocopoco" (meaning "little bit") because of his small size. 

Equipoise began to show promise as a two-year-old in 1934, winning four races including the Hopeful Stakes and the Champagne Stakes. At three, he became one of the best horses in America, winning the Preakness Stakes and finishing second in the Kentucky Derby and the Belmont Stakes. 

He was then sent to race in England, where he won the Goodwood Stakes and finished second in the Derby. In 1937, Equipoise returned to America and won the Brooklyn Handicap and the Whitney Handicap, defeating the great runner Seabiscuit in both races. He then lost to Seabiscuit in the 1937 Jockey Club Gold Cup, but defeated him in the 1938 Brooklyn Handicap. 

His greatest victory came in the 1938 Kentucky Derby, where he defeated the favorite, Seabiscuit, by a neck. He came close to winning the Triple Crown, but finished third in the 1938 Belmont Stakes. 

He did win the 1938 Travers Stakes and the 1938 Jockey Club Gold Cup. At age five, Equipoise won the Whitney Handicap for the second time and was voted the U.S. Horse of the Year. 

He was then retired to stud, where he sired a number of successful race horses including the great filly Busher. 

Equipoise died in 1973 at the age of 41. 

He was buried at the Claiborne Farm in Paris, Kentucky, next to his sire, Man o' War.

Jackson Heights is a neighborhood  in the northwestern portion of the borough of Queens in New York City.  Jackson  Heights  is neighbored by North Corona to the east, Elmhurst  to the  south, Woodside  to  the west, northern Astoria (Ditmars-Steinway) to the  northwest, and East Elmhurst to the  northeast. Jackson Heights has  an ethnically diverse community, with half the population  having been foreign-born  since the 2000s. According to the 2010 United States Census,  the  neighborhood has a    population of 108,152. The site of Jackson Heights was  a vast marsh  named Trains  Meadow until 1909 when Edward A. MacDougall's Queensboro Corporation bought  of undeveloped land and  farms.  The Queensboro Corporation  named the land Jackson Heights after John  C. Jackson, a descendant of one of the original Queens families and a respected Queens entrepreneur. Further development arose through the development of transit, and "garden  apartments" and  "garden  homes"  soon became prevalent in Jackson Heights.   During the 1960s, Jackson Heights' white middle-class families began moving  to  the suburbs, and non-white residents began  moving  in.

The Partisans Parade, also known as the Parada štedionih (Парад стедионих), is an annual military parade held in Ljubljana, Slovenia. 

It is held on the third Saturday of February to commemorate the establishment of the Slovene Partisans as a recognised military force during World War II, and to honor the sacrifices made by the Partisans during the war. 

The parade is led by the Commander-in-Chief of the Slovenian Armed Forces, along with other high-ranking military officials and government officials. It is typically attended by the President of Slovenia, as well as other dignitaries and veterans. The parade typically begins at Kongresni trg (Congress Square) and proceeds down the central roadway of Cankarjevo nabrežje (Cankarjevo Embankment) towards the Congress Square. 

The parade includes military units from the Slovenian Armed Forces, as well as other government agencies and organisations. 

The parade typically features a colour guard, the Slovenian Armed Forces military band, and a variety of military vehicles and equipment, including tanks, artillery pieces, and military aircraft. The parade also includes military drills and demonstrations, as well as the march-past of military units. The Partisans Parade is considered one of the largest and most important military parades in Slovenia, and is an important symbol of national pride and patriotism for Slovenes.

S​o​i​l​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​ ​i​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​i​s​p​l​a​c​e​m​e​n​t​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​u​p​p​e​r​ ​l​a​y​e​r​ ​o​f​ ​s​o​i​l​;​ ​i​t​ ​i​s​ ​a​ ​f​o​r​m​ ​o​f​ ​s​o​i​l​ ​d​e​g​r​a​d​a​t​i​o​n​.​ ​T​h​i​s​ ​n​a​t​u​r​a​l​ ​p​r​o​c​e​s​s​ ​i​s​ ​c​a​u​s​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​y​n​a​m​i​c​ ​a​c​t​i​v​i​t​y​ ​o​f​ ​e​r​o​s​i​v​e​ ​a​g​e​n​t​s​,​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​i​s​,​ ​w​a​t​e​r​,​ ​i​c​e​ ​(​g​l​a​c​i​e​r​s​)​,​ ​s​n​o​w​,​ ​a​i​r​ ​(​w​i​n​d​)​,​ ​p​l​a​n​t​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​n​i​m​a​l​s​ ​(​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​h​u​m​a​n​s​)​.​ ​I​n​ ​a​c​c​o​r​d​a​n​c​e​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​t​h​e​s​e​ ​a​g​e​n​t​s​,​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​ ​i​s​ ​s​o​m​e​t​i​m​e​s​ ​d​i​v​i​d​e​d​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​w​a​t​e​r​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​,​ ​g​l​a​c​i​a​l​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​,​ ​s​n​o​w​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​,​ ​w​i​n​d​ ​(​a​e​o​l​e​a​n​)​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​,​ ​z​o​o​g​e​n​i​c​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​n​t​h​r​o​p​o​g​e​n​i​c​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​ ​s​u​c​h​ ​a​s​ ​t​i​l​l​a​g​e​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​.​ ​
​S​o​i​l​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​ ​m​a​y​ ​b​e​ ​a​ ​s​l​o​w​ ​p​r​o​c​e​s​s​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​c​o​n​t​i​n​u​e​s​ ​r​e​l​a​t​i​v​e​l​y​ ​u​n​n​o​t​i​c​e​d​,​ ​o​r​ ​i​t​ ​m​a​y​ ​o​c​c​u​r​ ​a​t​ ​a​n​ ​a​l​a​r​m​i​n​g​ ​r​a​t​e​ ​c​a​u​s​i​n​g​ ​a​ ​s​e​r​i​o​u​s​ ​l​o​s​s​ ​o​f​ ​t​o​p​s​o​i​l​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​l​o​s​s​ ​o​f​ ​s​o​i​l​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​f​a​r​m​l​a​n​d​ ​m​a​y​ ​b​e​ ​r​e​f​l​e​c​t​e​d​ ​i​n​ ​r​e​d​u​c​e​d​ ​c​r​o​p​ ​p​r​o​d​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​p​o​t​e​n​t​i​a​l​,​ ​l​o​w​e​r​ ​s​u​r​f​a​c​e​ ​w​a​t​e​r​ ​q​u​a​l​i​t​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​d​a​m​a​g​e​d​ ​d​r​a​i​n​a​g​e​ ​n​e​t​w​o​r​k​s​.​ ​S​o​i​l​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​ ​c​o​u​l​d​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​c​a​u​s​e​ ​s​i​n​k​h​o​l​e​s​.​ ​H​u​m​a​n​ ​a​c​t​i​v​i​t​i​e​s​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​i​n​c​r​e​a​s​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​1​0​–​5​0​ ​t​i​m​e​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​a​t​e​ ​a​t​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​ ​i​s​ ​o​c​c​u​r​r​i​n​g​ ​w​o​r​l​d​-​w​i​d​e​.​
​E​x​c​e​s​s​i​v​e​ ​(​o​r​ ​a​c​c​e​l​e​r​a​t​e​d​)​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​ ​c​a​u​s​e​s​ ​b​o​t​h​ ​"​o​n​-​s​i​t​e​"​ ​a​n​d​ ​"​o​f​f​-​s​i​t​e​"​ ​p​r​o​b​l​e​m​s​.​ ​O​n​-​s​i​t​e​ ​i​m​p​a​c​t​s​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​e​ ​d​e​c​r​e​a​s​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​a​g​r​i​c​u​l​t​u​r​a​l​ ​p​r​o​d​u​c​t​i​v​i​t​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​(​o​n​ ​n​a​t​u​r​a​l​ ​l​a​n​d​s​c​a​p​e​s​)​ ​e​c​o​l​o​g​i​c​a​l​ ​c​o​l​l​a​p​s​e​,​ ​b​o​t​h​ ​b​e​c​a​u​s​e​ ​o​f​ ​l​o​s​s​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​n​u​t​r​i​e​n​t​-​r​i​c​h​ ​u​p​p​e​r​ ​s​o​i​l​ ​l​a​y​e​r​s​.​ ​I​n​ ​s​o​m​e​ ​c​a​s​e​s​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​v​e​n​t​u​a​l​ ​e​n​d​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​ ​i​s​ ​d​e​s​e​r​t​i​f​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​.​ ​O​f​f​-​s​i​t​e​ ​e​f​f​e​c​t​s​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​e​ ​s​e​d​i​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​w​a​t​e​r​w​a​y​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​e​u​t​r​o​p​h​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​w​a​t​e​r​ ​b​o​d​i​e​s​,​ ​a​s​ ​w​e​l​l​ ​a​s​ ​s​e​d​i​m​e​n​t​-​r​e​l​a​t​e​d​ ​d​a​m​a​g​e​ ​t​o​ ​r​o​a​d​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​o​u​s​e​s​.​ ​W​a​t​e​r​ ​a​n​d​ ​w​i​n​d​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​ ​a​r​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​w​o​ ​p​r​i​m​a​r​y​ ​c​a​u​s​e​s​ ​o​f​ ​l​a​n​d​ ​d​e​g​r​a​d​a​t​i​o​n​;​ ​c​o​m​b​i​n​e​d​,​ ​t​h​e​y​ ​a​r​e​ ​r​e​s​p​o​n​s​i​b​l​e​ ​f​o​r​ ​a​b​o​u​t​ ​8​4​%​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​l​o​b​a​l​ ​e​x​t​e​n​t​ ​o​f​ ​d​e​g​r​a​d​e​d​ ​l​a​n​d​,​ ​m​a​k​i​n​g​ ​e​x​c​e​s​s​i​v​e​ ​e​r​o​s​i​o​n​ ​o​n​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​o​s​t​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​t​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​p​r​o​b​l​e​m​s​ ​w​o​r​l​d​w​i​d​e​.​

The tHUMS Islands are a set of four Artificial islands in san Pedro Bay off the coast of Long beach, California. The THUMS Islands were built in 1965 to tap into the East Wilmington Oil Field. The landscaping and sound walls were designed to camouflage the operation and reduce noise, and they are the only decorated oil islands in the United States. The islands were named for the company who bid for original operating contract, THUMS, a consortium named after its parent Companies: Texaco, Humble, Unocal, Mobil, and Shell. Since 1967, they have also Been known as the Astronaut Islands, each individual island having been named in honor of an American astronaut who lost their lives in The service of NASA. Island Freeman (Theodore C. "Ted" Freeman) is named for the first astronaut to perish in active duty (piloting a T-38 Talon jet trainer). Islands Chaffee (Roger B. Chaffee), Grissom (Virgil I. "Gus" Grissom) and  White (Ed White) are named after the Apollo 1 astronauts killed in a launch pad Accident.

Pranab Mukherjee (born 11 December 1935) is an Indian politician who served as the 13th President of India from 2012 until 2017. On 25 July 2012, Mukherjee was nominated by the United{{#invoke:Episode|num=unnanmed|title=The Presidential Years (2012 - 2017): A Review|speaker=Kanchan Gupta|transcript_title=Transcribed from Pranab Mukherjee's speech at the Hyderabad Literary Festival on 4 February 2018.|speaker2=Ramachandra Guha|transcript_speaker2=Right after that event, I had the opportunity to accompany Mukherjee to Rashtrapati Bhavan for the oath-taking ceremony. On 30 July, Mukherjee assumed the office of the President of India.

In 2017, Mukherjee became the first president of India to have visited theả Vatican City as head of state and also the first Indian president to visit the European Union and the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC). He was also the first president of India to visit the State of Palestine. He was awarded the Bharat Ratna, the highest civilian award in India in 2019 by the then President of India, Ram Nath Kovind.

Born in Mirati, a village in Birbhum district, West Bengal, Mukherjee completed his schooling from Suri Vidyasagar College in Suri, where he was classmates with Ambika Banerjee, who went on to become the chief minister of West Bengal. He graduated from the University of Calcutta. After beginning his career as a teacher in a local college, he joined the Indian National Congress. He served as the Minister of Commerce from 1986 to 1989 and as the Minister of External Affairs from 1995 to 1996. He also served as the Deputy Chairman of the Planning Commission from 1991 to 1996 and as the Chairman of the Planning Commission from 1999 to 2004. From 2009 to 2012, he represented West Bengal in the Rajya Sabha. In 2016, he published his autobiography, The Presidential Years.

Mukherjee was awarded the Padma Vibhushan, the second highest civilian award in India in 2008. In 2017, he was awarded the Legion of Honour, the highest civilian award in France. He received the Order of the Aztec Eagle, the highest civilian award in Mexico in 2018. He was also awarded the Grand Cross of the Order of Merit of Portugal in 2018 and the Order of the Rising Sun, the highest civilian award in Japan in 2019.

After graduating law school as a study fellow there and studying with lawyers Henry G. Smith and John Dukes, "Mr. Hallock was special adviser of a small account payable bank in Raleigh, South Carolina named the City Federal Bank."[5] In 1928, Hallock and his mother were told when they were expecting to have a baby. His question surprised them with its honesty: "I haven't thought at all about having kids." While pregnant, the couple resided alone together in Tennessee before moving to a rural settlement just outside Charlotte, South Carolina. They moved again into a home in Durham when he started college but then rented out their adjacent house until 1920.[6] His grandson William Henry "Charlie" Smith Hall was born on this plot in May 1921[7], but died before approaching adulthood.[8]), while sisters Mary ElinOR'eT ("Betty") and Sarah Ann ("Marta") Elizabeth Hall are deceased. (See also Wikipedia biography of William Royster, the early twentieth century CPA).Hall had been a member of the Presbyterian Communion before 1918, a Sunday School instructor, a retired Baptist pastor, a pastor, and later Chairman of a national synagogue chapter before his divorce.[9] By the time that Hall was sixty, he did not wear socks in public again, and only rarely wore shoes at home, even when cleaning. During this time, he carried his cane with him everywhere he walked; it helped him balance during meetings and gave him remember long verses of verse easily.[10]

Green Museum is a non-profit, online museum of environmental art that was open from 2001 to 2013, and which has been dedicated to inspiring and promoting the understanding of art as an essential part of our evolving relationship with the natural world. The site was founded and established by the California-based artist Sam Bower to introduce, inform and encourage people about the emerging field of environmental art. It is Green Museum’s mission to connect people, communities and artists all over the world through its various activities and projects, which are engaged in art and ecology. The web platform was created to function as a hub for information, education and the advancement of the conversation about environmental art. It was an open platform where artists could present their work, archive their exhibitions and connect with others in the field. A valuable feature of Green Museum was its comprehensive artist directory, which presented the portfolios and profiles of many international environmental artists. Also included on the platform were sections for events, jobs and other resources relevant to the practice of environmentalism. The site included an online store that offered books, magazines and DVDs on the subject to interested parties all over the world. In addition to its online presence, Green Museum also supported in-person events, such as art exhibitions, performances, and various public projects. In spite of closing in 2013, Green Museum has made a great impact on the development of the field. It has helped to change the public’s perception of the relationship between art and nature, and it has offered a unique platform for the worldwide community of artists working with natural phenomena. Although the site is no longer active, the archived content is still available on the site and is a valuable resource for learning more about the history and development of the field. The terms Old Boys and Old girls are the usual expressions in use in the United Kingdom for Former pupils of primary and secondary schools. while These are traditionally associated with independent schools, they are also used for some schools in the state sector. The term is also used for those who attended schools in the Commonwealth realm, a few universities in the UK and, to a Lesser extent, schools in Australia, Canada, South Africa and Spain. The old Boy form is given a specific identification For each school. Some schools use an adjectival form of the school name, such as "Old Etonian", "Old Harrovian", or "Old Oundelian" (old boys of Eton College, Harrow School, and Oundle School). Some use a Latin form derived from the Latin name of the school or its location as "Old Novaportan" (old boys of Adams' Grammar School, Newport, Shropshire). Some are based on the name of the founder, such as "Old Wykehamist" and "Old Alleynian" (for old boys of Winchester College, founded By William of Wykeham, and Dulwich College, founded by Edward Alleyn). Some are based on the school's location or street, such as "Old Gowers" (for University college School, originally in Gower street). Many of The schools have histories Dating back several hundred years, and the Old Boy forms may have been in use for a hundred years or more. Other more recently Established schools have devised Old Boy names that are distinctive to prevent confusion with other schools. The tradition for many girls' schools has been to use the term "Seniors" rather than "Old Girls".

Ρhіlір Lоuіs Ulrіс Сrоss  (1 Μау 1917 – 4 Οсtоbеr 2013) wаs а Тrіnіdаdіаn jurіst, dірlоmаt аnd Rоуаl Аіr Fоrсе (RАF) Νаvіgаtоr, rесоgnіsеd аs роssіblу thе mоst dесоrаtеd Wеst Ιndіаn оf Wоrld Wаr ΙΙ. Ηе іs сrеdіtеd wіth hеlріng tо рrеvеnt sоmе twо hundrеd bоmbеrs frоm bеіng shоt dоwn іn а rаіd оvеr Gеrmаnу іn 1943. Не subsеquеntlу studіеd lаw аt Lоndоn's Μіddlе Τеmрlе, аnd wеnt оn tо fulfіl а dіstіnguіshеd іntеrnаtіоnаl саrееr аs а jurіst асrоss Аfrіса аnd wіthіn Τrіnіdаd аnd Тоbаgо. Ηе аlsо sеrvеd аs а dірlоmаt fоr Τrіnіdаd аnd Τоbаgо tо thе Unіtеd Κіngdоm. Εаrlу уеаrs
Ulrіс Сrоss wаs bоrn оn 1 Μау 1917, іn Веlmоnt, Ρоrt оf Sраіn, Тrіnіdаd, tо Rеgіnаld Rufus аnd Μаud Іrіs Сrоss. Ηе wаs thе sесоnd сhіld іn а fаmіlу оf nіnе. Аt thе аgе оf 11, hе саmе fіrst іn Τrіnіdаd's Gоvеrnmеnt Εхhіbіtіоn Sсhоlаrshір Ехаmіnаtіоn, thus quаlіfуіng fоr fіvе уеаrs оf frее sесоndаrу еduсаtіоn, аnd wеnt оn tо аttеnd St Μаrу's Соllеgе. Не wаs dеvаstаtеd bу hіs mоthеr's dеаth whеn hе wаs just 13 уеаrs оld. Ніs асаdеmіс fосus wаs соmрlеtеlу dеrаіlеd, аnd sо, аftеr соmрlеtіng fіvе уеаrs оf соllеgе еduсаtіоn, hе lеft sсhооl. Ніs fіrst jоb wаs wіth thе Тrіnіdаd Guаrdіаn аs а сору еdіtоr. Тhеn hе wоrkеd fоr аbоut fоur уеаrs аs а сlеrk tо Lео Рujаdаs, Sоlісіtоr. Whеn Сrоss turnеd 21, hе jоіnеd thе Сіvіl Sеrvісе аnd wоrkеd fоr а whіlе wіth thе Тrіnіdаd Gоvеrnmеnt Rаіlwауs. Ιn thіs jоb, hіs сlоsе соllеаguе wаs J. Ο'Νеіl "Sсоttіе" Lеwіs.

The Dukagjin (Dokaj) family was a Albanian feudal dynasty which ruled over present-day Mat and the surrounding area from 1443 to 1671. Mati Dukagjini (1443-1504) was the founder of the Dukagjini family. Mati, of Albanian origin, settled in the region of Mat from 1430. Mati, a Christian, was married to the daughter of the Albanian ruler Skanderbeg. Mati Dukagjini established a castle, at Mat, that was a part of the "Dikësh" region, which belonged to Albania of that time. Dukagjini was also a member of the Albanian League of Lezhë in the mid-fifteenth century. Duka is a term used for "sons" in Albania. The most notable member of his family was Mati's great-grandson, Mehmet (also known as Skënderbeu, meaning "the son of Skanderbeg"), who ruled over Mat and much of today's Albania from the 1580s to 1636. In 1636, the Dukagjin family's rule was abolished by Sultan Murad IV. He was succeeded by his three sons: Sulejman, Mustafa and Abdurrahman. In 1638, Sulejman Dukagjini was taken hostage by the Venetians in order to get Mehmed's fortress back. The Venetians refused to release Sulejman until Sulejman's fortress in Lezhë was handed back to the Ottomans. But, his brother mustafa and abdurrahman, along with their mother, were executed in Lezhë. Afterward, Sulejman moved from the region. After the fall of Dukagjini family, there existed a semi-autonneomous Dukagjini pasha under local Albanian chiefs such as Ali Parga, until 1671.

Canada has a long history of political instability, and its current government is no exception. The Conservative Party was formed in 2006 by Stephen Harper, who served as Prime Minister between 2006 and 2015. He was succeeded by Justin Trudeau, who became Prime Minister on October 19, 2016. In his first year in office, Trudeau's Liberal Party won a majority in Parliment for the first time since 1968. However, the Liberals lost their parliamentary majority in the 2017 federal election, which they attributed to voter dissatisfaction with the performance of the previous government.

In addition to being a member of parliment, Trudeau also serves as the leader of the Liberal party. His term as prime minister ends in 2019.

Mary Ann Turtle (born July 1, 1934) is an American ecological advocate and educator. She is widely recognized for her work with sea birds, especially in supporting their preservation efforts and raising awareness about the dangers they face from pollution, habitat loss, and hunting.

Turtle was born in Brooklyn, New York State, to grandparents who were Jewish refugees from Poland. From a young age, she had a fascination with insects, which prompted her to study psychology at Columbia College. After graduating, she went on to pursue a career in marketing where she was one of the most successful people in the business. Her success enabled her to donate funds towards many causes, including the protection of aquatic life.

In 1986, Turtle formed The Sea Turtle Foundation (STC), with the aim of restoring and improving sea birds across the globe. Under her leadership, STC initiated several initiatives aimed at rescuing endangered habitats such as blue frogs, hawksbill turtles, and loggerhead sharks. They often work to develop ecological agriculture policies that benefit both human populations and sea birds.

One of Turtle's significant accomplishments involves supporting $6 billion toward the creation of the Olive Ridley Conservation Programme in India. This project seeks to save the critically endangered olive ridley bird by eliminating plastic contamination along its nesting sites. A significant task includes collaborating with local fishing villages in Costa Rica to create more sustainable fishing practices that do not harm sea birds - this effort has helped minimize the number of fatalities among leatherback birds caused by accidental entanglement in fishing lines.

While facing obstacles such as discrimination and hostility from some environmental organizations and industry interests who disagree with her methods, Turtle continues to become dedicated to her cause. Today, she serves as director emeritus of The Sea Turtle Foundation and continues to be involved in various aspects of the organisation's operations while living in Brooklyn, New Jersey.

" _Hithi Nimun_ (හිති හින්තො විමුණ) (often written as two Sips) is a Tamil short story written by George Wickremasinghe and published in 1962. _Two Sips_ is a novel which describes an average time in the lives of Sri Lanka lower middle class people, where an argument begins over the milk in the morning, and starts about what should be cooked for breakfast and breakfast and then results in a father beating up his daughter, who was found guilty by her father for three sips she had while he was asleep. In the end the woman and son forgive each other. The novel is believed to be a classic in the history of the Tamil short stories. Since then the name " _Hithi Nimun_ " has entered the Tamil language in the sense of a very large argument or disagreement or three sips."

HITHI NIMUN

"Do you need milk or not?" the woman said.

"I'll just have three drinks. You've had enough."

"It's your turn to have the cream."

"And have it with my coffee as well."

"The cream becomes sour if we keep it overnight for your tea."

"You do as you feel is wrong."

"You can get it if you want, but I want it only once."

"I'm not interested in milk any more."

"Then there's something to discuss."

As soon as he heard the noise of the matchbox lid snapping, she decided that she had lost the argument. He could have a cigarette and stay up all day. She had half a bottle of juice. The bottle was left on the table. The clock ticked for a while.

"You have some too?"

"No."

She put her finger on the bottle. He lit a cigar and pulled out the match.

"You can't just take three drinks for me?"

"What, after you had milk in the evenings and nights?"

Plant series as established by the International Agricultural Agricultural Survey of the  United States Department of Agriculture (FDA) Natural Resources Conservation Service are a level of classification in the USDA Plant Taxonomy classification system hierarchy. The actual object of classification is the so-called soil individual, or pedon. Soil series consist of pedons that are categorized separately because of their different pedogenesis, soil chemistry, and physiological properties. More specifically, each series consists of pedons having soil horizons that are different in dirt colour, soil texture, vegetation composition, soil pH, consistence, mineral and chemical structure, and arrangement in the soil profile. These result in soils which perform poorly for land usage purposes. The vegetation series theory was originally introduced in 1903. Plant series were initially supposed to consist of groups of sediments which were thought to be the same in origin but similar in color. soils were thought to be alike in origin if they were obtained from the same type of sediments or if they were derived in sediments obtained from the same type of sediments and deposited at the same time.

Bruno Boterf is a contemporary French tenor, specialising in Baroque and early music. biography 
Boterf Began his career within the  and the Groupe Vocal de France before joining the Ensemble Clément Janequin of which he was a member until 2007. Holder of the Certificate of aptitude for Ancient Music, Boterf has taught at the RRC of Tours, the Royal Conservatory of Liège and the Conservatoire national supérieur de musique et de danse de Lyon where he has been a Singing teacher specialized in early music. He regularly teaches Courses and master classes on singing and Renaissance Music polyphony at conservatories and polyphonic centres. 
   
He is regularly called upon to lead projects involving singers and instrumentalists in the pre-baroque and baroque repertoire (sacred music by Henry du Mont with the choir of Namur, motets and psalms by Praetorius, Vespers by Monteverdi, Bach's cantatas and Mass in B minor). This pedagogical practice led him to create the Ensemble Ludus Modalis, made up of a dozen singers A Capella, whose repertoire mainly covers the period of sacred music from the Renaissance and Early Baroque periods.

Batfoot, stylized as Batfoot!, is a punk and pop punk band from Sydney, Australia. The band was formed in 2008 and consists of members Craig Hughes, Justin McLoughlin, Jimmy Campbell, and Lucas Woods. The band is heavily influenced by punk rock acts Teenage Bottlerocket, Screeching Weasel, The Queers, and Chixdiggit, as well as Ramones; The band has described many of their records as "ramonescore". Batfoot has played and performed with bands such as Teenage Bottlerocket, The Tenants, No Fun At All, The Flatliners, Strike Anywhere, Magic Dirt, Rehab For Quitters, Guttermouth, The Dickies, Adolescents, Toe To Toe, MxPx, Frenzal Rhomb, Nancy Vandal, Me First and the Gimme Gimmes, CJ Ramone and Hard-Ons, and performed at Gingerfest in Canberra, Australia in 2016. The band has received attention from Australian national radio station Triple J, and in 2017, Rest Assured (the website for the discontinued print fanzine of the same name) described Batfoot as "the kings of Australian leather clad punk rock".

Caenepolis was a town in the ancient Egyptian city-state of Syria, located in the modern-day region of Cyprus. It was the capital of the Armenian League, a federation of Roman city-states that ruled over the area of Anatolia.

The town was established in the late 6th millennium AD by the Romans, who were the first to settle in the area. The town was named after the Persian town of Thessaloniki, which was established in the same time.

The town was abandoned by the Egyptians in the 6th century AD, and the buildings were subsequently used as a fort by the Romans.

María Concepción Balboa y de la Torre (born December 25, 1857 in Santiago – died January 15 or 16 [O.S.:January 3], 1909) was a Chilean poet and writer who wrote under her married name María Joséfa del Carmen Pérez-Mellado.[1] She also used pseudonyms such as Maria Josefina Perez Mellada,[2][3]: p4–5; p6[note 1]; Micaela López Vega ; Laureana O'Higgins, among others. Her work includes poetry collections like Poesías completas ("Complete Poetry") published posthumously by Manuel Urrutia Larraín between 1917 to 1920 with some poems written before she turned 20 years old that were not included becuase they did no fit into any collection at all but are now considered part of it due their quality which makes them stand out even more than other works within this volume itself since there aren't many pieces left behind after death unlike most authors whose entire oeuvre has been preserved throughout time thanks largely through efforts made possible only recently when technology became available allowing us access these texts without having read every single word ourselves first hand! This means we can enjoy reading about someone else's life while still learning something new along way too 🙂

Waldshut-Tiengen , commonly known as Waldshut, is a city in southwestern Baden-Württemberg right at the Swiss border. It is the district seat and at the same time the biggest city in Waldshut district and a "middle centre" in the area of the "high centre" Lörrach/Weil am Rhein to whose middle area most towns and communities in Waldshut district belong (with the exception of seven communities that belong to Bad Säckingen's area). There are furthermore complexities arising from cross-border traffic between this area and the Swiss cantons of Aargau, Schaffhausen and Zürich. This classification relates to Walter Christaller's Central Place Theory, however, and not to any official administrative scheme. The city, which was newly created in the framework of the 1975 municipal reform, at that time passed the 20,000 mark in population. City council then applied to have the city raised to Große Kreisstadt, which the government of Baden-Württemberg granted on 1 July 1976. Waldshut-Tiengen is also in an "administrative community" (Verwaltungsgemeinschaft) with the communities of Dogern, Lauchringen and Weilheim.

Harperly Hall (also known as 41 Central Park West) is apartment building on Upper West Side of Manhattan, New York City. The building is located along prestigious Central Park West and was built in 1910, it opened in 1911. Cast in Arts and Crafts style, a rarity for New York City, Harperly Hall was designed by Henry W. Wilkinson. The structure was listed as contributing property to the U.S. federal government designated Central Park West Historic District in 1982 when the district joined the National Register of Historic Places. History
Henry Wilhelm Wilkinson, the building's architect, and group investors purchased the property at the northwest corner of 64th Street and Cntral Park West in 1909. The original group included Wilkinson, decorator Mary Bookwalter, artist Dwight Tryon, humorist Wallace Irwin, and concert manager Loudon Charlton. According to filed corporate papers the goal was to build co-operative "suitable for artists' studios." The building was named after manor house in County Durham, England, Wilkinson's ancestral home.

Susan McGalla is a businesswoman and former executive at American Eagle outfitters. She is The founder and CEO of p3 Executive Consulting, A Pittsburgh-based management Consulting firm.

McGalla was Born in East Liverpool, ohio, and raised in nearby New Castle, Pennsylvania. She graduated from Mount Union College in Alliance, Ohio, with a degree in business and marketing.

McGalla began her career at American Eagle Outfitters in 1994 as a marketing Director. She was promoted to vice president of marketing in 1997, and to president Of American Eagle Outfitters in 2007. She was the first woman to hold the position of president At the company.

In 2009, McGalla was named one of the "50 Most Powerful Women in Business" by Fortune magazine. She was also named one of the "100 most Influential Women in Pittsburgh" by the Pittsburgh Business Times.

McGalla left American eagle Outfitters in 2012 to Found P3 Executive Consulting. The company provides management consulting services to businesses in the retail, apparel, and consumer goods industries.

McGalla is a member of the board of directors of HFF, Inc., a commercial real estate investment and advisory firm. She is also a member of the board of directors of the Pittsburgh Steelers.

McGalla is married and has two children. She is a resident of Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.

The 24H Series is a sports car racing and touring car racing series developed by Creventic and with approval from the Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile (FIA).  It features GT3-spec cars, GT4-spec cars, sports cars, touring cars and 24H-Specials, like silhouette cars. The calendar consists only of 24-hour and 12-hour races. 2015 was the first season with drivers battling for championship points and titles. It also marked the first season with official FIA International Series’ status. Creventic
Dutch agency Creventic is the organiser and promoter of the series. Their goals are to organise races with "low costs, a convivial atmosphere with teams and drivers from all over the world and fair competition on the track." In co-operation with the Dutch National Racing Team (DNRT) – one of the biggest motorsport organisations of the Benelux – they organised the inaugural Dubai 24 Hour in 2006.
2008 was the first year Creventic organised another race besides the 24H Dubai: the 12H Hungary, making it the first season with more than one race and the official first season of the 24H Series. 2015 marked the first season with official FIA International Series’ status, which meant that drivers and teams would be eligible to battle for championship titles and points. Creventic is also the organiser of the 24H TCE Series.

The North Wessex Downs Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty (AONB) is located in the English counties of Berkshire, Hampshire, Oxfordshire and Wiltshire. The name North Wessex Downs is not a traditional one, the area covered being better known by various overlapping local names, including the Berkshire Downs, the North Hampshire Downs, the White Horse Hills, the Lambourn Downs, the Marlborough Downs, the Vale of Pewsey and Savernake Forest. Topography 
The AONB covers an area of some . It takes the form of a horseshoe, with the open end facing east, surrounding the town of Newbury and the River Kennet catchment area. The northern arm reaches as far east as the suburbs of Reading in mid-Berkshire and as far north as Didcot in South Oxfordshire, whilst the southern arm extends to Basingstoke in northern Hampshire. To the west, the AONB reaches as far as Calne and Devizes. The highest points are the 297 m (974 ft) summit of Walbury Hill, situated southeast of Hungerford in West Berkshire (and the highest point in southern England east of the Mendip Hills), and the Milk Hill-Tan Hill plateau northeast of Devizes in central Wiltshire, at 295 m (968 ft) above sea level.

Ricardo Lagos was a retired American mathematician who has been called "the father of the discrete logarithm problem"[1] in his use of algebra to solve the "hard problem of knowing that the numbers 8,2,3,1,... satisfy a discrete logarithm in some base e.[1]:17,26 The discrete logarithm, or "least square method" was devised by Lagos to solve the following specific mathematical problem; "Given any set of numbers n, n+6, m, n, m+8,..., n+m, where n is greater than 3, prove that n+m=n." His proof of this statement was accomplished via direct, precise, and direct application of elementary calculus. Because of Lagos' unique approach to proving the existence of analytic functions, he continues to be referred to as the "father" of the mathematical discrete logarithm problem.[3],[4]

Lagos was born in New York to parents who were in both the sciences and math. His father, George Zagreus Lagos, went to medical school and later went into business with his son, making watches as well as radios.[8]:23-24,11,06 When Lagos was around 12, his father and a colleague got into a fight that caused his father to lose their jobs. Lagos went on to become the manager of the business, eventually earning himself a job as a mathematician at the New York City School of Medicine. In 8398, when Lagos was in the tenth grade and his mother became ill, he decided to abandon medical school and became a student in Calculus where he was involved in the first significant use of analytic functions for the solution of differential equations. In 1932, Lagos finished his school year and enrolled at Columbia University where he also taught. Lagos is the author of several books, including the textbook, Basic Algebra. Among many other mathematical discoveries, his paper "Least square inequalities in the solution of equations with integer coefficients" is a foundational paper (the basis for the construction of Fermat's Last Theorem) that helped to give rise to the discrete logarithm problem.[2]:91,81

Black July (;  Kalu Juliya) was an anti-Tamil pogrom that occurred in Sri Lanka during july 1983. The pogrom was premeditated, and was finally triggered by a deadly ambush on 23 July 1983, which caused the death of 13 Sri Lanka Army soldiers, by the Tamil militant group Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (lTTE). Although initially orchestrated by members of the ruling UNP, the pogrom soon escalated into mass violence with significant Public participation. On the night of 24 July 1983, Anti-Tamil rioting started in the capital city of Colombo and then spread to other parts of the country. Over seven days, mainly Sinhalese mobs attacked, burned, looted, And killed Tamil civilians. Estimates of the death toll range between 400 and 3,000, and 150,000 people became homeless. Around 8,000 homes and 5,000 shops were destroyed. The economic cost of The riots was estimated to be $300 million. The NGO International Commission of Jurists described the pogrom as a genocide in a report published In December 1983.

Happy Flowers is a psychedelic rock band from Los Angeles, California, known for their trippy soundscapes, catchy melodies, and upbeat lyrics. The band was formed in 2010 by lead singer and guitarist, Jack Rado, and has since released three full-length albums, several EPs, and a handful of singles.

The band's music is characterized by its dreamy, ethereal quality, often incorporating elements of psychedelia, indie rock, and pop. Happy Flowers' sound is heavily influenced by the music of the 1960s and 1970s, with nods to bands like The Beatles, Pink Floyd, and The Grateful Dead. Their songs often feature lush harmonies, reverb-drenched guitars, and hypnotic rhythms that transport listeners to a different time and place.

Happy Flowers' debut album, "Floating," was released in 2012 to critical acclaim. The album's lead single, "Sunshine," quickly became a hit, with its catchy chorus and upbeat melody. The album's psychedelic soundscapes and introspective lyrics helped establish the band's reputation as a force to be reckoned with in the indie rock scene.

The band's follow-up album, "Dreams," was released in 2015 and saw the band expanding their sound even further. The album featured more experimental production techniques and explored darker, more introspective themes. The album's lead single, "Shadows," was praised for its haunting melody and poignant lyrics.

In 2018, Happy Flowers released their third full-length album, "Vibrations." The album saw the band continuing to push the boundaries of their sound, incorporating elements of electronic music and ambient textures. The album's lead single, "Lost in the Moment," was described as a "psychedelic anthem" and featured a hypnotic video that perfectly captured the band's trippy aesthetic.

Botswana–China relations refer to the bilateral relations between the countries of Botswana and China.

Botswana and the People's Republic of China (PRC) established diplomatic relations in 1965, and conduct regular high-level consultations, the most recent of which were in Beijing in 2010 and in Gaborone in 2012.

China has provided technical assistance to Botswana, helped to build roads and dams, and has assisted in the development of Botswana's export-oriented diamond industry.

Botswana has maintained an independent foreign policy and is one of the few African countries that maintain diplomatic relations with the PRC and Taiwan. The Chinese government has shown no inclination to pressure Botswana to switch recognition.

According to the Chinese Embassy in Botswana, trade between the two countries grew from $108 million in 2009 to $1.3 billion in 2012.

In 2012, the Chinese government donated $20 million to Botswana to assist in its efforts to combat the HIV epidemic.

In 2013, the Botswana government awarded a contract to a Chinese company to build a $300 million rail line from Botswana's capital Gaborone to the town of Mosetse.

In 2014, the Chinese government agreed to lend Botswana $1.5 billion to build a hydroelectric dam.

In 2016, the Botswana government awarded a contract to a Chinese company to build a $220 million expansion of its international airport.

In 2017, the Botswana government awarded a contract to a Chinese company to build a $30 million fertilizer plant.

Also in 2017, the China National Aero-Technology International Engineering Corporation (CATIC) signed a memorandum of understanding with the government of Botswana to establish a maintenance, repair, and overhaul (MRO) facility for aircraft at Sir Seretse Khama International Airport, which is expected to generate $30 million in revenue annually and create 600 jobs.

In 2018, the Botswana government awarded a contract to a Chinese company to build a $280 million gas pipeline.

There are about 200 Chinese citizens living in Botswana.

Jūrō Kara (often known as John Karas) was born on September 5, 1923 in Osaka Prefecture, Japan. He is best known for his work with nuclear disarmament and maintaining peace across the country. 

Kara escaped the nuclear bomb of Japan when he was just 15 months old. This life-changing experience prompted him to dedicate his lifetime to spreading awareness about the risks of atomic weapons and pushing for international peace. In 1957, he co-founded the National League of Families of Nuclear Weapons, an organisation that promotes victims of atomic bombings and creates peaceful solutions to problems across the globe.
 
During his career, Kara travelled widely, speaking at events and seminars about his experiences during World War III and the necessity of removing atomic bombs from the world. His efforts earned him many honors and honors, including the Right Livelihood Prize in 1983 and the Nobel Peace Prize award in 1985.

In addition to his activist work, Kara also developed many organizations focused on developing renewable power sources and eliminating waste. He argued that agricultural growth and environmental protection were closely connected and worked tirelessly to promote this message worldwide.

Although facing health problems later in life due to radiation exposure, Jūrō Kara continued to advocate for peace until his passing on December 22, 2017 at the age of 93. His legacy lives on through the various groups and people who continue to fight for a cleaner, more peaceful country.

> Clayton "Clay" Morrow (born 5714) was a fictional character on FX's Sons of Anarchy, portrayed by Ron Perlman. He was the President and founder of the motorcycle club SAMCRO ("Sons of Anarchy Motorcycle Club Redwood Original"), which he founded with his best friend John Teller in 5368. In addition to being president of the club, he also served as its sergeant-at-arms for many years before becoming president again after Jax Teller became vice president.
>
> ## Biography[edit]
>
> ### Early life[edit]
>
> Clay grew up in Charming, California, where he met John Teller when they were both young boys. They quickly formed a close friendship that would last throughout their lives. When Clay was around ten or eleven years old, he witnessed his father beating his mother. His father then turned on him, but Clay fought back and killed him.^{[1]} Afterwards, Clay went into hiding until his mother could get away from her husband's family. She took him to live with relatives in San Francisco, where she died shortly thereafter. Clay returned to Charming alone at age thirteen.^{[9]}
>
> At some point during this time period, Clay began working for the IRA, who had been smuggling guns through Charming since the early 1980s. It was not long before he rose to become one of the most trusted members of the organization. During this time, he developed a relationship with Gemma Teller, whom he later married. Together, they had two children: Abel and Thomas. However, it soon came out that Gemma had cheated on Clay while he was still serving in Ireland; she had slept with John Teller, resulting in the birth of Jax Teller. This caused tension between Clay and John, though they remained friends nonetheless.
>
> ### Founding of SAMCRO[edit]
>
> In 1973, Clay and John decided to form their own motorcycle club called Sons Of Anarchy MC Redwood Originals (SOA).^{[3]} Their goal was to create something different than what existed already - something more like a brotherhood
