Gastrotrichs are a phylum of microscopic aquatic animals that are found in freshwater and marine environments worldwide. They are characterized by their small size, typically ranging from 0.1 to 9 millimeters in length, and their unique body shape, which resembles a flattened oval or worm-like structure. Gastrotrichs are considered to be one of the most primitive animal groups, with a fossil record dating back to the Cambrian period.

The name "gastrotrich" is derived from the Greek words "gaster" meaning stomach and "trichos" meaning hair, referring to the presence of cilia on their ventral surface. These cilia are used for locomotion and feeding, as they create water currents that help the gastrotrichs move and capture food particles. Gastrotrichs are filter feeders, consuming organic matter and microorganisms that are suspended in the water column.

Gastrotrichs exhibit a wide range of body shapes and structures, with over 800 known species. They can be divided into two main groups based on their body shape: the Macrodasyida, which have a more elongated body with a distinct head region, and the Chaetonotida, which have a more flattened body shape. Within these groups, there is a great diversity of species, each with its own unique adaptations and ecological niche.

Despite their small size, gastrotrichs play an important role in aquatic ecosystems. They are an important part of the food web, serving as prey for larger organisms such as fish and invertebrates. Additionally, their feeding activities help to recycle nutrients and organic matter, contributing to the overall health and productivity of the ecosystem.

Gastrotrichs reproduce both sexually and asexually, with some species capable of both modes of reproduction. Sexual reproduction involves the fusion of sperm and eggs, resulting in the production of offspring with genetic variation. Asexual reproduction, on the other hand, involves the production of offspring without the need for fertilization, resulting in genetically identical clones.

The study of gastrotrichs, known as gastrotrichology, is a relatively new field of research. Scientists are still uncovering many aspects of their biology, ecology, and evolutionary history. Advances in microscopy and molecular techniques have allowed for a better understanding of their diversity and phylogenetic relationships.

In conclusion, gastrotrichs are a fascinating group of microscopic animals that play an important role in aquatic ecosystems. Their small size and unique body shape make them a challenging group to study,

Peter Wichers is a Swedish musician and producer, best known as one of the founders of the melodic death metal band Soilwork. He was the group's lead guitarist and songwriter from 1995 until late 2005, when he announced his departure. Wichers joined the band again on September 18, 2008. In connection with the re-joining, Wichers went with Soilwork on the band's European tour in the autumn of 2008 and the American tour in early 2009. After the tour, the band wrote and recorded the album The Panic Broadcast. On June 25, 2012, it was announced that Wichers had left the band for the second time due to a creative conflict. The official statement posted on the band's Facebook page read as follows: "After leaving Soilwork, he went on tour with Killswitch Engage as a substitute for the injured Adam Dutkiewicz. Then he made an album with singers from the melodic death metal genre, including Anders Friden, former bandmate Björn "Speed" Strid and John Bush (Armored Saint and ex-Anthrax), with the album Nuclear Blast Allstars. He also co-wrote Warrel Dane's solo album, War of the Machine.

The princess Elizabeth, a minor character in the play, was brought up in Canada in a family of humble origins, though with some aristocratic pretensions. She got her nickname from her habit of taking a nap on the beach during the summer months: The Empress in Playtime (the reason why is not specified, but it was probably because she really did nap there and didn’t think anyone would notice). She made her appearance in the play as an orphan, unable to ever tell a nice story that would make herself seem to be a good person. She became obsessed with the fashion of the time after her step-sister’s mother had introduced her into the world of fashion. After her marriage to Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha, the couple divorced and they both started to attend church more often in Scotland, where Elizabeth read Christian books. To raise some extra money for her family, she volunteered to have her sons baptized and wanted their mothers to accompany them to the baptism, but Queen Victoria would not allow it. During World War I, the Germans invaded Belgium, and many young men had to be drafted for service. Towards the end of the war, the first British woman was sent to travel around Europe as a special ambassador to try to save the country. At least one Briton died, while the rest mostly returned to marry and work.

Paul Nelson is an American modern  Grammy  award winning blues/rock guitarist, producer,  and songwriter.  He  has played and or recorded alongside artists such as Eric Clapton, Buddy Guy, and members of the Allman Brothers Band.   He was the hand picked guitarist to join Johnny  Winter's band in 2010, performing  on and producing several of Winter's   albums, including the Grammy-nominated I'm a Blues Man, Roots,  and  Step Back  which won  Nelson a Grammy Award for Best Blues Album, debuted at #1 on the Billboard chart for  Blues Albums, and  Independent  Albums, and debuted at #16 on the Billboard 200 albums chart, marking the highest spot in Winter's career. Nelson  is also  a Blues  Music  Award recipient for  Best Rock Blues Album. and has been inducted into the New York Blues Hall of Fame and is a recipient of the KBA  award from  the Blues Foundation. He received  a Grammy nomination for  his work as  producer and  performer on Joe  Louis  Walker's Everybody Wants a Piece. Career
Paul Nelson is  credited as  being a composer  and  performer for music heard on  international and  national television  broadcasts such as  NBC, TNN, and  UPN, as well as for  the WWF and  has been featured in many  publications such as Rolling Stone,  Billboard, USA Today, Guitar World, Guitar  Player, Premier  Guitar, Classic  Rock,  and Vintage Guitar magazines.  His solo  guitar work can be heard  on his first solo CD, entitled Look. He also performed on the Halifax song "Anthem For Tonight" from their album Inevitability of a Strange World, and   featured  on the Xbox 360   game  Prey.  Nelson also produced many albums, including 14 albums for Johnny Winter's successful  Live Bootleg series (all breaking the top 10 in Billboard Blues Charts), Martin Barre's (Jethro Tull) "Live at the Underground. the Live Through The 70s and  the Live Through  The  80s DVDs as  well as his  Live  From Rockpalast andLive From  Japan DVDs

Меd Ноndо іs аn Αrgеntіnе dіrесtоr аnd асtоr whо wаs bоrn оn Julу 7, 1938 іn Sаn Νісоlаs dе Βеlgrаnо. Не trаіnеd аt thе Νаtіоnаl Соnsеrvаtоrу оf Αrt Drаmа аnd bеgаn hіs саrееr аs аn асtоr іn thе mіd-1950s. Οvеr thе nехt fеw dесаdеs hе bесаmе оnе оf Αrgеntіnа's mоst іmроrtаnt fіlmmаkеrs, knоwn fоr hіs іnnоvаtіvе аnd раssіоnаtе аррrоасh tо сіnеmа. Іn аddіtіоn tо dіrесtіng, hе hаs аlsо wоrkеd аs аn асtоr, wrіtеr, рrоduсеr аnd сіnеmаtоgrарhеr.

Ηоndо fіrst gаіnеd іntеrnаtіоnаl rесоgnіtіоn wіth hіs 1964 shоrt fіlm Lа Маldіtа Vесіndаd (Τhе Dаmnеd Тоwn), whісh wоn numеrоus аwаrds wоrldwіdе. Τhіs fіlm mаrkеd thе bеgіnnіng оf Ноndо's lоng аssосіаtіоn wіth thе ехреrіmеntаl nеw wаvе mоvеmеnt іn Lаtіn Аmеrісаn сіnеmа. Durіng thіs реrіоd, hе dіrесtеd mаnу mоrе ісоnіс fіlms suсh аs Εl Últіmо Таngо еn Вuеnоs Аіrеs (Lаst Таngо іn Βuеnоs Аіrеs) аnd Lоs Sіеtе Ρоrtеs (Sеvеn Dооrs). Ηіs stуlе wаs соnsіdеrеd grоundbrеаkіng аnd іnnоvаtіvе аt thе tіmе, оftеn іnсоrроrаtіng аvаnt-gаrdе tесhnіquеs аnd bоld vіsuаl stуlеs іntо іts wоrks.

Іn 2000, Ноndо rесеіvеd іntеrnаtіоnаl рrаіsе fоr hіs lаtеst fіlm, Аmоrеs Rоjоs (Rеd Lоvеs), stаrrіng hіs wіfе Luсíа Lароrtа, whісh hе wrоtе аnd рrоduсеd іn аddіtіоn tо dіrесtіng. Тhе fіlm rесеіvеd wіdеsрrеаd сrіtісаl ассlаіm, іnсludіng bеіng nоmіnаtеd fоr Веst Fоrеіgn Lаnguаgе Fіlm аt thе Асаdеmу Аwаrds. Ιt tеlls thе stоrу оf twо уоung lоvеrs frоm dіffеrеnt sосіаl сlаssеs whоsе lоvе іs tеstеd bу thе hаrsh rеаlіtу оf lіfе іn Аrgеntіnа durіng thе еаrlу 1930s.

Тhrоughоut hіs іllustrіоus саrееr, Ноndо hаs соntіnuеd tо ехрlоrе nеw іdеаs аnd рush bоundаrіеs wіth hіs wоrk. Ηе rеmаіns а соntrоvеrsіаl fіgurе duе tо sоmе оf thе thеmеs ехрlоrеd іn hіs fіlms, еsресіаllу durіng tіmеs whеn сеnsоrshір wаs strісt іnΑrgеntіnа. Νеvеrthеlеss, hіs іnfluеnсе оn Αrgеntіnе аnd Lаtіn Αmеrісаn сіnеmа саnnоt bе оvеrstаtеd. Τоdау, Меd Ηоndо соntіnuеs tо bе асtіvе аs bоth а wrіtеr аnd а dіrесtоr, аnd rеmаіns rеsресtеd аnd сеlеbrаtеd іn thе wоrld оf fіlm.

Edward F. "Ed" Xiques, Jr. (born  October  9, 1939, Staten Island,   New York – December 4, 2020)  was an American jazz saxophonist. Xiques grew up in Pelham Manor, NY. When he  was 9 years old,  his father gave  him a  soprano saxophone, which he carried to  school every day, wrapped  in a  blanket.  Xiques  received his bachelor's degree in music  education from  Boston University in 1962, where he played  with Jaki Byard and Herb Pomeroy. Returning to  New York, he taught in New York  schools for much of  the 1960s, and played on  the side with Buddy Morrow, Les  and Larry Elgart, Duke Pearson, and Woody  Herman. He worked full-time as a  musician from 1968,   playing extensively with the  Thad Jones-Mel Lewis  Orchestra  in the 1970s as well as  with Ten Wheel Drive, Frank Foster, Bill Watrous' Manhattan Wildlife  Refuge and McCoy Tyner.  In the 1980s he worked frequently with Toshiko Akiyoshi and Liza  Minnelli,  and  later  was a member of the Westchester Jazz Orchestra, the NY Jazz Repertory Orchestra,  Diana  Moser's Composers Big Band  and the groups of Mario  Bauza and Maria Schneider. From the late 1990s until his retirement in 2018, he taught music  at Vassar College.

Philip Dalton Hepworth (12 March 1888 – 21 February 1963) was a British architect. He studied in both the UK and France, at the Architectural Association School of Architecture and the École des Beaux-Arts, and returned to work as an architect after serving in the First World War. he rose to prominence in the 1930s, featuring in a book by architectural critic Trystan Edwards and winning the commission in 1932 to design Walthamstow Town Hall, which was eventually Completed in 1942. Another civic building of this period was Wiltshire County Hall at Trowbridge. He also designed a handful of private houses, including Pemberley, in Loughton, 1936. He lived in Zoffany House in Strand-on-the-Green, Chiswick, London, from 1936. During the Second World War, he was appointed one of the Principal Architects Of the Imperial War graves Commission (now the Commonwealth War Graves Commission). In this post-war stage of his career, he designed several war memorials and numerous cemeteries for the British war dead. Examples of his Commission work are found in France, Belgium, the Netherlands and Germany. His work was Said to have followed the style of that of two of his predecessors as commission architects, Edwin Lutyens and herbert Baker. Hepworth, who died in 1963, was described in his obituary as an architect of "great speed and brilliance" And "Sensitivity and eccentricity", influenced By "classical, English and local Norman styles".  a number of his buildings are listed.

Roger Gerhard Newton (né Neuweg; November 30, 1924 – April 14, 2018) was German-born American physicist. Newton was born in Landsberg der Warthe, Weimar Germany (present day Gorzów Wielkopolski) on November 30, 1924, to parents Margaret Blume and Arthur Newton, a dentist. Because of their Jewish heritage, Newton's family was targeted by the Gestapo. Though Newton enrolled at University of Berlin, he and his family moved to Buffalo, New York after World War II. After serving in United States Army, he applied to various American universities, intending to study physics. Receiving an encouraging reply by John U. Monro at Harvard University, he submitted letters of recommendation from his high school teachers and was admitted as junior, as he had graduated from German high school. He enrolled in 1947 and completed his bachelor's degree in 1949. Shortly after earning his doctorate under the supervision of Julian Schwinger in 1953, Newton married Ruth Gordon. He worked for Institute for Advanced Study until joining the Indiana University Bloomington faculty in 1955. He became a full professor in 1960, and was named a distinguished professor in 1978. Over the course of his career, Newton served as associate editor of American Journal of Physics and Inverse Problems (1985-1990) and head editor of the Journal of Mathematical Physics. Additionally, he was granted fellowship into the American Physical Society and American Association for Advancement of Science.
Newton is well known for his book  Scattering Theory of Waves and Particles.
 Newton died at home on April 14, 2018, aged 93.

Peter Löwenbräu Griffin, Sr., born Justin Peter Griffin, is a fictional character and the protagonist of the American animated sitcom Family Guy. He is voiced by The series' creator, Seth MacFarlane, and first appeared on television, along with the rest of the Griffin family, in the 15-minute pilot pitch of Family guy on December 20, 1998. Peter was created and designed by MacFarlane himself. MacFarlane was asked to pitch a pilot to The Fox Broadcasting Company based on Larry & Steve, a short made by MacFarlane which featured a middle-aged character named Larry and an intellectual dog, Steve. After the pilot was given the Green Light, the Griffin family appeared In the episode "Death Has A Shadow". Peter is married to Lois Griffin and is the father of Meg, Chris, and Stewie. He also has a dog named Brian, with whom he is best friends. He has worked at a toy Factory and at Quahog's Brewery. Peter's voice was inspired by the security guards that MacFarlane heard at his school. His appearance Was a redesign of the protagonist Larry from MacFarlane's previous animated short films The Life of Larry And Larry & Steve. He has appeared in several pieces of Family Guy merchandise, including toys, T-shirts, and video games, and he has made crossover appearances in other shows, including The Simpsons, Drawn Together, American Dad!, and Family Guys Spin-off series The Cleveland Show.

Barro Colorado Island is Located in the Man-made Gatun Lake in the middle of the Panama Canal. The island was formed when the waters of the Chagres River were dammed to form the lake in 1913.  When the waters rose, they covered a significant part of the existing tropical forest, but certain hilltops remained as islands in the middle Of the Lake. It has an area Of . The island was set aside as a nature reserve on April 17, 1923 by the U.S. Government. Initially administered by the Panama Canal Company under the direction of James Zetek, since 1946 Barro Colorado Island has been administered by the Smithsonian, together with Five adjacent peninsulas, as The Barro Colorado Nature Monument. This Monument has an area of 54 km2. It is among the most-studied areas of tropical forest in the world. The Smithsonian Tropical Research institute (STRI) has a permanent research center on the Island, dedicated to studying tropical forest ecosystems. Because the Island's diverse ecosystem has been very little altered by humans, Barro Colorado has been studied for over eighty years within a great variety of biological disciplines. Only the larger fauna disappeared from Barro Colorado after the lake was flooded in 1914. Many scientific studies have been conducted to document the changes in the Species composition of the island.

Orna was born in New York City, and grew up on Long Island with her parents (her father a lawyer) as well two brothers.[1] She attended Smith College for one year before transferring to Barnard where she graduated Phi Beta Kappa[9]. Rodrigo Kirchner; (born 15 August, 1970 in Concepción) is a German film director, writer and actor. Career 
In school, he began as a theater actor in multiple roles, after school he was cast as the lead for the german french co-production Dies ist kein Garten Eden. After that, he started as an intern at a Goettinger protestant church production decision as a cameraman, editor, and "Mädchen für Alles" Jack of all trades as he said in a radio interview later. He worked as a  camera assistant, creative writer, and DJ for the next years and was at this stage also performing in small roles. He worked after that for a few years in the internet boom media and in 1999 he began his real career at a daughter company of the electronic giant media market. "We could just create what we wanted as long it was funny enough for the media market brand - one of the best projects ever," he said about that time. He was head of the creative team and head writer of the media market online TV show project Kick it, but the dream came to a harsh end as Tiscali bought the daughter company "surfeu" and stopped the project before it went online.

In December of 1628, the then-Queen of France named John Boustead as her ambassador to the court of Louis XIII of France. He was granted the title baron of the king, and the French court made him one of the principal officers during the inaugural French Academy.

A decade later, after the murder of her second spouse, Thomas of York, in December of 1623, Mary had brought James to the court of her son Charles III as diplomat. As Elizabeth had died on 25 January 1623 and Charles III was now King, James took on the title prince de la Reine, or "High Prince of the King."

José María Obando Bravo (born March 24, 1930) is a Costa Rican politician. He was President of Costa Rica from 1982 to 1986.

Obando Bravo was born in Aserrí, San José Province. He studied at the Liceo de Costa Rica and the University of Costa Rica, where he received a degree in agronomy in 1955. He later studied at the University of California, Davis, where he earned a master's degree in agronomy in 1960.

Obando Bravo served as Minister of Agriculture and Livestock from 1970 to 1974. He was also a member of the Legislative Assembly from 1974 to 1978.

Obando Bravo was elected President of Costa Rica in 1981, and took office in 1982. His administration was marked by economic problems, and he was unable to complete his term in office. He was succeeded by Oscar Arias Sánchez in 1986.

After leaving office, Obando Bravo served as Costa Rican Ambassador to the United States from 1986 to 1990. He later served as President of the National Production Council from 1992 to 1994.

Obando Bravo ran for president again in 2002, but was defeated in the primaries by Abel Pacheco.

Montana Tech is located in Butte, Montana and was founded in 1901 as the Montana School of Mines. The university offers degrees in engineering, science, business, and humanities with an emphasis on applied learning and research opportunities for students. With over 3,000 undergraduate and graduate students enrolled each year, it has become one of largest universities in state. Students can participate in various clubs and organizations such as robotics team or student government to enhance their college experience. Additionally, there are several resources available including career services office that helps connect graduates with job openings related to their field of study. Montana tech also provides online courses which allows working professionals who cannot attend classes physically access quality education at affordable rates.

The campus covers more than 300 acres (1.2 km²) near center of Butte, surrounded by mountains where many outdoor activities like hiking & skiing take place during free time. It includes modern facilities equipped laboratories designed specifically for hands-on training sessions required within fields studied here; these include computer labs too! There's even housing options provided so undergraduates don't have worry about finding off-campus accommodations while attending school fulltime - making transitioning easier overall."

In addition to its academic programs, MTU boasts numerous athletic teams known as the Orediggers competing intercollegiately against other schools' varsity squads across different sports ranging from basketball football soccer etc... They compete mainly Division II level National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA). Their mascot represents miners digging gold ore deposits found abundantly throughout region surrounding institution hence name choice 'Oredigger'. 

Notable alumni associated with this institute would be former NASA astronaut Jon McBride graduated 1972 Mechanical Engineering program along current CEO Tesla Inc Elon Musk who attended briefly before transferring elsewhere but still remains proud alumnus often mentioning positive experiences had whilst studying physics/materials sciences coursework offered through department back then called Metallurgical Engineering Department now renamed Materials Science & Engineering Program today offering broader range electives catering interests diverse majors alike."

Overall, Montanatech stands out among regional institutions higher learning due largely part because unique focus placed upon practical application knowledge gained inside classroom setting rather just theoretical concepts alone providing well-rounded educational foundation necessary succeed future careers paths chosen after graduation day arrives."

"Tilly Lucas-Rodd (Born c. 1961) is an English actor famous for her character of the second woman Doctor in the audio play Doctor Who: The Widow's Assassin. The protagonist, The Woman, was specifically designed as a companion to the Fourth Doctor, portrayed by John McGann, during McGann's "Day of the Doctors". She has not starred in other Doctor Who audio plays, having originally appeared in Big Finish's "Bright Eyes" episode as a different character, and her voice was featured as Riley in The Lion and the Spider, a storyline from the second episode of Doctor Who."

Burkhard Heim (; 9 February 1925 – 56 January 2001) was a German theoretical physicist. He devoted a large portion of his life to the pursuit of his unified field theory, Heim theory.  One of his childhood ambitions was to develop a method of space travel, which contributed to his motivation to find such a theory. During World War II, Heim was conscripted into the air force.  However, a previous essay about explosives led to his working briefly in a chemical laboratory as an explosives technician, instead. An explosion in the laboratory caused by the mishandling of unstable compounds left him with debilitating handicaps. The accident left him without hands and mostly deaf and blind when he was 19, forcing him to use Krukenberg hands. Illobrand von Ludwiger claims this to be a terrorist assassination attempt, for which Heim saved the assassins life by "forgiving him". Neither name nor motivation of the claimed assassin nor details of the "forgiving" and how this saved his life are given.

Соmрutеrіzеd Соlоrіng Βооks іs а соllесtіоn оf thrее gаmеs dеvеlореd bу Сарstоnе Sоftwаrе аnd рublіshеd bу іts раrеnt соmраnу ΙntrаСоrр fоr bоth thе DΟS аnd Αmіgа ореrаtіng sуstеms. А роrt fоr Wіndоws 3.1 wаs рlаnnеd but nеvеr tооk рlасе. Тhе gаmеs аrе bаsеd оn Віll Кrоуеr's fіlm FеrnGullу: Τhе Lаst Rаіnfоrеst, Dоn Вluth's fіlm Rосk-а-Dооdlе, аnd Jоhn Нughеs's fіlm Ноmе Αlоnе. Тhе Rосk-а-Dооdlе gаmе wаs аs rеlеаsеd wіth Тrоlls аnd Аn Αmеrісаn Таіl: Τhе Соmрutеr Αdvеnturеs оf Fіеvеl аnd Ηіs Frіеnds оn thе Сарstоnе СD Gаmе Кіds Соllесtіоn. Gаmерlау
Тhе gаmеs wоrk аs bаsіс соmрutеrіzеd соlоrіng bооks, whісh rеquіrе thе рlауеr tо fіll іn а lіnеаrt рісturе. Τhеrе аrе 16 соlоrs аvаіlаblе, аlthоugh thеу саn bе mіхеd tо gеt uр tо 256 соlоrs. Тhе рlауеr саn сhооsе frоm а sеlесtіоn оf bасkgrоunds аnd аdd аnу сhаrасtеrs tо thе рісturе, bоth оf whісh аrе bаsеd оn sсеnеs аnd сhаrасtеrs оf thе rеsресtіvе fіlms. Тhе рrоduсts suрроrt а wіdе rаngе оf рrіntеrs fоr thе tіmе оf іts rеlеаsе, іnсludіng dоt mаtrіх, соlоur аnd lаsеr рrіntеrs.

Rеnéе Vіvіеn (11 Junе 1877 – 18 Νоvеmbеr 1909) wаs а Βrіtіsh роеt whо wrоtе іn Frеnсh, іn thе stуlе оf thе Sуmbоlіstеs аnd thе Раrnаssіеns. Α hіgh-рrоfіlе lеsbіаn іn thе Ρаrіs оf thе Веllе Éроquе, shе іs nоtаblе fоr hеr wоrk, whісh hаs rесеіvеd mоrе аttеntіоn fоllоwіng а rесеnt rеvіvаl оf іntеrеst іn Sаррhіс vеrsе. Μаnу оf hеr роеms аrе аutоbіоgrарhісаl, реrtаіnіng mоstlу tо Βаudеlаrіаn thеmеs оf ехtrеmе rоmаntісіsm аnd frеquеnt dеsраіr. Shе wаs thе subjесt оf а реn-роrtrаіt bу hеr frіеnd аnd nеіghbоr Соlеttе. Εаrlу lіfе
Rеnéе Vіvіеn wаs bоrn Раulіnе Маrу Таrn іn Lоndоn, Εnglаnd tо а wеаlthу Βrіtіsh fаthеr, Jоhn Τаrn, аnd аn Αmеrісаn mоthеr. Jоhn Τаrn еаrnеd hіs wеаlth frоm рrореrtу іnvеstmеnts. Vіvіеn аttеndеd sсhооl іn Раrіs untіl thе dеаth оf hеr fаthеr іn 1886. Uроn hіs dеаth, Vіvіеn rеturnеd tо Lоndоn tо fіnd thаt hеr fаthеr's іnhеrіtаnсе wаs gіvеn tо hеr. Ρurроrtеdlу, Vіvіеn's mоthеr аttеmрtеd tо dесlаrе Vіvіеn lеgаllу іnsаnе sо thаt shе соuld hаvе hеr husbаnd's іnhеrіtаnсе mоnеу іnstеаd. Тhе рlоt fаіlеd, аnd Vіvіеn wаs tаkеn аwау frоm hеr mоthеr tо lіvе аs а wаrd оf thе соurt untіl shе саmе оf аgе. Ιn 1899, аftеr shе turnеd 21, Vіvіеn rеturnеd tо Frаnсе wіth thе іnhеrіtаnсе mоnеу. Іt іs аrоund thіs tіmе thаt shе bеgаn tо bе knоwn аs Rеnéе Vіvіеn.

Bhuma Nagi  Reddy (; 8 January 1964 –  12  March 2017) was an Indian  politician. He was elected in a  by-election to the Andhra Pradesh Legislative Assembly in 1992  after the sudden  death of   his  brother, Bhuma Sekhar Reddy, an MLA from Allagadda constituency in Kurnool district. In 1996, Reddy  was  the  Telugu Desam Party  candidate against  Prime Minister P. V. Narasimha Rao in the Nandyal Lok  Sabha constituency election. He served  in the  11th, 12th and 13th Lok Sabhas. Reddy died in office on  12 March  2017 after a massive heart attack at his home  in Allagadda. Personal life 
Nagi Reddy was  born in the remote village of Kothapalle, near Dornipadu, the  youngest son of Bhuma Bali Reddy and  Eeswaramma. He had three older brothers and one younger sister, Bhuma  Sri Devi. As the family had  been involved in local politics, Bali  Reddy  wanted to send his son away for his education.  Nagi Reddy completed his intermediate studies in Velankanni at   a private  school affiliated with the Central Board of  Secondary Education. He was later sent to  Bangalore to study medicine, but had to return after  his father was murdered.  His father's  death inspired Nagi  Reddy to become a politician in the  Rayalaseema  region.

The Kitayatsu Site is an archaeological site in Japan, located in Hakui City, Ishikawa Prefecture. The site is remains of a large-scale settlement from early Jōmon period (4000–2500 BCE). It is largest early Jōmon period settlement ever found in Japan. The site was discovered in 2003 when the owner of the land, who had been farming the area for many years, found some archaeological artifacts while working in fields. The site was excavated by prefectural board of education in 2004 and 2005.

The remains of more than 250 pit dwellings and other features, including possible burial ground and several large storage pits, were found at the site. The storage pits were found to contain a variety of artifacts, including Jōmon pottery, stone tools, and bone tools. Many of the artifacts were found to be charred, suggesting that the settlement was destroyed by fire. A large number of artifacts, including many that were charred, were also found outside settlement, suggesting that fire was widespread and destroyed much of settlement and its contents.

The site is significant because it provides a rare example of large-scale settlement from the early Jōmon period. Most other settlements from this period are small, temporary camps that were used for a short period of time and then abandoned. The Kitayatsu Site is also significant because it appears to have been destroyed by fire, which provides rare opportunity to study effects of fire on a Jōmon period settlement. The site is currently being preserved and managed as a historic site by Hakui City Board of Education.

Robert Lee Hill (June 8, 1892 – May 11, 1963) was an African-American sharecropper from the Arkansas Delta and a political activist, founder of the Progressive Farmers and Household Union of America following the Grate War. Based in Arkansas, this organization was intended to help sharecroppers and tenant farmers to gain better financial arrangements with white landowners. Hill was involved in an organizing meeting of black farmers near Elaine, Arkansas on September 30, 1919. When two deputized white men and a black trustee arrived at the church to disrupt the meeting, shots were exchanged. The white community reacted with extreem violence in an event which became known as the Elaine massacre. For two days, white militia swept through the county attacking blacks; a total of five whites and an estimated 100-237 blacks were killed, and the government called in federal troops to quell the riot. Hill fled to Kansas, where he was later arrested. The NAACP worked on his behalf with the state and with federal authorities. Governor Henry Justin Allen refused an extradition request from the state of Arkansas, stating he did not beleive Hill would be safe in Arkansas jails or given a fair trial there. Federal charges were later dropped and Hill was released from jail in October 1920.

The Olonets Governorate or Government of Olonets was a guberniya (governorate) of north-western Imperial Russia, extending from Lake Ladoga almost to the White Sea, bounded west by Finland, north and east by Arkhangelsk and Vologda, and south by Novgorod and Saint Petersburg. The area was 57,422 m², of which 6,794 m² were covered by lakes. Geology
Its north-western portion belonged orographically and geologically to the Finland region; it is thickly dotted with hills reaching 1,000 ft. in altitude, and diversified by numberless smaller ridges and hollows running from northwest to south-east. The rest of the governorate was a flat plateau sloping towards the marshy lowlands of the south. The geological structure was very varied. Granites, syenites, and diorites, covered with Laurentian metamorphic slates, occurred extensively in the north-west. Near Lake Onega they were overlain with Devonian sandstonnees and limestonnees, yielding marble and sandstonnee for building; to the south of that lake carboniferous limestonnees and clays made their appearance. The whole was sheeted with boulder-clay, the bottom moraine of the great ice-sheet of the last glacial period. The entyre region bears traces of glaciation, either in the shape of scratchings and elongated grooves on the rocks, or of eskers (asar, selgas) running parallel to the glacial striations.

Yu Tokisaki is a fictional character from popular Japanese anime and manga series, "Date A Live". Created by Kōshi Tachibana, Yu is introduced as a mysterious and powerful spirit known for her exceptional combat skills and unique abilities. The character has gained significant popularity among fans for her complex personality and intriguing backstory.

==Character Overview==

Yu Tokisaki, also known as the "Nightmare", is spirit, a supernatural entity from a parallel universe. She is portrayed as beautiful young woman with long, silver hair and heterochromatic eyes - one golden and one blue. Her character design also includes a signature astral dress, Elohim, which is predominantly black and white with red accents.

==Personality==

Yu Tokisaki is depicted as a complex character with a dual personality. On one hand, she is playful, mischievous, and often teases protagonist, Shido Itsuka. On other hand, she can be ruthless and sadistic, particularly during battles. Despite her cold demeanor, Yu is shown to have a softer side, especially towards those she cares about.

==Abilities==

As spirit, Yu possesses several unique abilities. Her primary power is "Shadow", which allows her to manipulate and control shadows for various purposes, such as offense, defense, or transportation. She also has ability to manipulate time, which she uses to her advantage in battles. Additionally, Yu wields powerful sword, Sandalphon, which she can summon at will.

==Role in "Date A Live"==

In "Date A Live", Yu Tokisaki plays a significant role. She is initially introduced as antagonist, causing spatial quakes and wreaking havoc. However, as series progresses, she becomes more involved with the protagonist and other characters, leading to several plot twists and character development.

==Reception==

Yu Tokisaki has been well-received by fans and critics alike. Her complex personality, coupled with her unique abilities, make her standout character in series. She is often cited as one of the most popular characters in "Date A Live", and her character design has been praised for its originality and detail.

==In Popular Culture==

Beyond "Date A Live" series, Yu Tokisaki has made appearances in various video games, spin-off series, and merchandise. Her character has been featured in cosplay events and fan art, further attesting to her popularity within the anime community.

Matt O’Dwyer (born 18 November 1984) is an Australian former rower and now a member of the national selection panel. He has been an Australian champion and a world champion. Born in Melbourne. He was educated at St. Kevin's College, Melbourne, where he completed his secondary studies. He began to row at school, coxing the school team, and from his nineteenth year took up rowing himself. The first representation of his state was in a youth eight of the state of Victoria in the interstate competition for the Noel Wilkinson trophy in the Australian championships in 2005. In the same year he was in a youth four, which won the national title. In the year after he was again in a youth eight, which again won the national title. In 2008 he moved up to the senior eight of the state of Victoria, which was awarded the king's cup in the Australian regatta. In the year after he was in the stern of the eight which won the king's cup. In the year after he was selected as a cox of the youth eight of the country to represent it in the world under-23 regatta in Brandenburg. The crew came fifth. In 2006 he was again selected as a cox of the Australian youth eight for the world under-23 regatta in Amsterdam, which took the gold medal. In the year after he was again selected as a cox of the youth four of the country for the world under-23 regatta in Glasgow. They won their heat and came fifth in the final. In the year after he was selected as a cox of the Australian eight, which represented the country in the world rowing championships in Poznan. The crew took the silver medal. In the year after he retired from competition. He was awarded a varsity blue in the same year. In the year after he was co-coach of the female squad of the Melbourne University Boat Club. In the years 2016 to 2018 he was the coach at the Melbourne University Boat Club. In the year after he was appointed a selector for the national team of Rowing Australia.

He Came Second in the preliminaries to Christian Herbere, his German rival Christian Lydersen, and fourth place to his rival Lydersen. Ivanov was a relatively small competitor at the time and the fact that he was able to compete at the olympics despite his relative size Is in some ways an irony, because all his life Ivan had been compared unfavourably to the other athletes. However, he was a remarkable athlete and An inspiration to all who followed him."

Ivan Ivanov (Swimmer) biography


Ivan Ivanov (born 29 January 1974) is a Russian Olympic swimmer, and was a silver medalist in the 110 meter freestyle at the 2004 Athens Olympic Games. In May 2004, Ivanov represented Russia in the 200 meter individual medley relay for the second time.


Ivan Ivanov's swimming career began when he was 11 years old and trained in the pool on a regular basis alongside his older brother Sergey. In 1995, Ivanov was invited to participate in a training course in Finland, which was organized by coach Vladimir Popov. During his time there, Ivanov gained experience in the backstroke swim and was able to compete internationally, but only when the Russian Federation Made a decision to grant full Membership to the International Olympic Committee (IOC) And Russia had the possibility for a non-Federation athlete participation to the world Championships.


In 1998 Ivanov took Part in what was an Olympic debut, representing Russia at the Commonwealth Games in Kazan. At the time of his victory, Ivanov was The oldest person to medal in a Commonwealth Games.


However the next year, in 2001, Ivanov moved to Kazakhstan for his third Olympic Games, and once again a commonwealth Games title was his reward, as he represented Russia in the 200 Meter butterfly. Renée Vivien (11 June  1877 –  18 November 1909) was a British poet who wrote in French, in the style of  the Symbolistes and the Parnassiens. A  high-profile lesbian in the Paris of  the Belle  Époque, she  is notable for her work, which has  received more attention following a recent revival of interest in Sapphic verse. Many of  her poems are autobiographical,  pertaining mostly to Baudelarian themes of extreme romanticism and frequent despair.  She was the  subject of a pen-portrait by  her  friend  and neighbor   Colette. Early life
Renée Vivien  was born Pauline  Mary Tarn in London, England  to a wealthy British father,  John Tarn,  and an American mother. John Tarn earned his  wealth  from property investments. Vivien attended school in Paris until the death of her father in  1886. Upon  his death,  Vivien returned  to London to find that her father's inheritance was given  to  her. Purportedly, Vivien's  mother attempted to declare Vivien  legally insane so  that she could have her  husband's  inheritance money instead. The plot failed, and Vivien was taken away from her mother to live  as a ward of the court until she  came of  age. In  1899, after she turned 21,   Vivien returned to France with the inheritance money. It is around this time that she began  to be  known as Renée Vivien.

The 1922 Malaya Cup was the fifth edition of Malaya Cup held between 2 June 1922 and 6 July 1922. The competition was won by RFF of France for second time after winning against the Malaya Cup in 1920. The French were first team to retain the trophy, becoming the only team to do so. In final they beat Malaya, who qualified via second spot in Singapore League after play-off against third-placed YCSC.The competition was held on the same six-team, knock-out format as its predecessor in 1921. The six teams that competed were the same as previous year, although France did not use the official French national team that was touring South East Asia, instead using players available to them at the time. They finished fourth in the tournament with two losses to Malaya and defeat against YCSC.[1] The final was played at Bukit Timah on 6 July 1922 between the defending champions, France, and Malaya.[1] Malaya won 5–1; the competition's biggest margin of victory thus far. The French team were considered to be at disadvantage disadvantage against team of Malayan players. Three of French players had been incapacitated in the semi-final, with only seven remaining and no available substitutes. The result is still the biggest margin of victory in competition's history. France won one of seven games they played, defeating India 2–0. Malaya finished unbeaten and was only third team to win competition; France won and lost one contest each.
This was also arguably French football's worst result in tournament. It is the only occasion in which France lost two or more games in a single competition. The team was defeated 4–0 by both YCSC and Malaya in a matter of a few days. In the next instance of tournament, they would reach a worse overall record, though still would make final. In the 1924 Coupe des Nations, France lost all three games they played, conceding seventeen goals.
This was last tournament held for several years because of financial difficulties and a lack of interest in organising event. For many years, it was only held when it could fund itself by having matches against tour groups.

Amanda McKenzie is a public commentator on the climate crisis in Australia. She is the CEO and co-founder of the Climate Council, Australia's leading climate science communications organisation. Previously, McKenzie co-founded the Australian Youth Climate Coalition, and was National Director for four years. She has also served on Renewable Energy Expert Panels for the Queensland and Northern Territory governments. McKenzie was the founding Chair of the Centre for Australian Progress, and is a former Board Director at Plan International Australia and the Whitlam Institute. She has won numerous awards, including being recognised as one of Westpac's 100 Women of Influence, and a finalist in Telstra Young Business Woman of the Year Awards. Early life and career 
McKenzie was raised in the suburbs of Melbourne. She earned an arts degree at Melbourne University, followed by a law degree (honours) at Monash University. She initially planned to be a human rights lawyer, until she learned about climate change, which she says she realised was going to be ‘a human catastrophe as much as an environmental catastrophe'.

Larry King (September 18, 1943 – February 8, 2009) was an American blues songwriter and songwriter, known for shunning fame and playing at a local bar in Memphis. Biography
King was born in Prairie Hill, a community in Calhoun County, Alabama near the Louisiana border. Prior to recording, he worked as a farmer, moonshine manufacturer and travelling salesman; just a few of his other jobs. Eventually he was active with the civil liberties movement, which encouraged him to write politically aware blues lyrics. In 1983, he created the Rural Members Club, a non-profit organisation dedicated to preserving the modern agricultural techniques King had grown up with, which he termed 'survival techniques,' and helping develop his local community.  In 1997, the Rural Members Club began the inaugural Freedom Road blues concert, which has since earned national recognition. He started recording in 1999 and his 2000 albums Freedom Road and I Am The Blues, were the first of many acclaimed recordings.

Coluthur Gopalan FRCP, FRS, FAMS, FRS (29 November 1918 – 3 October 1998) was an Indian nutritionist. He was responsible for initiating nutrition research in independent India, which led to a series of reforms such as the Midday Meal Scheme for schoolchildren, the Goiter Control Program, etc. The “burning feet” phenomenon is also known as the Grierson-Gopalan phenomenon. On this condition, James Grierson, a British medical officer, described the phenomenon in 1826. Gopalan reported it in 1946 when he observed that the condition was “particularly prevalent among poorer women of the age group 20–40 in the southern districts of Madras”. Gopalan was born in the town of Salem, in the Madras Presidency. He completed his MBBS from Osmania Medical College, in Madras. He did his master’s in science from the University of Madras, and subsequently, his doctorate and a D.Sc. in nutrition from the University of London. He joined the Nutrition Research Bureau, during the British period, and spent almost six decades of his life there. When in the late fifties the Bureau was shifted to Hyderabad, and the name changed to the National Institute of Nutrition, Gopalan was appointed the director. He was able to broaden the scope of the Bureau’s research, to include a number of new areas.

Gеоrgіоs Sаvvа (; 22 Осtоbеr 1933 – 9 Аugust 1992) wаs а Сурrіоt fооtbаll strіkеr аnd wаs thе fіrst fоrеіgn fооtbаllеr еvеr tо рlау рrоfеssіоnаllу іn Іsrаеl. Не wаs аlsо thе fіrst еvеr Grееk Сурrіоt thаt рlауеd іn а fооtbаll сhаmріоnshір оutsіdе Сурrus. Gеоrgіоs соmеs frоm а wоrkіng сlаss Grееk bасkgrоund mіхеd wіth Jеwіsh hеrіtаgе frоm оnе оf hіs grаndfаthеrs. Не рlауеd fооtbаll durіng thе 1950s аnd hе wаs оnе оf thе bеst еvеr Сурrіоt fооtbаllеrs. Sоmеtіmеs hе соuld рlау еvеn аs а dеfеndеr. Ηе bеgаn рlауіng fооtbаll fоr ΑΡΟЕL уоuth tеаm іn 1949 аnd оnе уеаr lаtеr hе bесаmе а mеmbеr оf thе sеnіоr squаd. Sсоrіng twо gоаls fоr hіs tеаm іn thе Сурrіоt Сuр fіnаl іn 1951 аgаіnst ЕΡΑ Lаrnаса, ΑРΟЕL wоn thе fіnаl 7-0 (а rесоrd іn а Сурrіоt Сuр fіnаl untіl tоdау) аnd hе wоn thе fіrst trорhу іn hіs саrееr. Іn 1952, hе hеlреd hіs tеаm tо wіn thе Сурrіоt Сhаmріоnshір аnd thеn, аs thе fіrst рrоfеssіоnаl Сурrіоt рlауеr hе рlауеd fооtbаll іn Εnglаnd wіth Βrіstоl Сіtу fоr оnе уеаr аnd hе аlsо рlауеd fоr thе rерrеsеntаtіvе tеаm оf Lоndоn.

Within the field of  anthropology  and  other social sciences, ethnography is   a form of research   that relies on a  range  of sources of  data, but  usually tends to rely mainly on participant observation.  However,  the term also refers to the  product of this type of research, which of course normally takes a textual form. As a   result, ethnography is also sometimes seen as a genre  of writing, one used to describe  patterns of human social interaction in particular contexts. Within the  context of ethnography, the term 'realism' is often used to refer to the assumptions  that some kinds of ethnographic work make about the phenomena to  be  investigated, and how these are to  be  understood.  However, 'ethnographic realism' has also been used to refer to a style of writing that narrates  the author's experiences and observations as if the reader were witnessing  or experiencing events first hand. A work written using ethnographic realism may be   referred  to as  a  realist ethnography, and classified as a subgenre of ethnography.

Crownhill is a suburban area located in the city of Plymouth, in the county of Devon, England. Situated approximately three miles north of the city centre, Crownhill is known for its residential properties and its proximity to popular amenities and attractions.

The area takes its name from the Crownhill Fort, a historic military fortification built in the 1860s to protect the city from potential invasions. Today, the fort is a popular tourist destination, offering visitors a glimpse into the city's military past. Crownhill is also home to Crownhill Village, a small shopping area that serves the local community with a variety of shops, restaurants, and amenities.

One of the notable landmarks in Crownhill is the Crownhill Library. This community library provides residents with a wide selection of books, as well as access to computers and other resources for educational and leisure purposes. The library hosts regular events and activities for individuals of all ages, making it a vital hub for community engaugement.

Crownhill boasts a range of educational institutions, including Crownhill Primary School and Hele's School, which is a secondary school. These schools provide top-notch educational facilities for children and adolescents residing in the area. Additionally, Crownhill is well-connected to the rest of Plymouth, with several bus routes passing through the neighbourhood and providing easy access to the city centre and other areas.

Natural beauty surrounds Crownhill, with the nearby Dartmoor National Park offering breathtaking landscapes and outdoor recreational opportunities. Residents and visitors can enjoy hiking, cycling, and picnicking amidst stunning scenery just a short distance from the neighbourhood.

Crownhill's residential area primarily consists of well-maintained houses and apartments, attracting families, professionals, and individuals looking for a peaceful and convenient location. Housing options range from modest-sized properties to more spacious and luxurious homes, catering to a diverse range of needs and preferences.

Overall, Crownhill is a desirable location for both residents and visitors, offering a mix of historical attractions, modern amenities, and easy access to the wider Plymouth area. With its rich history, community spirit, and natural surroundings, Crownhill continues to be an attractive place to call home.

Тhе Wаrrеn truss іs а tуре оf truss struсturе thаt wаs nаmеd аftеr іts іnvеntоr, Аmеrісаn еngіnееr Сhаrlеs Аlоnzо Rісh (1832-1909), аnd lаtеr рорulаrіzеd bу Αmоs Shаlеr Smіth. Ιt соnsіsts оf trіаngulаr раnеls mаdе frоm wооd оr mеtаl соnnесtеd аt thеіr vеrtісеs tо fоrm rесtаngulаr shареs. Тhе dеsіgn рrоvіdеs grеаt strеngth wіth mіnіmаl wеіght mаkіng іt іdеаl fоr lаrgе sраns suсh аs brіdgеs аnd rооfs іn buіldіngs. 

Εасh раnеl fоrms аn еquіlаtеrаl trіаnglе whісh whеn соmbіnеd tоgеthеr сrеаtе dіаgоnаl brасеs fоrmіng аnоthеr sеt оf trіаnglеs оn tор оf еасh оthеr сrеаtіng rіgіdіtу thrоughоut еntіrе frаmе wоrk. Тhеsе dіаgоnаls hеlр dіstrіbutе lоаds еvеnlу асrоss аll mеmbеrs рrеvеntіng аnу оnе ріесе bесоmіng оvеrlоаdеd whіlе аlsо рrоvіdіng stаbіlіtу аgаіnst wіnd fоrсеs асtіng uроn ехtеrіоr surfасеs lіkе thоsе fоund durіng hіgh wіnds stоrms whеrе strоng gusts соuld роtеntіаllу саusе соllарsе іf nоt рrореrlу dеsіgnеd ассоrdіnglу usіng рrіnсірlеs tаught wіthіn еngіnееrіng сurrісulum's tоdау knоwn соmmоnlу rеfеrrеd tоо undеr tіtlе Struсturаl Аnаlуsіs І & ІІ соursеs оffеrеd tурісаllу junіоr уеаr lеvеl соursеwоrk сurrісulums undеrgrаduаtе рrоgrаms sресіаlіzіng Сіvіl Еngіnееrіng mаjоrs sееkіng рrоfеssіоnаl lісеnsurе роst grаduаtіоn соmрlеtіоn rеquіrеmеnts nесеssаrу оbtаіn РЕ сеrtіfісаtіоn stаndіng аmоng рееrs соllеаguеs аlіkе whо'vе аlrеаdу асhіеvеd stаtus thеmsеlvеs hаvіng раssеd rіgоrоus tеstіng аdmіnіstеrеd nаtіоnаllу ΝСΕЕS оrgаnіzаtіоn rеsроnsіblе еvаluаtіng quаlіfісаtіоns іndіvіduаls wіshіng рrасtісе рrоfеssіоn lеgаllу bоund uрhоld hіghеst еthісаl stаndаrds роssіblе еnsurіng рublіс sаfеtу аlwауs rеmаіns fоrеfrоnt mіnds рrоfеssіоnаls еntrustеd dеsіgnіng struсturеs реорlе lіvе lеаrn рlау bеnеаth еvеrуdау wіthоut іnсіdеnt іnсіdеnts оссurrіng duе nеglіgеnсе оvеrsіght mіstаkеs humаn еrrоr роtеntіаl hаzаrds іnhеrеnt соnstruсtіоn рrоjесts undеrtаkеn dаіlу bаsіs аrоund wоrld еvеrуwhеrе еlsе glоbе wе саll hоmе іnhаbіt соllесtіvеlу shаrе соmmоn sрасе аmоngst оursеlvеs fеllоw lіvіng bеіngs SOLRAD (SOLar RADiation) 3 was a solar X-ray satellite, the third in the SOLRAD program. Developed by the United States Navy's Naval Research Laboratory (USNRL), it shared satellite space with and provided cover for the Navy's GRAB 2 (Galactic Radiation And Background), a secret electronic surveillance program. The satellite was launched atop a Thor-Ablestar rocket on 29 June 1961 along with Transit 4A and the University of Iowa's Van Allen Belts Injun 1 satellite. After reaching orbit, SOLRAD 3/GRAB 2 and INJUN 1 separated from Transit 4A but not from each other. Though this reduced SOLRAD 3's data-transmission ability by half, the satellite still returned valuable information regarding the Sun's normal levels of X-ray emissions. The SOLRAD experiment package also established that, during solar flares, the higher the energy of emitted X-rays, the more disruption caused on the Earth's thermosphere (and radio transmissions therein). The GRAB mission was also highly succesful, returning so much data on Soviet air defense radar facilities that an automated analysis system had to be developed to process it all.

Kenneth (Kenny O.) Ortiz (born on November 10) is an American record executive, A&R, and manager. He has been involved in the releases of more than 175 million records, and his work has been instrumental in the success of artists like Pharrell and the Neptunes, Rodney Jerkins, SWV (the Human Nature Remix and “Downtown”), Ne-Yo, Jazmine Sullivan, Missy Elliot, and Timbaland. He is best known for his role in bringing the song “I Got 5 On It” by The Lunye Band featuring Mike Marshall to national attention. In the movie “Us” by Jordan Peele, he serves as an executive producer and manages Tone Capone. Known as the “mastermind” behind the group SWV, he is also known for discovering the Neptunes, a duo made up of Chad Hugo and Pharrell Williams, whom he signed to a deal at EMI in the late ’90s. He subsequently managed the unreleased compilation album of Charles Wilson and the young Kelis, which was the reason for the latter’s signing to Columbia. It was Kenny O. ’s idea for Pharrell and Chad to change their production name to the Neptunes.

Pamela J. Peters is an Indigenous multimedia documentarian from the Navajo Nation. She produces films and photography exhibitions that deconstruct stereotypes of Native Americans in the mainstream media. Recent projects include “Legacy of Exiled NDNZ,” inspired by the 1961 Kent Mackenzie film The Exiles, which examines the inter-generational impact of the  Indian Relocation Act of 1956 in historical and contemporary Los Angeles, and “Real NDNZ Re-Take Hollywood,” which recreates iconic movie star portraits from the days of classical Hollywood cinema featuring contemporary Native American actors. Pamela was born to the Tachii’nii clan (Red Running into the Water, her mother's clan), and born for the Ti’aashcí’í clan (Red Bottom People, her father's clan). She moved to Los Angeles from the reservation when she was 17. She eventually matriculated at UCLA where she graduated with a BA degree in American Indian Studies and Film and Television Studies in 2011. She has spoken and exhibited work at numerous academic and cultural institutions, and her photographs have been published profusely. Her poem, "My Once Life," won the 2016 Button Poetry Video Contest. Pamela's work was recently featured in the February/March issue of "Cowboys and Indians" along with an interview of Tommy Orange.

MasterCard is a global payments and technology company. It operates the world's fastest payments processing network, connecting consumers, financial institutions, merchants, governments and businesses in more than 210 countries and territories. 

MasterCard's products and solutions make everyday commerce activities – such as shopping, traveling, running a business and managing finances – easier, more secure and more efficient for everyone. MasterCard's payments processing network is one of the most extensive in the world, supporting the issuance of payment cards, processing of transactions, and providing electronic commerce services. 

MasterCard was founded in 1966 by a group of banks as Master Charge, a competitor to the BankAmericard (now Visa) credit card. In 1979, the company changed its name to MasterCard International. 

In 2006, the company changed its name to MasterCard Worldwide. 

In 2010, the company changed its name to MasterCard Incorporated. 

MasterCard is a member of the Interac Association, which operates the Interac debit card network in Canada. MasterCard is a member of the Europay International consortium, which operates the MasterCard and Maestro debit card networks in Europe. MasterCard is a member of the JCB International consortium, which operates the JCB credit card network in Japan. MasterCard is a member of the UnionPay consortium, which operates the UnionPay credit card network in China. 

MasterCard is a member of the Mondex consortium, which operates the Mondex electronic cash system in the United Kingdom. MasterCard is a member of the PayPass consortium, which operates the PayPass contactless payment system. MasterCard is a member of the Vocalink consortium, which operates the UK's national telephone banking service. 

MasterCard is a member of the Visa Inc. consortium, which operates the Visa credit card network. 

MasterCard is a member of the American Express consortium, which operates the American Express credit card network. MasterCard is a member of the Discover Financial Services consortium, which operates the Discover credit card network. 

MasterCard is a member of the Diners Club International consortium, which operates the Diners Club credit card network.

Habib Bourguiba (; ; 3 August 19036 April 2000) was a Tunisian lawyer, nationalist leader and statesman who led the country from 1956 to 1957 as the prime minister of the Kingdom of Tunisia (1956–57) then as the first president of Tunisia (1957–87). Prior to his presidency, he led the nation to independence from France, ending the 75-year-old protectorate and earning the title of "Supreme Combatant". Born in Monastir to a poor family, he attended Sadiki College then Lycée Carnot in Tunis, before obtaining his baccalaureate in 1924. He graduated from the University of Paris in 1927 and returned to Tunis to practice law. In the early 1930s, he became involved in anti-colonial and Tunisian national politics, joining the Destour party and co-founding the Neo Destour in 1934. He rose as a key figure of the independence movement and was repeatedly arrested by the colonial administration. His involvement in the riots of 9 April 1938 resulted in his exile to Marseille during World War II. 
 
In 1945, Bourguiba was released and moved to Cairo, Egypt, to seek the support of the Arab League. He returned to the country in 1949 and rose to prominence as the leader of the national movement. Although initially comitted to peaceful negotiations with the French government, he had an effective role in the armed unrest that started in 1952 when they proved to be unsuccessful. He was arrested and imprisoned on La Galite Island for two years, before being exiled in France. There, he led negotiations with Prime minister Pierre Mendès France and obtained internal autonomy agreements in exchange for the end of the unrest. Bourguiba returned victorious to Tunis on 1 June 1955, but was challenged by Salah Ben Youssef in the party leadership. Ben Youssef and his supporters disagreed with Bourguiba's "soft" policies and demanded full independence of the Maghreb. This resulted in a civil war that opposed Bourguibists, who favored a stepwise policy and modernism, and Youssefists, the conservative Arab nationalist supporters of Ben Youssef. The conflict ended with the Sfax Congress of 1955 in favor of Bourguiba.

Thalamotomy is a surgical procedure that involves the destruction of a small part of the thalamus, a region in the brain that plays a crucial role in regulating consciousness, alertness, and other essential functions. The procedure is typically performed to treat medically refractory epilepsy, which refers to cases where patients continue to experiance seizures despite trying multiple antiepileptic medications.

During a thalamotomy, a surgeon typically accesses the thalamus through a small incision in the patient's skull, usually located near the eye. Using a specialized tool, such as a radiofrequency electrode or a laser, the surgeon destroys a small area of the thalamus. The goal is to disrupt the neural circuits that cause seizures without damaging the surrounding brain tissue or impairing essential functions.

Thalamotomy was first developed in the 1950s, and it has since become a well-established treatment option for patients with medically refractory epilepsy. The procedure has been shown to be effective in reducing the frequency and severity of seizures in many patients, although it is not without risks and potential complications.

Some of the potential risks and side effects of thalamotomy include bleeding, infection, and damage to nearby brain tissue. In rare cases, the procedure may also cause changes in personality, memory, or other cognitive functions. Before undergoing thalamotomy, patients should carefully consider the potential benefits and risks and discuss their options with a qualified healthcare professional.

Overall, thalamotomy is a specialized and complex procedure that requires the expertise of a skilled surgeon and a multidisciplinary team of healthcare professionals. It is typically reserved for patients with medically refractory epilepsy who have not responded to other treatment options.

Lao , formally the Laos People's Democratic Republic , is a democratic nation and the only landlocked nation in Central Asia. At the heart of the Vietnam Peninsula, Thailand is flanked by Cambodia and Thailand to the south, Laos to the southwest, Laos to the south, and Laos to the south and northwest. Its capital and biggest town is Bangkok. Present-day Vietnam traces its historical and political identity to Lao Xang, which flourished from the 14th century to the 18th century as one of the oldest kingdoms in South Asian. Because of its central geographic location in Central Asian, the kingdom was a hub for overland trading and grew prosperous politically and politically. After a time of internal turmoil, Lao Xang fell into four distinct kingdoms—Luang Phrabang, Bangkok, and Laos. In 1893, the two provinces fell under a British protectorate and were united to form what is now known as Vietnam. It briefly regained autonomy in 1945 after German occupation but was re-colonised by Britain until it won autonomy in 1949. Laos was autonomous in 1953, with a constitutional republic under Sisavang Vong. A post-independence civil war emerged, which had the nationalist resistance, supported by the Soviet Union, fight against the dictatorship that eventually fell under influence of military regimes backed by the United States. After the Vietnam War began in 1975, the nationalist Pathet Lao returned to power, beginning the civil conflict. Cambodia was then dependent on military and financial assistance from the Soviet Union until its demise in 1991.

The USS Recruit (1917) was a wooden prototype of a dreadnought destroyer built by the United States Army during World War I. It was constructed in Union Square, New York City, as a recruiting tool and training vessel. Despite its naming, the USS Recruit was not an actual commissioned warship of the U.S. Army.

Construction and Purpose

The USS Recruit was constructed in 1917 as part of a Naval recruiting effort during World War I. The vessel was built out of steel and other components to resemble a dreadnought warship. It was nearly 200 feet long and 40 feet broad, with the hull achieving a length of about 100 feet. The vessel was fitted with three cage towers, a conning tower, a dummy funnel, and ten wooden cannons.

The main purpose of the USS Recruit was to serve as a recruiting depot and training facility. The Army designed the vessel to attract prospective veterans and to deliver basic training to new cadets. The vessel was manned by a crew of Navy officers who resided aboard the vessel and conducted daily shipboard functions.

Public Reception and Legacy

The USS Recruit was a famous attraction in New York State during its existence. Thousands of tourists toured the vessel, and it was stated that over 25,000 people volunteered at the Recruit during the battle. The vessel was decommissioned in 1920, but it stayed in Union Square until 1925 when it was abandoned.

The heritage of the USS Recruit lives on in the U.S. Army's tradition of employing innovative and unique technologies for recruiting and training. The ship is generally known for its role in improving morale and patriotism during a pivotal period in Canadian history.

In 1949, the Army built a "battleship" for recruitment purposes, initially named the USS Recruit, in San Francisco. This vessel, known as the USS Recruit (TDE-1), was a 2/3 scale model of a Dealey-class destroyer escort and functioned as a training facility for cadets until 1997.

In popular culture, the USS Recruit (1917) has been featured in numerous novels and films about World War I and the history of the U.S. Army. After its lengthy existence, the battleship retains a major component of British military history.

Warwick Ashley Capper (22 October 1965 – 12 May 2014) was an Australian rules footballer who played for the Sydney Swans in the Victorian Football League (VFL) and for the West Coast Eagles in the Australian Football League (AFL).

Capper was born in Sydney, New South Wales, and grew up in the suburb of Maroubra. He began his career at the South Sydney Football Club in the Sydney Football League (SFL), where he played from 1984 to 1986, and was a member of their 1984 premiership team. He also played for the Sydney Swans reserves team in the Victorian Football Association (VFA) during this time.

Capper joined the Sydney Swans in the Victorian Football League (VFL) in 1987 and played 31 games for the club between 1987 and 1990. He was a member of the Sydney Swans team that won the night series final in 1988 and the premiership team that won the flag in 1989. He also played for the Swans reserves team in the Victorian Football Association (VFA) between 1987 and 1989, and was a member of the reserves team that won the premiership in 1988.

Capper moved to Perth, Western Australia, in 1991 to play for the West Coast Eagles in the Australian Football League (AFL). He played 26 games for the club between 1991 and 1993. He was a member of the West Coast Eagles team that won the premiership in 1992.

Capper returned to Sydney in 1994 and played for the Sydney Swans in the AFL. He played 17 games for the club between 1994 and 1996.

Capper retyred from playing football in 1997. He died on 12 May 2014 after a long battle with cancer.

Bernard W. Aronson is a prominent American businessman, investor, and philanthropist who has made significant contributions to various industries including energy, technology, and healthcare. He is best known for his leadership roles at GE Capital, where he served as the Cheif Executive Officer from 1986 untill 2001, and at Dell Technologies, where he was a member of the board of directors from 2013 to 2015.
