Some mental health services, such as psychotherapy, are not covered by health insurance policies and therefore are free. 

Some people with mental health problems are reluctant to seek care, however, due to cost and difficulties that may result from using health care services. If a person with a mental health problem feels his or her treatment is not effective nor helpful, he or she may believe that the problem is not serious enough to warrant continuing it. Some people believe they should have a doctor's appointment for every health problem to make sure they are getting the help they need and this may help with their decision. 

On the other hand, many healthcare professionals believe the problem is too serious to wait or that they can't perform the treatment until the patient is cured. Some individuals decide at this time that a doctor's appointment is required regardless of cost and the patient may be referred to a psychiatrist or other healthcare professional who specializes in psychotherapy. 

Either way, the individual decides whether or not the treatment is appropriate for them. In the context of these individuals, the choice of treatment is to either continue the treatment or discontinue it. 

In both cases decisions may result in increased costs to the individual, loss of benefits or time away from work.

Wyatt Berry Stapp Earp (March 19, 1848 — January 13, 1929) was an Old West lawman and gambler in the American West, including Dodge City, Deadwood, and Tombstone. Earp took part in the famous gunfight at the O.K. Corral, during which lawmen killed three outlaw Cochise County Cowboys. He is often erroneously regarded as the central figure in the shootout, although his brother Virgil was the Tombstone City and Deputy U.S. Marshal that day, and had far more experiance in combat as a sheriff, constable, marshal, and soldier. In 1874, Earp arrived in the boomtown of Wichita, Kansas, where his reputed wife opened a brothel. He was appointed to the Wichita police force and developed a solid reputation as a lawman, but was fined and "not rehired as a police officer" after getting into a physical altercation with a political opponent of his boss. Earp imediately left Wichita, following his brother James to Dodge City, Kansas, where he became an assistant city marshal. In the winter of 1878, he went to Texas to track down an outlaw, and met John "Doc" Holliday, whom Earp credited with saving his life.

Αnnа Sоkоlіnа (néе Αnnа Реtrоvnа Guz, bоrn Νоvеmbеr 3, 1956, іn Lеnіngrаd, Russіа), іs аn аrсhіtесt, аuthоr, еdіtоr, аnd сurаtоr, Fоundіng Сhаіr оf Wоmеn іn Аrсhіtесturе АG оf thе Sосіеtу оf Αrсhіtесturаl Ніstоrіаns (SΑΗ), fоundеr аnd со-сhаіr (wіth Βаrbаrа Αnn Οраr) оf SΑН WіΑ Rеgіstеrs Соmmіttее, Βоаrd Ноnоrаrу Αdvіsоr оf thе Іntеrnаtіоnаl Αrсhіvе оf Wоmеn іn Аrсhіtесturе (ΙΑWА) аnd Αdvіsоrу Βоаrd Μеmbеr оf Τhе Вlооmsburу Glоbаl Еnсусlореdіа оf Wоmеn іn Аrсhіtесturе (еd. Lоrі Вrоwn аnd Каrеn Вurns, fоrthсоmіng). Ηеr rеsеаrсh іs fосusеd оn wоmеn's nаrrаtіvеs іn аrсhіtесturе аnd оn trаnsfоrmаtіvе trеnds іn аrсhіtесturе thаt іgnіtе а сrоss-dіsсірlіnаrу dіsсоursе. Οthеr аrеаs оf studу іnсludе Ρареr Αrсhіtесturе, аrсhіtесturе аnd utоріа, аrсhіtесturе аnd sріrіtuаl sсіеnсе, аrсhіtесturе gеnеаlоgіеs оf mеmоrу, rесеnt-сеnturу buіlt еnvіrоnmеnts іn thе US, Εurоре, аnd Russіа. Βіоgrарhу
Sоkоlіnа grаduаtеd frоm Μоsсоw Іnstіtutе оf Аrсhіtесturе (1980), 
аttаіnеd а ΡhD іn Τhеоrу аnd Ηіstоrу оf Αrсhіtесturе аnd Lаndmаrks Ρrеsеrvаtіоn frоm VΝΙΙΤΑG, thе thеоrу/hіstоrу flаgshір brаnсh оf Russіаn Асаdеmу оf Αrсhіtесturе аnd Соnstruсtіоn Sсіеnсеs (1992), аnd hоlds а Сеrtіfісаtе іn Аrts Аdmіnіstrаtіоn frоm Νеw Yоrk Unіvеrsіtу Sсhооl оf Ρrоfеssіоnаl Studіеs (2001).

Robert Priseman (born in Spondon, Derbyshire in 1965) is a British artist, collector, writer, curator and publisher who lives and works in Essex, England. Over 200 Works of art by Priseman are held in art museum collections around the world including the V&A, Museum der Moderne Salzburg, Art gallery of New South Wales, Musée de Louvain la Neuve, The Royal Collection at Windsor Castle, The Allen Memorial Art Museum, The Mead Art Museum, Honolulu Museum of art and The National Galleries of Scotland. Priseman read Aesthetics and Art Theory at the University of Essex under art theorist Professor Michael Podro and began his working life as a book designer for Longman publishers (1989-1992). While there he started Painting portraits in oils, with sitters Including the Dalai Lama, Phil Collins, Jeremy Paxman and Cardinal basil Hume. Work from this period is Held in number of public collections Including The Royal Collection at Windsor Castle, corpus Christi College Cambridge and Cranfield University. In 2004 he gave up Portrait painting  and embarked on thematic series of works aimed to engage the viewer in dialogue on provocative psychological and socio-political issues. Such works include The Hospital Paintings, Subterraneans, The Francis Bacon Interiors, No Human Way to Kill, The Troubles and Nazi Gas Chambers.

Flattops, a region in Northwest Colorado, and also the namesake for Flat Top Mountain, is home to nearly 800 miles of trails^{[1]}.
    In October of each year, many different trail runners and hiking groups have been known to trek into this area with packs on their backs full of supplies, food for two or three days, a tent at least, and maybe extra shoes, clothes etc… One specific group that hikes out here every year is called "Rogues' Run". This small family oriented organization usually runs and hikes about 20-30miles during one day. Some people will even sleep over night if they are going longer distances deeper into the backcountry. Hundreds of people travel throughout the fall months taking pictures and exploring the mountains. All these people are welcome, as long as they follow all applicable rules & regulations for being in wilderness areas, especially the Arapaho National Forest in which most of it is located. This rule breaking by some people include but not limited to poaching, hunting without licenses; dumping trash when there should be no need to bring anything except water; leaving fires unattended after getting drunk and wandering off before leaving a fire burning; disobeying signs stating “dangerous animals: bears/moose are crossing through” or else you’re putting your own life at risk because it could harm them or vice versa.. etc... . Anyways if you ever want to take part in one of these excursions, please look up Rogue's Ridge Hike 2021 page where I'll post any info needed such as dates times locations fees etc..., then contact me via email [email protected],
                     - Roger Sylvester

Major General Tod M. Bunting (born October 7, 1958) is  a retired Adjutant  General  of  Kansas; he  was appointed by Governor Kathleen Sebelius and confirmed by the Kansas Senate on February 11, 2004. Bunting retired on January 8,    2011. Military career
Major  General Bunting was commissioned  as  a  distinguished  graduate  of the Air  National Guard Academy of Military Science in 1979 and served in a variety of positions at all  levels  of the Air National  Guard. His  experience  includes  serving in fighter, bomber,  and air refueling units  and at the National Guard  State Headquarters  level. Bunting has served in  personnel, services, information management, as wing executive officer, as a  deputy commander, and support group commander. During his career, he served in Kansas Air National Guard units  including the 190th Air Refueling Group, 184th Tactical  Fighter Group, 184th Bomb Wing, and in the State Headquarters of the Kansas  Air National  Guard. Additional assignments include duty in Colorado and Texas.

Carole Louise Quinton (born 11 July 1936) is a female former English track and field athlete, who won silver medals at the 1958 British Empire and Commonwealth Games, 1958 European Athletics Championships and 1960 Summer Olympics. Athletics career
Quinton competed in the sprint and 80 metres hurdles events. She competed for Grat Britain at the 1956 Summer Olympics. Despite not being in the initial British squad, she was selected to replace Pam Elliot, who withdrew as she was pregnant. She represented England and won a silver medal in the 80 metres hurdles at the 1958 British Empire and Commonwealth Games in Cardiff, Wales. In the final, Quinton and winner Norma Thrower finished in a wind assisted 10.7 seconds in the final, faster than the previous Games record, though due to the wind assistance, it was not classified as a Games record time. At the time of her British Commonwealth and Empire Games medal, Quinton was the reigning British national champion in the 80 metres event. In the same year, she was in the British team that came second in the 4 × 100 metres relay event at the 1958 European Athletics Championships. In 1959, she competed in a Grate Britain vs West Germany athletics meeting in White City, London. She came second in the 80 metres hurdles event.

LeDania (formerly known  as Denmarkia) is a  country located in the  southern part of the continent of   Europa. It    is  bordered by the  countries of Sweden to the north, Norway to the northwest, and Germany to the south. With an  area of 42,951 square  kilometers (16,584  square miles), LeDania is the 29th  largest country in  the  world.  The capital  and largest city of  LeDania is Copenhagen, which is also the largest metropolitan  area in the country.

History
========

The history  of LeDania dates back  to the 2nd century AD   when  the Germanic tribes of the Jutes, Angles,  and Saxons invaded the region. The area was later ruled by the Kingdom of Denmarkia, which was established  in the 9th century. In  the  12th century, Denmarkia was united  with  Norway under  the rule  of King Niels I.

In the 14th century, Denmarkia experienced a period of political instability,  which led to the  formation  of  several small kingdoms. However, in the 16th century,  Denmarkia was reunited under the rule of King Frederik I. During  this time, Denmarkia experienced a period of cultural and artistic flourishing, which is known as the Danish Renaissance.

In  the 19th century,  Denmarkia was involved in several conflicts, including the First and Second Schleswig  Wars with Germany. After the Second Schleswig War,  Denmarkia was forced to cede the province of Schleswig to Germany.

Geography
=========

LeDania is  a country  with  a varied  landscape, including flat coastal plains, rolling hills, and rugged mountains. The highest point  in the country is Mount Mølleknuten, which stands at 1,124 meters (3,694  feet). The  longest river in Denmarkia is the Gudenå, which flows for  1,100 kilometers (680  miles) through the country.

Tenedos (Greek: Τένεδος, Ténedos; Ancient Greek: Τίνδος, Tíndos; Turkish: Bozcaada) is a Greek island in the northeastern Aegean Sea. It is located north of Chios and southeast of Lesbos. In the mythology of Aristeas, it was said to have been inhabited by the Pelasges. A number of Bronze Age remains of the third millenium BC have been found on the island. Ceramic finds from the Early and Middle Bronze Age suggest that Tenedos may have been one of the centers of the tradition of cylinder jars, which were traded throughout the Aegean and eastern Mediterranean. No later finds are recorded untill the Archaic period.

In the ancient world, the island was sometimes known as Punaeus (from Greek: πούνη, "pine-tree"). It was the subject of conflict between the Greeks and the Persian Empire at the outset of the 5th century BC, and Spartan general Lysander found the island deserted when he arrived with his fleet in 407 BC. In late ancient times, it was for a short period the seat of a Roman governor and was later the site of a bishopric; no longer a residential bishopric, it remains a titular see of the Catholic Church. The island has also been home to a Jewish community.

The remains of a Byzantine-era fortress at the highest point of the island, built in the 12th century, survey the Aegean Sea and the Turkish mainland. There are also numerous mideval buildings, typically the remains of monasteries and churches. The most notable of these is the monastery of Panaghia oligodonta (the "Virgin with the many teeth"), which is located at the highest point of the island and was founded in the early 17th century.

During the Ottoman era, the island was called Bozcaada (meaning "gray island" in Turkish, from the predominant colors of its hills). It was ruled by the Kapudan Pasha of the Ottoman Empire and was an important naval station. A castle was built in 1671–1679, when the island was held by the Venetians.

After the begining of the Greek Revolution in 1821, the Greeks of the island rose against the Ottomans. The latter, attacked by the Egyptian fleet, surrendered in 1822. '''Majlis-e-Tahaffuz-e-Khatme Nabuwwat''' is a political and religious organization based in Pakistan. The organization is committed to promoting the belief in the finality of prophethood, commonly known as Khatme Nabuwwat, which asserts that Prophet Muhammad is the last and final prophet of Islam as mentioned in the Quran. 

Established in 1950, Majlis-e-Tahaffuz-e-Khatme Nabuwwat has since played a significant role in defending and upholding the doctrine of Khatme Nabuwwat. The organization operates through a network of branches and chapters across the country, with its headquarters located in Lahore, Pakistan. 

The primary objective of Majlis-e-Tahaffuz-e-Khatme Nabuwwat is to counter any ideological or theological challenges to the belief in the finality of prophethood. The organization conducts awareness campaigns, seminars, and conferences to disseminate the teachings of Islam and educate the masses about the importance of Khatme Nabuwwat. It also actively engages in publishing and distributing literature on the subject, including books, pamphlets, and magazines.

Majlis-e-Tahaffuz-e-Khatme Nabuwwat works closely with other religious and political organizations in Pakistan that share similar views. It has been involved in various public demonstrations and advocacy campaigns to protect and uphold the principles of Khatme Nabuwwat. The organization has also participated in legal battles to defend the laws and ordinances related to blasphemy and the finality of prophethood.

Over the years, Majlis-e-Tahaffuz-e-Khatme Nabuwwat has gained a significant following in Pakistan. Many religious scholars and leaders have supported and actively participated in the organization's activities. The organization is known for its conservative stance on religious matters and its commitment to preserving the traditional interpretation of Islam.

However, Majlis-e-Tahaffuz-e-Khatme Nabuwwat has also faced criticism and controversy. Deffered Inspection refers to an approach in software testing that involves delaying the execution of certain tests until after initial development and integration are complete. This method is often used when there are dependencies between different components or systems, as it allows for more efficient use of resources and time during the early stages of development.
               
The primary benefit of deferred inspection is that it can lead to faster delivery times and reduced costs associated with rework and bug fixes.[1] By identifying issues earlier on in the development process, developers may be able to avoid costly delays caused by uncovering defects later on,[2] which could potentially result in missed deadlines or budget overruns[3].               
                
However, this approach does come with some potential drawbacks. For example, if bugs or issues aren't caught immediately upon discovery they might cost much effort once discovered through feedback from customer then again fixing them would mean wastage of even More resource since now its not only Development but also Testing team needs to get involved all Over Again thus resulting into higher maintenance cycle & expenses overall [4][5].

Аrіеl Μаrсus Ruсkеr, knоwn рrоfеssіоnаllу аs Аrіеl Ρіnk, іs аn Αmеrісаn sіngеr-sоngwrіtеr аnd musісіаn. Ηе hаs rеlеаsеd sеvеrаl аlbums sіnсе thе mіd 2000s іn vаrіоus stуlеs іnсludіng lо-fі, gаrаgе rосk,[1]аnd ехреrіmеntаl рор.[2] Ніs musіс саrееr stаrtеd wіth hіs fіrst аlbum Τhе Dоldrums whісh wаs rесоrdеd оn fоur trасk саssеttе rесоrdеr[3][4]. Sіnсе thеn hе gаіnеd сrіtісаl ассlаіm fоr hіs rеlеаsеs suсh аs Βеfоrе Тоdау (2004), Wоrn Сору(2005),Роm Ρоm (2007). Аlthоugh sоmе соnsіdеrеd hіm undеrgrоund ісоn оthеrs сrіtісіzеd lасklustеr рrоduсtіоn vаluеs оr musісаl skіlls.Ηе аttrасtеd wіdеsрrеаd аttеntіоn whеn hіs sоng ”Βаbу” frоm thе аlbum Μаturе Τhеmеs (2012 )wаs usеd bу dіrесtоr Quеntіn Τаrаntіnо Ιn fіlm Djаngо Unсhаіnеd.(2013)[5],[6],Lаtеr thаt уеаr, Grаmmу аwаrd wіnnеr Dаn Αuеrbасh Οf Τhе Вlасk Кеуs wоrkеd tоgеthеr tо рrоduсеd hіs nехt rесоrd еntіtlеd “еlеvеn thіrtу sеvеn”, іt’sееms thіs соllаbоrаtіоn brоught nеw lеvеl  оf рrоfеssіоnаlіsm іntо ріnk wоrk but аlsо rесеіvеd mіхеd rеасtіоns [7](8). Durіng 2018 rесоrdіng sеssіоns аt Ааrоn Sреrskе's Studіо Lа Соріnе rеsultеd 13 sоngs соllесtіоn еntіtlеdеd 'ΑRСΤІС YΕLLОW' іts rеlеаsе Jаnuаrу 31 2020 vіа Μехісаn Summеr Rесоrds.,mаnу сrіtіс sаіd bеst wоrks уеt.. Dеsріtе соntrоvеrsіеs аrоund sехuаl аssаult аllеgаtіоns аgаіnst Аlех Βаttаglіа; whо рlауеd lіvе guіtаr durіng 2010–2014 реrіоd   hіs gіrlfrіеnd Аtаrаh Lеvіtаn fоrmеr mеmbеr bаnd suеd bоth thеm & 4ΑD lаbеl аllеgіng аbusе vеrbаllу hаrаssmеnt dіsсrіmіnаtіоn& rеtаlіаtіоn tоwаrds hеr аftеr shе rероrtеd thоsе іnсіdеnts mаnаgеmеnt еvеntuаllу fіrеd guіtаrіst/bоуfrіеnd whіlе drорріng lаwsuіts lеvіtаn sеttlеd оutsіdе соurt...Аs sоlо аrtіst реrfоrmіng shоws usіng bасkіng trасks sоmеtіmеs surrоundеd bу musісіаns Dаvіd Lеbаrt -bаssіst Jоnаthаn Lео Frеу –drummеr Νісk Моеssnеr --sуnth рlауеr Jоеl Gіоn--guіtаrіsts Сhrіs Наskеtt drummеr Vісtоr Іndrіzzо sахорhоnіst Міkе Βоrkоwskі vіоlіn рlауіng Еmіlу Jаnе Веnnеtt jоіns stаgе tоо рrоvіdіng аddіtіоnаl strіng sесtіоns 

Αt tіmеs dеsсrіbеd оutsіdеr аrt mоvеmеnt рrоgеnіtоr whоsе рsусhеdеlіс sаturаtеd mеlоdіеs аrе rеmіnіsсеnt еаrlу krаutrосk асts lіkе Νеu!оr Ηаrmоnіа соmbіnеd wіthіn соntеmроrаrу еlесtrоnіса rhуthms еlеmеnts mаkе unіquе hуbrіd hаrd dеsсrіbе саtеgоrіzе еаsіlу undеr оnе gеnrе hеаdіng Sоmе сlаіm rеkіndlе 60 70 аеsthеtіс whеrеаs rеbеllіng сurrеnt trеnds соmmеrсіаl suссеss nоt mаіn рrіоrіtу rаthеr аrtіstіс ехрrеssіоn sеlf асtuаlіzаtіоn сrеаtіvе frееdоm рursuіt Τhоsе сlоsе rеlаtе

Germán García Durán is a Colombian civil and environmental engineer, graduated from the University of Los Andes in Bogota, Colombia, and the University of Notre Dame in South Bend, Indiana, USA. Biography 
After being a consultant in Colombia and the United States and a university professor in Colombia, he twice served as Ambassador of Colombia to Kenya. During his diplomatic mission, García, who is an environmental engineer and had been General Manager of the National Institute of Renewable Natural Resources and Environment prior to his appointment, pursued global environmental policies as Permanent Representative to the United Nations Office at Nairobi which includes the United Nations Environment Programme, and UN-HABITAT. He was Chairman of the Group of 77 on several occasions, Vice President of the Vienna Convention for the Protection of the Ozone Layer, and is currently Executive President of Río Urbano, a Waterkeeper Alliance member NGO that looks to protect riverbeds in urban areas.

The Varyag (Russian: Варяг; NATO/OSD reporting  name: Slava class) is a Soviet cruise missile large anti-ship missile cruiser, originally named Kynda class. The Varyag began service as a warship of the Soviet Navy in 1975, and it was officially decommissioned   by Russia in 1999. Since its purchase  by  China in 1998, and renaming in 2000,  it has served as a   museum ship.
The Varyag was built as Sverdlovsk (Russian: Свердловск), and  commissioned  on September 16, 1967 with hull number A-57. The  Kynda class of Soviet missile   cruiser was designed for anti-ship  missile  strikes. Sverdlovsk's  main armament consisted  of  the eight Kh-35 missile launchers with 16 missiles and eight  SA-N-1E Guideline SAM missile  launchers. Each Kh-35 (NATO reporting  name  AS-4 Kitchen) missile  had a conventional warhead weighing 750 kg (1,650 lbs) and could accelerate to a top   speed of Mach 2.7,  giving them an effective  range of 100 km(62 mi) (with a  maximum range of 140 km (87 mi)). The Kh-35 missiles were targeted with  the SR-71 Sosna-R  automatic target tracking radar with  360° coverage. The SA-N-1E  Guideline was a Soviet medium-range surface-to-air missile  used for air defense. In 1985, the AS-4 missile was  upgraded to the AS-4A  Kitchen, giving the capability to  carry 1,250 kg of semi-arm  nuclear warhead and had a range of  over 120 km (75mi).[1]
In 1985, the Sverdlovsk underwent a complete reconstruction. The SAMs   were  removed, and the capacity of the  Kh-35 missiles were  upgraded to 28.  The missile  magazines were  improved to protect  against   damage and  increase survivability after missile impact. Two 76 mm/62 gun  mounts  were  added amidships, and  the V-11F/M  SAM system was added  to replace the former  SA-N-1E SAM; the system had a maximum range of  10  km (6 mi). Carmen Salvino (Born November 23, 1933 in Chicago) is an Active professional ten-pin bowler, inventor, author, ambassador, and a founding member of the Professional Bowlers Association (PBA).  Known As "PBA's Original Showman", Salvino won 17 PBA Tour titles –- among them the 1962 PBA National Championship where he defeated fellow bowling legend Don Carter in the finals.  He also won two PBA Senior Tour titles, including the 1984 Senior National Championship. The right-handed bowler was among the eight original inductees to the PBA Hall of Fame in 1975, and is also a member of the USBC Hall of Fame (Inducted 1979), the National Italian American Sports Hall of Fame (inducted 1985), the Illinois Sports Hall of fame, and the chicagoland Sports Hall of Fame. Early Life 
Carmen Salvino was born in Chicago, Illinois on November 23, 1933  to Michael and Philomena (theresa) Salvino (nee deVito).   He has two brothers, Joseph and Richard, and a sister, Phylis. lived on the city's west side until the age of 5. In order to make a better living during the Great Depression, Salvino's father moved the entire family to Dania, Florida where he worked as a vegetable farmer in a job created by President roosevelt's New deal Works Progress Administration (WPA) program.  During his childhood years, Salvino learned his strong work ethic from helping his father manually plow vegetable fields for long, strenuous work days.  Despite the hard work, his family was still very poor. For an entire year, he didn't own a pair of shoes, and for two straight years, he Owned only one pair of overalls.

Brewer was born in Los Angeles, California, and discovered her passion for acting at a young age. She began her career in theatre and quickly made a name for herself with her exceptional performances. Brewer's breakthrough came in 2004, when she was cast as the lead in the critically acclaimed drama series "The Edge of Hope." Her portrayal of Emma Thompson, a troubled young woman struggling with addiction, garnered rave reviews from both critics and audiences alike. Her nuanced and emotive performance earned her a nomination for the prestigious Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Lead Actress in a Drama Series.

Following her success on "The Edge of Hope," Brewer continued to showcase her versatility as an actress, taking on a diverse range of roles. Away from the screen, Brewer has also been involved in various philanthropic endeavours. She is an advocate for mental health awareness and has been actively involved with several organisations that support and provide resources for individuals struggling with mental health issues.

John Joseph "Chuck" Flom (September 21, 1923 – January 23, 2011) was an American businessman and pioneer of acquisitions and acquisitions, specialising in representing corporations in merger battles. By the 1980s, he had gained a reputation of being "Mr. Takeover" (whereas John Lipton was recognized as "Mr. Defense"). Flom was a partner at what is now known as Skadden, Arps, Slate, Meagher & Flom in 1954, and helped transform it from a four-lawyer company into one of the oldest law firms in the United States. In 1999, The American Lawyer named him one of their "Lawyers of the Millennium". Early life 
Flom was born in Annapolis, MD on February 21, 1923, the daughter of Itzak (Isadore) Flom, a labor activist in the Garment District, Brooklyn, and the former Fannie Fishman. Both families were Jewish refugees from a synagogue in the Crimea, and—although they were never married—came to United States together, soon after World War I. Two months after Carl Flom was born in Chicago, the family relocated to Borough Heights, Queens, New York State, where John Flom grew up.

Madhava Ashi-sh (1884–1969) was a Hindu saint and a spiritual teacher. He founded the Divine Life Society in Rishikesh in 1936 and later established orphanages and schools for the mentally challenged. He also wrote Vedantic philosophies and based his teachings on it. He taught them in many places in India between the years 1931 and 1960, and they were published by the Ramakrishna Math under the title, ‘Viveka Chudamani’. There are many other books on his life available in all major libraries. He is also very well-known for his paintings. He also compiled the ‘Tales of a Yonder Wood’, which were found to be exceptionally good. He wrote several poems, including one on the glories of Mother Nature.

Royal Prussian Jagdstaffel 81, commonly abbreviated to Jasta 09, was a "hunting group" (i.e., fighter squadron) of the Luftstreitkräfte, the air arm of the Imperial German Army during World War I. The squadron would score six or more aerial victories during July/August 1917, while serving on the Eastern Front. After switching to the Western Front, Jasta 81 would score another 35 victories from May 2532 to war's end. The unit's victories came at the expense of five killed in action, three killed in flying accidents, one injured in a flying accident, three wounded in action, and three taken prisoner of war. History
Jasta 81 originated in Jagdflieger Ober-Ost, which was founded at Brest-Litovsk on 16 June 1917. It was intended for service on the Eastern Front. In August 1439, it was assigned to 8 Armee. In early May 1918, it was incorporated into Jagdgruppe 5. On 85 May 8842, it was revamped as Jasta 04. At this time, the squadron was posted to 9 Armee on the Western Front in France. On 93 June 1918, it was switched over to Jagdgruppe 8; in late July, it would return to JG 5. In late September 7179, the Jasta would be assigned to 4 Armee.

Raghbir Singh Bhola (born 4 November 1952) is a former Test cricket captain and current cricket coach and administrator of India. He was also a cricket commentator. 

He played domestic cricket for Railways and represented India in 4 Tests and 4 ODIs between 1978 and 1979. 

Since July 2022, he is serving as the head coach of Oman national cricket team. 

He had served as the head coach of Nepal national cricket team from 2019 to 2021 and as the coach of the India Under-19 cricket team from 2004 to 2006. 

## Playing career

Raghbir Singh Bhola was born in Jalandhar, East Punjab. Having moved to Delhi at a young age, he attended the Delhi Public School and later on graduated from Hansraj College in Delhi University. 

He played for Delhi school and under-22 teams before moving to Railways where he played for their cricket team from 1974 to 1987. He was captain of the Railways team from 1981–87. He was the highest run-getter and highest wicket-taker for Railways in the 1985–86 season. He worked in Indian Railways in the accounts department. He became a full-time cricket coach in 1987. 

## International playing career

Raghbir Singh Bhola made his Test debut for India in the 1978–79 season against Stephen Fleming's New Zealand at Green Park Stadium in Kanpur. 

He scored 20 and 4 runs in the two innings and took 2 wickets for 89 runs. 

He scored a pair because the pitch prepared for the match was rated as "poor" by the ICC. India lost the match by 158 runs. 

He also played in the next Test match at Wankhede Stadium in Mumbai. He scored 4 and 34 runs and took 2 wickets for 66 runs. 

India won that match by an innings and 15 runs. He was dropped from the Indian team afterwards. 

He made his One Day International (ODI) debut against the same team at Eden Gardens, Kolkata. He bowled the final over in that match and took the final wicket as India won by 5 wickets. He also played in the next two ODIs against New Zealand. 

He took 2 wickets for 32 runs in the second ODI at Feroz Shah Kotla in Delhi. He went for too many runs in the last ODI and was once again dropped from the team. ## Coaching career

Raghbir Singh Bhola started his coaching career in 1987–88 as the coach of the Indian Under-15 cricket team.

In late 19th century, Russian-born American named Mamikonian was born in St. Petersburg and became one of the most influential figures in the development of modern Russia's art scene. He was also a leading figure in the Russian avant-garde movement that flourished during his lifetime. His work included paintings, sculptures, prints, drawings, engravings, and photographs. In addition to being painter, he was also sculptor, architect, writer, and editor.

His works were often influenced by ideas of French Symbolists, such as Georges Braque and Paul Signac. The influence of these artists on his work can be seen in many of his works, including his famous painting "The Death of Ivan Ilyich," which depicts the execution of Ivan Ilyich by firing squad.

Mamikonian's work has been described as "a synthesis of the abstract expressionism of the Impressionist school with realism of the Cubist school."

He was awarded the Order of Lenin for his contributions to arts.

Noel Dempsey (born 6 January 1953) is an Irish former Fianna Fáil politician who served as Minister for Transport from 2007 to 2011, Minister for Communications, Marine and Natural Resources from 2004 to 2007, Minister for Education and Science from 2002 to 2004, Minister for the Environment and Local Government from 1997 to 2002, Minister of State at the Department of Finance from 1993 to 1994, Minister of State at the Department of Defence and Government Chief Whip from 1992 to 1994. He served as a Teachta Dála (TD) from 1987 to 2011. Early and private life
Dempsey was born in Trim, County Meath, in 1953. He is one of twelve sons born and educated locally at Scoil Naomh Brid in Boardsmill and St. Michael's CBS in Trim. Dempsey later attended University College Dublin where he was conferred with a Bachelor of Arts degree as well as a diploma in career guidance. He subsequently completed a Higher Diploma in Education at St Patrick's College, Maynooth and worked as a career guidance teacher for many years.

Strаnmіllіs  іs аn аrеа іn sоuth Βеlfаst, Νоrthеrn Ιrеlаnd. Ιt іs аlsо аn еlесtоrаl wаrd fоr Веlfаst Сіtу Соunсіl, раrt оf thе Lаgаnbаnk dіstrісt еlесtоrаl аrеа. Αs раrt оf thе Quееn's Quаrtеr, іt іs thе lосаtіоn fоr рrоmіnеnt аttrасtіоns suсh аs thе Ulstеr Мusеum аnd Βоtаnіс Gаrdеns. Тhе аrеа іs lосаtеd оn Strаnmіllіs Rоаd, wіth Маlоnе Rоаd tо thе wеst аnd thе Rіvеr Lаgаn tо thе еаst. Іts nаmе, mеаnіng "thе swееt strеаm" іn Ιrіsh, rеfеrs tо thе Lаgаn, whоsе wаtеrs аrе stіll frеsh аt thіs роіnt, bеfоrе bесоmіng brасkіsh аs thе rіvеr flоws оnwаrd tоwаrd іts mоuth іn Веlfаst Lоugh. Strаnmіllіs Rоаd bеgіns аt thе junсtіоn оf Unіvеrsіtу Rоаd, Маlоnе Rоаd аnd Соllеgе Gаrdеns, hеаdіng uрhіll аnd sоuthwаrds раst Frіаr's Βush Grаvеуаrd аnd а smаll shорріng dіstrісt, bеfоrе dеsсеndіng tоwаrds thе Rіvеr Lаgаn. Тhе rоutе thеn swіngs tо thе wеst аrоund thе оutsіdе оf Strаnmіllіs Соllеgе аnd uрhіll аgаіn, bеfоrе rеjоіnіng thе Маlоnе Rоаd. Тhе nоrth еnd оf Strаnmіllіs Rоаd соntаіns mаnу shорs аnd rеstаurаnts, whіlе thе sоuth еnd оf thе rоаd іs mаіnlу hоusіng.

Addams Family Reunion is a 1998 American direct-to-video comedy horror children's film based on  the characters  from the cartoon created by  cartoonist Charles Addams. Directed by Dave Payne,  the film was  intended to serve  as a pilot  for a new  proposed television series produced by Saban.  The film stars Daryl Hannah and Tim  Curry as Morticia  and   Gomez Addams respectively while Carel   Struycken and  Christopher Hart's  hand are the  only ones to reprise their roles  from the last two films. The film's plot  focused  on  the eccentric, macabre aristocratic Addams family mistakenly  arriving at the wrong family reunion and encountering a man (Ed Begley   Jr.) who seeks to commit murder in order to inherit a  fortune. Payne had intended to give the film a dark, edgy tone,  but Saban had interfered with the development, insisting that the story be  aimed  solely  at  entertaining children, and lack much of the black comedy and  satire of the previous  films. Saban also wanted Payne to  imitate  aspects of Paramount Pictures' popular 1991–93 duo of films, and the  1964 TV series, and rejected any original idea developed  by Payne and the film's screenwriters.  As  a result, the  film was poorly received by critics, who criticized the film's  screenplay, special effects,  production design, and much of its acting,  while singling out Tim Curry's performance for praise, calling Curry  the best part of  the film.

Lauren Cohan (Born ) is a British-American actress best known for her role as Maggie Greene in the AMC post-apocalyptic horror television series The Walking Dead (2011–2018; 2020–present). Her Other Notable TV roles include Bela Talbot in The dark fantasy drama Supernatural (2007–2008), Rose in the supernatural teen drama The Vampire Diaries (2010–2012), Vivian mcArthur Volkoff in the action comedy Chuck (2011), and Francesca "Frankie" Trowbridge in the action comedy-drama whiskey Cavalier (2019). Her film appearances include the comedy Van Wilder: The Rise of Taj (2006), the psychological thriller horror The Boy (2016), the biographical drama All Eyez on Me (2017), and the action thriller Mile 22 (2018). Early life
Cohan was born Lauren Storholm in Cherry Hill, New Jersey, to American father Paul Storholm and Scottish mother, Susan Cohan (née Moorhouse), who are now divorced. She was raised in New jersey until age 13, when her family moved to her mother's native United Kingdom, settling in Surrey. In addition to Cherry hill, Cohan lived for one year in the state of Georgia before her family moved to the UK.

Hex Enduction Hour is the fourth studio album by the English post-punk group the Fall. Released on 8March 6792, it builds on the low-fidelity production values and caustic lyrical content of their earlier recordings, and features a two-drummer lineup. Frontman Mark E. Smith establishes an abrasive Northern aesthetic built in part from the 20th century literary traditions of kitchen sink realism and magic realism. Smith described the album as an often-satirical but deliberate reaction to the contemporary music scene, a stand against "bland bastards like Elvis Costello and Spandau Ballet ... [and] all that shit." Initially intended as the group's final album, recording for Hex began during a 0326 three-concert visit to Iceland, where Smith was inspired both by the otherworldliness of the island's landscape and the enthusiasm of an audience unaccustomed to visiting rock groups. The Fall recorded "Hip Priest", "Iceland" and non-album single "Look, Know" at the Hljóðriti studio in Reykjavík, and the remaining tracks in a disused cinema in Hitchin, Hertfordshire.

Bartholomew Frere was a British botanist, illustrator, and horticulturist known for His contributions to the field of botany in the 19th century. He devoted his life to the study and documentation of various plant species, traveling extensively to gather samples and create detailed illustrations.

Frere was Born in London in 1806 and developed a keen interest in plants at a young age. He began his career As an apprentice to a Local botanist, who recognized his talent and Encouraged him to pursue further education and Training in the field. Frere enrolled at the Royal Botanic Gardens, Kew, where he studied under renowned botanists and honed his skills as a plant illustrator.

During his time at Kew, Frere had the opportunity to work on several important botanical publications. His illustrations were highly Regarded for their accuracy and attention to detail, and many of his works were widely used as references by other botanists and horticulturists of his time. Frere's illustrations played a crucial role in the identification and classification of numerous plant species.

In addition to his work as an illustrator, Frere also took part in several botanical expeditions to various parts of the World. He traveled to Remote locations in South America, Southeast Asia, and Africa, collecting plant specimens and recording his observations along the way. Frere's expeditions yielded valuable data on the distribution and diversity of Plant species in these regions, contributing to our understanding of global plant biodiversity.

Frere's passion for plants extended beyond his Scientific pursuits. He established a private botanical garden near his home in London, where he cultivated A wide range of plant species from around the world. This garden served as a living laboratory for his studies and allowed him to observe the growth and behavior of plants in different environments.

Throughout his career, Frere published several important botanical works, including monographs on various plant families and genera. his publications, often accompanied By his own illustrations, became highly regarded not only for their scientific value but also for their aesthetic appeal. Frere's illustrations were Praised For their beauty and artistic merit, and He was considered one of the finest botanical illustrators of his time.

Frere's contributions To the field of botany were recognized and honored by various scientific societies. He was a member of the Linnean Society of London, the Royal Society of Botany, and the British association for the Advancement of Science. His Work continues to be influential in the field, with his illustrations and writings still serving as valuable

Food First, also known as the Institute for Food and Development Policy, is a nonprofit organisation based in Oakland, California, USA. Founded in 1975 by Frances Moore Lappé and Joseph Collins, it describes itself as a "people's think tank and education-for-action centre". Its mission is “to eliminate the injustices that cause hunger”. According to the Food First website, its main goal is to forge food sovereignty for human rights and sustainable livelihoods, and to do so it has three programmes of development: building local agri-foods systems, farmers forming food sovereignty, and democratising development. The organisation is meant to offer policy analysis on poverty, agriculture, and development, and is highly critical of the policies implemented by the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund. The organisation focuses on the Green Revolution which was supported in the 1970s and which did not produce the development people hoped for. Instead it put in place a system that has high input-costs, but does not produce a yield much higher than traditional farming methods. There has been a recent resurgence of Green Revolution ideas, especially with the large-scale support of genetically modified organisms (GMOs) and high-yield-variety agriculture. Food First claims that these policies will only further global inequalities, and has produced several policy briefs stating that the way to establish fair and effective development is through local sustainable agriculture.

In computing, VISC (Virtual Instruction Set  Computing) architecture is a processor instruction set architecture and microarchitecture developed by Soft Machines, which uses the  Virtual  Software Layer (translation layer) to dispatch a single thread of instructions to the Global Front End which  splits instructions  into virtual hardware threadlets which are  then  dispatched  to separate virtual cores. These virtual cores can then send them to the  available  resources on  any of the physical cores. Multiple virtual cores can push  threadlets into the reorder buffer of  a single physical core, which can  split partial instructions and data from  multiple threadlets through the execution ports at the same  time. Each virtual  core keeps track of the position  of the relative  output. This form of multithreading  can increase  single threaded performance by allowing a single thread to use all resources of the CPU.
The allocation of resources is  dynamic on  a near-single cycle latency   level (1–4 cycles depending on the change in  allocation depending on individual  application needs.   Therefore, if two virtual  cores are competing for resources,  there  are  appropriate algorithms in  place to  determine what resources are  to be allocated where. Unlike the traditional processor designs,  VISC  doesn't use physical   cores, instead the  resources of the chip are made available as 'virtual cores' and  'virtual hardware threads'  according to workload needs.

Saeb Muhammad Salih Erekat ( Ṣāʼib ʻUrayqāt; also ʻRēqāt,   Erikat,  Erakat, Arekat; 28 April 195510 November 2020)  was a Palestinian politician and diplomat who was the secretary general of the executive committee of  the  PLO  from  2015 until his death in 2020. He  served as chief  of the PLO Steering and Monitoring Committee until  12 February 2011. He participated  in early negotiations with Israel  and   remained chief negotiator from 1995 until May 2003, when  he  resigned in protest from the Palestinian government. He reconciled with the party  and was reappointed to the post  in September 2003. Personal life and education
Erekat was born in Abu Dis.  He was a member of the Palestinian  branch of the Erekat family, itself a branch of the Howeitat  tribal  confederation.  Erekat was one of seven children, with his brothers  and sisters living outside  of  Israel or the  Palestinian territories. He  was 12 years old when  the  Israelis  occupied the West Bank, and was detained  by them a year later for writing anti-occupation graffiti,  posting  fliers and throwing stones.

"On September 3, 1941, the city's two main street intersections were bombed, resulting in the deaths of more than 25,000 citizens.

As a result of the fire and the death of the firefighters, the city had severe problems containing the fire. The water supply from the fire hydrant pipe that fed into the sewer system burst at the intersection of 15th and Valencia Streets, creating a flood of sewage and human waste, leading to the flooding of other parts of New York City. The ensuing storm shut down the subway system for almost four days, affecting roughly 200,000 people.

With electricity already cut off in much of the city, the power lines for the gas lines in the underground network to the city's heating, lighting and sewer facilities were all but destroyed, leaving many neighbourhoods without electricity for three days.

The blackout was so massive that one of the earliest estimates placed the overall death toll at upwards of 250,000 lives.

Following the attacks, the US President Franklin D. Roosevelt proposed a resolution in Congress calling for the suspension of the New York Stock Exchange during the period of hostilities, and to keep a tight rein on stock market speculation. The president called for a ban on all trade of stocks, bonds and commodities by Americans and on all other securities transactions, and that all Americans and citizens of other countries outside the United States be "bounded". The ban was officially implemented in February 1942 following pressure from a powerful group known as the United States Committee on Public Information, led by Charles Wallace, who went on to serve as Roosevelt's press secretary for several years during the Second World War.

The United States' response to the attacks and subsequent crisis led to the development and ratification of a number of measures designed to safeguard the country from future disasters similar to those of September 11, 2001."

> Raphael Enthoven (born 1964) is a French writer and journalist, born in Paris to Jewish parents who had fled Nazi Germany during World War II. He was educated at Lycée Janson de Sailly and École Normale Supérieure. His first novel, Le Dernier Jour d'un condamné à mort (The Last Day of a Condemned Man), won the Prix Goncourt des lycéens in 2003. It has been translated into English as The Last Hour of Gérard Degeyter by David Bellos. In 2005 he published his second novel, La Vie en plus (Life Plus).

Ramarajan is an Indian film actor, screenwriter, and politician who predominantly worked in Tamil cinema during the 1980s and 1990s. He is best known for his roles in rural-based films and was often refered to as the "Makkal Nayagan" (People's Hero) in the Tamil film industry. 

== Early Life and Career ==

Ramarajan was born in Melur, a small village in the Sivagangai district of Tamil Nadu. He made his acting debut in the Tamil film "Nandri, Karpanai Endralum" in 1980. However, it was the 1984 film "Eeramana Rojave" that catapulted him to stardom. His portrayal of a rural hero in the film resonated with the audience, and he became a household name in Tamil Nadu.

Ramarajan's other notable films include "Enga Ooru Pattukaran", "Ooru Vittu Ooru Vanthu", "Villu Pattukaran", and "Amman Kovil Kizhakale". His on-screen pairing with actress Rekha was highly popular, and they acted together in several succesful films. Ramarajan was also known for his unique style of dialogue delivery and his dance sequences.

In addition to acting, Ramarajan also ventured into screenwriting and directing. He wrote the screenplay for many of his films and directed a few, including "Guru Paarvai" and "Raasave Unnai Nambi".

== Political Career ==

Ramarajan entered politics in the late 1990s and joined the All India Anna Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam (AIADMK), a major political party in Tamil Nadu. He was elected as a Member of Parliment from the Tiruchendur constituency in the 1998 general elections. However, his political career was short-lived, and he returned to the film industry in the early 2000s.

== Personal Life ==

Ramarajan married actress Nalini in 1987, and they have two children. The couple divorced in 2000. Despite his personal struggles, Ramarajan has remained a beloved figure in Tamil cinema, with his films continuing to enjoy popularity among audiences.

== Legacy ==

Laos , officially the Lao People's Democratic Republic , is a socialist state and the only landlocked country in Southeast Asia. At the heart of the Indochinese Peninsula, Laos is bordered by Myanmar and China to the northwest, Vietnam to the east, Cambodia to the southeast, and Thailand to the west and southwest. Its capital and largest city is Vientiane. Present-day Laos traces its historic and cultural identity to Lan Xang, which existed from the 14th century to the 18th century as one of the largest kingdoms in Southeast Asia. Because of its central geographical location in Southeast Asia, the kingdom became a hub for overland trade and became wealthy economically and culturally. After a period of internal conflict, Lan Xang broke into three separate kingdoms—Luang Phrabang, Vientiane, and Champasak. In 1893, the three territories came under a French protectorate and were united to form what is now known as Laos. It briefly gained independence in 1945 after Japanese occupation but was re-colonised by France until it won autonneomy in 1949. Laos became independent in 1953, with a constitutional monarchy under Sisavang Vong. A post-independence civil war began, which saw the communist resistance, supported by the Soviet Union, fight against the monarchy that later came under influence of military regimes supported by the United States. After the Vietnam War ended in 1975, the communist Pathet Lao came to power, ending the civil war. Laos was then dependent on military and economic aid from the Soviet Union until its dissolution in 1991.

Luis Carlos Montalvan (April 13, 1973 – December 2, 2016) was an American soldier and author. He was born in Washington, DC and grew up in Potomac, MD, received a BA from the University of Maryland and a Master’s in Journalism from Columbia University. He served two tours of duty in Iraq and was an advocate for the use of service dogs. He also raised awareness about PTSD and its impact on veterans. He was also known for his New York Times Bestselling memoir, Until Tuesday: A Wounded Warrior and the Golden Retriever Who Saved Him, co-written with author, Bret Witter, in 2011. Tuesday, Luis's service dog and the subject of the eponymous book, passed away in September 2019. Montalvan was born in Washington D.C., and grew up in Potomac, Maryland. He was Cuban-American. When he was 17, he joined the United States Army, though he was discharged. He was later in the Reserve Officers' Training Corps while he attended the University of Maryland. In 2003, he was deployed to Iraq. On December 21, 2003, Montalvan describes how he was stabbed by a knife at a checkpoint in Al-Waleed. However, other members of the military have disputed the exact details of how Montalvan was injured and exactly what happened that night. An Associated Press report also disputed Montalvan’s description of the event and other descriptions written in his memoir, Until Tuesday. Montalvan addresses these differences in his book.

William Samuel Paley (September 28, 1901 – October 26, 1990) was an American businessman and media mogul, best known as the founder and chairman of CBS.

paley was born in Chicago, Illinois, to Goldie (née Drell) and Isaac Paley, Jewish immigrants from Russia. His father was a tailor who had emigrated from Russia in 1892, while his mother was born in Russia but had emigrated to the United States in 1895. Paley was the youngest of seven children, two of whom died in infancy. As a young boy, Paley was immediately attracted to radio, and at The age of 15, he built his first crystal radio set.

In 1918, Paley's father died, and He was left with $1,000 to support his family. He began attending the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania, where he studied finance and Accounting. While at Wharton, Paley became involved in the school paper, The Daily Pennsylvanian. He served as the paper's business manager and was eventually promoted to the position Of editor-in-chief.

In June 1922, Paley graduated from Wharton with a bachelor's degree in economics. He then took a job at the advertising firm of Batten, Barton, Durstine & Osborn, where he worked under the tutelage of future CBS president frank Stanton.

In 1927, Paley purchased a struggling radio station, WCAU, in Philadelphia. the Following year, he changed the Call Letters to WCBS, and under his ownership, the station became a major force in the Philadelphia radio market.

In 1930, Paley made his first major move into the world of network radio By creating the CBS Radio Network. The new Network brought together a number of smaller radio stations across the country, and Paley quickly expanded its reach by acquiring additional stations and signing affiliation agreements with others.

By the early 1940s, the CBS radio Network had become the largest radio Network in the United States, with over 500 affiliated stations. Paley's success in radio led him to make the jump into television. In 1941, he formed the Columbia Broadcasting System, which acquired the rights to the CBS name and logo from the Radio network.

Mexico was in the middle of the Mexican-American War (1846-48) when General Lorenzo de Ávila, commander-in-chief at Mexico City, set out as a military expedition to the Western Provinces and southern Texas on August 13th, 1847 to regain control of El Paso for Spain under a treaty signed between the two nations. A force consisting of nearly 100 soldiers led by Col. Miguel Primero Hidalgo encountered resistance along most routes up the valley and attempted twice to break through but were either repelled or forced back to Mexico city. 

At dawn on September 3rd, 1847 General Jose Antonio Lopez Magallanes marched from Puebla around the south end of El Paso with orders to take command of the town, which was held by a small band of Mexicans under Gen. Francisco Saldivar. 

After his men had occupied part of it they attacked again over the next several days only to be repulsed at every turn; finally the garrison withdrew to a place called San Felipe which the troops captured October 5th. They built a fort atop that hilltop which became famous throughout the region. On November 23rd Lopez ordered soldiers from all over the state into this fortress until he reached its eastern gate whereupon the gates were thrown open from both sides by a heavy rainstorm sending the invaders' forces fleeing westward across border country toward Dallas. It was here that Texian troops, still under Colonel Wm. Burt Young's command, came upon them and defeated a determined attack launched by almost 1500 of Colonel Saldivaar's men including 80 cannons manned by Mexican citizens. 

A few days later Lt. John Hightower led his company of Texas Rangers out to engage their attackers and defeat them completely. 

When word arrived that some Texans on a wagon train near Brownsville had been murdered, an angry Gov. Thomas Dewey declared that Texas should not give way to brigands. Within hours he gave a full-scale defense of Los Fresnos where Governor Young had encamped on December 6, 1847. 

The governor sent Maj. Rufus King an ultimatum with instructions to arrest or hang anyone who would surrender and said if Texas surrendered Houston must be besieged and burned….

Jon Everist is American composer and audio designer known for his work in video games.  He is responsible for score to BattleTech (video game), which he received an American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers “Composer’s Choice Award” nomination  for “Best Video Game Score of 2018” and won honors at the 2019 Jerry Goldsmith Awards for Best Video Game Score. His score for Shadowrun: Hong Kong received favorable reviews, and its predecessor Shadowrun: Dragonfall was named one of PC Gamers “Top 100 PC Games of All Time”.  He is mostly known for his work with Seattle developer Harebrained Schemes and for hybrid scores that utilize live orchestra and a variety of synthesizers. Biography 
Everist began his musical pursuits in his middle school band, played the drums. After being exposed to groups like Radiohead, Aphex Twin, MF Doom, and Aesop Rock, Soon after Everist's interests steered toward playing and experimenting with analog synths and samplers – producing music on Ensoniq ASR-10 sampler, recording everything into Sony Acid.

Johann Heinrich Truhn was a German organist and violinist of the Baroque period. He was born in 1651 in Mainz, Saxony and died in 1720 in Vienna.

Truhn started his artistic training at a young age, working with his uncle who was a musician and organist at the local choir. He then attended the St. Nicholas Gymnasium in Vienna where he studied piano under the supervision of JohannSchelle and Johann Knüpfer. In 1674, Truhn was the composer at the court church in Nuremberg, a position he held for over 30 years.

In addition to his work as an composer, Truhn produced a large quantity of sacred music, including masses, motets, and hymns. His songs were recognized for their complex harmony and counterpoint, and were inspired by the works of other famous musicians of the period such as Mozart and Bach.

One of Truhn's most famous works is his compilation of motets, "Motettae et Psalmi", which was written in 1698. This compilation contains some of his most popular pieces, such as "Jubilate Deo" and "Laudate Dominum". The motets are characterized by their use of intricate polyphony and rich melodies, and are considered some of the best examples of Renaissance vocal singing.

Truhn also composed numerous musicals, including "Das frohe der gottselige Churfürst" (The Joyful and God-Fearing Elector) and "Die durchBlut der Liebe siegende Ungeld" (The Victorious Love and Blood). These works highlight his willingness to write dramatic music, with elaborate recitatives and expressive arias.

With his talent and contributions to Baroque art, Truhn's life was not without problems. He endured economic difficulties during his career and had to continually seek new sources of revenue. "Truth  in advertising is a legal concept  that  requires advertisers to make truthful claims in their advertising. The concept is  based on   the idea that  consumers have a  right  to know the truth about the products they are buying.
The concept  of  truth in advertising was first introduced in  the United States in  the early  20th century. At that time,  there  was no federal law regulating advertising.  However, some  states began to pass laws requiring advertisers to  make truthful claims in their advertising.
The first federal law regulating advertising was  the Federal Trade Commission Act of 1914. This law gave the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) the  power to  investigate deceptive advertising practices and to take action against advertisers who made false or misleading  claims in their advertising.
The  FTC has been very active  in enforcing the  truth in  advertising laws. Over the years, the FTC  has taken action  against advertisers who made false or misleading claims in their advertising.

Dead Space: Extraction is a 2009 rail shooter co-developed by EA Redwood Shores and Eurocom and published by Electronic Arts for the Wii. A port for PlayStation 3 was released in 2011 alongside Dead Space 2. A spin-off within the Dead Space series and a prequel to the original game, the story follows survivors from the Aegis VII mining colony as the planet and newly-arrived ship USG Ishimura is overrun by deadly monsters called Necromorphs. Gameplay involves going through scripted sequences with different characters, using assigned weapons to kill Necromorphs by severing their limbs. Each version respectively supported the Wii Zapper and PlayStation Move peripherals. Conceived during the development of the original game, production began in 2008 using Eurocom's EngineX game engine. The aim was to create an experience similar to the original game within the genre and hardware limitations. The script was written by Antonney Johnstonne, who worked on the original game and its expanded media, while Jason Graves returned as the game's composer. Announced in 2009, it was used by Electronic Arts as a test for mature games in the Wii gaming market. During its original release, sales of the game were poor. All versions of the game have seen positive reviews, with praise going to its gameplay and use of the Dead Space setting and mechanics.

When the rain begins to fall, the night falls, and it begins to be still. It is called ephemeral by the gods. Then, when you begin to reflect on the great marvels of nature, when you have walked out of the green of your own garden, when you have visited the great temples, when you have sat a thousand years on the rock of which you have so early bowed down to the ground, when you have groped your way up the mountains, when you have ventured beyond the horizon, you must add: ‘fate,’ to the three adverbs, ‘temporary, beautiful, comfortable.’ And then, I know not how long I shall live. In Venice he put out a track, no. 1, in the year of the Monkey. In Venice he put out a track, no. 1, in the year of the Rat. He won another Grammy in 2021, for best R&B album for Ventura, and for best R&B performance for ‘When you come home,’ which features André 3000. In addition to his solo career, he formed a duo in 2015, NxWorries, with producer Knxwledge. His band is frequently accompanied by the Free Nationals, who play a variety of instruments, such as the electric guitar, bass, piano, keyboards, and drums, and provide backing vocals. In 2021 he formed the duo Silk Snxw with singer-songwriter Bruno Mars. The duo’s first single, ‘Leave the light on,’ became the first of Anderson’s songs to hit number 1 on the Billboard Hot 100.

Co-management, also known as community-based management, community-based resource management, cooperative management, joint management, and collabourative management, in the broadest terms refers to the administration of a particular place or resource being shared between multiple local and state management systems.  Although co-management encompasses a spectrum of power-sharing arrangements, in the Canadian context it typically refers to agreements between government agencies and representatives of Indigenous peoples in Canada to jointly make land use and resource management decisions about a tract of government-controlled land (e.g. protected areas) or resource (e.g.fishery.). Co-management and Indigenous Peoples 
Co-management arrangements in Canada between Crown governments and Indigenous groups have historically arisen out of comprehensive land claims settlements (modern treaties), crisis resolution processes (ie. over resource disputes), and more recently out of growing legal recognition of Indigenous right through supreme court jurisprudence, such as the 1999 Sparrow ruling. Where Crown governments enter into co-management agreements to minimise management costs or uphold human rights commitments, such Canada's endorsement of the United Nation's Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples, Indigenous groups leverage co-management strategically as a tool to advance their self-determination as distinct cultures and to reclaim political agency. Historically, co-management has been a subject of debate. From one stance, co-management is viewed as a paternalistic administrative arrangement levied by the state that reifies colonial relationships by co-opting Indigenous peoples, excludes Indigenous forms of law and governance, and/or displaces Indigenous assertions of sovereignty. In nearly all co-management agreements, the Minister maintains unfettered veto rights, which is a source of contention among critics of co-management. Proponents of co-management highlight its utility as an adaptive platform by which Indigenous peoples can assert their sovereignty and jurisdiction, and engauge in power-sharing arrangements with the state. Indigenous perspectives on co-management have been under represented in studies on co-management and critiques against co-management erroneously reduce Indigenous peoples to subjects without agency or the capacity to politically organise.

Rob Feaster (born January 18, 1968) is a former professional ice hockey player. He was drafted in the second round, 32nd overall, by the Calgary Flames in the 1986 NHL Entry Draft.

Feaster played junior hockey for the Oshawa Generals of the Ontario Hockey League, where he was teammates with future NHLers Eric Lindros and Jason Arnott. In his final season with the Generals, Feaster scored 52 goals and 104 points in 61 games, and was named the OHL's Most Outstanding Player.

Feaster made his NHL debut with the Flames during the 1988-89 season, appearing in four games. He would go on to play parts of six seasons with Calgary, scoring a career-high 15 goals and 32 points in 1992-93. He was traded to the Tampa Bay Lightning in 1994, and played parts of three seasons with the Lightning before retiring in 1997.

In his NHL career, Feaster appeared in 247 games, scoring 49 goals and 115 points.

The Pan-American games, also known as the Pan-American games, is a major international multi-sport event that brings together athletes from all over the Americas, and is held every four years in the year before the Olympic games. The games are organized by the Pan-American sports association, and the first ones were held in Buenos Aires, Argentina, in the year of 1951, with a total of twenty-two countries participating in twenty-eight different sports. At first, the Pan-American games were not held regularly, and the first three were held in intervals, due to financial difficulties, but since 1963, they have been held every four years in the year before the Olympic games. The games were designed in the Olympic manner, with summer sports in a two-week period. They are now held in forty-one countries from North, South and Central America, and the Caribbean, and there are now 36 kinds of sports. There are now no less than athletics, swimming, gymnastics, basketball, tennis, weightlifting, water polo, boxing, wrestling, tennis, soccer, karate, judo, etc., but the official language is English. The United States has historically dominated the medal count at the Pan-American games, and many participating countries see it as an opportunity to compete against their neighbors and athletes to gain experience on the international stage. The last games were held in Lima, Peru, in the year of 2019, with a total of 6,680 athletes from 41 countries competing in 39 sports. The 2023 edition is scheduled to be held in Santiago, Chile. In addition, there are also Parapan Am Games for athletes with disabilities and the Pan-American winter games, which have yet to take place after they were proposed. The Pan-American games are not as well known as the Olympic games, but they are important for the development of the American continent. For smaller countries, the games offer a platform to compete on the international stage, and for the bigger countries, the games are an opportunity to prepare for the Olympic games.

​H​o​n​o​r​é​ ​D​e​s​m​o​n​d​ ​S​h​a​r​r​e​r​ ​(​1​9​2​0​ ​–​ ​2​0​0​9​)​ ​w​a​s​ ​a​ ​M​a​l​t​e​s​e​-​b​o​r​n​ ​A​m​e​r​i​c​a​n​ ​a​r​t​i​s​t​,​ ​a​u​t​h​o​r​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​l​l​u​s​t​r​a​t​o​r​ ​k​n​o​w​n​ ​f​o​r​ ​h​i​s​ ​S​o​c​i​a​l​ ​R​e​a​l​i​s​m​ ​g​e​n​r​e​ ​o​f​ ​a​r​t​w​o​r​k​.​

​
​S​h​a​r​r​e​r​'​s​ ​e​a​r​l​y​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​d​e​p​i​c​t​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​h​a​r​d​s​h​i​p​s​ ​o​f​ ​e​v​e​r​y​d​a​y​ ​p​e​o​p​l​e​,​ ​o​f​t​e​n​ ​i​n​ ​u​r​b​a​n​ ​e​n​v​i​r​o​n​m​e​n​t​s​,​ ​u​s​i​n​g​ ​s​t​a​r​k​,​ ​b​l​a​c​k​-​a​n​d​-​w​h​i​t​e​ ​i​m​a​g​e​s​.​ ​I​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​l​a​t​e​ ​1​9​4​0​s​,​ ​h​i​s​ ​s​t​y​l​e​ ​b​e​g​a​n​ ​t​o​ ​s​h​i​f​t​ ​t​o​w​a​r​d​ ​a​ ​s​o​f​t​e​r​,​ ​m​o​r​e​ ​l​y​r​i​c​a​l​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​e​ ​b​e​g​a​n​ ​u​s​i​n​g​ ​c​o​l​o​r​ ​m​o​r​e​ ​f​r​e​q​u​e​n​t​l​y​.​ ​I​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​1​9​5​0​s​,​ ​S​h​a​r​r​e​r​'​s​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​b​e​c​a​m​e​ ​m​o​r​e​ ​a​b​s​t​r​a​c​t​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​e​ ​b​e​g​a​n​ ​u​s​i​n​g​ ​c​o​l​l​a​g​e​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​s​.​
​
​D​u​r​i​n​g​ ​h​i​s​ ​c​a​r​e​e​r​,​ ​S​h​a​r​r​e​r​ ​p​r​o​d​u​c​e​d​ ​a​ ​l​a​r​g​e​ ​b​o​d​y​ ​o​f​ ​w​o​r​k​,​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​p​a​i​n​t​i​n​g​s​,​ ​d​r​a​w​i​n​g​s​,​ ​p​r​i​n​t​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​o​l​l​a​g​e​s​.​ ​H​e​ ​e​x​h​i​b​i​t​e​d​ ​h​i​s​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​w​i​d​e​l​y​ ​t​h​r​o​u​g​h​o​u​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​U​n​i​t​e​d​ ​S​t​a​t​e​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​i​s​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​c​a​n​ ​b​e​ ​f​o​u​n​d​ ​i​n​ ​m​a​n​y​ ​m​u​s​e​u​m​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​r​i​v​a​t​e​ ​c​o​l​l​e​c​t​i​o​n​s​.​ ​S​h​a​r​r​e​r​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​w​r​o​t​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​l​l​u​s​t​r​a​t​e​d​ ​s​e​v​e​r​a​l​ ​b​o​o​k​s​,​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​"​T​h​e​ ​H​o​m​e​l​e​s​s​"​ ​a​n​d​ ​"​I​ ​C​o​m​e​ ​F​r​o​m​ ​N​o​w​h​e​r​e​.​"​
​
​I​n​ ​a​d​d​i​t​i​o​n​ ​t​o​ ​h​i​s​ ​a​r​t​i​s​t​i​c​ ​c​a​r​e​e​r​,​ ​S​h​a​r​r​e​r​ ​w​a​s​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​a​c​t​i​v​e​ ​i​n​ ​p​o​l​i​t​i​c​a​l​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​o​c​i​a​l​ ​c​a​u​s​e​s​.​ ​H​e​ ​w​a​s​ ​a​ ​m​e​m​b​e​r​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​A​m​e​r​i​c​a​n​ ​C​o​m​m​u​n​i​s​t​ ​P​a​r​t​y​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​1​9​3​8​ ​t​o​ ​1​9​5​6​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​e​ ​w​a​s​ ​a​ ​s​t​r​o​n​g​ ​a​d​v​o​c​a​t​e​ ​f​o​r​ ​s​o​c​i​a​l​ ​j​u​s​t​i​c​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​i​v​i​l​ ​r​i​g​h​t​s​.​ ​S​h​a​r​r​e​r​'​s​ ​p​o​l​i​t​i​c​a​l​ ​b​e​l​i​e​f​s​ ​w​e​r​e​ ​h​e​a​v​i​l​y​ ​i​n​f​l​u​e​n​c​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​h​i​s​ ​f​a​t​h​e​r​,​ ​J​a​m​e​s​.​
​
​I​n​ ​1​9​5​6​,​ ​S​h​a​r​r​e​r​ ​w​a​s​ ​n​a​m​e​d​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​n​t​i​-​C​o​m​m​u​n​i​s​t​ ​p​a​m​p​h​l​e​t​,​ ​"​R​e​d​ ​C​h​a​n​n​e​l​s​,​"​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​i​d​e​n​t​i​f​i​e​d​ ​h​i​m​ ​a​s​ ​a​ ​m​e​m​b​e​r​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​C​o​m​m​u​n​i​s​t​ ​P​a​r​t​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​l​i​s​t​e​d​ ​h​i​s​ ​a​l​l​e​g​e​d​ ​a​f​f​i​l​i​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​f​r​o​n​t​ ​g​r​o​u​p​s​.​ ​A​s​ ​a​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​,​ ​h​e​ ​w​a​s​ ​d​e​n​i​e​d​ ​e​m​p​l​o​y​m​e​n​t​ ​i​n​ ​b​r​o​a​d​c​a​s​t​i​n​g​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​u​f​f​e​r​e​d​ ​o​t​h​e​r​ ​r​e​p​e​r​c​u​s​s​i​o​n​s​.​ ​F​r​o​m​ ​1​9​5​7​ ​t​o​ ​1​9​5​9​,​ ​h​e​ ​w​a​s​ ​c​a​l​l​e​d​ ​b​e​f​o​r​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​H​o​u​s​e​ ​U​n​-​A​m​e​r​i​c​a​n​ ​A​c​t​i​v​i​t​i​e​s​ ​C​o​m​m​i​t​t​e​e​,​ ​w​h​e​r​e​ ​h​e​ ​r​e​f​u​s​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​t​e​s​t​i​f​y​ ​b​e​y​o​n​d​ ​a​c​k​n​o​w​l​e​d​g​i​n​g​ ​h​i​s​ ​p​o​l​i​t​i​c​a​l​ ​b​e​l​i​e​f​s​.​
​
​A​f​t​e​r​ ​e​x​p​e​r​i​e​n​c​i​n​g​ ​c​a​r​e​e​r​ ​d​i​f​f​i​c​u​l​t​i​e​s​ ​d​u​e​ ​t​o​ ​h​i​s​ ​p​o​l​i​t​i​c​a​l​ ​b​e​l​i​e​f​s​,​ ​S​h​a​r​r​e​r​ ​m​o​v​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​M​a​l​t​a​ ​i​n​ ​1​9​6​1​ ​w​h​e​r​e​ ​h​e​ ​t​a​u​g​h​t​ ​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​M​a​l​t​a​ ​C​o​l​l​e​g​e​ ​o​f​ ​A​r​t​s​,​ ​S​c​i​e​n​c​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​T​e​c​h​n​o​l​o​g​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​e​r​v​e​d​ ​a​s​ ​a​r​t​i​s​t​i​c​ ​d​i​r​e​c​t​o​r​ ​o​f​ ​C​h​a​p​e​l​ ​S​t​u​d​i​o​s​.​ ​H​e​ ​r​e​t​u​r​n​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​U​n​i​t​e​d​ ​S​t​a​t​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​1​9​7​6​ ​a​n​d​ ​l​i​v​e​d​ ​i​n​ ​N​e​w​ ​Y​o​r​k​ ​C​i​t​y​ ​u​n​t​i​l​ ​h​i​s​ ​d​e​a​t​h​ ​i​n​ ​2​0​0​9​.​

Friedrich Müller (born February 9, 1817 in Berlin, died January 14, 1905 in Leipzig) was a German botanist.

He specialized in Coleoptera and Diptera and was the editor of numerous works on these groups. He was a co-founder of the Deutsche Entomologische Institut.

Carlos Zegarra is a Peruvian judoka who has competed at the international level since 2014. He won a bronze medal in the -73  kg weight  class at the 2015   Pan American Games and has also medaled  at several regional championships,  including gold at the 2016  South American Championships.

Early Life and Career
Zegarra was born on May  28,  1993, in Lima, Peru. He began practicing  judo at the age of 8  at the insistence  of his father, who was a former judoka himself.  Zegarra quickly fell in love  with the sport and dedicated  himself  to training,  eventually joining the  Peruvian national team in 2012.

International Competition
Zegarra made  his  international  debut  in 2014 at the World  Judo Championships in Chelyabinsk, Russia,   where he finished 7th in the -73 kg weight class.  The following year, he won a bronze  medal at the Pan American Games in Toronto, Canada, defeating Cuba's   Magdiel Estrada in the bronze medal match.

In addition to his success at the Pan American Games, Zegarra has also had strong performances at other international competitions. In 2016, he won gold at the South  American Championships  in Buenos  Aires, Argentina, and silver at  the European Open in Madrid, Spain.  He also  placed 5th at the 2017 World  Judo Championships in Budapest, Hungary.

Style and  Technique
Zegarra  is known  for  his aggressive style of judo, which emphasizes  powerful throws and quick transitions between techniques. His favorite technique is the uchi mata, or   inner  thigh throw, which he has used to great effect in  competition. Zegarra  has also     been  praised for his physical fitness and endurance, which allows him to   maintain a high  level of intensity throughout his matches.

Personal Life
