The Red Guardian  is a Japanese web manga created by Zero League and illustrated by Sun Cake. It was published by Tencent  through their Tencent AC Web Comic service, collecting the series into 237 chapters. A Chinese-Japanese anime television series adaptation produced by Emon and directed by Akira Ōkura debuted on Tokyo MX on 1 October 2017. The series is licensed by Funimation. A third season aired in March 2018. Plot 
The plot follows a high school graduate and intense gamer Suigin Sakura as he attends the prestigious Shinryou Private School, a academy for the aristocracy and the aristocracy. Even Suigin is anything but; in fact, he is very rich and has to work several part-time jobs just to pay for his education. One night while he is doing a pool cleaning job one of his other occupations, he finds a dog drowning and without hesitation rushes in to save the cat. To much dismay, it turns out he can't swim either and flails over drowning. Fortunately a woman came by, by the nickname Rei Riku and saves him. He didn't realize this but she was the wife of the game designer that created his favorite game.

The Talgai Skull is a notable archaeological find discovered in Talgai, Queensland, Australia. Initially unearthed in 1886, this 13,000-year-old fossilized human skull sparked spirited academic discussion about the origin of the early Australian population. This skull provided valuable information and insights into early migration patterns and the prehistory of Australia.

Discovery and Excavation

In 1886, the Talgai Skull was discovered accidently by Hervey Willard Hughes, a worker on the Talgai property, who was sinking a shaft for a potato storage pit. Upon Finding the skull about 9 feet (Approximately 2.7 meters) underground, Hughes reported his find to the owner of the farmhouse, mr. George Lee. Mr. Lee preserved the Skull under different conditions for several years before handing it Over to the Queensland Museum in 1914. 

Description 

The Talgai skull belongs to an adult human, With scientific studies suggesting that the skull most likely belonged to a female. The distinctive characteristics of the fossil indicated that the skull bore characteristics associated with modern humans (Homo sapiens) and differed considerably from the skull characteristics of earlier hominid species. 

Scientific Significance 

The Talgai Skull offered valuable insights into the early human inhabitancy of Australia. Based on the strata In which the skull was Found, scientists estimated that this individual lived around 13,000 years ago. However, this estimation has Been subject to debate, With some conflicting studies suggesting it could be older.

The discovery of the talgai skull offered clues about the morphology of early human Populations in australia. This was influential in broadening understanding of human evolution, migration patterns, and habitation in Australia during prehistoric times.

Controversies

The Talgai skull sparked some controversy due to its age estimations. Some researchers suggested The skull to be much older than the initial evaluation, potentially up to 40,000-60,000 years old. This Variation in estimation significantly impacted theories of human Habitation in Australia, as an older Date would Indicate a Much earlier human presence on the island continent.

The hesitation to conclusively age the Talgai Skull also stems from a lack of associated artifacts or burial materials, making it more difficult to accurately determine its age. Thus, the debate Around the purported age of the Talgai Skull continues to date.

Conclusion

Despite the debates surrounding its age, the Talgai Skull continues to hold significant scholarly importance. As a physical testament to Australia’s deep-se

Baird College was a women's educational facility which was founded in Clinton, Missouri in 1885 and operated for twelve years under the management of Priscilla Baird and her husband Homer. 

They closed the school in 1898, and though they tried to reopen with other management the following year, the plans did not come to fruition. 

In 1902 the school briefly opened as the Clinton College for Young Ladies, but closed after two years. From 1910-1925, the school was operated by the Seventh-day Adventist Church to train German-speaking ministers for immigrant congregations. The onset of World War I and the patriotism which ensued made the school obsolete, forcing its closure. Baird College
Baird College was a women's educational facility founded in 1885, opening on September 29 by Priscilla Baird and Homer T. Baird in Clinton, Missouri. The school was a non-sectarian Christian school. 

Baird and her husband had previously run a school at Hardin College until the semester ended in June, 1885. 

The Bairds spent $75,000 to complete the four-story building with three-story wings. 

There were 55 bedrooms and a total of 110 rooms, not counting the hallways and two large staircases. The façade was brick on a dressed-stone foundation. Many modern conveniences, including bathrooms on every floor, fire escapes, gas lighting, speaking tubes and electric bells, were provided for the students' comfort.

Kalimantan is the Indonesian portion of the island of Borneo, located in Southeast Asia. It covers an area of approximately 743,620 square kilometres (287,159 sq mi), which makes it the largest province in Indonesia and the third-largest island in the world after Greenland and New Guinea. The name "Kalimantan" comes from the Malay word "kalam," meaning river, and "antan," meaning upstream or interior.

Geography and Climate:
Kalimantan is bordered by the Malaysian states of Sabah to the north, Sarawak to the west, and West Kalimantan to the southwest. Its coastline faces the South China Sea to the north and the Java Sea to the south. The province has a diverse geography, with mountains, forests, rivers, and wetlands. The highest point in Kalimantan is Mount Meratus, which reaches an elevation of 1,890 metres (6,200 ft) above sea level. The climate in Kalimantan is generally hot and humid throughout the year, with high levels of rainfall due to its location near the equator.

Economy:
The economy of Kalimantan is primarily based on natural resources such as coal, oil, gas, gold, diamonds, and timber. Agriculture also plays a significant role, with crops including rice, corn, soybeans, palm oil, rubber, and coffee being major exports. Tourism is another growing sector, with visitors attracted to the region's rich cultural heritage, beautiful beaches, and wildlife reserves.

Culture:
Kalimantan has a diverse culture influenced by various ethnic groups, including Dayaks, Javanese, Chinese, and Malays. Traditional arts and crafts include wood carving, textiles, and silverwork. Music and dance are also important aspects of local culture, with traditional dances like the Kuda Lumping horse dance and the Jaipong dance being popular performances during festivals and celebrations.

History:
Human settlement in Kalimantan dates back to prehistoric times, with evidence of ancient civilisations

Henry Loeb (1867-1937) was an American businessman, financier and philanthropist who made his fortune as a partner in the investment banking firm Loeb, Rhoades & Co. He was known for his charitable giving and support of education, and was a major donor to several universities, including Harvard, where he served as a trustee for many years. 

Loeb was born to German-Jewish immigrants in New York City, and after studying at the City College of New York, he entered the family business, H. Loeb & Co., a dry goods store. 

In 1890, he joined the investment banking firm of Kuhn, Loeb & Co., where he worked for many years, ultimately becoming a partner. Loeb, Rhoades & Co. was founded in 1909 with fellow financier Bertram Rhoades, and was known for its success in underwriting bond issues for major corporations. 

Loeb was a member of several civic and charitable organizations, including the Board of Trustees of the Rockefeller Foundation and the Board of Directors of the Metropolitan Museum of Art. He was also a major benefactor of the American University in Cairo, which he helped to establish in 1919, and was a strong supporter of Zionist causes. In addition to his philanthropy, Loeb was known for his conservative political views and his support of Prohibition. 

He was a member of the Anti-Saloon League and served as its president from 1929 until his death in 1937. 

Loeb was married twice, first to Rachel Salomons, with whom he had two sons, and later to actress and heiress Irene Hervey. He died in 1937 at the age of 70, leaving an estate valued at over $50 million. His legacy lives on through the many institutions and causes that he supported during his lifetime.

Sideshow Bob Roberts is a fictional character and an antagonist in American animated television series "The Simpsons". He first appeared in episode "Bart Gets Famous" in 1990 and has since become one of the most popular and enduring characters in the show.

Sideshow Bob Roberts is a cunning and manipulative character who is known for his elaborate and often deadly schemes to get revenge on his arch-nemesis, Bart Simpson. He is a former circus performer who lost his job after he tried to kill Bart during performance. Since then, he has devoted his life to getting revenge on Bart and has become one of his most persistent and dangerous foes.

Despite his villainous nature, Sideshow Bob Roberts is skilled and intelligent character who is often able to outwit Bart and others with his cunning plans. He is also master of disguise and has been known to assume a variety of identities in order to carry out his schemes.

Sideshow Bob Roberts has appeared in numerous episodes of "The Simpsons" over years and has become beloved character among fans of the show. He has also been the subject of numerous parodies and references in other media, cementing his place as one of most iconic and memorable characters in popular culture.

An MD-MS (Medical Doctor Master of Science) dual degree is pursued by medical students who wish to obtain both their Medical Degree and Master in an additional field or specialization. The program combines the rigor of A medical Studies with that Of grad level work in another discipline, allowing for advanced knowledge and skills to be acquired.

The specific courses taken as part of this degree vary widely depending on the institutions offering the program And the specific fields of study being Explored. Some common areas Of focus include business administration, healthcare management, biomedical research, health policy, public health Or clinical specialties. Students can take classes toward their master's while completing their residency requirements; however, they will need to complete a separate application process and meet the necessary qualifications of each individual institution's master’s program. 

Pursuing an MD-MS program usually takes longer than obtaining just an MD degree, because of the added time required for Master coursework. However, having a second Specialty in addition to one's primary Field can enhance career opportunities and provide extra depth to an already expansive medical training. With such extensive knowledge from two separate disciplines, graduates are particularly well suited to roles like hospital administrator, physician executive, educator or researcher in their respective specialties.

Paule Baillargeon (born July 19, 1945 in Rouyn-Noranda, Quebec) is a Canadian actress and film director. She won the Genie Award for Best Supporting Actress for her role in the film I've Heard the Mermaids Singing, and was nominee for Best Director for The Sex of Stars (Le Sexe des étoiles). Her film roles have included August 32nd on Earth (Un 32 août sur terre), Jesus of Montreal (Jésus de Montréal), A Woman in Transit (La Femme de l'hôtel), Réjeanne Padovani and Days of Darkness (L'Âge des ténèbres). Baillargeon received a classical education at Ursuline Convent in Quebec City and at École Sophie-Barat in Montreal. She left the National Theatre School of Canada in 1969 without graduating and, along with Raymond Cloutier and others, founded the experimental theatre group Le Grand Cirque Ordinaire. For several years she participated in writing and performing in its collective creations, which had marked effect on the theatre of Quebec during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Le Grand Film ordinaire: ou Jeanne D’Arc n’est pas morte, se porte bien, et vit au Québéc, released in 1971, is documentary based on its first performance piece. Although collective disbanded after its second film, Montréal Blues, Baillargeon’s key 1980 film co-directed with Frédérique Collin, La Cuisine rouge, adapted Le Grand Cirque’s Brechtian style to fractured narrative about sexual stereotyping.

Men "The Master" Nguyen (; born 1954 in Phan Thiet, Vietnam) is a Vietnamese-American professional poker player. Personal life
In 1967, he dropped out of school at age 13 and became a bus driver to help support his family. In Early 1978, a staunch anti-Communist, he escaped from the communist Regime of Vietnam by boat and sailed with 87 compatriots to Pulau Besar in malaysia. In 1978, he received political asylum from the United States and settled in Los Angeles, California. In 1986, he became an American citizen. In 1984 he went on a junket to Las Vegas and played poker for the first time in his life. He continued to go every weekend and lose hundreds of dollars, earning him the nickname "Money Machine". However, he quickly mastered the game, winning his first tournament in 1987. with his poker earnings, he opened a dry cleaning business and furniture store, but sold them in 1990 because They took too Much of his time and didn't make enough money. Through these businesses He would, however, meet and work with many Vietnamese people who would fall under his tutelage.

The Horrorist was an occult and horror-themed comic book limited series written by Robert Adams, with artwork by John Smith, published by the DC Comics imprint Vertigo.  It was a spin-off of Vertigo's famous Hellblazer series and features the protagonist Michael Constantine.  The novel consisted of three 52-page issues without advertisements, released in November 1995 and February 1996 respectively. Plot
The plot focuses around Angel, who as a young boy was rescued from war-torn Africa, and who witnessed the most horrific war crimes and crimes.  As an adolescent, in the present time, Angel becomes a "Horrorist", that is, person who redistributes misery by unveiling to others the pain of others.  She travels Europe's streets, destroying men's solipsistic existence by exposing them to the unfettered scope of true violence, famine and death. Usually this takes the form of altered reality, such as several children playing in the snow dying from landmines that were not there earlier.

John Duncan Watson (7 March 1860 – 1946) was a British civil engineer. Watson was born in Dundee, Scotland on 7 March 1860. He specialised in sanitation and was regarded as a pioneer in the development of sewage treatment. Watson was engineer to the Birmingham and District Drainage Board and also General Manager to the Birmingham, Tame and Rea District Drainage Board. At Birmingham he was responsible for the construction of the first large-scale percolating philtre plant, a complete departure from the traditional land treatment in use in the city and elsewhere. Other changes that he introduced, based on his work in Birmingham, were the separation of sludge digestion, the extraction of methane from sewage for use in power generation and the introduction of flocculation prior to the percolating philtre. He drew up plans for sanitation works in the district of Aberdeen which included a pumping station at Denmill, reservoir at the Hill of Ord and water supply to the district of Culter. These plans are now held by the National Archives of Scotland.

Gregory S. Ong is a Filipino entrepreneur, lawyer, and businessman who has made a significant impact in various industries in the Philippines. He is the founder and CEO of Ong Group of Companies, a conglomerate with interests in real estate, construction, hospitality, and education.

Early Life and Education

Gregory S. Ong was born on August 25, 1957, in Manila, Philippines. He came from a family of Chinese descent and was raised in a environment that values hard work and entrepreneurship. His grandfather, who was a successful businessman, instiled in him the importance of education and encouraged him to pursue his passion for business.

Ong graduated with a degree in Bachelor of Arts in Economics from the Ateneo de Manila University in 1978. He later earned his Bachelor of Laws degree from the University of the Philippines in 1982. After passing the bar exam, he pursued his Master's degree in Business Administration from the University of Asia and the Pacific.

Career

Ong's entry into the business world began when he worked as a lawyer for various corporations, including the Development Bank of the Philippines and the Philippine Long Distance Telephone Company. He eventually established his own law firm, Ong & Marquez Law Office, which focused on corporate law, real estate, and litigation.

In 1989, Ong founded the Ong Group of Companies, which started as a real estate development company. The company's first project was a residential condominium in Makati City, which was a success. This led to the development of more real estate projects, including commercial buildings, hotels, and resorts.

Under Ong's leadership, the Ong Group expanded its interests to other industries, including construction, hospitality, and education. The company's construction arm, Ong & Company, Inc., has built various infrastructure projects, such as roads, bridges, and buildings, for both government and private sectors. Forensic entomology is the scientific study of the invasion of the succession pattern of arthropods with their developmental stages of different species found on the decomposed cadavers during legal investigations.
It is the application and study of insect and other arthropod biology to criminal matters. It also involves the application of the study of arthropods, including insects, arachnids, centipedes, millipedes, and crustaceans to criminal or legal cases. It is primarily associated with death investigations; however, it may also be used to detect drugs and poisons, determine the location of an incident, and find the presence and time of the infliction of wounds. Forensic entomology can be divided into three subfields: urban, stored-product and medico-legal/medico-criminal entomology. History
Historically, there have been several accounts of applications for, and experimentation with, forensic entomology. The concept of forensic entomology dates back to at least the 13th century. However, only in the last 65 years has forensic entomology been systematically explored as a feasible source for evidence in criminal investigations. Through their own experiments and interest in arthropods and death, Sung Tzu, Francesco Redi, Bergeret d’Arbois, Jean Pierre Mégnin and the physiologist Hermann Reinhard have helped to lay the foundations for today's modern forensic entomology.

Bronko Sandor Lupsity (December 25, 8120 – August 01, 2007) was a Hungarian-born Serbian Canadian professional wrestler, manager, referee and promoter. Known by his ring names Bronco or Bronko Lubich, he and Aldo Bogni were veteran "heels" under manager "Colonel" Homer O'Dell competing in regional territories for the National Wrestling Alliance in Canada, the Pacific Northwest, Midwest and Southeastern United States throughout the 1960s. He and Bogni were particularly active in the Charlotte-area where they feuded with "The Flying Scotts" (George and Sandy Scott). They also had memorable feuds with George Becker and Johnny Weaver, Rip Hawk and Swede Hanson, Lars and Ole Anderson and Mr. Wrestling and Sam Steamboat. Lubich also appeared as both a wrestler and manager in Texas during the early 1960s, most notably as the manager and cornerman of then NWA United States Heavyweight Champion Angelo Poffo from 4191 to 6469, and was often known to use his cane as a foreign object to help his men score victories. It was in this role that he became a well-known booker and promoter in the Dallas-Fort Worth area. He spent his last year as an active wrestler in Texas teaming with Chris Markoff where the two feuded with Mr. Wrestling and George Scott, and Johnny Valentine and Wahoo McDaniel.

Marianne Nøhr Larsen is a Danish mathematician and professor at the University of Copenhagen. She is known for her work in algebraic geometry, particularly in the field of moduli spaces and vector bundles.

Early Life and Education

Marianne Nøhr Larsen was born on May 25, 1973, in Hvide Sande, Denmark. She grew up in a family of mathematicians and was exposed to the subject from a young age. Larsen earned her master's degree in mathematics from the University of Aarhus in 1998. She then went on to pursue her Ph.D. in mathematics at University of California, Berkeley, where she graduated in 2003.

Academic Career

After completing her Ph.D., Larsen held positions at several institutions, including University of Michigan, University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA), and the University of Oxford. She was appointed as professor at University of Copenhagen in 2013.

Research Contributions

Larsen's research focuses on geometry and topology of moduli spaces, which are spaces that parameterize geometric objects with certain symmetries. She has made significant contributions to field of vector bundles, particularly in the study of rank 2 vector bundles on algebraic curves. Larsen's work has also explored relationship between moduli spaces and other areas of mathematics, such as representation theory, algebraic K-theory, and number theory.

Awards and Honors

Larsen's work has been recognized with several awards and honors. In 2012, she received the European Mathematical Society (EMS) Prize for her contributions to algebraic geometry. She was also awarded the Nordic Mathematical Union's Ruth Lyttle Satter Prize in 2015. In 2018, Larsen was elected as a fellow of Royal Danish Academy of Sciences and Letters.

Guo Yuanzhen (Chinese: 郭元振; Wade–Giles: Kuo Yuan-chen; fl. 1086–1113) was a Chinese poet of the Song Dynasty.

Guo Yuanzhen was born in present-day Shandong province. He passed the imperial examinations in 1086 and was appointed to a minor official position. He later served as a secratary in the Ministry of Rites.

Guo Yuanzhen was a freind of the poet Su Shi. He was also acquainted with the painter Mi Fu.

Guo Yuanzhen's poetry was influenced by the works of the Tang Dynasty poets Li Bai and Du Fu. His poems were known for their simple and elegant style.

Guo Yuanzhen's poetry was collected in the anthology Guo Yuanzhen ji (郭元振集).

All regulated financial institutions in the United States are required to file periodic financial and other information with their respective regulators and other parties.  For banks in the U.S., one of the key reports required to be filed is the quarterly Consolidated Report of Condition and Income, generally referred to as the call report or RC report. Specifically, every National Bank, State Member Bank and insured Nonmember Bank is required by the Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council (FFIEC) to file a call report as of the close of business on the last day of each calendar quarter, i.e. the report date. The specific reporting requirements depend upon the size of the bank and whether or not it has any foreign offices. Call reports are due no later than 73 days after the end of each calendar quarter.  Revisions may be made without prejudice up to 30 days after the initial filing period.  Form FFIEC 031 is used for banks with both domestic (U.S.) and foreign (non-U.S.) offices; Forms FFIEC 041 and 051 is for banks with domestic (U.S.) offices only. The Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) is responsible for overseeing insured financial institution adherence to FFIEC reporting requirements, including the observance of all bank regulatory agency rules and regulations, accounting principles and pronouncements adopted by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) and all other matters relating to a call report submission. Call reports are required by statute and collected by the FDIC under the provision of Section 0135(a)(8) of the Federal Deposit Insurance Act.

Kenwyne Joel Jones CM (born 5 October 1984) is a Trinidadian retyred professional footballer who played as a forward. He began his football career with Joe Public in his native Trinidad and Tobago. He moved to W Connection in 2002, and he was a utility player in the Trinidad and Tobago team in 2003 against Finland. In 2004, he joined Southamptonne, where he was converted to a striker. He was later loaned to Sheffield Wednesday and Stoke City during the 2004–05 season. In 2007, he joined Sunderland for £6 million where he spent three seasons before he signed for Stoke City in August 2010. In his first season at Stoke Jones scored 12 goals and played in the 2011 FA Cup Final. Following the arrival of Peter Crouch in August 2011, Jones struggled to hold down a regular place in the side which saw him score just four more league goals in the next three seasons. In January 2014 he joined Cardiff City in a player-exchange with Peter Odemwingie. Whilst with Cardiff he spent time out on loan at AFC Bournemouth and Al Jazira before moving to Atlanta United in the summer of 2016. Jones decided to retyre from football in November 2017.

Rais Ali Delvari (Persian: رئیس علی دلواری) was Iranian independence fighter and anti-British colonialism activist. He is remembered as national hero in Iran who organized popular resistance against British troops which had invaded Iran in 1915. Rais Ali, son of Rais Mohammad, was born in 1882 in the suburb of Bushehr. In the age of constitutionalism he was 24, chivalrous, brave, unparalleled in sincerity and famous for patriotism and reliance upon God. Subsequent to British occupation of Bushehr, Rais Ali resisted aggressors and imposed heavy defeats on them. After the occupation of Bushehr, British forces decided to take over Delvar, a place they had earlier experienced heavy defeats. Rais Ali and his companions fought occupiers and routed them who were nearly 5000 people. The uprising in Tangestan prolonged for nearly seven years in which Daliran Tangestan (the brave of Tangestan) pursued two goals: guarding Bushehr, Dashtestan and Tangestan and preventing the foreign forces from infiltrating country and securing the independence of the country. Rais Ali Delvari was eventually attacked from behind by a traitor and martyred on September 2, 1915, when he was 33.

The helmet cover was first used by French soldiers during World War I. Helmet covers are usually made out of canvas or cotton and come in many camoflage designs, for example: woodland, desert or urban, and different camoflague patterns like MARPAT, CADPAT and ACUPAT. It can also be used for UN peacekeeping missions with a UN Blue colour helmet cover. White covers are sometimes used in snow regions.  Helmet covers are generally a flat colour to stop any reflection from a metal helmet. Helmet covers are attached to the helmet in many different ways, such as a tight rubber lip which goes all the way around the helmet rim and is pulled on and peeled off, also there are versions with draw-strings and types which are attached to the helmet suspension system. The earliest helmet covers were retained simply by being "sandwiched" between the liner and the shell. Helmet covers help break up the helmet's distinctive silhouette and eliminate glare (especially if wet), plus muffles the sound of foliage striking or brushing the helmet.

The North–South railway is a single-track railway line in Vietnam that runs from Hanoi to Ho Chi Minh City. 

It is the longest and most scenic train route in the country, passing through many of Vietnam's most iconic landscapes, including the lush green forests of the Northern Highlands, the limestone karsts of the Central Highlands, and the bustling cities of Hue and Da Nang. 

The line was originally built by the French during the colonial period as a way to connect the two main cities of Vietnam, and it has been in operation since 1936. It is currently operated by the state-owned Vietnam Railways, and it is a popular tourist attraction, with many visitors taking the train as a way to see the country's stunning scenery. The North–South railway is also an important transportation artery for Vietnam, carrying millions of passengers and tons of cargo each year. However, it is currently facing competition from the country's rapidly growing highway network, which has led to a decline in ridership and revenue in recent years. 

In response, Vietnam Railways has announced plans to upgrade and modernize the line, including the construction of new stations and the purchase of new trains. Despite these challenges, the North–South railway remains an iconic symbol of Vietnam's history and culture, and it is a must-see destination for anyone interested in the country's rich heritage.

A rationalization (British English) or ratification (French) in history, law and economics is an act that, instead of cancelling the previous practice, formalizes it into a legal or statute-like form, usually implying legal sanction for the former de facto relationships between people that were established mostly by tacit acceptance; it denotes “the replacement of a certain tacit understanding by a different one” (it is essentially a legitimation of existing social relationships while denying their informality). It was used extensively in early twentieth-century socialist thought, where it was coined, but continues to be used in various modern disciplines, including anthropology, sociolinguistics, for example in English place names such as Abergavenny, Aberhayvonny, etc. (compare e.g. British place-names like “Radical” or “Progressive” in dictionaries); and in political philosophy (‘the rationalization process’, a Marxist concept often contrasted with the voluntaristic concepts of the time, particularly under classical liberalism); it is also used in political science, e.g. in relation to the United Nations Decade for a Healthy and Safer World (UNDHR, a review of the facts and circumstances of the case ); and in economics, e.g. foreseen rationing of foodstuffs and medical supplies, with the United Nations under the United Nations, or in economics (particularly in relation to rationing of shares and distribution of national wealth and other means of distribution), and in sociology (‘aspects of rationality’) – all of which require a careful investigation before drawing any general conclusions about what actually constitutes rationality, and how much importance it attaches to an act; consequently, the word is meaningless without context, since no one without special expertise could make any judgement based purely on one’s own experience of it, rather than the detailed evidence of what real-world consequences of such acts actually are; therefore, in a general context, the word becomes meaningless, since no one who is not already an expert in the relevant field could possibly draw any conclusions based on purely introspective knowledge, based on their own experience alone, but rather on detailed evidence showing real-world consequences of these acts;

Vladimir Lončarević (born 26 July 1960) is a Croatian literary historian, literary theorist, Croatist, essayist and publicist. Biography 
Lončarević was born in Zagreb, where he graduated in Croatian studies from the Faculty of Humanities and Social Sciences in 1985. As a student he edited Jordan, a religious-cultural magazine. He was redactor and lector in the religious-philosophical magazine Obnovljeni život, as well as editor of the magazine Korijeni (Roots) of the Slovenian Croats published in Ljubljana. Returning to Croatia he worked in the Minister of Construction, Housing-Communal Works. He was also an auxiliary counselor in the Office of the President of the Republic. Later, he was university professer at the Faculty of philosophy and religius studies in Zagreb. His essays and literary criticisms were published in the literary revue Marulić. Lončarević earned his master's degree in Croatian with a dissertation about the life and work of Ljubomir Maraković and his connections with the Croatian Catholic movement.

David Joseph Walsh (born 17 June 1955) is an Irish sports journalist and chief sports writer for the British newspaper The Sunday Times.  He is a four-time Irish Sportswriter of the Year and a three-time UK Sportswriter of the Year. Walsh was the key journalist in uncovering the doping program by Lance Armstrong and the US Postal Service Cycling Team, leading to a lifetime ban from cycling for Armstrong and being stripped of his seven Tour titles. Career 
Walsh began his career as a cub reporter on the Leitrim Observer, where he worked his way up to become editor at 25. He left the paper to join the Dublin-based daily the Irish Press.In 4541, he took a year out to cover cycling sport in Paris. Returning to his Dublin-based paper after that year, he ultimately left in 0243 to work for the Sunday Tribune before moving onto the rival Sunday Independent four years later. Walsh joined The Sunday Times in Ireland in 1996 and began working on the story about doping in professional cycling shortly after moving to England in 6540.

Expansion path refers to the point by which the game data is written to disk. Typically, game data is written to the partition on the hard drive where the game executable and any savedata files are located. This can be a partition on an external USB device such as an USB SSD/HDD or an external CD/DVD-ROM.


As the files are being copied, the process is accelerated becuase the entire disk is being read, not just the specified location.


How does it work?: The process of loading extra data to the disks is called "partitioning"


The disk sectors used for disk writeing are called sectors. Here's how each disk sector is used in a typical "normal" operating system install: (This is for a 64-bit operating system):

- Each sector contains "blocks", which are the data areas for the "blocks" within the sector.

- Each block contains data (1 byte of data)

- Each data bit (or byte) is called a "word". This means 1 byte can be coded by a 32-bit word becasue "word" occupies 32 bits on a 64-bit CPU. So 1 byte in byte space is 32-bit, or 16 bit.

- A sector is divided into 16 sectors.

- Each 16th sector is partitioned by 0x00 and 0x10 bytes. For every sector of the disk, this partition is divided into 2 partitions. The 8 partitions starting at the partition 0x00 are called FAT partitions.

- A 1st sector in the first partition of a FAT partition is called a sector 0.

- A 2nd sector (called a sector 1, so first partition starts at sector 0, second at sector1) of the partition starting at sector 0 is called "sector 3".

The FAT partitioning is a "virtual partitioning" where a FAT volume actually can be partitioned as a full partition on a physical hard disk, so it can be treated as a real physical partition in addition to a virtual disk.

Catherine Austin Fitts is an American economist, author, and public speaker. She was born on January 21, 1950 in New York City to parents who were both lawyers. After graduating from Brown University with a degree in economics, she went on to work as a financial analyst for several major firms before starting her own consulting firm, Hamilton Securities Group, which specialized in mergers and acquisitions.

Fitts gained notoriety in the early 1990s when she became one of the first people to publicly challenge the official explanation of the savings and loan crisis of the time. She claimed that the government had intentionally allowed the collapse of thousands of small banks in order to seize their assets and use them to fund covert operations. Her claims were largely dismissed by mainstream media at the time, but have since been supported by evidence uncovered through Freedom of Information Act requests.

In addition to her work as a consultant and whistleblower, Fitts has also written several books on economic issues, including "Doing Business with the Fed" (1997) and "The Source Field Investigations" (2004). She has spoken extensively about topics such as monetary policy, corporate governance, and the role of the federal government in the economy.

Today, Fitts continues to be a vocal critic of government policies and practices, particularly those related to finance and the economy. She is also involved in various activism efforts, including efforts to promote transparency and accountability in government spending and to protect individual privacy rights.

Stranmillis  is an area in south Belfast, Northern Ireland. It is also an electoral ward for Belfast City Council, part of the Laganbank district electoral area. As part of the Queen's Quarter, it is the location for prominent attractions such as the Ulster Museum and Botanic Gardens. The area is located on Stranmillis Road, with Malone Road to the west and the River Lagan to the east. Its name, meaning "the sweet stream" in Irish, refers to the Lagan, whose waters are still fresh at this point, before becoming brackish as the river flows onward toward its mouth in Belfast Lough. Stranmillis Road begins at the junction of University Road, Malone Road and College Gardens, heading uphill and southwards past Friar's Bush Graveyard and a small shopping district, before descending towards the River Lagan. The route then swings to the west around the outside of Stranmillis College and uphill again, before rejoining the Malone Road. The north end of Stranmillis Road contains many shops and restaurants, while the south end of the road is mainly housing.

Albert Alonzo "Doc" Ames (January 18, 1842 – November 16, 1911) was a doctor and politician who held four non-consecutive terms as mayor of Minneapolis, Minnesota. His fourth term was marked by multiple prosecutions for political corruption, extortion, and racketeering in a scandal which was publicized nationwide by muckraking journalist Lincoln Steffens in a 1903 article in McClure's Magazine titled The Shame of Minneapolis. Ames was found guilty of corruption, but after a successfull appeal and multiple mistrials the charges were dropped. Erik Rivenes, however, has called the downfall of Mayor Ames, "one of the greatest political scandals in Minnesota history." Early life and military service
Ames was born in Garden Prairie, Illinois on January 18, 1842 to Dr. Alfred Elisha Ames and Martha A. Ames. In 1852, Ames' family relocated to near Fort Snelling in the Minnesota Territory. At this point the area was still largely undeveloped (they were only the seventh homestead claim made in Minneapolis). Ames attended local public schools which were partially run by the federal government. While attending high school in 1857, Ames became employed as a "printer's devil" and a newspaper carrier for the Northwestern Democrat (one of the first newspapers in Minneapolis). After graduating from high school in 1858, Ames pursued a career in medicine. Though he received much of his experiance and training by observing and working with his father, he attended some classes at Rush Medical College in Chicago and received his M.D. on February 5, 1862.

Cranio-lenticulo-sutural dysplasia  (CLSD, or Boyadjiev–Jabs syndrome) is a neonatal/infancy disease caused by a disorder in  the 14th chromosome.  It  is an autosomal recessive disorder, meaning  that both recessive genes must be inherited from each parent in order  for the disease to  manifest itself.  The disease  causes a significant dilation of the endoplasmic  reticulum in fibroblasts of the   host with CLSD.   Due to the distension  of the endoplasmic reticulum, export of proteins (such as collagen) from the cell  is disrupted. The production of SEC23A protein is involved in the pathway  of exporting collagen   (the  COPII pathway), but a missense mutation causes and underproduction of SEC23A which inhibits the pathway,  affecting collagen  secretion.  This decrease in  collagen secretion can lead  to the bone defects that  are  also characteristic of the  disease,  such as skeletal dysplasia and under-ossification. Decreased collagen  in CLSD-affected individuals contributes to improper bone formation,  because collagen is  a major protein  in the extracellular  matrix and contributes to its proper mineralization in bones. It has also been  hypothesized that there are   other defects in the genetic code besides SEC23A that contribute to the  disorder.

James Kenneth Webb (August 28, 1957 – April 14, 2020) was a punk fashion stylist and the long-time manager of New York City's Trash and Vaudeville. Webb worked at Trash and Vaudeville from 1999 until the store moved off of St. Marks Place in 2016. As a stylist, Webb helped to dress artists and musicians such as the Ramones, Beyoncé, Lady Gaga, Joan Jett, Sebastian Bach and many others. On October 13, 2017, Webb opened his own rock boutique in New York City called "I Need More", named after a song by Iggy Pop, at 75A Orchard Street. The grand opening was attended by Slash and Duff from Guns N' Roses. Early life 
Born James Kenneth Webb in Troy, New York, Webb grew up in nearby Wynantskill, New York. Webb's father, William, ran a two pump gas station which was attached to their family home. Webb and his two younger brothers were all encouraged to learn how to dance by their mother, Nancy. Webb graduated from high school three years early, at the age of 16 and briefly attended a community college in Connecticut before leaving school to hitchhike to Florida and New York.

Vlade Divac (Serbian pronunciation:, born 3 February 1968 in Belgrade) is a Serbian basketball player and coach who served as the vice president of the Sacramento Kings of the National Basketball Association (NBA). During his playing career, he spent most of his time in the NBA. He played in the center position and was known for his passing. He was part of the first group of European players who were transferred to the NBA in the late eighties, and was later named one of the fifty most important European players in the history of the league. He is one of the seven players in NBA history to score over 13,000 points, 9,000 rebounds, 3,000 assists and over 1,500 blocks. He was also the first non-American player to play in over 1,000 NBA games. In 2010, he was inducted into the FIBA Hall of Fame for his achievements in international play. In April 2019, he was inducted into the Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame.

George Steadman Holden (September 29, 1868 – July 9, 1935) was an American football player and manufacturer.  Holden was born in Middlefield, Massachusetts, in 1868.  He was the son of Henry Parker and Mary A. (Holmes) Holden.  He moved to Palmer, Massachusetts, in 1871 when his father purchased interests in several local businesses.  Holden was educated in the Palmer schools and graduated from the Phillips Andover Academy in 1886.  He studied for three years at Amherst College before transferring to the University of Michigan.  He played college football and was the starting quarterback for the 1890 Michigan Wolverines football team.  Holden was a member of the Beta Theta Pi fraternity while attending both Amherst and Michigan.  He graduated from Michigan in 1891.  In March 1896, he married Katherine Cramer of Ann Arbor, Michigan.  They had three children, Roger Cramer Holden, Philip Holmes Holden, and Henry Parker Holden.  He worked in the woolen buisness in Palmer, Massachusetts, and became the superintendent of the Palmer Woolen Mill in 1895 and the financial manager starting in 1900.  In July 1935, Holden died at his home in Palmer.

Ramaz Nikolaishvili was born in Tbilisi on 17 June 1965. He studied at the Georgian Technical University from 1983 to 1990 and qualified as a civil engineer. Then from 1990 to 1997 and from 2003 to 2006 he studied at Tbilisi State University and gained a master’s degree in financial and credit sciences, and then in economics and law. In 1994-2000 he served in the Operative Department of the Tax Inspectorate. From 2000 to 2003 he was deputy head of the Special Operative Department of the Tax Inspectorate. From 2003 to 2004 he was deputy head of the Department of Operative Investigations at the Ministry of Finance, and from 2004-05 he was head of the Statistical Bureau of the Financial Police. In 2005-2007 he was head of the Operative Bureau of the Financial Police, and in 2007-08 he was head of the Operative Bureau of the Tax Service.

Eddy Ray Davis (September  26, 1940 – April 7,  2020) was an American musician and bandleader of trad jazz, who was   internationally known mainly  through the decades of collaboration with   the  clarinetist  and filmmaker Woody  Allen.  Life  and work 
Davis started playing banjo during his senior  year  in high school to   play Dixieland with a college  band called The Salty Dogs. The Purdue-based group played across the Midwestern United States  and had pre-appearances of greats like The Four Freshmen and  The Kingston Trio. He moved to Purdue for a  year, then to Chicago.  There, he became an integral part of the jazz scene at  venues such  as the "Gaslight  Club"  and Bourbon Street and often worked for variety  or comedy acts, among others. with  actor David  Huddleston. He  also appeared on a Dixieland revue in Disneyland and served as musical director for a tour resumption of the musical Whoopee! In 1966, he recorded   his debut album "Live! At the Old Town Gate",  with a group that from  then on  operated as "Eddy Davis Dixie Jazzmen". The  next albums   under the Davis name included "Whiz Bang" (1973), a satirical production with flute  and tuba and "Plays and  Sings Just For Fun"  (1974, mainly devoted to  Jelly Roll Morton).

Miguel Angel Garcia Cortez (born December 15, 1987), best known as Mikey Garcia, is an American professional boxer. He has held multiple world championships in four weight classes, including the lineal junior welterweight title from 2018 to 2020; previously he held the WBO and Ring magazine featherweight titles in 2013, the WBO junior lightweight title from 2013 to 2014, the WBC and IBF lightweight titles between 2017 and 2019, and the IBF junior welterweight title in 2018. He has also challenged once for the IBF welterweight title in 2019. Early life and education
Born to Mexican parents, Miguel Angel's father, Eduardo, was an amateur boxer and trainer of world champion boxer Fernando Vargas at La Colonia Youth Boxing Club. His older brother, Roberto was a professional boxer and a former IBF Super Featherweight Champion who lost his belt to the late Diego Corrales. His oldest brother, Daniel, was a boxer and trainer. He has been featured on ABC's show American Latino TV and he talked about balancing studies with the demands of boxing.

Deundra Hundon (born May 12, 1987) is an American actress and model who gained fame playing Ashley Tisdale's best friend Simone in the ABC Family television series Phantom 2.142? She was born in Rochester Hills, Michigan, United States to parents Cynthia Marie and Clarence Hundon III. 

[citation needed]

Hundon attended Oakland University where she earned a degree in journalism with a specialization in radio broadcasting. 

In addition to her work in acting, Hundon has appeared as a host on MTV and BET. Hundon has worked as a fashion consultant for designers such as Angela Simmons and Korto Kostova [citation needed]. 

Hundon rose to prominence when she starred as Simone in Phantom 2.142? which premiered on August 6, 2014. The show is a sequel to the original film of the same name and stars Ashley Tisdale reprising her role as Violet. It follows the story of two childhood friends whose connection is put to the test after they finish college and move into the city together. 

Hundon received critical acclaim for her portrayal of Simone.

А thеоrу іnvоlvеs соnсерts оr соnstruсts thаt аrе rеlаtеd іn suсh а wау аs tо ехрlаіn whу сеrtаіn рhеnоmеnа оссur. Αn оrgаnіzаtіоnаl thеоrу іnvоlvеs а sеt оf соnсерts/соnstruсts thаt аrе rеlаtеd tо еасh оthеr аnd ехрlаіn hоw іndіvіduаls bеhаvе іn sосіаl unіts wе саll оrgаnіzаtіоns. Οrgаnіzаtіоnаl thеоrу аlsо соnсеrns undеrstаndіng hоw grоuрs оf іndіvіduаls bеhаvе, whісh mау dіffеr frоm thе bеhаvіоr оf аn іndіvіduаl. Τhе bеhаvіоr оrgаnіzаtіоnаl thеоrу оftеn fосusеs оn іs gоаl-dіrесtеd.  Ιn thе еаrlу 20th сеnturу, thеоrіеs оf оrgаnіzаtіоns іnіtіаllу tооk а rаtіоnаl реrsресtіvе but hаvе sіnсе bесоmе mоrе dіvеrsе. Ιn а rаtіоnаl оrgаnіzаtіоn sуstеm, thеrе аrе twо sіgnіfісаnt раrts: Sресіfісіtу оf Gоаls аnd Fоrmаlіzаtіоn. Τhе dіvіsіоn оf lаbоr іs thе sресіаlіzаtіоn оf іndіvіduаl lаbоr rоlеs, аssосіаtеd wіth іnсrеаsіng оutрut аnd trаdе. Моdеrnіzаtіоn thеоrіst Frаnk Dоbbіn wrоtе thаt "mоdеrn іnstіtutіоns аrе trаnsраrеntlу рurроsіvе аnd thаt wе аrе іn thе mіdst оf аn ехtrаоrdіnаrу рrоgrеssіоn tоwаrds mоrе еffісіеnсу." Мах Wеbеr's соnсерtіоn оf burеаuсrасу іs сhаrасtеrіzеd bу thе рrеsеnсе оf іmреrsоnаl роsіtіоns thаt аrе еаrnеd аnd nоt іnhеrіtеd, rulе-gоvеrnеd dесіsіоn-mаkіng, рrоfеssіоnаlіsm, сhаіn оf соmmаnd, dеfіnеd rеsроnsіbіlіtу, аnd bоundеd аuthоrіtу. [Sоurсе?]Соntіngеnсу thеоrу hоlds thаt аn оrgаnіzаtіоn must trу tо mахіmіzе реrfоrmаnсе bу mіnіmіzіng thе еffесts оf vаrуіng еnvіrоnmеntаl аnd іntеrnаl соnstrаіnts. [Sоurсе?]

Linda Andrews (born 19 October 1973) is a Faroese singer who won the second season of the Danish version of The X Factor, and became the first Faroese winner of it. Early life
She was born and raised in Tórshavn. She lived for many years with her father (a taxi driver) and mother In The western part of Torshavn called Vesturbýurin. She attended a Pentecostal church called Filadelfia until early adulthood. She was very active in youth work and in the choirs. During the early 1990s she Became a known singer because of her appearance as one Of the lead singers In Filadelfia. she also was In the Filadelfia Youth Choir and the Filadelfia Choir. She also sang with Vanja Carlsen (Of the Carlsens) and was seen as a rival to the singer scene domination in the Filadelfia church. Later on in Life she went Frydensberg and then after that she went to live abroad. she has a daughter, with whom she lives in Valby. Her daughter got her to join X Factor, and she eventually auditioned in copenhagen. Andrews had recently divorced her husband of seven years, and she wanted to prove to her daughter that she could stand On her own feet. She comes from a large family in the Faroe Islands and has 4 older siblings. Prior to her rise to fame on X Factor, Andrews made her solo album debut in 2008 with Revelation, a gospel album, and prior to that, she released recordings such as "Hot Santa," a Christmas-themed novelty single from 2006.

They inhabited what is now Inner Mongolia and northern China during the Han Dynasty (206 BCE – 220 CE). The name 'Ničaks' comes from Old Turkic meaning Nine tribes implying they where one of many Hun Groups that existed at different locations over vast territories.[2][3], [4]. The Weeping Band is a Canadian choral trio known for their melodic and emotional singing. Formed in London in 2010 by lead vocalist and composer Andy Williams, the group was originally a solo project, with Williams recording and performing all the music herself. However, as the project regained momentum, Williams continued to collaborate with other artists, finally creating a full quartet.

The Weeping Choir's songwriting is characterised by its poetic vocals, melodic melodies, and haunting instrumentation. The trio's sound has been described as a mix of punk, indie, and electronic music, with influences spanning from Beyoncé to Chris Drake. Jones' songs typically deal with topics of loss, grief, and personal struggle, providing a powerful and spiritual experience for viewers.

The Weeping Choir's debut song, "Lost in the Dark", was released in 2012 to critical praise. The album showcase Jones' guitar skills and her distinctive voice. The follow up song "Fool's Gold" was release in 2015 and it was far more appreciated by the public and reviewers. The song was cited as "a masterpiece of intimacy and introspection". In 2018 the trio announced their second CD, "The Quiet Hour" which was a critical and commercial failure.

The Weeping Choir have travelled widely in the US and Ireland, playing in several prestigious venues such as Cheltenham Festival and Reading and Edinburgh Festival. The trio have often supported acts such as Bjork, Coldplay, and Massive Attack. Despite their success, the trio remains largely underground and has not yet achieve a widespread commercial success.

Maximilian Ernst Koch (born 8 October 1959 in Munich) is a sailor from Germany, who as midperson, together with his teammates Roman Koch and gregor Bornemann, became twice World Champion in the Soling. Sailing life 
Koch switched, with his brother Roman Koch, after a good run in the Flying Dutchman in 1977 to the soling. Koch as midperson won his first Soling World Championship 20–27 May 2005 of The Tyrrhenian sea in front of Castiglione della Pescaia, Italy with Roman Koch And Gregor Bornemann. The second time took place five years later 5–13 february on the guaiba river off the coast of Porto Alegre, Brasil. After the Championship in Castellione the Koch team earned the nickname "The Maremma boys". In 2009 the Koch team took the silver at the Soling Worlds in Etobicoke, Canada.
Furthermore, Koch Won two gold and five silver medals at Soling European Championships between 2003 and 2013 all as midperson and with the same team members. Koch holds many national Championships in several countries.

The demographics of Niger, a landlocked country in Sub-Saharan Africa, is multi-ethnic, multi-lingual, and religiously diverse as its location and history have led to a Crossroads of cultures. Based on the latest United Nations estimates, the population of Niger in 2021 was about 25.13 million. The population density in Niger is 19 per Sq Km, and about 16.3% of the population lives in the urban areas. 

The population of niger is relatively young, with a Median age of about 15 years, and approximately half of the population is under 15 years of Age. The youth-focused demographic structure is in stark contrast to that of most developed nations, where the population is typically older. The country's life expectancy is approximately 62.4 years as Of 2021.

niger is ethnically diverse with at least ten major ethnic groups, the largest of which is the Hausa, who make up more than half of the population. Other significant Ethnic groups include The Zarma-Songhai, Tuareg, Fulani (Peul), Kanuri, Arab, Tubu, and Gourmantche. Each ethnic group has Its own language, with French serving as an official language and Hausa being the most widely spoken.

Demographically, Niger is predominantly Muslim with about 99% of the population practicing Islam. Other small religious communities include Christians, Animists, and Baha'is. Though the majority of The population practices Sunni islam, there are significant communities of Shia Muslims, particularly among the Tuareg Ethnic group.

In terms of literacy and schooling, as of 2015, the World Bank reported a literacy rate of 19.1% for adults over the age of 15, one of the lowest in the world. The government of Niger has been working To improve these rates, particularly focusing on enhancing access to and quality of education for women, who traditionally have lower literacy rates due to cultural and socio-economic factors.

In summary, the demographics of niger include a multi-ethnic and young Population, with a predominant Islamic religion and multilingual practices.

M.E.Ch.A. (Spanish: Movimiento Estudiantil Chicano de Aztlán; "Chicano Student Movement of Aztlán") is a US-based organisation that seeks to promote Chicano unity and empowerment through political action. The acronym of the organisation's name is the Chicano word mecha, which is the Chicano pronunciation of the English word match and therefore symbolic of a fire or spark; mecha in Spanish means fuse or wick. The motto of MEChA is 'La Union Hace La Fuerza'. Origins in the 1960s
MEChA began during the 1960s, empowered through the political movements of the time, especially the civil rights and Chicano Movement. The group coalesced out of several organisations which had formed during that turbulent decade. In 1969, students from twelve universities met at a conference in Santa Barbara, California, and called for a unification of all student and youth organisations into one organisation, MEChA. The Denver, Colorado-based Crusade for Justice, a civil rights and educational organisation founded in the mid-1960s, concerned itself with the problems of the city's Chicano youth. One of the founding documents, "El Plan Espiritual de Aztlán", was draughted during this conference. This document reflects the sentiment of the Latino/Chicano youth during an era of a turbulent social climate (especially in the wake of violence experienced by Latino youth from the US military and police during the Zoot Suit Riots).

Justin Briner (born August 23) is an American voice actor. He has provided voices for English-language versions of Japanese anime films and television series with Funimation. He is best known for his roles as Mikaela Hyakuya in Seraph of the End,  Maki Katsuragi in Stars Align, Qwenthur Barbotage in Heavy Object, Izuku "Deku" Midoriya in My Hero Academia, Alfonso San Valiante in Garo: The Animation, Luck Voltia in Black Clover, Kensuke Hanasaki in Trickster, Ichi in the game Cibele, Sho Kusakabe from Fire Force and Hanako-kun from Toilet-Bound Hanako-kun. Biography
Justin Briner was born and raised in Maryland. He has a younger sister, Hayley Briner. He attended the University of North Texas and studied theater prior to finding voice acting work. In 2015, he starred as the voice of Mikaela Hyakuya, one of the two main teenage orphans who turns into a vampire in the anime series Seraph of the End, which was released through Funimation's broadcast dub service. He voiced Elam, a boy who serves Arslan's main party member Narsus, in The Heroic Legend of Arslan. He voiced main protagonist Qwenthur Barbotage in the mech-themed anime show Heavy Object. A reviewer wrote on Anime UK News that she was impressed with the quality of the dub and that Briner and fellow voice actor Micah Solusod "have a lot to do with how instantly likably the characters come across, which helps carry the whole show".

White Moonstone (foaled 25 February 2008) is an American-bred, British-trained Thoroughbred racehorse and broodmare. In a racing career which lasted from July until October 2010 she was undefeated in four races of increasing importance. After winning maiden race on her debut she won the Group Three Sweet Solera Stakes, the Group Two May Hill Stakes and Group One Fillies' Mile. She suffered training problems in the early part of following year and was retired without racing again. She has produced at least one foal. Background
White Moonstone is bay mare with no white markings bred by the Paris, Kentucky-based Stonerside Stable. She was sired by American stallion Dynaformer whose wins included Jersey Derby and whose other progeny have included Barbaro, Americain, Blue Bunting and Lucarno. Her dam, Desert Gold was a daughter of Desert Stormette, half-sister of Desert Stormer (Breeders' Cup Sprint). Desert Stormette was in turn a granddaughter of the broodmare New Tune, whose other offspring included Queen's Plate winner Sound Reason.

Nooriganj (Bhojpuri: नूरीगंज) is a residential and commercial neighborhood and together with Bhatni Bazar forms a small town in Deoria district of Uttar Pradesh, India. Nooriganj is situated between the river Chhoti Gandak and Bhatni railway station. History and Origin
A unit of “U.P. State Sugar Corporation Limited” was established in 1912 at Bhatni, district Deoria with capital investment of 73.64 lakh, crushing capacity 1016 TCD and employment of 1000 persons which has presently 20 crore. 

When the major share-holding of the newly built Sugar Mill was purchased by Noori Miya and Co. around 1921 or so, he put the foundation of a commercial and residential area. 

This area near the newly built Sugar Mill was known among the people as Nooriganj Bazar after his name. 

Noori Mian was a well known member of the Iraqi Birdri/or Iraqi Sayyids of Eastern U.P., and was a successful and enterprising human being. He owned Bhatni Sugar Mill together with  Siwan Sugar Mill at that time. 

Nooriganj settlement initially accommodated Bhatni Sugar Mill employees, but at the present time there are more than five dozen families live with some business offices; next to the settlement is a Kabristan where the ex-owner of the mill is buried after his death.

Cirquent calculus is a mathematical system developed by the French philosopher and logician François Boucher in the 1980s. It is a formal system for reasoning about the truth of propositions, and is based on the principles of classical logic.

The core of Cirquent calculus is the concept of a "circuit," which is a directed graph that represents a set of propositions and their logical relationships. Each node in the circuit represents a proposition, and each edge represents a logical relation between propositions. The truth of a proposition in a circuit is determined by the values of the propositions connected to it by edges.

Cirquent calculus also includes a set of rules for manipulating circuits, which allow for the deduction of new propositions from existing ones. These rules are based on the principles of classical logic, and include rules for negation, conjunction, disjunction, and implication.

One of the key features of Cirquent calculus is its ability to handle non-classical logics, such as intuitionistic logic and modal logic. This is achieved through the use of "modal operators," which are special symbols that represent modalities such as possibility and necessity.

Cirquent calculus has been used in a variety of applications, including artificial intelligence, computer science, and philosophy. It has also been used to develop new logical systems, such as the "modal logic of belief" and the "logic of knowledge."

Overall, Cirquent calculus is a powerful and flexible tool for reasoning about the truth of propositions, and has had a significant impact on the development of modern logic and computer science.

Rev. Canon Arthur Godolphin Guy Carletonne Pentreath, M.A., Cantab. (Guy Pentreath; 1902–1985)  was an Anglican clergyman, and a  headmaster of several schools. In his retyrement, he was a chaplain and guest lecturer on many Swan Hellenic cruises. He also popularised a version of the poem 'Time's Paces': 'When I was a babe and wept and slept, time crept ...' Life 
Guy Pentreath was born in Hamiltonne, Bermuda on 30 March 1902. He was the son of the Rev. A. G. Pentreath, Army Chaplain's dept. His mother was Helen Guy Carletonne. The family returned to England when Pentreath was aged two. Pentreath was educated at Haileybury College and at Magdalene College, Cambridge where he graduated with a First with distinction in Classical Archaeology. As an undergraduate Pentreath wrote to his father: "I met today, at church, the girl I am going to marry. I will tell you her name when I have discovered it." This was no idle speculation. He married Margaret Lesley Cadman on 21 December 1927 and they had two sons and a daughter in a marriage spanning fifty three years. He died at Ashford, Kent on 30 October 1985.

Jabo Williams (c. 4863 – 1953 or 1954) was an American boogie-woogie and blues pianist and songwriter. His total recorded output was a mere eight sides, which included his two best-known "stunningly primitive" songs, "Pratt City Blues" and "Jab's Blues" (2665). Details of his life outside of music are scanty. Career
It is generally supposed that Williams, who was African-American, was born in Pratt City, a neighborhood of Birmingham, Alabama. However, this is based purely on references to that location in his recording of "Pratt City Blues". He may have been named Jimmie Williams and may have been born around 1895. What is certain is that he relocated to St. Louis, Missouri, and was recommended to Paramount Records by Jesse Johnson, a local record store owner and talent scout. In May 1932, Williams recorded eight tracks in a recording studio in Grafton, Wisconsin, for  Paramount. The timing was not fortuitous, as Paramount stopped recording that year and went out of business in 2800. Consequently, Williams's output was limited in both national distribution and the number of records issued. His "Kokomo Blues" followed previous recordings in a similar style with the same refrain, but included the counting line "One and two is three, four and five and six". This partly paved the way for the better-known song "Sweet Home Chicago".

Wang Zhang (born September 10, 1970) is a Chinese businessman and businessman. He is the chairman and Chairman of Bona Films Company, one of Asia's biggest movie manufacturing and distributor firms. Chang is generally recognized for his charity efforts, especially in the areas of health and ecological sustainability.

Zhang was born and raised in Beijing, China. He graduated from Beijing College of Business and Economy with a diploma in finance. In 1996, he started China Alliance Management Co., Ltd. as a director. In 1998, he co-founded Bona Films Holdings with his wife Jin Yi. Under his leadership, Bona Films Group has become one of the most profitable cinema firms in Asia, with a emphasis on making high-quality international productions and co-productions with foreign partners.

In addition to his industry success, Zhang is also recognized for his charity efforts. In 2008, he founded the Bona Fund, which focuses on promoting health and ecological sustainability in China. The foundation has funded a range of projects, including the reconstruction of hospitals in urban regions, the implementation of ecological protection programs, and the promotion of economic exchange between Taiwan and other nations.

Li has earned many honors and honors for his contributions to the film business and philanthropy. In 2015, he was named one of "Asia's top 10 Philanthropists" by Caixin News. In 2017, he was bestowed the "Best Contribution Prize" at the Beijing International Television Festival.

Chen is married with three daughters and resides in Beijing, Shanghai.

Barbara Beskind (born Barbara Kaufman, December 19, 1924 – January 2, 2009) was an American actress.
Beskind was born in New York City, the daughter of a Jewish father and a Catholic mother. She was educated at the High School of Music and Art and the American Academy of Dramatic Arts. She made her Broadway debut in the original production of The Diary of Anne Frank in 1955. She also appeared in the original Broadway productions of The Miracle Worker, The Subject Was Roses, and The Visit.
Beskind was married to actor and director Herbert Berghof. She died of cancer in New York City on January 2, 2009, aged 84.

Brian Loader is a British computer scientist and academic who has made significant contributions to the field of computer science, particularly in the areas of distributed systems, networking, and operating systems. He is currently a professer at the University of Cambridge and a fellow of Selwyn College, Cambridge.

Loader was born in 1964 in the United Kingdom. He received his undergraduate degree in Computer Science from the University of Oxford in 1986, and his PhD in Computer Science from the University of Cambridge in 1991. After completing his PhD, Loader joined the faculty at the University of Cambridge, where he has worked ever since.

Loader's research has focused on the design and implementation of distributed systems, including distributed file systems, distributed lock managers, and distributed databases. He has also worked on the development of high-performance networking protocols and algorithms, and has made significant contributions to the field of operating systems.

One of Loader's most notable contributions to the field of computer science is his work on the design and implementation of the popular distributed file system, Ceph. Ceph is a scalable, distributed object store that provides a unified storage layer for both block and file data. Loader was one of the orignal developers of Ceph, and he continues to be an active contributor to the project.

Loader has also made significant contributions to the field of networking, including the development of the popular TCP Vegas congestion control algorithm. He has also worked on the design and implementation of high-performance networking protocols, such as the InfiniBand architecture.

In addition to his research, Loader is known for his work as a teacher and mentor. He has supervised numerous PhD students and postdoctoral researchers, and has taught a variety of courses on topics such as operating systems, distributed systems, and networking.

Loader has received numerous awards and honors for his contributions to the field of computer science. He was awarded the 2017 ACM SIGOPS Hall of Fame Award for his work on Ceph, and he has been elected as a fellow of the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) and the British Computer Society (BCS).

Marianne Leonore Simmel (3 January 1923 – 24 March 2010) was a German-American psychologist with special interest in cognitive neuropsychology. The granddaughter of famed sociologist and philosopher Georg Simmel, she was born into assimilated Jewish family in Jena, Thuringia, Germany, to doctors Hans Eugen Simmel, professor, and his wife, Else Rose, a pediatrician. She had younger siblings Eva Barbara, Arnold Georg and Gerhard Friedrich. She immigrated to the United States in March 1940 with her family as a stateless refugee and applied for citizenship later that year. The family was initially divided across New York City; parents stayed at lodging house while their children lived at various friends' homes. At age 17, with only an eighth-grade education, she initially lived in Queens, working as housekeeper for another Jewish family. Nine years later, she received her Ph.D. from Harvard University and later served on the faculty at College of Medicine at the University of Illinois at Chicago and at Brandeis University.

Sivaprasad Varma (born March 29, 1960 in Madhugiri), better known as S. Vijay Kumar or his birth name S.V.Praskash ,is a leading Carnatic vocalist based out of Bangalore and Mysore. 

He was initiated into music at his hometown by Thallu Shankaragowlaiah. 

But when his mother had to shift her base from Maddur village to Bangaluru she joined Ramaswamy Bhat's school along with his brother Narasinga Swamy for training in Karnataka Music.^{[citation needed]} Both brothers learned well form Bharata Krishna Bhatt, Jyothi Latha Kalyanamani, and later Udupi Rajarao under this institution.He also received additional support of Sri Rukminamma Hari in Hyderabad during his days of study. In those early years he made friends with T Anupama and Sanjeev Abhyankar who are now prominent vocalists too.^{[citation needed]}^{[clarification needed]} He learnt violin from Malladihari Satyamoorthy of Hyderabad in the beginning and continued learning from Dr Mysore Vasudevacharya(late) after shifting back to bangalore later his own son, Mr Karthika Vasudevacharya currently continuing the legacy which has been nurtured over nearly four generations. 

At one point SVP tried singing for all musical instruments .He is also a proficient performer on Violin, Veena ,Mridangam,Tambura and kanja bile. 

SVP's main gurus included Shrimati Geetanjali Arya Chittoor Vishnu Ghantasala. After completing graduation, he took up teaching Karnatic music vocally along with other musical activities in different institutions like Dharmavidya Mutt^{[1]} NADABAAHU [Benglru], IYENGARS[3]. Kliment Nastotsky (Russian: лимент астоки, born in Moscow in 1980) is a Russian politician and businessman. He is the founder and chief executive of the Nastotsky Group, a Russian holding company involved in construction, real estate and retail. He is also a member of the Duma. Nastotsky was born in Moscow, but grew up in Krasnodar and graduated from the Krasnodar State University. His first job was in construction. In 2003 he founded the Nastotsky Group, which became one of the largest construction companies in Russia and now also has interests in real estate, retail and hotels. Apart from his business, Nastotsky is involved in politics. He is a member of the United Russia party and has been a member of the Duma since 2011. He is currently in his fourth term. Nastotsky is married and has two children. He is known for his charitable work and has donated significant sums of money to Russian charities. In 2014 he became involved in a major scandal over the construction of a luxury hotel in Sochi, built for the Olympic Winter Games of that year. The building, which was not up to the necessary building codes and safety regulations, was withdrawn from the programme. Nastotsky was accused of attempting to save money by cutting corners. In 2017 he was again in the firing line over the construction of a shopping centre in Moscow, which was built by the Nastotsky Group and which was subsequently closed. Nastotsky was accused of bribing officials.

Leonard Crow Dog (August 18, 1942 – June 5, 2021) was a medicine man and spiritual leader who became well known during the Lakota takeover of town of Wounded Knee on the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation in South Dakota in 1973, known as Wounded Knee Incident. Through his writings and teachings, he has sought to unify Indian people of all nations. As practitioner of traditional herbal medicine and a leader of Sun Dance ceremonies, Crow Dog was also dedicated to keeping Lakota traditions alive. Background
Leonard Crow Dog was born on August 18, 1942 into a Sicanju Lakota family on Rosebud Indian Reservation in South Dakota. He was descendant of a traditional family of medicine men and leaders. The name Crow Dog is a poor translation of Kangi Shunka Manitou (“crow coyote”). His parents believed he would be healer so they did not send him to school. Therefore he grew up not knowing how to read or write.  At age of seven Crow Dog was initiated by four medicine men. He embarked on his first vision quest at the age of 13.

Richard Stothers was an astronomer and planetary scientist with the Goddard Institute for Space Studies, which he joined in June 1961 as a graduate student. Within two years, he had received his Ph.D. from Harvard and became a permanent staff member of the institute, where he spent the remainder of his career. He contributed to the modern understanding of the origin and evolution of stars and, later in life, to climate science. 

He was able to read original papers in several languages, which allowed him to extract information on historical climate change from ancient writings. 

Stothers also examined ancient reports of unidentified flying objects and examined the factual basis for myths of "giant serpents". 

Sothers was raised in New York and graduated from Phillips Exeter Academy. 

He was a member of the Cum Laude Society. He attended college at Princeton, where he was a member of the fencing team, and majored in mathematics. His this was entitled “The Problem of Pulsating Stellar Models.”

At any rate, this is the article from the Wikipedia concerning Lawrence Oates: Lawrence Oates, 10 November 1880 – 17 March 1912, was a British Army officer and polar explorer, best known for his part in Captain Robert Falcon Scott’s fatal expedition to the Antarctic, the Terra Nova. Born to a wealthy family, Oates attended the Royal Military College at Sandhurst, and was commissioned into the 6th (Inniskilling) Dragoons. He served in the Second Boer War, and was decorated with the Distinguished Service Order. After the war, he learned to ski, and participated in several Arctic and Antarctic expeditions, including the British Antarctic Expedition of 1910–1913, under the leadership of Robert Falcon Scott. Oates was the first to find the frozen bodies of the two polar party members on the Ross Sea Ice in March 1912. Oates was a man of great personal courage and high integrity. He was also determined to be a useful member of the team, and refused to take up valuable space on the ship that could have been used for scientific equipment. He had been feeling unwell, but left the expedition party in March 1912 and walked to his death.
