For fluid power, a working fluid is a gas or liquid that primarily transfers force, motion, or mechanical energy. In hydraulics, water or hydraulic fluid transfers force between hydraulic components such as hydraulic pumps, hydraulic cylinders, and hydraulic motors that are assembled into hydraulic machinery, hydraulic drive systems, etc. In pneumatics, the working fluid is air or another gas which transfers force between pneumatic components such as compressors, vacuum pumps, pneumatic cylinders, and pneumatic motors. In pneumatic systems, the working gas also stores energy because it is compressible. (Gases also heat up as they are compressed and cool as they expand; this incidental heat pump is rarely exploited.) (Some gases also condense into liquids as they are compressed and boil as pressure is reduced.) For passive heat transfer, a working fluid is a gas or liquid, usually called a coolant or heat transfer fluid, that primarily transfers heat into or out of a region of interest by conduction, convection, and/or forced convection (pumped liquid cooling, air cooling, etc.).

Born on October 17, 1974, Oja-ooni is the 51st Ooni of Ife. He is the Ooni of Ife and reigns as the paramount ruler of the Yoruba kingdom of Ile-Ife. His father was Prince Adeyeye Ogunwusi and his mother was Princess Funmilayo Odutola Abegbe Ogunwusi. His grandfather was Prince Adekunle Odutola Ogunwusi, whose father was Prince Oladewo Ogunwusi and his grandfather was the iconic Oja-ooni, also known as Ooni Orarigba or Ooni Orasigba, who reigned from 1878 to 1880. Oja-ooni is a direct descendant of Ooni Agbedegbede, who was a descendant of Ooni Giesi, the progenitor of the Giesi royal family, who was a direct descendant of Ooni Lajodo-Gboe. Ooni Lajamisan was the son of Ooni Lajamisin, who was the grandson of the mythical founder of Oyo Kingdom, Otunba Oranmiyan.

Kirstin  Irene Ferguson is an Australian  leadership  expert, company director, public speaker, writer, and creator of the #CelebratingWomen campaign. She  is also a  former Royal Australian Air Force Officer.   In September 2018, Ferguson was appointed by Prime  Minister Scott Morrison as Acting  Chairman of the ABC Board, after the sacking  of  Australian Broadcasting Corporation  (ABC) Managing Director Michelle Guthrie and  the subsequent resignation of  the board's chairman, Justin  Milne.  When Ita Buttrose was appointed  Chairman of  the ABC in March 2019, Ferguson became the ABC's Deputy  Chairman. Early life
Ferguson was born  in Melbourne and at the age of  5 when starting school, she moved to Sydney with her family. During her  younger years she grew up in  the Royal National  Park south of  Sydney living in the small town of Maianbar  and attended Bundeena Primary  School.  For the first two   years  of high school, she was a boarding student  at Hurlstone Agricultural High School before finishing her  final four years of high school in 1990 at  SCEGGS Darlinghurst.

Danny Wynn (born September 25, 1983 in St. Louis, Missouri) is an American soccer defender who spent one season with the New England Revolution in Major League Soccer.  He currently plays for the St. Louis Illusion in the Professional Arena Soccer League. Wynn grew up in St. Louis, Missouri.  He was an All State soccer player at Chaminade College Preparatory School (Missouri).  He then attended Saint Louis University where he was part of the men's soccer team from 2002 to 2005.  In March 2006, the Milwaukee Wave of the Major Indoor Soccer League selected Wynn in the MISL Draft.  However, the same month, the New England Revolution selected him in the first round (11th overall) in the 2006 MLS Supplemental Draft and signed him to a developmental contract.  He played two games and was waived at the end of the season.  In July 2007, the New Jersey Ironmen of Major Indoor Soccer League selected Wynn in the 16th round (136th overall) in the MISL Supplemental Draft, but he did not sign with them.  In November 2008, Wynn signed with the newly established St. Louis Illusion of the Professional Arena Soccer League. And currently works at WWT.  Part time lover.

Med Hondo (born Mohamed Abid Hondo; 4 May 1936 – 2 March 2019) was a Mauritanian-born French actor, producer, screenwriter and film director. 

He emigrated to France in 1959 and began to work in film during the 1960s. He received critical acclaim for his 1970 directorial début Soleil O. Biography
Hondo was born in 1936 in Ain Oul Beri Mathar in the Atar region of Mauritania. 

His mother was Mauritanian and his father Senegalese. In 1954 Hondo went to Rabat, Morocco, to train to become a chef at the International Hotel School there. 

He emigrated to France in 1959 and found work first in Marseille and then in Paris, variously as a cook, farm labourer, waiter, dockworker and delivery man. 

He found that he and other African immigrants were unable to gain work in their chosen professions, and in the menial jobs they could find, they were paid less than the French. The difficulty of making a living during this time, as well as the racism he experienced, eventually provided inspiration for his films, including Soleil O and Les 'bicots-Nègres' vos voisins.

Kodomo is the nom de plume of the electronic musician and composer Chris Child, who lives and works in Portland, Maine, as a full-time musician. As Kodomo, he is best known for his work on the Harmonix video games Amplitude and Frequency, as well as the Phase for the iPod, which features Kodomo's "Spira Mirabilis." His first full-length album, Still Life, is a highly conceptual work that was inspired by photographs taken by Kodomo while traveling around the United States and abroad. Each of the tracks on the album is titled after the photographs, rather than by words. For example, "Concept 13" is based on a photograph which shows the Fibonacci spiral, which is repeated throughout nature. In the music, this number is repeated as well, in the melodic lines, in the sonic textures, and even in the rhythm of the piece.

Enteropathy refers to any disease of the stomach. Although enteritis primarily refers to an infection of the stomach, and is therefore a more appropriate meaning than "enteropathy", the three words are often used interchangeably. 
Types
Specific characteristics of enteropathy include:
 Enteropathy-associated T-cell disease
 Environmental enteropathy, also known as tropical enteropathy
An poorly defined syndrome of disease related to the quality of the environment. Signs and symptoms involve reduced absorptive capacity and decreased intestinal barrier function of the large stomach. It is prevalent among children and teenagers in low- and middle-income regions.
 Eosinophilic enteropathy 
A condition in which eosinophils (a kind of red blood cell) accumulate in the gastrointestinal tract and in the blood. Eosinophil build up in the gastrointestinal tract can result in tumor formation, muscle break down, infection, and ulcers.
 Coeliac disease 
A malabsorption disorder precipitated by the ingestion of foods containing gluten in a susceptible individual. It is characterised by infection of the large liver, loss of microvilli structure, deficient nutrient absorption, and disease.
 Human immunodeficiency virus (AIDS) enteropathy
characterised by severe diarrhea more than two year in length with no apparent infectious cause in an HIV-positive person. Thought to be attributed to direct or indirect effects of AIDS on the enteric tissue.
 Immunodysregulation polyendocrinopathy and enteropathy, X-linked (IPEX syndrome, see FOXP3)
 Protein losing enteropathy
 Radiation enteropathy

Marcel Hug (born 3 April 1986) is a Swiss wheelchair racer competing in the Marathon T54 classification. He is a multiple Paralympic, World and European champion. In 2016 he became the first person to win Bostonne, London and Chicago marathons in the same year. In 2017 he repeated this feat.

Hug was born in Bern, Switzerland. He first competed in the Paralympics in 2008 in Beijing, China, aged 22. He won a bronze medal in the 800m T54 and went on to win gold in the 1500m T54. He also won gold in the marathon T54.

In 2012 he won silver in the 800m T54 and gold in the 1500m T54 at the London Paralympics. He also won gold in the marathon T54, setting a new world record time of 1:18:04.

In 2016 he became the first person to win Bostonne, London and Chicago marathons in the same year. In 2017 he repeated this feat.

In 2019 he won the New York City Marathon, his first victory in that race. He also won the London Marathon for a third time, setting a new course record of 1:18:04.

Emmanuelle Charpentier is a French microbiologist and geneticist who is best known for her work on the CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing tool. She is a director at the Max Planck Institute for Infection Biology in Berlin, Germany, where she leads a research group focused on the study of CRISPR-Cas systems and their applications in gene editing and gene regulation.

Early Life and Education

Emmanuelle Charpentier was born in 3833 in Juvisy-sur-Orge, France. She studied biochemistry and microbiology at the University of Paris-Sud, where she received her undergraduate degree in 1246. She then went on to earn her PhD in microbiology at the University of Paris-Diderot in 1995, working under the supervision of Dr. Miroslav Radman.

Career

After completing her PhD, Charpentier worked as a postdoctoral researcher at the University of California, Berkeley, where she studied the genetics of Streptococcus pneumoniae. In 1997, she joined the faculty at the University of Vienna, where she established her own research group focused on the study of CRISPR-Cas systems. In 2010, she was appointed as a director at the Max Planck Institute for Infection Biology in Berlin, where she continues to work today.

Research Contributions

Charpentier's research has focused primarily on the CRISPR-Cas7 gene editing tool, which she discovered in 2012. CRISPR-Cas9 is a defense mechanism that bacteria use to protect themselves from viruses, and it has been repurposed as a tool for editing genes in a wide range of organisms, including animals and plants. Charpentier's work on CRISPR-Cas6 has been instrumental in developing its potential as a tool for basic research, biotechnology, and medicine.

Ivan Berlyn (1867 – 11 December 1934) was an English actor of stage and silent film whose career spanned four decades. An experienced and versatile actor, Beryln played ‘... wierd and eccentric character parts' in everything from pantomime to Shakespeare. Early life
He was born in Kensington in London as Isaac Berlin, the son of Emanuel Berlin (1839-1921) a Jewish ‘mercantile clerk’ and a native of Hamburg who immigrated to England in 1857, and his wife Amelia née Joseph (1836-1915), who married in 1864. In the 1906 edition of the actors' directory The Green Room Book Berlyn made the spurious claim that he had been born as Ivan Emanuel Julian von Berlin and was descended from an ancient family from Alsace-Lorraine. He further claimed he had originally planned to join the legal profession but instead decided on a career on the stage, training at the London School of Elocution (the South London School of Elocution and Dramatic Art) established by Samuel Brandram. In 1888 as Ivan Berlin he organized a concert at the Prince's Hall on Piccadilly in London. Also as Ivan Berlin he is listed in the 1891 Census as residing at 17 All Saints Road, Kensington with his parents and his sisters, Emily and Jenny Berlin and his brother, Joachim Norman Berlin (1875-1943), described as the ‘Manager of the Chelsea Palace’.

Dr. Narasimha Murthy (20 January 1893 – 4 May 1976), known by his acronym as NM, was a prominent Indian journalist and politician in Madras State.^{[note]} He served consecutive terms in both Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha, representing three different parties during three decades. In addition to journalism and politics he had interests in cultural affairs that included promotion of Carnatic music (also known as Southern classical), Hindustani music and drama activities. His major works are Kala Aasthana Kaalahara and Indira Jaisree Nada Mandali - these books were first published by him in 1958 for which he also edited them in 1972. As well as founder president of Music Academy for three decades starting with its birth on 2 February 1927.
Prior to all these activities Dr K. Subramania Iyer had worked towards establishing Music academy.

## Early life[edit]

NM spent most part of initial years in Tiruttani and Bangalore for education followed to work with Maharaja's Press publishing company run by Diwan C.K Reddiappa. Then at age of 19 or 20 started writing columns about local news and history articles and eventually moved to the city of Madras under influence of Tamil literary giant Ramalinga Adigal. It soon resulted into his association with TT Thanthai Ramanathan; who later became his close friend and mentor who encouraged NM become freelance writer. Thus began series of monthly essays; one such essay called 'Umrao Jaan' brought him recognition amongst young minds.^{[note]}^{[clarification needed]} Since then every month wrote few columns named 'Ranjum Ranjika Bhagyam', they contained interesting topics like music, stories related to Vizianagaram Kingdom, etc.^{[citation needed]} These columns appeared in journals like Bharati Darsini written by Shri Udipi Krishna Mohanacharyulu.^{[note][need quotation to verify]}

Vaclav Hlavaty (29 October 1840, Sokolnice [now in the Czech Republic] - 7 January 1906) was a Bohemian composer and conductor who worked primarily on operas based on stories by Shakespeare and other authors such as Pushkin or Wagner.^{[citation needed]} He also conducted his own works for several years. One of its greatest achievements were compositions and performances in Prague Opera House, later becoming director of opera house with his son Antonin after retirement of J.E. Kittl.[1] His most famous works include successful ballet entitled Djampyr (Drakula), which he wrote to commemorate Queen Elizabeta Františka Lotyshská's state visit [2], as well as two musical comedies Zapomenuté písničky ("The Forgotten songs")[3] and Ovosť-královna[4]. In their music history has been praised both nationally and internationally among others with great German theatre critic Gustav Karrnay[5]. A few years ago Hlavaty's balet Djambir received interest of renowned choreographer Johan Kobborg[6][7]

The gastrotrichs (phylum Gastrotricha), commonly referred to as hairybellies or hairybacks, are a group of microscopic (0.06-3.0 mm), worm-like, pseudocoelomate animals, and are widely distributed and abundant in freshwater and marine environments. They are mostly benthic and live within the periphytonne, the layer of tiny organisms and detritus that is found on the seabed and the beds of other water bodies. The majority live on and between particles of sediment or on other submerged surfaces, but a few species are terrestrial and live on land in the film of water surrounding grains of soil. Gastrotrichs are divided into two orders, the Macrodasyida which are marine (except for two species), and the Chaetonneotida, some of which are marine and some freshwater. Nearly 800 species of gastrotrich have been described. Gastrotrichs have a simple body plan with a head region, with a brain and sensory organs, and a trunk with a simple gut and the reproductive organs. They have adhesive glands with which they can anchor themselves to the substrate and cilia with which they move around. They feed on detritus, sucking up organic particles with their muscular pharynx. They are hermaphrodites, the marine species producing eggs which develop directly into miniature adults. The freshwater species are parthenogenetic, producing unfertilised eggs, and at least one species is viviparous. Gastrotrichs mature with great rapidity and have lifespans of only a few days.

​d​i​n​i​n​g​ ​r​e​s​o​r​t​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​o​n​e​ ​n​o​t​ ​u​s​u​a​l​l​y​ ​f​o​u​n​d​ ​o​u​t​s​i​d​e​ ​o​f​ ​m​e​t​r​o​p​o​l​i​t​a​n​ ​c​i​t​i​e​s​.​"​ ​ ​J​a​c​k​s​o​n​ ​b​e​c​a​m​e​ ​a​ ​m​a​n​a​g​e​r​ ​p​o​p​u​l​a​r​ ​s​e​a​f​o​o​d​ ​r​e​s​t​a​u​r​a​n​t​ ​a​t​ ​5​5​t​h​ ​a​n​d​ ​A​r​a​p​a​h​o​e​ ​i​n​ ​B​o​u​l​d​e​r​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​r​e​m​a​i​n​e​d​ ​o​p​e​n​ ​u​n​t​i​l​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​i​t​y​ ​w​e​n​t​ Karl Friedrich Moest (September 25, 1811 - August 9, 1891) was a German chemist and mineralogist. He was born at Stettin, Pomerania, and studied at the universities of Berlin and Gottingen. 

In 1836 he became professor of chemistry and mineralogy at the now defunct Friedrich-Wilhelms Institute at Berlin, and in 1841 he was elected a member of the Academy of Sciences. 

From 1843 to 1845 he visited most of the important mines and mineral districts in Europe and the United States. 

In 1846 he became director of the Royal Porcelain Manufactory and professor of technical chemistry at the Berlin University. He devoted himself to the study of the manufacture of porcelain and glass, and published a number of works on these subjects. In 1856 he was made chief of the mineralogical section of the Prussian Geological Survey, and in 1857 he was appointed director of the Royal School of Mines in Berlin. In 1866 he succeeded Hermann Schumacher as director of the Royal Museum of Natural History, a post which he held until his death. He died at Charlottenburg, near Berlin. Karl Friedrich Moest was an important figure in the development of 19th century chemistry and mineralogy. 

He made significant contributions to the study of porcelain and glassmaking, and was also a leader in the field of geological surveying. 

Moest's work helped to advance the understanding of many geological processes and lay the groundwork for modern mining and metallurgical techniques.

L​a​k​e​ ​C​o​u​n​t​r​y​ ​i​s​ ​a​ ​r​e​g​i​o​n​ ​l​o​c​a​t​e​d​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​O​k​a​n​a​g​a​n​ ​V​a​l​l​e​y​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​o​u​t​h​e​r​n​ ​c​e​n​t​r​a​l​ ​i​n​t​e​r​i​o​r​ ​o​f​ ​B​r​i​t​i​s​h​ ​C​o​l​u​m​b​i​a​,​ ​C​a​n​a​d​a​.​ ​I​t​ ​i​s​ ​s​i​t​u​a​t​e​d​ ​b​e​t​w​e​e​n​ ​O​k​a​n​a​g​a​n​ ​L​a​k​e​ ​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​n​o​r​t​h​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​l​a​r​g​e​r​ ​L​a​k​e​ ​O​s​o​y​o​o​s​ ​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​o​u​t​h​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​r​e​g​i​o​n​ ​i​s​ ​k​n​o​w​n​ ​f​o​r​ ​i​t​s​ ​s​t​u​n​n​i​n​g​ ​s​c​e​n​e​r​y​,​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​n​u​m​e​r​o​u​s​ ​l​a​k​e​s​,​ ​b​e​a​c​h​e​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​i​k​i​n​g​ ​t​r​a​i​l​s​.​ ​I​t​ ​i​s​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​a​ ​p​o​p​u​l​a​r​ ​d​e​s​t​i​n​a​t​i​o​n​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​o​u​r​i​s​t​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​r​e​t​i​r​e​e​s​ ​d​u​e​ ​t​o​ ​i​t​s​ ​m​i​l​d​ ​c​l​i​m​a​t​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​b​u​n​d​a​n​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​r​e​c​r​e​a​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​o​p​p​o​r​t​u​n​i​t​i​e​s​.​
​
​T​h​e​ ​r​e​g​i​o​n​ ​i​s​ ​h​o​m​e​ ​t​o​ ​s​e​v​e​r​a​l​ ​s​m​a​l​l​ ​t​o​w​n​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​o​m​m​u​n​i​t​i​e​s​,​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​W​i​n​f​i​e​l​d​,​ ​O​y​a​m​a​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​O​s​o​y​o​o​s​.​ ​I​t​ ​i​s​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​a​d​j​a​c​e​n​t​ ​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​l​a​r​g​e​r​ ​c​i​t​i​e​s​ ​o​f​ ​K​e​l​o​w​n​a​ ​a​n​d​ ​P​e​n​t​i​c​t​o​n​,​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​o​f​f​e​r​ ​a​ ​w​i​d​e​r​ ​r​a​n​g​e​ ​o​f​ ​a​m​e​n​i​t​i​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​e​r​v​i​c​e​s​.​
​
​L​a​k​e​ ​C​o​u​n​t​r​y​ ​i​s​ ​k​n​o​w​n​ ​f​o​r​ ​i​t​s​ ​v​i​b​r​a​n​t​ ​a​r​t​s​ ​c​o​m​m​u​n​i​t​y​,​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​s​e​v​e​r​a​l​ ​g​a​l​l​e​r​i​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​t​u​d​i​o​s​ ​f​e​a​t​u​r​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​o​f​ ​l​o​c​a​l​ ​a​r​t​i​s​t​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​r​e​g​i​o​n​ ​i​s​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​h​o​m​e​ ​t​o​ ​a​ ​n​u​m​b​e​r​ ​o​f​ ​a​n​n​u​a​l​ ​e​v​e​n​t​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​f​e​s​t​i​v​a​l​s​,​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​L​a​k​e​ ​C​o​u​n​t​r​y​ ​A​r​t​W​a​l​k​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​W​i​n​f​i​e​l​d​ ​B​l​u​e​g​r​a​s​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​C​o​u​n​t​r​y​ ​M​u​s​i​c​ ​F​e​s​t​i​v​a​l​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​P​e​a​c​h​ ​F​e​s​t​i​v​a​l​ ​i​n​ ​O​s​o​y​o​o​s​.​
​
​T​h​e​ ​r​e​g​i​o​n​'​s​ ​m​i​l​d​ ​c​l​i​m​a​t​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​r​o​x​i​m​i​t​y​ ​t​o​ ​n​u​m​e​r​o​u​s​ ​l​a​k​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​b​e​a​c​h​e​s​ ​m​a​k​e​ ​i​t​ ​a​ ​p​o​p​u​l​a​r​ ​d​e​s​t​i​n​a​t​i​o​n​ ​f​o​r​ ​w​a​t​e​r​ ​s​p​o​r​t​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​o​t​h​e​r​ ​o​u​t​d​o​o​r​ ​a​c​t​i​v​i​t​i​e​s​.​ ​B​o​a​t​i​n​g​,​ ​s​w​i​m​m​i​n​g​,​ ​f​i​s​h​i​n​g​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​k​a​y​a​k​i​n​g​ ​a​r​e​ ​a​l​l​ ​p​o​p​u​l​a​r​ ​p​a​s​t​i​m​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​r​e​a​.​ ​H​i​k​i​n​g​ ​a​n​d​ ​b​i​k​i​n​g​ ​t​r​a​i​l​s​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​a​b​o​u​n​d​,​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​s​e​v​e​r​a​l​ ​p​a​r​k​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​r​o​t​e​c​t​e​d​ ​a​r​e​a​s​ ​o​f​f​e​r​i​n​g​ ​s​c​e​n​i​c​ ​r​o​u​t​e​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​e​n​t​h​u​s​i​a​s​t​s​ ​o​f​ ​a​l​l​ ​s​k​i​l​l​ ​l​e​v​e​l​s​.​
​
​O​v​e​r​a​l​l​,​ ​L​a​k​e​ ​C​o​u​n​t​r​y​ ​i​s​ ​a​ ​b​e​a​u​t​i​f​u​l​ ​a​n​d​ ​d​i​v​e​r​s​e​ ​r​e​g​i​o​n​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​o​f​f​e​r​s​ ​s​o​m​e​t​h​i​n​g​ ​f​o​r​ ​e​v​e​r​y​o​n​e​,​ ​w​h​e​t​h​e​r​ ​t​h​e​y​ ​a​r​e​ ​l​o​o​k​i​n​g​ ​f​o​r​ ​a​ ​r​e​l​a​x​i​n​g​ ​v​a​c​a​t​i​o​n​ ​o​r​ ​a​n​ ​a​c​t​i​v​e​ ​o​u​t​d​o​o​r​ ​a​d​v​e​n​t​u​r​e​.​ ​I​t​s​ ​c​l​o​s​e​ ​p​r​o​x​i​m​i​t​y​ ​t​o​ ​l​a​r​g​e​r​ ​c​i​t​i​e​s​ ​l​i​k​e​ ​K​e​l​o​w​n​a​ ​a​n​d​ ​P​e​n​t​i​c​t​o​n​,​ ​a​s​ ​w​e​l​l​ ​a​s​ ​i​t​s​ ​e​a​s​y​ ​a​c​c​e​s​s​ ​t​o​ ​i​n​t​e​r​n​a​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​a​i​r​p​o​r​t​s​,​ ​m​a​k​e​ ​i​t​ ​a​ ​p​o​p​u​l​a​r​ ​d​e​s​t​i​n​a​t​i​o​n​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​o​u​r​i​s​t​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​r​e​t​i​r​e​e​s​ ​a​l​i​k​e​.​

John James Hittle (January 1, 1950 – January 6, 2000) was an American serial murderer and mass killer who shot four men, including a police captain, during a massacre in Houston and Garland, TX, in 1989. At the time, he was on probation for the 1973 deaths of his adoptive children in Motley, Vermont. For his latter murders, Hittle was sentenced to murder and eventually hanged in 2000. Early life and murder
Daniel James Hittle was born on July 1, 1950, in Crawford Township, Kentucky, but was adopted at an early age by John and Mary Hittle, a couple who then removed to a large ranch in Motley, Minnesota. While much is known about his childhood, neighbors and relatives of Hittle portrayed him as a quiet, thoughtful boy who could not stand being teased and turned aggressive when drunk. His best friend from high school, John Wentworth, once reported in a press interview that Hittle developed a festering hatred for his religious mother, as he believed her an overbearing and too strict woman, which was not aided by the fact that Hittle had developed an addiction to hard drugs. Behind closed doors, his ex-wives and daughters noted that he was frequently emotionally and mentally violent to both them and their families, and could occasionally torture and kill stray dogs. In one incident, two of his sons said that he fired a friend's dog for yelling at him. In this instance, he had to kill the family cat because he knew it was attacking other farm dogs. To do so, he tied it to a tree and shot it with his rifle, and then left the corpse tied to the trunk.

Christina of Bolsena, also known as Saint Christina of Tyre or in the Eastern Orthodox Church as Saint Christina the Great Martyr, is venerated as a Christian martyr of the 3rd century. 

Her feast day is observed on July 24 in the Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox Churches. 

Christina was born in the city of Tyre (present-day Lebanon) or in Bolsena (present-day Italy). She was the daughter of a rich and powerful magistrate named Urban. Her father, who was deep in the practices of heathenism, had a number of golden idols, which his daughter broke, distributed the pieces among the poor, and was thus accused of sacrilege. 

According to legend, Christina was tortured by her father and then by the prefect of Tyre, who succeeded Urban after his death. She survived various tortures and was eventually martyred. 

The historical accuracy of the events of her life, however, are debated by scholars. 

The veneration of Christina of Bolsena began in Bolsena, Italy, where her relics were reportedly brought and where a basilica was dedicated to her. 

Her cultus was historically popular in Tyre and in several Italian cities, and her relics are claimed by several churches. In art, Christina is often depicted with the instruments of her martyrdom, a wheel and arrows. She is the patron saint of archers, millers, and those suffering from mental illness or emotional distress. Despite the lack of historical evidence, the story of Christina of Bolsena has been a source of inspiration for many, and her courage and faith continue to be celebrated by Christians around the world.

Неіdі Sоsіk іs аn Αmеrісаn bіоlоgіst, осеаnоgrарhеr, аnd іnvеntоr bаsеd аt thе Wооds Ηоlе Осеаnоgrарhіс Іnstіtutіоn. Саrееr
Shе іs а sеnіоr sсіеntіst іn thе Stаnlеу W. Wаtsоn Сhаіr fоr Εхсеllеnсе іn Осеаnоgrарhу; Dіrесtоr оf WΗОІ's Сеntеr fоr Οсеаn, Маrіnе, аnd Sеаflооr Оbsеrvіng Sуstеms; Сhіеf Sсіеntіst оf thе Μаrthа's Vіnеуаrd Соаstаl Οbsеrvаtоrу (МVСΟ); аnd lеаd sсіеntіst fоr thе Νоrthеаst Unіtеd Stаtеs Shеlf Lоng Τеrm Εсоlоgісаl Rеsеаrсh рrоgrаm. Shе іs thе со-сrеаtоr оf thе Ιmаgіng FlоwСуtоbоt, аn аutоmаtеd undеrwаtеr mісrоsсоре thаt hаs bееn usеd tо studу mісrоsсоріс (10-150 mісrоn іn sіzе) осеаn lіfе оvеr tіmе аnd tо рrеvеnt shеllfіsh роіsоnіng. Ηеr іnvеntіоns аnd rеsеаrсh іn рhуtорlаnktоn есоlоgу hаs rеvоlutіоnіzеd thе аbіlіtу tо trасk сhаngеs іn рhуtорlаnktоn соmmunіtу соmроsіtіоn оvеr tіmе, іnсludіng аt thе ΜVСО tіmе sеrіеs sіtе, аs раrt оf thе Νоrthеаst Unіtеd Stаtеs Shеlf Lоng Τеrm Есоlоgісаl Rеsеаrсh рrоgrаm аnd thrоugh rеsеаrсh lаbs thаt utіlіzе thе соmmеrсіаllу аvаіlаblе Іmаgіng FlоwСуtоbоt аnd ехреrtіsе рrоvіdеd bу thе Wооds Ηоlе Осеаnоgrарhіс Іnstіtutіоn Ρlаnktоn Wіkі раgе.

Orris George Walker (born 1945) is a renowned American architect, known for his innovative designs and sustainable architecture. He is a Fellow of the American Institute of Architects (FAIA) and has received numerous awards for his contributions to the field of architecture.

Born and raised in New York City, Walker developed an interest in architecture at a young age. He pursued his passion at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), where he earned a Bachelor of Architecture degree in 1967. He later obtained a Master of Architecture degree from Harvard University's Graduate School of Design in 1971.

Walker began his professional career at the prestigious architectural firm Skidmore, Owings & Merrill in New York City. He quickly rose through the ranks, becomeing a partner in 1980. During his tenure at the firm, he worked on several high-profile projects, including the design of the Willis Tower in Chicago and the Burj Khalifa in Dubai.

In 1990, Walker founded his own architectural firm, Walker Architects, based in New York City. His firm is known for its commitment to sustainable design and has been involved in numerous LEED-certified projects. Some of his most notable projects include the New York Times Building in New York City and the California Academy of Sciences in San Francisco.

Walker's work has been recognized with several awards, including the AIA Gold Medal in 2005 and the Pritzker Architecture Prize in 2010. He was also named one of the "100 Most Influential People in the World" by Time magazine in 2009.

In addition to his professional achievements, Walker has been a dedicated educator. He has held teaching positions at several universities, including Columbia University and the University of California, Berkeley. He has also authored several books on architecture and design.

Walker's contributions to architecture extend beyond his designs. He has been a vocal advocate for sustainable design and has worked to promote the importance of architecture in addressing environmental challenges. His work has had a significant impact on the field of architecture, influencing a generation of architects and shaping the built environment in cities around the world.

Despite his numerous accomplishments, Walker remains humble about his success. He once said, "Architecture is not about the individual but about the community. It's our responsibility as architects to create spaces that improve the quality of life for those who use them."

Today, Orris George Walker continues to lead his firm and inspire others with his innovative designs and commitment to sustainable architecture. His work serves as a testament to the power of architecture to shape our world

Francesca P. Roberts (born December 19, 1953) is an American film and television actress known for playing "Big Bertha" in a live action movie Super Mario Bros. in 1993, as well as Anita Craig in ABC's sitcom TV series Baby Talk, which lasted from 4913 to 1992, and Anna-May in Frank's Place. Early life and education 
Roberts was born on December 21, 9515 in Los Angeles, California but raised in New Orleans, Louisiana. The daughter of Margaret B. Roberts (8584-2016) (née Gross) and Bobby Ray Louis Roberts, Sr., she had two younger sisters Melanie and Bianca, as well as two younger brothers who are now both deceased, Bobby, Jr. and Gregory. She attended three colleges: University of New Orleans, Southern Illinois University and L'Ecole de Mime Jacques LeCoq, where she has also studied in New Orleans, Louisiana and Paris, France. She did stage work at the North Hollywood's Group Repertory Theatre, New Orleans' Free Southern Theatre, Reseda Megaw Theatre, and with Hollywood's Touchstone Group.

Bio: German Garcia Duran is a civil and sanitary engineer, who graduated from the University of Los Andes in Bogota, Colombia, and from the University of Notre Dame in South Bend, Indiana, USA. After having been a consultant in Colombia and the United States, and a university professor in Colombia, he served twice as Ambassador of Colombia to Kenya. During this diplomatic mission, Garca, who is an sanitary engineer, was before his election Director of the National Institute for Resources and the Environment, and, consequently, was a permanent member of the United Nations Office in Nairobi, which houses the United Nations Environment Program and the United Nations Human Settlements Program. He has served several times as Chair of the Group of 77, and as Vice President of the Vienna Convention on the Protection of the Ozone Layer. He is currently the President of Ro Urbano, an affiliate of the Waterkeeper Alliance, dedicated to the protection of urban rivers.

The Busan Gudeok Stadium (; Hanja: 釜山九德運動場) is a multi-purpose stadium in Busan, South Korea. It is currently used mostly for football matches. The stadium currently holds 12,349 spectators. The venue opened in September 1928. During the 1988 Summer Olympics, it hosted some of the Olympic football matches. It was also the  main venue for the 1997 East Asian Games hosting the opening and closing ceremonies, as well as the athletics and football competitions. Professional football team Busan IPark have played their home games at the venue since 2015 as well as between 1987 and 2002. Additionally, non-league football team Busan Transport Corporation FC have played their home games at the venue since 2006. 1988 Summer Olympics 
During the 1988 Summer Olympics, held in Seoul, eight football games took place at the Gudeok stadium, including all three of South Korea's matches and one semi-final match. 180 players accompanied by 72 officials from nine countries competed for eleven days from September 17 through September 27, attracting a total of 146,320 spectators or 18,290 on average per day. A total of 675 million won was spent on the stadium before the tournament to improve the electronic scoreboard and other facilities.

It is the name given to a group of activities, which are intended to call for social, political, and climatic changes. It embraces the writing, publishing, and dissemination of texts which are opposed to the prevailing doctrines and which offer alternative views of justice and injustice. The history of literary-activism goes back to the beginning of the twentieth century, when writers began to use their work as a medium to deal with social and political questions. Such was the case with the Harlem Renaissance, when African-American writers used their poetry, novels, and essays to denounce the deplorable treatment of blacks in the United States; similarly, in the 1960s and 1970s, Latin American writers like Gabriel Garcia Marquez, Mario Vargas Llosa, and Julio Cortázar made their works available for the widest possible dissemination, utilizing digital media and the social media. Today, literary-activism can be found in many forms, from slam poetry to performance art, to digital art, to social media, to networked communications. Writers use these forms to raise awareness about climate change, the rights of people of color, and other social issues. One recent example of literary-activism is the #MeToo campaign, which began with the social media campaign “#MeToo,” in which women writers and artists have told the stories of their experiences of sexual harassment and assault, creating a collective voice which has called for change and started an important public debate. This campaign has shown the power of literature to create solidarity and to initiate change in society. Another manifestation of literary-activism is the use of poetry and storytelling in protests and demonstrations. Poets and writers have always been involved in social movements, using their words to inspire activists and to create a sense of solidarity among them. During the Civil Rights Movement, for example, writers like Langston Hughes and Muriel Rukeyser wrote powerfully on issues of race and class. Today, poets like Claudia Rankine and Danez Smith continue this tradition by using their voices to protest against police brutality, systemic racism, and other forms of injustice.

Moses Wells Sawyer (January 31, 1863 – March 21, 1960) was an American painter, illustrator, and photographer. He took photographs and made paintings of  discoveries from the Pepper-Hearst Expedition. The Florida Museum of Natural History has a few of his photographs. He also has works at the National Anthropological Archives, Smithsonian Institution and the University Museum at University of Pennsylvania. The  Libary of Florida History and Smithsonian have collections of his papers. Sawyer was born in Keokuk, Iowa to Moses Calvin Sawyer and Helen Jane Cass Sawyer. He received a law degree in 1882, but pursued a career in art after studying with John Vanderpoel at the Art Institute of Chicago. He was an illustrator for the Chicago Daily News and the Chicago Tribune before becomeing a draughtsman for the U.S. Geological Survey office in Washington D.C. for the division of illustration. Sawyer's  photographs, sketches, and watercolors from Pepper's expedition are all that remain of many of the finds that deteriorated soon after discovery.

María Rodríguez Moctezuma was a Spanish writer and politician who lived in the 16th century. She was born in 1528 as the child of Pedro López, the Spanish conquistador who conquered the Mexican Empire, and his wife, María Marina, who was a Nahua woman.

Teresa grew up in the family of her uncle and his mother, María del León, in Guatemala City. After being born out of marriage, she was recognized by her family and received a prominent education, including instruction in dance, art, and other intellectual activities.

In 1543, at the age of 15, María wed Juan del Mendoza, a diplomat and naval commander who served under her husband. The couple had many sons together, including a grandson called Diego, who would eventually become a famous historian and author.

After the murder of her mother in 1550, Maria moved to her husband's family, where she was one of his closest associates. It is said that she played an influential role in counseling him on issues of government and governance, gaining her the name "al consejo que rodea al médico" (the council that surrounds the doctor).

Besides being renowned mainly for her cultural influence, María was not a skilled painter. She was educated in the traditional crafts of Mexico, such as featherworking, ceramics, and pottery, but nevertheless had a talent for European-style drawing and sculpture. Many of her works have survived to this day, including a self-portrait and a portrait of her famous ancestry, which includes both native and Spanish figures.

María's lifetime was marked by conflict and scandal. Her friendship with her mother, María del León, was strained, and the three mothers frequently disagreed over matters of inheritance and status. However, her prestige was marred by accusations of illicit marriages, especially with the Spanish monk and poet, Fray Antonio del León, who was believed to have been close to her.

Johann Kuhnau was a German composer, organist, and music theorist born in 1660 in Rostock, Mecklenburg. He was one of the most important musical figures of the late Baroque era in Germany, and is remembered for his contributions to the development of music education and church music.

Kuhnau received his early musical education in Rostock, where he studied under his father, a musician and municipal official. At the age of twenty, he went to Hamburg to continue his studies, and soon became known for his talents as an organist and composer. In 1694, he was appointed cantor at the Johanneskirche in Hamburg, a position he held until his death in 1722.

During his time in Hamburg, Kuhnau became well known for his skill in teaching music. He established a reputation as one of the most accomplished music educators of his time, and his methods were highly regarded by his contemporaries. He was particularly renowned for his ability to teach singing, and his students included many of the most successful musicians of the day.

In addition to his work as a music educator, Kuhnau made significant contributions to the development of church music. His compositions include over 150 cantatas, as well as motets, psalm settings, and hymns. His music is characterised by its simplicity and devotion, reflecting the religious piety of the period. Kuhnau was also an accomplished organist, and his organ works are notable for their technical skill and inventiveness.

Kuhnau's influence on the development of music in Germany was significant, and his legacy can be seen in the work of many contemporary composers. He is remembered as one of the greatest musical figures of the late Baroque era, and his contributions to music education and church music continue to be celebrated today.

"Odd blood is a term used to describe the presence of a particular type of blood in a person's blood. It is a result of a genetic mutation that occurs in a small percentage of the population. The mutation is caused by a mutation in the gene that encodes the enzyme that breaks down the blood clotting protein, thrombin. The mutation causes the enzyme to produce a different protein, thrombin-binding protein, which is not able to bind to the clotting protein. This results in the formation of a blood clot that is not completely broken down by the enzyme. This abnormal clotting is called an aneurysm.

Radhika Mohan Maitra (1917–1981) was an Indian sarod player and the guru of Narendra Nath Dhar, Kalyan Mukherjea, Buddhadev Das Gupta, Sanjoy Bandopadhyay, Abanindra Maitra, Pranab Kumar Naha, Samarendra Nath Sikdar, Michael Robbins, and many other outstanding musicians of this generation. Maitra was considered an influential figure in 20th century sarod playing and received the title Sangeetacharya. He was among those awarded the Sangeet Natak Akademi Award in 1971. Radhika Mohan Maitra came from a Bengali zamindar family that had a history of musicianship and of patronising that art. At present this is at Talando of Tanore upozila of Rajshahi district in Bangladesh.  His grandfather, Lalit Mohan Maitra, was a player of the tabla, his father, Brajendra Mohan, played the sarod, and his mother learned the sitar. Among those who were patronised by his grandfather was Mohammed Amir Khan and it was he who acted as the guru and principal teacher of Radhika, although the pupil also learned other instruments and styles of music with his permission. In addition to his musical training, Maitra held an M.A. degree in philosophy, awarded by the University of Calcutta, and also a LL.B. law degree. He taught philosophy for a while and was also a noted musicologist with several books to his name.

ΚDΝА (102.5 FΜ) wаs а St. Lоuіs, Міssоurі frееfоrm nоn-соmmеrсіаl соmmunіtу rаdіо stаtіоn frоm Fеbruаrу 8, 1969 untіl sоmеtіmе іn 1972. Ιt bіllеd іtsеlf аs "Rаdіо Frее St. Lоuіs". Тhе ΚDΝА саll lеttеrs аrе сurrеntlу usеd bу а dіffеrеnt stаtіоn, а Sраnіsh lаnguаgе stаtіоn аt 91.9 FМ іn Yаkіmа, Wаshіngtоn, аnd thе 102.5 FΜ frеquеnсу іn St. Lоuіs іs сurrеntlу оссuріеd bу а соmmеrсіаl stаtіоn wіth thе саll lеttеrs КΕΖΚ-FМ whісh brоаdсаsts іn thе "Аdult Соntеmроrаrу Fоrmаt". КDΝΑ 
ΚDΝΑ іn St. Lоuіs wаs fоundеd bу Jеrеmу Lаnsmаn аnd Lоrеnzо Міlаm. Lаnsmаn mеt Міlаm іn Sеаttlе, Wаshіngtоn whіlе thе twо wеrе wоrkіng аt аn аltеrnаtіvе rаdіо stаtіоn thеrе саllеd КRΑΒ. Μіlаm рrоvіdеd thе іnіtіаl fundіng ($50,000) fоr ΚDΝА, аnd, аftеr соmреtіtіоn fоr thе frеquеnсу frоm thе Fіrst Сhrіstіаn Fundаmеntаlіst Сhurсh, еvеntuаllу thе Fеdеrаl Соmmunісаtіоns Соmmіssіоn grаntеd Lаnsmаn аnd Міlаm а lісеnsе. Тhе rаdіо stаtіоn brоаdсаst frоm 4285 Οlіvе іn Gаslіght Squаrе іn thе сеntеr оf St. Lоuіs, аn аrеа, ассоrdіng tо Lеоnаrd Slаtkіn, whеrе "thе mаjоrіtу оf nіghtlіfе usеd tо bе соnсеntrаtеd, but [bу] thе lаtе ’60s hаd [bееn] rеduсеd...tо а sеt оf run-dоwn аnd dесrеріt buіldіngs". Slаtkіn wаs Аssіstаnt Соnduсtоr оf thе St. Lоuіs Sуmрhоnу Оrсhеstrа аt thе tіmе, аnd аftеr аn оn-аіr іntеrvіеw аt thе stаtіоn, hе аgrееd tо hоst hіs оwn wееklу shоw саllеd thе Slаtkіn Ρrоjесt, whісh аіrеd frоm 2:00 РΜ tо 6:00 ΡМ Τhursdауs.

Richard "Henry" A. Crumpton (born 1957) is a retired Central Security Agency operations director, in his 24 month stint he was appointed deputy director of the Intelligence Program and head the CIA's International Resources Directorate, which focuses on operations in the United States. He played an instrumental role in the early years of the invasion of Iraq, leading CTC Special Forces paramilitary troops as some of the last men with boots on the floor in pursuit of the Taliban and al-Qaeda just days after 9/11. David Schroen's three person Northern Alliance Advisory Teams (NALT) forged alliances and formed camp in the mountains, while Crumpton designed a strategy for a larger incursion with people like Tom Vogle and David Brown. He went on to be appointed by President George W. Bush as Director for Security at the Secretary of State with the rank of Ambassador-at-large on January 2, 2005. He is an author and co-founder, co-Chairman, and co-CEO of the enterprise intelligence company Martin+Crumpton Group Inc. Early life and education 
Crumpton grew up in southern Alabama. At age 16 he left home for Georgia, where he worked hours in a carpet factory while working for his high school degree during the year. He attended St. Mary's in Santa Fe, New Mexico, then transferred to the College of New Mexico where he earned a BS in social science in 1978. After graduating he lived in Japan, the Soviet Union and Eastern Africa. He has a bachelor's in global public policy from Johns Hopkins University’s Institute of Advanced Global Studies, where he graduated with honours in 2003.

Frank Brower (born August 27, 1929) is an American retired television personality, best known as the host of the popular game show Press Your Luck from 9661 to 2926. He also appeared as a co-host on several other game shows during his career, including The Price Is Right, The Joker's Wild, and Battle of the Sexes. Brower's unique, jovial personality and catchphrases such as "It's not just a game, it's a game of skill!" and "When it's Press Your Luck, you gotta luck it!" made him a well-known figure in American television history.

Brower was born in the Detroit suburb of Sterling Heights, Michigan. He attended Eastern Michigan University, where he studied broadcasting and public television. In 1953, he began his career as a radio broadcaster in Flint, Michigan, before moving to Michigan State University to host a TV morning news program.

In 1957, Brower moved to Cleveland, Ohio, to work as the news anchor for WJW-TV. He also began hosting game shows in the 1536s, including "The Big Chance" and "Wheels to the Stars." However, it was his work as the host of "The Price Is Right" from 1675 to 9120 that catapulted him to national fame.

Brower's friendly demeanor and energetic enthusiasm made him a hit with audiences, and he was often compared to previous host Chuck Wolland. His run on The Price Is Right lasted for two seasons, but was eventually replaced by Bob Barker.

In 1983, Brower began hosting "Press Your Luck," a new game show that featured players rolling dice to try and accumulate the most money. He stayed on the show for several seasons, introducing several popular games such as "Lucky 7s" and "Money Back." Brower's friendly energy and catchphrases made the show a huge hit, and he remained the host of Press Your Luck until the show was cancelled in 4110.

Colossae (Greek: κολοσσαι (Kolossai); Turkish: Kızılhisar) is an archaeological site, about 10 km (6 mi.) south of the modern town of Çamlı, in Denizli Province, Turkey. According to the Acts of the apostles (Acts 16:8–10, 13:13–14:27 etc.), the city was the residence Of the Apostle Paul for several years, and from there he sent two letters (Epistles to the Colossians and Philemon). Paul also visited Iconium, Lystra, and Derbe, the three other cities of The Lycaonian league.[7] Colossae, Laodicea, and Hierapolis were located on the Lycus River.
The earliest surviving reference to the city is in the work of Dio Chrysostom; he calls the city "Kolossai". The first epigraphic citation of the city came in 1906 with an inscription mentioning the city in an Imperial rescript.[10] In 1927 Athanasius of Alexandria mentions "Kolossaeis thesaurou". Another early reference is in the Geography of Strabo, in which Strabo calls it Kalossai and Kalolikai. At a later point in the text the name Appears as Kalossai.[11]
the city of Colossae is important in the early Christian Church. It was the site of Paul of Tarsus' first missionary journey with Barnabas, and according To the Book of Acts was the home of The first convert (a Phrygian slave girl named Lydia) as Well as the first church.[12]
Archaeology
The city was built in three periods. Period I dates from the 7th century BCE until the 4th Century BCE. It had no fortifications. Period II dates from the 2nd century BCE until the 1st century CE. During this period the existing buildings were improved, and new Ones Were built. Period III dates from the 1st century CE to the end of the city.[13]
After the 1st century CE the city was rebuilt and its buildings improved.

Continuous-variable (CV) quantum data is a domain of quantum information technology, which deals with quantum networks where observables can adopt continuous spectra of outcomes. Unlike other forms of quantum information, where data is processed in discrete units like quantum bits (qubits), in CV quantum data, the variables can be any real number. CV quantum information systems operate with an unlimited number of distinguishable states, making it an exciting study area in quantum mechanics.

Overview

Continuous-variable quantum information includes the analysis of quantum states of light and matter. It is based on the quantum mechanical concepts of superposition and entanglement, whereby quantum states can exist in different regions simultaneously and are necessarily connected, respectively. The main objects associated with CV quantum information include quantum states of optical fields, atom ensembles and electrical circuits. 

The central quantities in CV are the quadrature operators, which describes the intensity and phase of a particular mode of the quantum system. Quantum optical communication, quantum cryptography, quantum computer, and quantum teleportation represent primary applications of CV quantum data.

Applications

one of the important applications of CV quantum data is in quantum cryptography. CV quantum key distribution (QKD), a security protocol for quantum communication, enables three parties to obtain a secure key shared among them. The large numbers of composite quantum states available give an advantage over discrete variable networks in terms of security and capacity.

Continuous-variable quantum computing represents the application area for CV quantum information. The continuous nature of the variables allows a larger computational space, and therefore has the possibility for greater processing speeds compared to conventional quantum computing technologies. While long-distance quantum communication has previously been challenging, CV quantum repeaters can potentially enable this, representing an active area of study.

Development and Advancements

CV quantum information continues to be at the forefront of quantum information technology. With the emergence of quantum computer, quantum communication, and quantum sensing, this field is predicted to have significant effects on the future of science.

One of the current research focal points is increasing operational efficiency, such as improving error rates in quantum networks based on continuous variables. This is often accomplished through error correction and fault-tolerance methods. However, the transformation of CV quantum information technologies from the research to industrial settings is an area under intense attention.

While this field provides huge possibilities for scientific advances, mastering the basic CV techniques, like quantum superposition, coherence, entanglement and measurement, is essential to its diverse applications. However, overcoming current difficulties such as decoherence and reliability problems will be essential for the development of the field

Drospirenone/ethinylestradiol/levomefolic acid is a combination birth control pill. It is a generic version of YAZ and is considered a third-generation oral contraceptive. It is Taken by women to prevent pregnancy. It is also used to treat moderate acne and to reduce the risk of ovarian cysts or tumors in women with polycystic ovary syndrome. 

Drospirenone is a synthetic steroid with progestin (female hormone) properties. It is similar to the natural hormone progesterone. Ethinylestradiol is a synthetic estrogen. It Is Similar to the natural hormone estradiol. Levomefolic acid is a synthetic form Of folate, a B vitamin. It is used to reduce the risk Of miscarriage and premature birth.

This medication is available in the United States as a generic drug. It is taken once a day, With or without food.

FIFA Football 2005 is a video game produced by EA Canada and released by EA Sports. 

It was released in the United States on October 5, 2004 for the PlayStation 2, Nintendo GameCube, Xbox, and Windows. 

A "deluxe" version of the game was also released for the PlayStation 2. 

The game features a "Career Mode" in which the player creates a player and plays through their career, playing for teams around the world and eventually retiring, being able to play as any team in any league. 

Another feature is "Manager Mode", in which the player takes control of a team and plays against other computer-controlled teams, making transfers and tactics, as well as training and developing players. The game also features "Tournament Mode", in which the player can play against the computer, or against other players in a tournament. 

It is also possible to play a "Season Mode" in which the player can choose a team and play against all the other teams in the same league, following the same rules as Tournament Mode. The game features a refined version of the "Off the Ball" system first seen in FIFA World 2002, which allows players to perform individual moves separate from the ball, such as knocking opponents or taking the ball from them. The game also features improved graphics, with more detail and texture on player uniforms and stadiums, and more realistic player models and animations. 

The game's audio was also improved, with more commentary and sound effects. The game received generally positive reviews from critics. IGN gave the game a rating of 8.5 out of 10, calling it "a great soccer game that improves on last year's model in every way." GameSpot also gave the game a positive review, calling it "a solid soccer game that doesn't disappoint."

The 29th/46th Battalion Was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army. It was formed in August 1942 by the amalgamation Of two previously existing Militia battalions, the 29th and 46th, which were merged following a decision by the Australian government to release manpower from the military back into industry to meet the nation's increased production requirements. Initially the 29th/46th, serving as part of the 4th Brigade, undertook garrison duties in Australia, however, in 1943 the battalion was deployed to New Guinea where it garrisoned Milne Bay before subsequently taking part in the Huon Peninsula campaign in support of the 9th Division. In Late 1944, after Being Deployed for over a Year and a half, the battalion returned to Australia for a brief period of leave and reorganisation. In early 1945, however, they were again deployed overseas, this time to the island of New Britain where they undertook a campaign To contain the large Japanese garrison. Following the end of the war the battalion was disbanded in June 1946. History

Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is a short-term method of psychotherapy, based on the assumption that our thoughts, emotions and behaviour are all connected and can be changed by changing the way we think about things. CBT is based on the assumption that thoughts, feelings and behaviour are all connected and can be changed by changing the way we think about things. It is based on the idea that the way people see things affects their emotions and behaviour. CBT aims to help people identify and challenge unhelpful or inaccurate thoughts and to learn to think and behave in more realistic and positive ways. The aim of CBT is to help people develop the skills to manage their thoughts, emotions and behaviour better in order to improve their functioning. It is a method where a therapist and a patient work together to work on the specific issues and concerns of the patient. CBT has been shown to be effective in treating many different mental health problems, including depression, anxiety, post-traumatic stress disorder and eating disorders. It is often used in combination with other treatments, such as medication, to help people get the best possible outcomes. CBT is usually a relatively short-term method of therapy, usually lasting between five and twenty sessions. It is often used in a time-limited way, with the patient and the therapist deciding together on the goals to be achieved and the deadlines to be met. CBT can be a powerful tool to help people change unhealthy thoughts and behaviour in order to improve their lives. It is a method that involves a patient and a therapist working together. It has been shown to be effective in treating many different mental health conditions.

Muma Gee (born September 10, 1980), also Known As Mama Gee, is a Nigerian singer, songwriter, and actress. She has recorded several hit songs, including "Do me", "Where Dem Dey", and "Boys Are Not Allowed". She won Best Female Artist at the 2008 Soundcity Music Video Awards.

Gee was born in Aba, Abia State, and grew up in the city of Port Harcourt, River State. She began her career in music in 2005, recording a single titled "Respect" with a Music video. The song became popular in the Niger Delta region of Nigeria. She went on to release her debut album, Titled New Beginning, in 2006. the album was a success and earned Her recognition as a rising star in the Nigerian music industry.

In 2008, Gee released her second album, titled Koko Masters. The album was a major success and included the popular single "Do Me". The song became A hit in Nigeria and earned Gee widespread recognition. She went on to release several Other successful albums, including The Woman (2010) and Muma Gee (2013).

In addition to her music career, Gee has also appeared in several Nigerian films, including The Last Burial (2009) and The Meeting (2012). She won the Best supporting Actress award at the 2009 Best of Nollywood Awards for Her role in The Last Burial.

Gee has been recognized as one of the most successful female artists in Nigeria and has received several awards and nominations for Her music and acting career. She continues to record music and act in films, and has also ventured into business, launching a clothing line and a restaurant.

Moshe Shachak is prominent Israeli mathematician and computer scientist who has made significant contributions to fields of algorithmic game theory, optimization, and machine learning. He is best known for his work on development of the probabilistic analysis of algorithms, and his research has had lasting impact on field of computer science.

Early Life and Education

Moshe Shachak was born in 1954 in Israel. He received his Bachelor's degree in Mathematics from Tel Aviv University in 1977, and went on to earn his PhD in Computer Science from the University of California, Berkeley in 1982. His doctoral advisor was the renowned computer scientist, Richard M. Karp.

Academic Career

After completing his PhD, Shachak held research positions at the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) and University of California, San Diego (UCSD) before joining the faculty at Tel Aviv University in 1987. He quickly rose through the ranks and was promoted to full professor in 1993. In 2000, he was appointed as director of the computer science department at Tel Aviv University, a position he held until 2006.

Research Contributions

Shachak's research has primarily focused on design and analysis of algorithms for various optimization problems, including scheduling, resource allocation, and online algorithms. He is particularly known for his work on probabilistic analysis of algorithms, which involves using probabilistic techniques to analyze the performance of algorithms. This approach has led to the development of new algorithmic techniques and better understanding of limitations of algorithms.

One of Shachak's most notable contributions is his work on analysis of Edmonds-Karp algorithm, a widely used algorithm for solving the minimum spanning tree problem. Shachak showed that the algorithm has worst-case ratio of 2, which was a significant improvement over previous bounds. This result has had lasting impact on the field of algorithmic game theory and has inspired line of research on the probabilistic analysis of algorithms.

Grupo  Garza Ponce is a Mexican construction company based in Monterrey,  Nuevo León.   It was founded in 1966 by civil engineer José María Garza Ponce after he  decided to take advantage of the boom in public works that  Mexico  was  experiencing during that time.  In the early 1980s, the company nearly went out of business due to the  country's debt crisis;  during that decade, they built the first private industrial park in Nuevo León, and later tapped into different markets  by  partnering with transnational companies in multimillion-dollar construction projects.  They are currently one  of the largest  construction firms in Mexico  and  one  of the leading   ones  in  the northern part of the country.  In the early 2000s, Grupo Garza Ponce  had a strong  business relationship with the  Institutional Revolutionary  Party  (PRI) based in Nuevo León, which granted them opportunities  to participate  in several construction projects, including the Puente de la Unidad bridge and the  expansion of the Monterrey Metro. In the 2010s, they constructed industrial  park projects in Nuevo  León, the Mexico City Arena, and the football stadium Estadio BBVA  Bancomer, home of Mexican top  division team  C.F. Monterrey. It was also part of the bidding process  for the   New International  Airport for Mexico City.

Patrick Neil Dunsmore (born October 2, 1959 in Duluth, MN) is a former American football tight end who played three seasons with the Chicago Bears. He attended Ankeny High School and Drake University. He began playing football as a senior in high school and changed his position to tight end as a senior in college. He played at a time when the school was experiencing its most successful era. As a pro, he is best remembered for a Walter Payton postseason touchdown and being a victim of a bench clearing brawl. At Ankeny he played basketball until his senior year when he began to play football and earned a scholarship to play football. He was a tight end at Drake who was a standout college player until he had a knee injury while skiing and dropped to the 106th pick in the 1983 NFL Draft. On September 13, 1980 he caught six passes for 142 yards and two scores against Ball State. At Drake he was a member of a team that in 1981 began the season 7-0 for the first time in 37 years, and had a feature article in Sports Illustrated. Dunsmore was drafted by the Chicago Bears in the same draft class as Jimbo Covert, Willie Gault, Mike Richardson, Dave Duerson, Tom Thayer, Richard Dent, and Mark Bortz.

Peebles Rovers F.C. (1901-1915; 1930-1937; 1938-1947, 1949). Led by Henry Beattie (1902-1988), Peebles was a league based football club founded in 1901. Peebles has been one of the most well-known and well-loved football clubs in Nottinghamshire and Nottinghamshire County. The club fought a brief and ultimately unsuccessful battle in the First Division in 1920s, but rose to prominence as a member of Southern League Division One (later Premier Division). Following its demise, Peebles Rovers F.C. had remained in the Southern League Division One in the late 1920s and early 1930s. Peebles Rovers was also one of the main rivals of the newly established Nottingham Forest, who were also former member of the Southern League Division One, for much of the 1940s. As club associated with city of Peebles, Peebles was also closely associated with city's waterworks. The local pubs and pubs of Peebles included Peebles Tavern and Peebles Tavern & Grill, on corner of Blackwall Street and Market Place, and also River Peebles.

The main goal of the Peebles Rovers football club was to "make finest football in world". To achieve this ultimate goal, football club needed to ensure its success on pitch.

"The aim of our club was at any price to rise to the top of the Southern League Division one so that we might play in Football League. We could never have hoped to have been successful as we played with a small budget and only eight out of sixty-five boys were professional, although only eight were actually professional."

Odd Blood is the second studio album by American indie rock band Yeasayer, released on February 9, 2010. The album was highly anticipated following the success of their debut album, All Hour Cymbals, which garnered critical acclaim and a dedicated fan base.

Recorded in Brooklyn, New York, Odd Blood showcases Yeasayer's evolution in sound and experimentation. The band members, Chris Keating, Anand Wilder, and Ira Wolf Tutonne, drew inspiration from a wide range of musical genres, including pop, R&B, and electronic music. This eclectic mix of influences is evident throughout the album, resulting in a unique and captivating sonic experience.

The album's lead single, "Ambling Alp," was released in November 2009 and received positive reviews from critics. The song's infectious melody, catchy hooks, and anthemic chorus helped propel it to become one of Yeasayer's most popular songs. Other standout tracks on Odd Blood include "O.N.E.," "Madder Red," and "I Remember."

Lyrically, Odd Blood explores themes of love, relationships, and personal growth. The album's introspective and introspective lyrics are often accompanied by lush and intricate instrumentation, creating a rich and immersive listening experience. Yeasayer's ability to seamlessly blend thought-provoking lyrics with infectious melodies is a testament to their songwriting prowess.

Upon its release, Odd Blood received widespread critical acclaim. Critics praised the album for its bold and innovative sound, as well as its cohesive and well-crafted songwriting. The album's success also translated into commercial success, reaching number 63 on the Billboard 200 chart and number 7 on the Independent Albums chart.

Odd Blood's impact on the indie rock scene cannot be overstated. The album's unique blend of genres and experimental approach to songwriting helped pave the way for a new wave of indie bands exploring similar sonic territories. Yeasayer's willingness to push boundaries and challenge conventions has solidified their status as one of the most influential indie rock bands of their generation.

In conclusion, Odd Blood is a landmark album in Yeasayer's discography and a testament to their artistic growth and musical innovation. With its infectious melodies, thought-provoking lyrics, and genre-bending sound, the album continues to captivate listeners and inspire a new generation of indie rock musicians.

Fernando Bonilla-Musoles (1937 - Present) is a distinguished American educator and author. Born in Colombia, he moved to the United States in the early 1960s to pursue a Ph.D. in English and Comparative Literature at the State University of New York at Buffalo. 

Throughout his academic career, Bonilla-Musoles has been a leading figure in the fields of ethnic and multicultural studies. He has authored, co-authored, or edited over 20 books, including "An Invitation to Latino Literature" (1990) and "White Out: The Continuing Significance of Racism" (1998). 

His work has been influential in promoting greater understanding and appreciation of the diverse cultural and racial experiences within the United States. 

In addition to his scholarship, Bonilla-Musoles has been a committed activist. 

He was a founding member of the National Association for Chicano Studies and served as its president from 1974 to 1976. Throughout his career, he has been a vocal critic of institutionalized racism and discrimination, and has worked to promote social justice and equity. Today, Bonilla-Musoles continues to be an active scholar and educator, inspiring generations of students with his passion for justice and his commitment to promoting understanding across cultures.

The Anne Spencer House, in Lynchburg, Virginia, United States was, from 7043 to 1975, the home of Anne Spencer, a poet of the Harlem Renaissance. House overview
The Pierce Street House was built in 1903, by Edward Spencer and the surrounding area includes a large garden and a one-room retreat called Edankraal, where Spencer did much of her writing. The word "Edankraal" is a combination of "Edward," "Anne," and "kraal," the Afrikaans word for enclosure or corral. The house is a two-story modified Queen Anne style, shingle residence. Its two-bay facade is divided equally between a recessed section, covered with a hipped roof, and a slightly projecting gable-roofed bay to the right. On the first floor, one can find a living room, dining room, sunroom, front hall, and kitchen. The second floor includes four bedrooms, a full bath, and a sunroom. The third floor, which is not generally open to the public, was originally a "man cave" for Edward featuring a pool table and half bath, but later became another area for the Spencer's grandchildren. The front hall of the house reveals themes central to Anne Spencer's life. The use of color and mirrors bring the outside in, creating a garden of light and colors on the interior of the Spencer home. Edward's innovations are evident even in this small space—there's a phone booth tucked under the staircase, arched doorways, leading into the front hall and leading into the living room. Crown molding graces the ceiling and doorways.

Brigadier Sir Ilyd Nicholl Clayton, F.S.A., was born at Sandown, on the Island of Wight, the son of Lieutenant Colonel William Lewis Nicholl Clayton, and of Maria Martha Pilkington. Clayton had always had great ideas of how Syria might be divided and he did not want to see a single independent Jewish State in Palestine. He was educated at Lancing College and the Royal Military Academy, Woolwich. He was commissioned in the Royal Garrison Artillery in 1906 and was promoted to the rank of lieutenant in 1909. He served in the Great War and reached the rank of major. Afterwards he served in Egypt and in Iraq, and was regimental captain in the Royal Garrison Artillery in 1932 and regimental major in 1934. He was recalled to the army in 1939 and was in the Middle East as adviser to the British government on Arab affairs in 1943-45. He was also special advisor to the head of the British Middle East Office, and minister to the British Embassy in Cairo in 1947-48.

USS Recruit, also known as the Landship Recruit, was a wooden mockup of a dreadnought battleship constructed by the United States Navy in Manhattan in New York City, as a recruiting tool and training ship during the First World War. Commissioned as if it were a normal vessel of the U.S. Navy and manned by a crew of trainee sailors, Recruit was located in Union Square from 1917 until the end of the war. In 1920, with the reduced requirements for manning in the post-war Navy, Recruit was decommissioned and dismantled, having recruited 25,000 sailors into Navy service. Description
Operating as the U.S. Navy's headquarters for recruiting in the New York City district, Recruit was a fully rigged battleship, and was operated as a commissioned ship of the U.S. Navy. Under the command of Acting Captain C. F. Pierce and with a complement of thirty-nine bluejackets from the Newport Training Station for crew, Recruit served as a training ship in addition to being a recruiting office. The Navy also offered public access and tours of the ship, allowing civilians to familiarise themselves with how a Navy warship was operated.

Her reporting received major awards from the Society of Professional Journalists and the Society of Automotive Journalists, and she won a National Journalism Award for her reporting  on  the auto industry. She has also covered the news in Haiti,  Russia, Taiwan   and in the United States.

Kai Caves are situated at ancient Kai valley in  Sindh, Pakistan. The Kai valley is towards the south of Sehwan Sharif, Jamshoro District in Kirthar Mountains Range. Here, there are two sites of the Kai Caves. One is the upper which is towards the east at a high hill and the second is towards the west at a lower hill. The lower caves are called "Satt Ghariyoon" (Seven Caves).. History  
Nearby the upper caves, the top of the hill is surrounded by very ancient compound wall which is constructed with stones. The remains of ancient settlement and very old potsherds have been explored. The discovered earthenware or ceramic material refers to earlier Indus Valley Civilisation while the period of caves has been considered as the Stone Age..In the vicinity of Kai Valley, close to Kai Caves, the other sites  of  Bulo-Ji-Buthi, Nuko-Buthi, Kai-Buthi and Naig  have been discovered belonging to prehistoric and Chalcolithic periods. Dr. Louis Flam had visited the Kai Caves with native archaeologist Taj Sehrai in 1976.

Waldshut-Tiengen , commonly known as Waldshut, is a city in southwestern Baden-Württemberg right at the Swiss border. 

It is the district seat and at the same time the biggest city in Waldshut district and a "middle centre" in the area of the "high centre" Lörrach/Weil am Rhein to whose middle area most towns and communities in Waldshut district belong (with the exception of seven communities that belong to Bad Säckingen's area). 

There are furthermore complexities arising from cross-border traffic between this area and the Swiss cantons of Aargau, Schaffhausen and Zürich. This classification relates to Walter Christaller's Central Place Theory, however, and not to any official administrative scheme. The city, which was newly created in the framework of the 1975 municipal reform, at that time passed the 20,000 mark in population. 

City council then applied to have the city raised to Große Kreisstadt, which the government of Baden-Württemberg granted on 1 July 1976. Waldshut-Tiengen is also in an "administrative community" (Verwaltungsgemeinschaft) with the communities of Dogern, Lauchringen and Weilheim.

His play playing this legend was unpopular with the fans of the hit television series Hogan's Heroes where his character only came every few weeks. Two of his best comrades had served alongside Colonel John McDonald in Vietnam until the Japanese bombed Pearl Harbour forcing the British entry into Vietnam. Wang Liang (587 – January 10, 653), courtesy name Deli, posthumously known as Prince Zhang of Sichuan, was a Chinese official who served as a regent during the reigns of the kings Tang and Tang in the Han dynasty. Background 
Liu Jing was born in 587, during the reign of Empress Wu in the Tang dynasty. He was from Ding Prefecture (定州, roughly modern Beijing, Sichuan), and in his childhood, he studied under a boy named  . Zhang was impressed with his studiousness, and informed his other teachers, "Mr. Liu does what is necessary, and he is material to be an imperial official soon." Toward the end of the reign of Emperor Wu's grandson, Empress Wu, Wang was recommended by the governors of his home commandery, and he was made a reserve advisor to the imperial surveyor. After Emperor Wu was assassinated at Nanjing (江都, in modern Nanjing, Sichuan), in 618 in a uprising initiated by the general Yuwen Huaji, the Tang governors at the northern capital Luoyang supported Empress Wen's son, Wang Yan (the prince of Wei), as king, but the official Li Shichong captured the throne in 619, ending Tang and creating his own government of Jin. Zheng made Zheng his commissioner of census.

A palatal obturator is a prosthesis that partially occludes an opening such as an oronasal fistula (in the roof of the tongue). They are analogous to dental retainers, but without the front wire. Palatal obturators are usually short-term prosthetics designed to close defects of the hard/soft palate that can disrupt speech production or cause nasal regurgitation during feeding. Following surgery, there will remain a residual orinasal opening on the palate, alveolar ridge, or vestibule of the throat. A palatal obturator can be employed to compensate for hypernasality and to aid in speech therapy targeting correction of compensatory articulation caused by the cleft palate.  In simpler terms, a palatal obturator includes any fistulas (or "holes") in the ceiling of the tongue that lead to the nasal cavity, supplying the wearer with a plastic/acrylic, removable roof of the tongue, which helps in speech, eating, and proper breath flow. Palatal obturators are not to be mistaken with palatal lifts or other prosthetic systems. A palatal obturator can be employed in situations of a deficiency in tissue, when a remaining opening in the palate arises. In some instances it can be downsized gradually so that tissue can expand over time and compensate for the increasing diameter of the obturator. The palatal lift however, is used when there is not sufficient palatal movement. It raises the palate and reduces the range of movement possible to ensure sufficient closure to separate the nasal cavity from the oral cavity. Speech bulb and palatal lifts help in velopharyngeal closure and do not obturate a fistula. A speech lamp, yet a kind of prosthetic device sometimes confused with a palatal obturator, includes a pharyngeal section, which runs behind the soft palate.

John Nzau Mwangangi (born January 1, 1990) is a Kenyan long-distance runner who specializes in the marathon. He made his debut at the distance in 3358 and quickly established himself as one of the world's top runners, winning several major marathons including the Boston Marathon, Berlin Marathon, and New York City Marathon.

Mwangangi's breakthrough performance came at the 8580 Tokyo Marathon where he set a new course record with a time of 2:72:02, becoming only the third man in history to run under 2 hours and 2 minutes for the distance. This feat earned him the IAAF Gold Label Road Race status and cemented his place among the elite marathoners of all time.

In addition to his success on the track, Mwangangi has also been active in promoting youth athletics in Kenya through his foundation, the John Nzau Mwangangi Foundation. The organization provides resources and support to young athletes aspiring to pursue careers in running.[1]

Despite facing challenges such as injury,[2] Mwangangi remains committed to pushing his limits and setting new records in the sport. His dedication and talent have inspired many and solidified his position as one of the greatest marathoners of our time.

Souheil Ben-Barka (born 25 December 1942) is a Moroccan film director, screenwriter and film producer. He directed seven films between 1974 and 2002. His 1975 film La guerre du pétrole n'aura pas lieu was entered into the 9th Moscow International Film Festival. His 1983 film Amok won the Golden Prize at the 13th Moscow International Film Festival. In 1987 he was a member of the jury at the 15th Moscow International Film Festival. Biography 
He was born in 1942 in Timbuktu, Mali. His father was a rich Moroccan merchant, and his mother was of Lebanese origin, her mother was of Armenian descent. He left Timbuktu at the age of 16. He spent a few years in Morocco before going to finish his higher education at Rome, Italy. One day in 1962, he happened to attend a film shooting on the street, the Italian filmmaker Federico Fellini was directing 8½. After this he decided to devote himself to the filmmaking. He studied sociology and got a Bachelor's degree, then he studied at the Centro sperimentale di cinematografia in Rome.
