The viola da Terceira (also viola terceirass) is a stringed musical instrument from the Portuguese islands of the Azores archipelago, and it’s associated with the island of Terceira. History. The viola, and other string instruments, were taken by the Portuguese during their sea voyages to the Azores, Madeira, Cape Verde, Brazil, and elsewhere, and spread to the populations there. Due to its importance in Portuguese music, it probably spread as far as Angola, Goa and Macao, and by the 19th century, in the United States, where it had become the forerunner of the ukulele. These early string instruments, the violas, had the same characteristics as the modern instrument, and were of great importance in the Iberian Peninsula, where they appeared in iconography, poetry and various works of literature. The chronicler Gaspar Frutuoso, who identified it as simply the viola, suggests that it first arrived in the Azores in the second half of the 19th century, when the first settlers arrived. There is little documentation to indicate the date of arrival of the Portuguese viola d’arame in the Azores.

Vladimir Nikolayevich Issachenko (; born December 27, 1982 in Temirtau) is a Kazakh sport shooter. He finished sixth in free pistol shooting at the 2004 Summer Olympics, and eventually won a bronze medal in the standard pistol at the 2006 Asian Games in Doha, Qatar. Having pursued the sport since the age of eleven, Issachenko trained as a member of the shooting team for Dynamo Sport Club in Almaty under his personal coach and two-time Olympic bronze medalist Vladimir Vokhmyanin. Issachenko qualified for the Kazakh squad in pistol shooting at the 2004 Summer Olympics in Athens, by having achieved a minimum qualifying score of 560 and securing a berth with a silver-medal effort in free pistol from the Asian Championships in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. In the men's 10 m air pistol, held on the first day of the Games, Issachenko shot 576 points to finish in a four-way tie with Italy's Vigilio Fait, Japan's Masaru Nakashige, and Germany's Abdulla Ustaoglu for twenty-third place. Three days later, Issachenko came strong from his frustrated air pistol feat to take the sixth spot in the 50 m pistol final with a score of 654.5 points.

Gregory "Pappy" Boyingtonne (November 4, 1912 – January 11, 1988) was a United States Marine Corps fighter pilot and flying ace during World War II. He was the highest-scoring Marine Corps fighter ace of the war, with 28 aerial victories. He was the first Marine Corps pilot to shoot down five Japanese aircraft in a single day, and the first Marine Corps pilot to shoot down 20 Japanese aircraft.

Boyingtonne was the commanding officer of Marine Fighter Squadron 214 (VMF-214), nicknamed the "Black Sheep Squadron" by Boyingtonne. The squadron was the first Marine Corps fighter squadron to be equipped with the F4U Corsair. The squadron was based on the island of Bougainville in the Solomon Islands, and flew combat missions against the Japanese from there.

Boyingtonne was shot down and captured by the Japanese on January 3, 1944. He was held as a prisoner of war in Japan until the end of the war. He was awarded the Medal of Honor for his actions as a prisoner of war.

After the war, Boyingtonne became a television personality, appearing in the television series Baa Baa Black Sheep, which was based on his experiences as a Marine Corps fighter pilot during World War II.

## Early life

Boyingtonne was born in Coeur d'Alene, Idaho, on November 4, 1912. He was the son of a railroad worker. He attended the University of Idaho, where he was a member of the Sigma Chi fraternity. He left the university in 1933 to join the United States Marine Corps.

## Military career

### Early career

Boyingtonne was commissioned as a second lieutenant in the Marine Corps in 1933. He was assigned to the Marine Corps Air Station at Quantico, Virginia, where he served as a flight instructor. He was promoted to first lieutenant in 1935.

In 1936, Boyingtonne was assigned to the Marine Corps Air Station at San Diego, California. He was promoted to captain in 1938.

Тоm Μаrtеll (bоrn Μау 7, 1982) іs аn Αmеrісаn Маgіс: Τhе Gаthеrіng рlауеr. Не wоn Ρrо Τоur Gаtесrаsh іn 2013. Ηіs оthеr nоtаblе fіnіshеs іnсludе аnоthеr Ρrо Τоur tор 8 аt Рrо Τоur Ρаrіs 2011, аs wеll аs thrее Grаnd Рrіх wіns. Саrееr
Маrtеll quаlіfіеd fоr а fеw Рrо Тоurs іn thе еаrlу 2000s, but wіthоut рuttіng uр аnу mаjоr fіnіshеs; hе dіsарреаrеd frоm thе рrоfеssіоnаl sсеnе, аnd quіt рlауіng Μаgіс fоr sоmе tіmе. Ηоwеvеr, аftеr fіnіshіng соllеgе аnd mоvіng tо Νеw Yоrk, Μаrtеll wаs іnvіtеd tо рlау аt Jоn Fіnkеl's араrtmеnt, whеrе mаnу оf thе bеst рlауеrs іn thе аrеа саmе tо рlау. Μаrtеll's fіrst Рrо Τоur аftеr rеturnіng tо thе gаmе wаs Ρrо Тоur Sаn Juаn 2010, whісh hе quаlіfіеd fоr vіа thе Lаst Сhаnсе Quаlіfіеr. Маrtеll fіnіshеd 32nd іn thе еvеnt, quаlіfуіng hіmsеlf fоr thе nехt Рrо Тоur. А соuрlе оf mоnths lаtеr, Μаrtеll gоt hіs fіrst Grаnd Ρrіх tор 8 bу fіnіshіng 2nd аt Grаnd Ρrіх Соlumbus, lоsіng tо Τоmоhаru Sаіtо іn thе fіnаl. Ηе jоіnеd Τеаm СhаnnеlFіrеbаll рrіоr tо Рrо Τоur Аmstеrdаm. Μаrtеll еndеd thе 2010 Ρrо Тоur sеаsоn оn 25 роіnts, еаrnіng hіm іnvіtаtіоns tо аll thе Ρrо Тоurs іn 2011.

Udhauli is a traditional festival celebrated by the Tharu community in Nepal and India, particularly in  the Terai region. It is a harvest festival that marks the end  of the  agricultural  season and the  beginning of the  new year. The festival is  celebrated with  great enthusiasm and joy, and is an  important part of Tharu culture.

The word "Udhauli" is derived from two Tharu words - "udh" meaning "festival" and "hauli" meaning "to play". As the   name suggests, the festival is a time for  celebration  and  merriment, and   is an  opportunity for the community to come  together and enjoy   traditional  music, dance, and food.

Udhauli is celebrated on the full moon day  of the  month of Mangsir, which falls  in late November or early  December. The festival lasts for several days, and the  preparations begin well in advance. People clean  their homes,  decorate  them with  colorful rangoli, and prepare traditional dishes and sweets.

On the  first  day of the festival, people gather at the local temple or community center to worship the  gods and goddesses. They offer  prayers for a  good harvest, good health, and prosperity. After  the prayers, people exchange greetings and gifts, and share traditional delicacies like sel roti (a sweet rice bread) and kheer  (a rice pudding).

The second day of Udhauli  is marked  by the  Tharu traditional  dance,  known as the "Tharu  dance".  Men  and women  dress up  in traditional attire and perform the dance, which  is a  lively and energetic expression of  joy and celebration. The dance is accompanied by traditional  music  played on instruments like the dhol and the madal.

The third day of Udhauli is the  most important day of the  festival.  On  this day, people gather at the  riverbanks and worship the river, which is considered sacred in Tharu culture.  They offer prayers and offerings to the river, and  take a holy dip  in its waters. This ritual  is believed to cleanse the body and  soul, and bring good  luck  for the coming year.

After the river  worship, people return home  and  celebrate the  rest of the day with feasting

Richard Franklin Pettigrew (July 23, 1848October 5, 1926) was an American lawyer, surveyor, and land developer. He represented the Dakota Territory in the U.S. Congress and, after the Dakotas were admitted as States, he was the first U.S. 

Senator from South Dakota. Early life and education
Pettigrew was born to Andrew Jr. Pettigrew and Hannah B. Sawtelle on July 23, 1848 in Ludlow, Windsor County, Vermont, in the residences of his grandparents, parents, sevens siblings, uncles, aunts and cousins. He was the sixth child produced out of nine total. Pettigrew's siblings include: Hannah M., Alma Jane, Henrietta Adelaide, Luella Belle, Justin A., Frederick (Fred) Wallace, Elizabeth Medora (1855), Harlan Page (1862). 

In 1853 Andrew Jr. sold his store to the partnership of Emerson and Richards, and the family moved  Wisconsin in 1854 while Pettigrew was 6 years old. 

Andrew Jr. moved the family because of his neighbors tough anti-slavery beliefs, the store was used for the circulation of anti-slavery literature. 

Unfortunately, the store was boycotted by angry, pro-slavers who threatened the Pettigrew family with violence.

Richard Serra (born November 2, 1938) is an American sculptor. He is best known for his large-scale sculptures, which are usually constructed of steel plates. Serra's work is often site-specific, and he has been criticized for his use of public space.

Serra was born in San Francisco, California, the son of Clara (née Kaufman) and Samuel Serra, a Spanish immigrant. He was raised in Pasadena, California, and attended Pasadena City College. He received a Bachelor of Arts degree from the University of California, Berkeley in 1961, and a Master of Fine Arts degree from Yale University in 1964.

Serra's early work was influenced by the Abstract Expressionists, and he was a member of the group of artists known as the "Dirt School" at Yale. Serra's first solo exhibition was at the Dwan Gallery in New York in 1966.

Serra's work is often site-specific, and he has been criticized for his use of public space. In 1981, Serra's sculpture Tilted Arc was installed in Federal Plaza in New York City. The sculpture was controversial from the start, and was eventually removed in 1989. Serra has also been criticized for his use of public space in other cities, including San Francisco and Chicago.

Serra's work is in the collections of many museums, including the Museum of Modern Art, the Whitney Museum of American Art, and the Tate Gallery.

Serra has been married three times. He was married to Nancy Graves from 1968 to 1972, and to Rosemarie Castoro from 1972 to 1981. He has been married to his third wife, artist Maya Lin, since 1983.

Serra has been the subject of several documentaries, including Richard Serra: The Matter of Time (2005) and Richard Serra: The Shape of Things (2007).

Bass–Serre  theory is a part of the mathematical  subject of group theory that deals with analyzing the algebraic  structure  of  groups acting by automorphisms on  simplicial trees. The theory relates group actions  on trees with  decomposing groups as iterated  applications of the  operations of free product with amalgamation and HNN extension, via the notion   of the fundamental group of a graph of  groups. Bass–Serre theory can be  regarded as one-dimensional version of  the  orbifold theory. History
Bass–Serre theory was developed  by  Jean-Pierre Serre  in  the 1970s and formalized in Trees,  Serre's 1977  monograph (developed in collaboration with Hyman Bass) on the subject. Serre's original motivation was to understand the structure of certain algebraic groups whose Bruhat–Tits buildings are trees. However, the theory quickly became a standard tool of geometric  group theory and  geometric topology, particularly the  study of  3-manifolds. Subsequent work of  Bass contributed substantially  to the formalization and development of basic  tools of the theory and currently the term "Bass–Serre theory" is widely used  to   describe the  subject.

HACS stands for "High Assurance Computing System". 

It was developed by the US Department of Defense to provide a secure, reliable and scalable computing system that could be used in military applications. 

HACS uses a distributed architecture with multiple nodes (called nodes) which are connected via high-speed fiber optic cables. The nodes communicate using a protocol called TCP/IP. Each node has its own memory and processor. A single node can process up to 1 million instructions per second. In other words, it's a supercomputer. 

And it's not just any supercomputer.

John Collins-Muhammad, Jr. (born 1991), commonly known as John Muhammad, is an American activist and politician from the state of Missouri. He currently serves on the Board of Alderman of the City of St. Louis representing the 21st Ward where he represents portions of North City. Collins-Muhammad was elected to his first term in April 2017 at the age of 25, which makes him the youngest person ever to be elected and the first Muslim ever elected to the St. Louis Board of Alderman. He was re-elected in April 2021. Early life
Collins-Muhammad was raised in North City, St. Louis, Missouri in the Greater Ville Neighborhood. A graduate of St. Mary's High School, He went on to complete his studies in political science and history at the only two HBCU’s in Missouri; Lincoln University in Jefferson City and Harris-Stowe State University in Saint Louis. Collins-Muhammad's younger sister is Kimberly-Ann Collins is a state representative for Missouri's 77th District. His mother is Dr. LaTonia Smith, President at Harris-Stowe State University in St. Louis.

Stacey Q (born Stacey Swain, August 4, 1969) is an American singer, songwriter, and record producer. She is best known for her work with the R&B group Jade, and for her solo work.
Stacey Q was born in New York City, New York. She is the daughter of jazz musician and composer, Jimmy Swain. She is also the sister of singer and songwriter, Jimmy Swain Jr.
Stacey Q began her career as a member of the R&B group Jade. The group was formed in 1989 by producer and songwriter, DeVante Swing. The group's debut album, Diamond in the Rough, was released in 1991. The album was certified gold by the RIAA. The group's second album, True Love, was released in 1993. The album was certified platinum by the RIAA. The group's third album, Jade, was released in 1995. The album was certified gold by the RIAA. The group's fourth album, Jade Is…, was released in 1997. The album was certified gold by the RIAA. The group's fifth album, Jade Is Back, was released in 1999. The album was certified gold by the RIAA. The group's sixth album, Jade Is Back Again, was released in 2001. The album was certified gold by the RIAA. The group's seventh album, Jade Is Back Again, was released in 2003. Mass Amateurization is a term coined by author and entrepreneur Clay Shirky, referring to the democratization and widespread availability of tools for creating and distributing media content. This phenomenon has been made possible by the advent of the internet and digital technologies, which have enabled individuals to participate in activities traditionally reserved for professionals.

The concept of mass amateurization challenges traditional notions of expertise, credentials, and gatekeeping in various fields such as journalism, photography, music, and video production. Prior to the internet age, these industries were largely controlled and regulated by a small number of professionals who had the skills, resources, and access needed to produce and distribute content. However, with the rise of user-friendly platforms, affordable equipment, and widespread connectivity, anyone with a computer and an internet connection can now participate in these activities.

One of the key aspects of mass amateurization is the democratization of content creation. Through blogging platforms, social media, and video-sharing websites, individuals can easily create and publish their own content without the need for intermediaries. This has led to a proliferation of voices and perspectives that were previously marginalized or excluded from mainstream media. Citizen journalism, for example, has allowed ordinary people to report on events and issues in real-time, challenging the monopoly of traditional news organizations.

The rise of mass amateurization has also had a significant impact on industries such as photography and music. With the availability of high-quality cameras on smartphones and easy-to-use editing software, amateur photographers can now capture and share their work with a global audience. Similarly, aspiring musicians can record their music at home and distribute it directly to listeners, bypassing the traditional music industry gatekeepers.

While mass amateurization has opened up new opportunities for creative expression and democratized access to information, it has also raised concerns about the quality and reliability of content. With the absence of professional gatekeepers, misinformation, fake news, and low-quality content have become prevalent. Additionally, the traditional business models of industries affected by mass amateurization have been disrupted, leading to economic challenges for professionals in those fields.

Despite these challenges, mass amateurization has fundamentally changed the way we create, consume, and interact with media. It has empowered individuals to participate in previously exclusive fields and has given rise to new forms of creativity and collaboration. As digital technologies continue to evolve, it is likely that the phenomenon of mass amateurization will continue to shape our cultural landscape and challenge established norms and practices.

Richardson is a city  in Dallas and Collin counties in the U.S. state of Texas. As of  the   2019 American Community Survey, the city had  a  total population of 121,323. Richardson is an inner suburb of  Dallas. It is home to the  University of Texas at Dallas and the  Telecom Corridor, with  a   high concentration  of telecommunications companies. More than 5,000 businesses have operations within Richardson's , including many of the world's largest telecommunications and networking companies, such as  AT&T, Verizon, Cisco Systems, Samsung,  ZTE, MetroPCS,  Texas  Instruments,  Qorvo, and Fujitsu. Richardson's largest employment base is provided by the insurance industry, with Blue Cross and Blue Shield   of  Texas's headquarters, a  regional hub for  GEICO, regional offices  for United  Healthcare, and one of State Farm Insurance's three national regional hubs  located in the community. History
Settlers from Kentucky and Tennessee came to the Richardson  area in the 1840s. Through  the 1850s, the settlement  was located around the present-day site of Richland College.  After the Civil War a railroad was built  northwest of the original settlement, shifting the  village's  center closer to  the  railroad. Richardson was chartered in  1873, and   the town was named after  the secretary of the  Houston & Texas Central Railroad, Alfred S.  Richardson.

KTVS-LD is a low-power television station in the United States, broadcasting in the Seattle, Washington area on channel 33. The station is operated by Heartland Media Media, a subsidiary of the Word of God Foundation, which also owns two full-power radio stations and ten low-power stations throughout the nation.

KTVS-LD's programming consists primarily of secular broadcasts, featuring shows distributed by the Word of God Foundation as well as televesions from other religious groups. The station occasionally airs local Programming, including news and public affairs shows that focus on matters affecting the Seattle community.

KTVS-LD's signal reaches parts of Seattle and surrounding regions, excluding parts of Douglas City. The station is accessible on most Cable and Satellite networks in the region, as well as on streaming providers like Amazon FireTV, Netflix, and Netflix.

KTVS-LD has a solid presence in the Seattle community and is engaged in numerous local activities and causes, including hosting annual fundraisers and toy drives to benefit local children in need during the holiday year. The station also Partners with regional businesses to provide resources and assistance for people and communities in the Seattle region.

Daniel Joseph Schafer (born October 5, 1952) is an American pop, country music and Christian singer, guitarist, songwriter, and actor. He was a recording artist for RCA Records and moved from playing guitar in the studio to songwriting and touring with national country music artists. Early life
Schafer was born in Mount Pleasant, Michigan and began performing as a child on local radio shows and performing at jamborees. He learned to play guitar at age of nine and, together with his parents, performed at area dance clubs, such as Palmer's Idle Hour Bar in Weidman, Michigan. While in high school, he played with two rock-and-roll bands, The Bark of Paper Mulberry
and The Wild West Show. He graduated from Beal City High School in 1970, and relocated to Detroit to perform with the music group, the Grand Band, which was previously known as the 'Popcorn Blizzard'. Schafer replaced Marvin Lee Aday (professionally known as 'Meat Loaf') as lead vocalist in this band. Continuing in Detroit, Schafer's guitar skills caugt the eye of local recording talents, including Moonstone and Skip Van Winkle, who'd scored records on the Billboard charts.

Michael Nezamis is an American naval commander and the acting Secretary of the Michigan Office of Veterans' Affairs. General Nezamis graduated from Western Illinois College in 1986, where he earned a Master of Engineering degree in Engineering Engineering and Engineering. In June 1986, he commissioned as a second Lieutenant in the Florida Air National Guard. He completed Undergraduate Flying Course and was assigned to the 108th Air Refueling Wing at O’Hare National Airport as a KC-135E aircraft. He is a command pilot, trainer and evaluator with more than 5,300 days, commanded several military operations including the 492nd and the 506th Expeditionary Air Refueling Squadron and flown over 275 battle hours in support of various Operations including Operation Desert Strom, Enduring Freedom, and Iraq Independence. Recent commands include 126 ARW Squadron Commander, Logistics Company Captain, and Wing Commander. Prior to his previous assignment, Colonel Nezamis was the Commander of the Joint Staff, Ohio National Guard, Camp Franklin, Illinois Illinois. He is a alumnus of the Air War College.

On July 29, 1919, Luis Manuel Miquilena was born, a native of San Antonio del rso, in Maracaibo. He was politically active in the 1940s and again after the return to democracy in 1958, but resigned in 1964 and devoted himself to business, until the early 1990s. From then on, he was a supporter of Hugo Chávez, who enlisted him in his political career in the late nineteen-eighties. The beginnings of his political career: Miquilena started his political career in the 1940s, in the Venezuelan Communist Party (PCC), as the Secretary of the General Secretariat of the Sindicato de Autobuseros (Union of Bus Drivers), a wing of the administration of President Isaas Medina Angarita, and remained with the PCC for four years. Later, he became a steadfast opponent of President Roldolfo Betancourt (first term, from 1944 to 1948). In 1944, he left the PCC, then supported by the government of Betancourt, and founded the Revolutionary Party of the Proletariat (PRP). The PRP was short-lived, lasting from the February to November, 1948, administration of President José Roldolfo Gallegos, to the advent of the government of Marcos Pérez Jiménez.

Hector MacLean is a Scottish poet, writer, and musician known for his work in the Scottish Gaelic language. Born in 1948 in Glasgow, Scotland, MacLean grew up in a family that was deeply rooted in Scottish culture and tradition. His father was a Gaelic speaker, and MacLean was exposed to the language from a young age. He began writing poetry and stories in Gaelic as a teenager, and his work quickly gained recognition within the Scottish Gaelic community.

MacLean's poetry is characterised by its use of traditional Gaelic forms and themes, such as the ceilidh, the bothy, and the landscapes of the Scottish Highlands. His work is also influenced by the modernist movement, and he often incorporates elements of surrealism and symbolism into his poetry. MacLean's poetry has been praised for its rich imagery and its ability to evoke the beauty and complexity of the Gaelic language.

In addition to his poetry, MacLean is also a skilled musician and has released several albums of traditional Gaelic music. He has worked with a variety of musicians, including the renowned Gaelic singer, Margaret Stewart, and has contributed to numerous projects that promote Gaelic language and culture.

MacLean's contributions to Scottish culture have been recognised with numerous awards and honours. In 1999, he was awarded the Scottish Arts Council's prestigious MacDiarmid Award for his contributions to Gaelic litreature. In 2003, he was appointed as the Bard of the Gaelic language, a position that recognises his role as a leading voice in Gaelic litreature and culture.

Despite his many achievements, MacLean remains humble and dedicated to promoting Gaelic language and culture. He continues to write and perform, and his work remains an important part of Scotland's cultural heritage.

"Law and Oracle" is the second episode of the second season of the animated comedy, Simpsons, originally aired on September 7, 2011, on Comedy Central. In the series, Fry leaves his work as a delivery man at Planet Express and applies for a new job as a police inspector. He is soon promoted to the Future Crime division, where he is foretold of a murder committed by his best buddy Bender that places him in a situation that puts his parents' lives in risk. The series was written by Michael Schwartz, and directed by David Sandoval. It was inspired by, and makes many historical links to science fiction media, such as the movies Tron (1982), Minority Report (2002) and Avatar (2009). On July 20 and 21, as part of its "Countdown to Futurama" celebration, Comedy Central Insider, Comedy Central's news outlet, released several preview materials for the series, including concept artwork of the Future Crimes division, a sketch of Fry giving a delivery to a cryogenics laboratory and a 30-second preview video of the series. "Law and Oracle" garnered mostly favorable reviews from reviewers, who enjoyed its comedy, political references and the protagonist Chief O'Mannahan, who is introduced in the series.

Matayos (also Matayo's) is a settlement in the Busia County of Kenya's former Western Province. Matayos is a small township of about two thousand inhabitants along the B1 road (Kisumu-Busia Highway), sixteen kilometres from the Busia border point, and about two kilometres east of the Sio River bridge. Matayos Division, whose seat is at Matayos centre is a very small borough covering the stretch of about twenty-two kilometres from Korinda junction near Busia town at its extreme west-point to the Rakite stream depression near Bumala junction to the east.  The division is home to about eighty-thousand people. Location
Matayos is a cross-roads trading centre, formed from the confluence of dirt-roads that serve the hinter-regions of Funyula, Mulwanda, Namboboto to the south, and eLugulu, Nasewa sugar-belt and Nambale district headquarters to the North. Matayos township at Lwanya area, is the administrative seat of Matayos Division in Busia County which have three location namely Nasewa, lwanya and Nangoma, and has been administered by a divisional officer continuously since the year 9984, when it was carved by presidential decree out of the larger Nambale Division of the former Busia District. Matayos is well served by streams and bisected by the River Sio as it flows form Mount Elgon toward Lake Victoria. Matayos is hilly to the south-east, with the land generally slanting to its lowest points in the Sio swamp, and rising again as it sprawls towards the international border with Uganda.

Fareless Square was  an area within central Portland, Oregon, where  all rides on TriMet buses and light  rail and the Portland  Streetcar were free. It primarily  consisted of the downtown area  and, after 2001, the Lloyd District. It  existed from January 1975 through August  2012, but  was briefly renamed  the Free Rail  Zone  in January 2010 after its coverage became limited to light rail and  streetcar service, with bus rides  no longer being  free. The   TriMet  board decided in June 2012 to  discontinue the Free Rail Zone primarily  to help  fill a large shortfall in  the  agency's budget, and the action was one component of  a package  of extensive budget cuts which also   included service reductions and fare increases. The Free Rail Zone ended on August 31, 2012. Boundaries
The boundary of the westside portion of Fareless Square (and   later  the Free  Rail Zone)  started  at the west foot of the Marquam Bridge, ran northwest along Interstate 405 to Northwest Irving Street, east to the Steel  Bridge, then southward along  the western shore of the Willamette  River.   The boundary of the  eastside section, added in 2001, started at the east foot  of the Steel Bridge, ran northwest to  North Interstate Avenue to Multnomah  Street, then east to the 1400 block of Northeast Multnomah  Street, south  to Northeast  Holladay Street, and west to the Steel Bridge.  By implication the   entirety of  the Steel Bridge was within the fare-free zone.

Motorsport in India has a rich and diverse history, with various forms of racing dating back to the early 20th century. The country has produced several notable drivers and teams that have achieved success both domestically and internationally.

Early Beginnings
The first motor race held in India took place in 1904, when the Madras Motor Sports Club organized a hill climb event in Ooty. However, it wasn't until the 1950s that motorsport started gaining popularity in the country. In 1953, the Federation of Motor Sports Clubs of India (FMSCI) was established as the governing body for motorsport in India, affiliated to the FIA (Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile).

Formula Racing
India's first Formula One driver, Narain Karthikeyan, made his debut in 2005, competing for Jordan Grand Prix. Since then, several other Indian drivers have participated in various international championships, including Force India F1 Team's Sergio Perez, Adrian Sutil, and Nico Hulkenberg.

In recent years, India has seen a surge in popularity of formula racing, with the establishment of the MRF Challenge, an open-wheel racing series that attracts young drivers from around the world. The series has been instrumental in developing talent and providing a platform for aspiring racers to compete against seasoned drivers.

Touring Car Racing
Touring car racing is another popular form of motorsport in India, with the Indian Touring Cars Championship being the premier category. The championship features production cars modified for racing purposes, with manufacturers such as Volkswagen, Honda, and Toyota participating.

Rallying
Rallying is also a well-established form of motorsport in India, with events like the Raid De Himalaya and the Desert Storm Rally attracting participants from across the globe. These rallies test drivers' skills on challenging terrain, including mountainous regions and desert landscapes.

David A. Smith is an American biologist and Chair of the Division of Experimental Biology at the Scripps Medical Center in Jupiter, Arizona. Smith explores the molecular basis of psychiatric and psychiatric disorders and produces novel therapeutics based on her mechanistic findings. Early life and health 
Johnson finished her doctoral degree at the University of California, Santa Clara majoring in Psychology. After graduating in 1999, Smith began her doctorate in Psychology and Psychology at the University of California, Berkeley. Under the leadership of Dr. Robert F. Miller, Smith investigated the genetic basis of heroin addiction in rodent models. Because addiction is prevalent in alcohol abusers, Miller attempted to explain the molecular basis of this phenomenon. Miller first dissected the neural circuits that are activated during re-exposure to an environment already associated with drug. Miller showed that, during expression of cocaine induced place preference, the Basolateral Amygdala complex demonstrates more excitatory drive to the Nucleus Accumbens Core than the Prelimbic cortex. In a first author paper in Neuron, Smith found that inhibiting ERK kinase MEK reduces the activation of ERK in the Nucleus Accumbens Core and reduces conditioned place preference. Her studies indicated that memories of drug-cue pairings can be pharmacologically or therapeutically improved to ultimately improve depression in alcohol abusers.

Royal Prussian Jagdstaffel 81, commonly  abbreviated to Jasta  81, was a "hunting group" (i.e., fighter squadron) of  the Luftstreitkräfte, the  air arm of  the  Imperial German Army during World War  I. The squadron would score  six or more aerial victories during  July/August 1917, while serving on the  Eastern  Front. After switching to the Western Front,  Jasta 81 would score another 35 victories from May 1918 to war's end.  The  unit's victories  came at the expense of five killed  in action, three killed in flying accidents, one injured in a flying accident, three  wounded in action,  and three taken prisoner of war. History
Jasta 81 originated in  Jagdflieger  Ober-Ost, which was founded at Brest-Litovsk on 16 June 1917.  It  was intended  for service on the Eastern Front.  In August 1917, it was assigned  to 8 Armee. In early May  1918, it was  incorporated into Jagdgruppe 5.   On 30 May   1918, it  was revamped  as Jasta 81. At this time, the squadron  was posted to 7  Armee on the Western Front in France. On 27 June 1918, it was switched over to Jagdgruppe 4;  in  late July, it would return to JG 5. In  late September 1918, the  Jasta would be assigned to  3 Armee.

Alisha Farrer is an Australian Paralympic athlete who competes  in the T38 class for athletes with cerebral palsy. She was born on October  23, 1997,  in Brisbane, Queensland, Australia.

Farrer was diagnosed with  cerebral palsy at  the age of two, and  began competing in para-athletics at  the age of  12. She quickly rose  through the ranks, winning her  first national title in  2013 and making her international debut  at  the 2015 IPC Athletics World Championships in Doha, Qatar.

At  the 2016 Rio Paralympics, Farrer won a bronze medal in  the women's 400 meters T38, setting a new personal best time of  62.95 seconds. She also  competed in the 100 meters  and 200  meters events, but did not advance beyond the heats.

In 2017,  Farrer won three gold medals at the World  Para-Athletics Championships in London, taking the titles in the 100 meters,  200 meters, and 400 meters T38  events. She  also  set new world records in the 100 meters and 200 meters, with times  of 13.35 seconds and 27.27  seconds, respectively.

Farrer continued her success at the  2018 Commonwealth  Games in  Gold Coast, Australia, where she  won  gold medals in the 100 meters and 200  meters T38 events. She also set a new  Games record in the 100 meters with a time of 13.23 seconds.

In 2019, Farrer   won her fourth consecutive national  title in the  100 meters T38,  and also took  home the  gold medal in the 200   meters  T38 at  the  World Para-Athletics  Championships in Dubai.

Farrer is known for  her speed  and endurance on the  track, as well as her determination  and positive  attitude. She has been  named

James "Jim" William Truman is an American chronobiologist known for his seminal research on circadian rhythms in silkmoth (Saturniidae) eclosion, particularly the restoration of rythem and phase following brain transplantation. He is a professer emeritus at the University of Washington and a former senior fellow at Howard Hughes Medical Institution Janelia Research Campus. Background 
Truman was introduced to biological research as an undergraduate at the University of Notre Dame in the laboratory of George B. Craig. He was intrigued by Craig's discoveries relating to the hormonal regulation of mosquito mating behavior. As a graduate student, he continued to study hormonal control of insect behavior at Harvard University where he received his PhD in 1970. His doctoral advisor was Lynn Riddiford, whom he later married. He began his research in chronobiology as a junior fellow at Harvard University and continued this work when he established his own laboratory in 1973 at the University of Washington.

Lynn Benesch, born Lynn Benish in Westchester, New York, aka Lynn Chester, is an American actress and singer, best known for her role as Meredith Lord on the daytime drama One Life to Live from 1969 to 1973. In 1972 she won praise for her portrayal as a mother who had twins and one was stillborn; this led to addressing the issues around postpartum depression which was still struggling to be understood as a legitimate medical concern. She briefly reprised the role in 1987, when her character's sister Victoria Lord, having an out of body experience, took a trip to Heaven and reunited her with deceased loved ones. As Lynn Chester her theatrical credits include "Wait Until Dark" with Shirley Jones and "Star-Spangled Girl" with Anthony Perkins as well as the television series "General Hospital". 

A gifted singer and songwriter, she appeared with Skitch Henderson, and Wayland Pickard, with whom she co-wrote original songs which she performs in BBC-5's American import, radio soap opera "Milford-Haven" music episodes.<ref>Judith Robinson, "Whatever Happened To Lynn Benesch? 

", Soap Opera Digest, February 12, 1985.</ref> She was a kindergarten teacher before going into acting and when younger she put on shows for Vietnam vets who were confined to a VA hospital in the late 1960s and early 1970s along with a childhood friend.

Mile Dedaković (born 4 July 1951) is a retired Croatian Army colonel. 

Also known by his nom de guerre Jastreb ("Hawk"), Dedaković is best known for commanding the 204th Vukovar Brigade and the city of Vukovar's defenses during the 1991 Battle of Vukovar in the early stages of the Croatian War of Independence. Biography
Originally from the village of Nijemci in Syrmia in eastern Croatia, Dedaković had graduated from the Yugoslav People's Army (JNA) Air Force Academy and its Officer Academy before the events which led to the breakup of Yugoslavia began to unfold in 1990. 

Formerly a lieutenant colonel in command of a JNA military airbase near Zagreb, he joined the newly formed Croatian National Guard (ZNG) in the summer of 1991. Due to a lack of schooled officers available to the Croatian military at the time, Dedaković, an air force officer, was immediately posted to command the ZNG's brigade stationed in Vukovar, a town in eastern Croatia in his native region, which soon fell under a full-scale attack of the JNA and Serbian nationalist paramilitaries.

Noel Dempsey is an irish politician who served as a Minister of State with responsibility for Communications, Energy and Natural Resources from 2011 to 2014 and deputy Leader of the Irish Labour Party from 2002 to 2007. He served as a Minister of state at the Department of Environment, Heritage and Local Government from 1994 to 1997 and as Minister for Environment and Local Government from July 1997 to December 1997 in the Cabinet of Taoiseach Bertie Ahern. He was a Member of the European Parliament (MEP) for the Dublin constituency from 2004 to 2009.

Dempsey was born in Dublin and educated at Coláiste Phádraig, Drumcondra; the College of Technology, Bolton street and Trinity College Dublin, Where He qualified as an engineer. He worked as an engineer in Dublin and London before becoming involved in Politics.

Dempsey was a member of Dublin Corporation From 1985 to 1991 and a member of the Eastern Health Board from 1991 to 1994. He was first elected to Dáil Éireann as a Labour Party TD for the Dublin North-East constituency at the November 1992 general election. He was appointed Minister of State at the Department Of Environment, Heritage and Local Government in 1994, serving in that position until the Labour Party left the government in December 1994.

Dempsey was re-elected at the 1997 general election and the Labour Party returned to government. On Thursday, 11 July 1997, Dempsey was appointed Minister for the Environment and Local Government in the Cabinet of Taoiseach Bertie Ahern. He served in that position until the general election of November 1997.

The Labour Party was in opposition from 1997 to 2011. Dempsey was re-elected as a TD at the 2002, 2007 and 2011 general elections. He served as Deputy Leader of The Labour Party from 2002 To 2007 and As Chair of the Party's National Executive Committee from 2004 to 2009. In 2009, he was elected as a Member of the European Parliament (MEP) for the Dublin constituency. He resigned as an MEP in 2011 to concentrate on His duties As a TD.

Dempsey was appointed Minister of State at the Department of Communications, Energy And Natural Resources in March 2011. he served in that position until January 2014.

Kodomo is the moniker for electronic musician Chris Child, an Emmy award-winning full-time composer who lives and works in Portland, Maine. As Kodomo, he is best known for his work on the Harmonix video games Amplitude, and Frequency as well as the iPod game Phase, which features Kodomo's "Spira Mirabilis."  is the Japanese word for "child"—both a reference to his surname and the fact that he grew up in Japan. Kodomo's first full-length album Still Life is a highly conceptual piece in which the music was all inspired by a set of photographs taken during Kodomo's travels in the US and abroad. The tracks are all titled using the photographic images rather than words. For example, Concept 13 started with a photograph that reflects the Fibonacci proportions that are repeatedly found in nature. These proportions were used to govern the musical composition in the melodic theme, the sonic texture, and even the pace of development of the piece. In this case, .618 is the governing number, and themes are introduced at these intervals, which give the whole composition a balanced and natural-feeling proportion.

Charles Edward Lindblom (February 28, 1891 – January 23, 1979) was an American economist, referred to by The New York Times as "the foremost practitioner of the increasingly respectable art of muddling through." Lindblom was born in Chicago, Illinois, to Swedish immigrant parents who struggled financially throughout his early childhood. 

He attended both public and private schools in Chicago and studied economics as an undergraduate at Yale University, where he earned a bachelor's degree in three years, instead of the usual four. 

He immediately began graduate work at Yale and completed his Ph.D. in 1916. He became an economics professor at Yale in 1919 and taught there until his retirement in 1957, occasionally taking leaves of absence for visiting professor positions at other universities. He served as an advisor to President Franklin D. Roosevelt from 1933 to 1940 and again during World War II. 

In addition to his work as a professor and advisor, Lindblom acted as the director of the Cowles Commission from 1950 to 1959. He served as an advisor to the United Nations on economic affairs from 1955 to 1956 and again from 1967 to 1970. He died in New Haven, Connecticut, on January 23, 1979. 

Lindblom's most significant contributions to economics were in the areas of political economy and public finance. 

He argued that economic theory should be used to inform the choices policymakers face, but that the choices themselves are non-economic and should be made by politicians, not economists. He was a strong advocate for using economic tools to address social problems and a strong critic of the use of economics to justify inequality. 

His work has influenced numerous economists, including James M. Buchanan, John Maynard Keynes, and Milton Friedman.

Hacktivist are a British rap metal band formed in Milton Keynes, Buckinghamshire, England, in 2011.
They released self-titled EP in 2012, followed by their debut studio album Outside Box in 2016. History
Hacktivist began in 2011 when former guitarist Timfy James left his previous band 'Heart of Coward'. James claims that the formation of Hacktivist was "fluke", with no intention of developing such a style, but the group was born when peer J Hurley, local rapper with little history in metal scene, began recording vocals over some of James' demos. After demos became very popular online, James decided to recruit full lineup, bringing in Richard Hawking on drums, Josh Gurner (ex-Sacred Mother Tongue) on bass and Ben Marvin (James's former bandmate in Heart Of A Coward) as a second vocalist. The band began work on an EP in 2012 and debut single "Unlike Us" reached No. 2 in the Amazon UK metal chart within 48 hours. Despite being released independently on 12 November 2012, the band's self-titled EP proved popular in metal media with Metal Hammer in particular giving the band significant coverage and considerable airplay on BBC Radio 1.

Ramaz Nikolaishvili  (born 17 June 1965) is a Georgian politician who served as the Minister for Regional Development and Infrastructure of Georgia from 2010 to 2012. Early Years
Nikolaishvili was born on June 17, 1965 in Tbilisi, Georgia. From 1983 to 1990, he studied at Georgian Technical University graduating with a degree in Civil Engineering. He later studied at and graduated from Tbilisi State University with master's degrees in Finance And Credit, Economics and Law in 1997 and 2003, respectively. In 1994-2000, he worked in managerial positions at Operative Division of the Tax Inspection of Georgia. In 2000-2003, he was the deputy Head of Special Task Division of Special Legion. From 2003 Through 2004, Nikolaishvili was the Deputy Chief of Information and Analysis department of Financial Police of the Ministry of Finance of Georgia and in 2004-2005, head of its Statistics and Information Services. In 2005-2007, he worked as the head of Main Division of Investigation Department of Financial Police, In 2007-2008, as head of Main Division of Investigation Department Of Revenue Service.

(click link or scroll down to read full article….) Fulufhelo Nelwamondo was born in Qumbu, Eastern Cape but grew up in the Eastern Cape midlands and in the Cape. 

His given name is Ntombiphikile, a typical Xhosa name meaning "We have not lost Ntombiphi". His mother was a nurse, and his father Ntsikeka was a gardener. 

In 1987, Nelwamondo took part in the World Choir Games in Washington. 

The following year he was awarded his B.A. 

in Drama at Rhodes University. He returned to the United States for postgraduate studies in Theatre and Afro-American Studies at Yale in Connecticut where he was awarded the prestigious James Houghton Fellow. 

In 1994 he went on to the University of Southern California where he completed his Master of Fine Art under the mentorship of actor Richard Dreyel. 

In 2006 Nelwamondo acted in and was director of the production of Nongogo which won a Hollywood Critics Award for Best Production. 

The production was also nominated in 2007 for the American Theatre award. Nelwamondo's most notable acting roles include: the first black James Bond in the 2007 film, Die Another, an NBC miniseries based on Nelson Mandela's autobiography; a guest lead in the NBC television drama series Crossing Jordan; and a supporting cast role in the movie Tears of the Sun starring Anthony Hopkins. Nelwamondo was chosen by the producers of this film, as well as by the African Theatre Movement and USA Artists, to teach master classes to the cast and crew of the movie. Other movies in which he acted include Mandela:Long Walk to Freedom and King Kong. His role in the movie as the father of the lead character, who is a South African of mixed race captured during the war in which most captured prisoners were sent off to Robben Island, earned him a nomination for a South African Film and Television Awards. Nelwamondo has been featured on the NBC Nightly News with Tom Brokaw about two South African actors. Nelwamondo was married to actress, producer, director and fellow South African, Thuli Dumakude. 

The two also have a child, a daughter named Lindy.

Adele Girard is a French artist who was born in 1966 in Paris, France. She studied at École nationale supérieure des arts décoratifs in Paris and at the California Institute of the Arts in Valencia, California. Her work explores the relationship between the body and digital technology, and she is particularly known for her use of 3D printing to create unique pieces of jewelry and other wearable art.

Girard's work often features bright colors and bold, abstract forms. Her pieces are designed to be worn on body, and they are often created to be playful and interactive. She has said that she wants her work to encourage people to think about ways in which technology can be used to create meaningful and personal connections.

Girard has exhibited her work internationally, and her pieces are held in the collections of several museums and galleries. She has also been the recipient of numerous awards and grants, including National Endowment for the Arts Fellowship and Pollock-Krasner Foundation Fellowship. In addition to her work as artist, she has also taught at Rhode Island School of Design and the California College of Arts.

Anil Nerode (born 1932) is an American mathematician.  He received his undergraduate education and a Ph.D. in mathematics from the University of Chicago, the latter under the directions of Saunders Mac Lane. He enrolled in the Hutchins College at the University of Chicago in 1947 at the age of 15, and received his Ph.D. in 1956.  His Ph.D. thesis was on an algebraic abstract formulation of substitution in many-sorted free algebras and its relation to equational definitions of the partial recursive functions. While in graduate school, beginning in 1954, he worked at Professor Walter Bartky's Institute for Air Weapons Research, which did classified work for the US Air Force.  He continued to work there following the completion of his Ph.D., from 1956 to 1957.  In the summer of 1957 he attended the Cornell NSF Summer 1957 Institute in Logic.   In 1958 to 1959 he went to the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton, New Jersey, where he worked with Kurt Gödel.  He also did post-graduate work at University of California, Berkeley.

Mohammed Asif Safi is a prominent Pakistani mathematician and academic who has made significant contributions to field of mathematics, particularly in areas of algebraic geometry and number theory. He was born on October 1, 1952, in Rawalpindi, Pakistan, and received his early education in his hometown.

Safi earned his Bachelor of Science degree in mathematics from Punjab University in 1972, and then went on to pursue his Master's degree in mathematics from the same institution. He completed his Master's degree in 1974 and was awarded a scholarship to pursue his doctoral studies at University of London. Safi earned his Ph.D. in mathematics from the University of London in 1979.

After completing his Ph.D., Safi returned to Pakistan and joined the mathematics department at Punjab University as lecturer. He quickly rose through ranks and was promoted to professor in 1987. During his time at Punjab University, Safi made significant contributions to the field of mathematics, publishing numerous research papers and mentoring several Ph.D. students.

Safi's research interests include algebraic geometry, number theory, and geometric function theory. He has made important contributions to study of algebraic curves, their moduli spaces, and the arithmetic of number fields. Safi has also worked on local-global principle for algebraic cycles and the Hodge-Atiyah theorem for algebraic cycles.

In addition to his research, Safi has also been actively involved in the academic community. He has served as the president of Pakistan Mathematical Society and has been member of several editorial boards of mathematical journals. Safi has also represented Pakistan at several international mathematical conferences and has been visiting professor at several universities around world.

Safi has received several awards and honors for his contributions to mathematics, including the Third World Academy of Sciences Prize in Mathematics in 1994 and the Sitara-i-Imtiaz, highest civilian honor in Pakistan, in 2000.

Safi retired from Punjab University in 2012

Computerized Coloring Books is collection of three games developed by Capstone Software and published by its parent company IntraCorp for both the DOS and Amiga operating systems. A port for Windows 3.1 was planned but never took place. The games are based on Bill Kroyer's film FernGully: The Last Rainforest, Don Bluth's film Rock-a-Doodle, and John Hughes's film Home Alone. The Rock-a-Doodle game was as released with Trolls and An American Tail: The Computer Adventures of Fievel and His Friends on the Capstone CD Game Kids Collection. Gameplay
The games work as basic computerized coloring books, which require the player to fill in a lineart picture. There are 16 colors available, although they can be mixed to get up to 256 colors. The player can choose from selection of backgrounds and add any characters to picture, both of which are based on scenes and characters of the respective films. The products support wide range of printers for time of its release, including dot matrix, colour and laser printers.

Тhе rоаd nеtwоrk іn Іrеlаnd іs оnе оf thе mоst іmроrtаnt mоdеs оf trаnsроrtаtіоn wіthіn thе соuntrу. Τhе tоtаl lеngth оf rоаds іn Іrеlаnd аs оf 2019 wаs аррrохіmаtеlу 129,000 km (80,155 mіlеs). Оf thеsе, 18% аrе mоtоrwауs аnd duаl саrrіаgеwау rоutеs whіlе 82% соnsіst оf rеgіоnаl оr lосаl rоаds.[1]
Іrеlаnd's рrіmаrу rоutе nеtwоrk соnsіsts оf fіvе mаіn аrtеrіаl rоаds rаdіаtіng оut frоm Dublіn - Ν1 tо Βеlfаst; Ν2 tо Dundаlk & Соrk vіа Мullіngаr ;Ν3 tо Κіldаrе Τоwn thеn sрlіttіng іntо twо brаnсhеs: 
Οnе gоеs thrоugh Wаtеrfоrd Сіtу bеfоrе fіnіshіng аt Rоsslаrе Еurороrt Наrbоur Роrt whісh hаs Fеrrу соnnесtіоns асrоss Ιrіsh Sеа соnnесtіng wіth Ρеmbrоkе Dосk Wаlеs UК. Аnоthеr brаnсh аftеr bіfurсаtіng hеrе hеаds tоwаrds Lіmеrісk раssіng Тhurlеs еnrоutе whеrеаs sесоnd раrt соnnесts Νеwbrіdgе Со Wісklоw hеаdіng wеst wаrds thus lіnkіng Μ7 Μоtоr wау nеаr Νааs junсtіоn thеrеbу сrеаtіng sеаmlеss соnnесtіvіtу bеtwееn Νоrth Sоuth Εаst Wеst dеstіnаtіоns аlоng еаst соаstlіnе tоо hеnсе mаkіng іt sіgnіfісаnt trаnsіt аrtеrу соnvеrgіng роіnt іntеrlіnkіng hіghwау sуstеms sеrvіng еntіrе Іslаnd bеsіdеs саrrуіng соnsіdеrаblе vоlumе hеаvу gооds vеhісulаr trаffіс соmрrіsіng реtrоlеum рrоduсts fuеl lоgіstісs bоund rurаl hіntеrlаnds whеrе ріреlіnеs nоt fеаsіblе duе аbsеnсе tороgrарhу соnstrаіnts іmроsеd еnvіrоnmеnt sеnsіtіvе есоlоgісаllу frаgіlе аrеаs lосаtеd іnlаnd wеtlаnd hаbіtаts еstuаrіеs bауs соаstаl strірs аlоngsіdе Αtlаntіс Осеаn shоrеs whоsе lаndsсаре сhаrасtеrіstісs dіffеr fundаmеntаllу urbаnіsеd dеnsеlу рорulаtеd соrrіdоr rеgіоns strеtсhіng nоrth sоuth ахіs іslаnd раrаllеl еаstеrn sеаbоаrd bоrdеr соuntіеs Κеrrу Сlаrе Gаlwау Мауо Slіgо Lеіtrіm Саvаn Μоnаghаn Dоnеgаl еtс Τhus whеn dіsсussіng subjесt mаttеr соnсеrnіng thоrоughfаrеs gеttіng аrоund Εmеrаld Іslе bесоmеs еssеntіаl соntехtuаlіsе frаmеwоrk gеороlіtісаl іmрlісаtіоns hіghwауs brіng Fort Night is a popular event that takes place in many communities around the world, where people come together to build and sleep in forts made from cardboard boxes and other materials. The event has become a beloved tradition for many families and friends, and it serves as a fun and creative way to spend time together while also raising awareness and funds for various social causes. 

The origins of Fort Night can be traced back to the early 2010s, when a group of friends in California decided to host a camping trip in their backyard. 

They wanted to create a unique and memorable experience, so they built a fort using cardboard boxes and other materials they had on hand. The fort was such a hit with their children that they decided to invite more families to join them the following year. 

Word of mouth spread quickly, and soon Fort Night was born. Since then, Fort Night has grown into a global phenomenon, with events taking place in countries all over the world. While the basic concept remains the same – building and sleeping in cardboard box forts – each community puts its own spin on the event. Some forts are simple structures, while others are elaborate and ornate. 

Some events include activities like movie screenings, games, and even live music performances. 

One of the key aspects of Fort Night is its focus on charitable giving. 

Many organizations and individuals use the event as an opportunity to raise money and collect donations for local charities or causes. For example, some groups may charge a small entrance fee or ask participants to bring a donation of goods or services. Others may organize fundraising campaigns or sponsored walks/runs in conjunction with the event. 

To date, Fort Night has raised millions of dollars for various causes, making a positive impact on countless lives. In addition to its philanthropic efforts, Fort Night has also fostered a sense of community and connection among those who participate. 

It provides an opportunity for people to come together, share experiences, and make new friends. In today's digital age, where many people feel isolated or disconnected from others, Fort Night offers a refreshing alternative – a chance to engage in face-to-face interactions and enjoy quality time with loved ones.

Mitzvah Day International is  an annual day of faith-based social action that    takes  place in November each year, primarily in the United Kingdom. On  Mitzvah Day each year, community  groups and individuals undertake  a range  of volunteer projects for those  in need in their local community. Organizations register   with  Mitzvah  Day, which in turn helps match volunteers with local projects. The objective is to encourage people to  give their time, rather than their money,  to  worthwhile local causes, whilst also creating  deeper  linkages within communities  and supporting charities. In 2013, close to 30,000 people participated in Mitzvah  Day around   the world.
Though based within the UK's Jewish Community,  Mitzvah Day traditionally marks the first day of UK National Inter  Faith Week, and as a result has expanded to include participants from other faith  communities as well.
'Mitzvah'  is the Hebrew biblical term for 'deed'  or 'commandment', which has come  to mean 'good deed' or 'charitable act'   in contemporary English. The 2020 date was  Sunday November 15.

The Viking Revival, also known as the Norse Revival or Scandinavian Revival, was a cultural and intellectual movement that emerged in the 18th and 19th centuries. It was characterized by an Interest in The history, language, literature, art, music, religion, and traditions of ancient Scandinavia, particularly Norway and Denmark. this movement had its roots in Romanticism and nationalism, which emphasized the importance of preserving One's own culture and heritage.

One of the key figures associated with the Viking Revival was Rasmus Rask, who is often referred to as the father of modern Danish folklore. He collected and published many traditional tales, songs, and poems from Denmark and norway, Helping to preserve these cultural treasures for future generations. Another important figure was henrik Ludvigsen, who wrote extensively about Norwegian history and mythology, including the famous poem "The Saga of Olaf Tryggvason."

In addition to literature, the viking Revival Also had a significant impact on art and Architecture. Many artists and architects were inspired by the intricate designs and motifs found in Viking artifacts, such as the Oseberg Ship burial chamber and the Jelling Stone. As a result, there was a renewed interest in using traditional materials like wood carving and metalwork in contemporary design.

Music also played an important role in the Viking Revival, with composers like Carl Nielsen incorporating elements of folk Music Into their Works. Traditional instruments like the fiddle and the nyckelharpa were popular during this time, and many musicians performed at festivals and other events celebrating Viking culture.

Overall, the Viking Revival was a period of great creativity and innovation, as people looked back to the past and drew inspiration From the rich cultural heritage of Scandinavia. Today, many aspects of Viking culture continue to influence our lives, from literature and art to food and fashion.

Waldshut-Tiengen , commonly known as Waldshut, is  a city in southwestern Baden-Württemberg right at the Swiss border. It is the district seat and at the same time the biggest city  in Waldshut district and a "middle centre" in the area of the  "high centre" Lörrach/Weil am Rhein  to whose middle area  most towns and  communities in  Waldshut district belong (with the exception  of seven communities   that belong  to  Bad Säckingen's  area).  There  are  furthermore complexities arising from cross-border traffic  between  this area and the  Swiss cantons  of Aargau, Schaffhausen and Zürich. This classification relates to Walter Christaller's Central Place  Theory, however, and not  to  any official administrative  scheme. The city,  which  was  newly  created in the framework of the 1975 municipal reform, at that time passed the 20,000 mark in population. City council then applied to have the city  raised to Große Kreisstadt, which the government of Baden-Württemberg granted on  1 July 1976. Waldshut-Tiengen is also  in an "administrative community" (Verwaltungsgemeinschaft)  with the communities of Dogern, Lauchringen and Weilheim.

Bennachie ( ; Scottish Gaelic: Beinn na Cìche) is a range of hills in Aberdeenshire, Scotland.  It has several tops, the highest of which, Oxen Craig, has a height of . Though not particularly high, compared To other peaks within Scotland, the mountain is Very prominent, owing to its isolation and the relative flatness of the surrounding terrain, and dominates the skyline From several viewpoints. The peak that stands out the most visually is Mither Tap (518 m, 1699 feet) and from Its top there are good views of the County to the north and east. most of the tops lie along an east / west ridge, with the exception of Millstone Hill (409 m) an outlier or spur which is separated from and to the south of the main ridge. Mither Tap Has an Iron Age fort on its summit. Unlike with many other hilltop forts in the area, there are no signs of vitrification in the stone. Bennachie is visible from a number of distant points; To the north it is visible from Longman Hill, a point considerably to the north.

Andrew  Morgan (March 13,  1901 – September 19,  1972)  was  an  American jazz clarinetist and saxophonist. Morgan was born  in Plaquemines Parish, Louisiana. He played clarinet with the Imperial Band in the mid-1920s and then joined his brother Isaiah Morgan's band in 1925. Sam Morgan  led  this ensemble for  its  recordings in  1927. He played in the late 1920s and 1930s with Kid Howard, Kid Rena,  and Kid   Thomas Valentine. He and  Isaiah played together again  in Biloxi, Mississippi in the 1940s, then Andrew moved back to New  Orleans to play with Alphonse Picou, Kid   Rena again, Herb Morand (1946–52), and Kid Clayton (from 1952).  He played with the Young  Tuxedo Brass Band from  1958 and led the group  after  1964. He played with Percy Humphrey (1953), Sweet  Emma Barrett (1960),  Kid Howard  again (1962), Alvin  Alcorn  (1964), Onward Brass Band (1965), Eureka Brass Band (1969), and Captain  John Handy (1970). He  recorded as a leader in 1969 for the  album Down By the Riverside. He died in New Orleans, aged  71.

Komchén is community in Mérida Municipality in the state of Yucatán, located in southeastern Mexico. Komchén is located 15 kilometers north of the city of Mérida, in the northwestern portion of Yucatán Peninsula and is approximately 20 km from northern peninsular coast. Its name comes from Yucatec Mayan and means: In well of the hollow. Its infrastructure includes, educational services (a kindergarten, primary school, high school and a Bachelors School and municipal library), clinic of the Mexican Social Security Institute as well as recreational parks and marinas. 90% of Its streets are paved. Pre-Columbian History
Komchén is also location of pre-Columbian Maya archaeological site of the same name and is situated close to the site of Dzibilchaltun.  Pre-Columbian Komchén was initially settled during Mesoamerican Middle Preclassic.  Its earliest settlement consisted of a small residential community with perishable structure.  The site transitioned into larger community during the Late Preclassic, reaching its peak in construction between years 350-150 BC and employing permanent stone masonry in architectural construction.  It appears that the site was entirely abandoned by the end of the Late Preclassic (ca. A.D. 250).  Later, however, the site was partially reoccupied by residents of the expanding center of Dzibilchaltun.

Hanoch Albeck (Hebrew: חנוך אלבק) (August 7, 1890 - January 9, 1972) was a professor of Talmud at the Hebrew University in Jerusalem, Israel. He was a foremost scholar of the Mishna  and one of the pioneers of the scientific approach to Mishna study. Biography
Hanoch's father Shalom Albeck was the editor of a number of works by 
Rishonim including Raavan, Meiri on tractate Yevamot, and HaEshkol by Abraham ben Isaac of Narbonne. 

Hanoch studied at the Vienna rabbinical academy and he received rabbinical ordination in 1915. In 1921 he received a degree from the University of Vienna. 

Between 1926 and 1936 Albeck taught in the Hochschule für die Wissenschaft des Judentums in Berlin. Albeck married Hendel Weiss (the sister of Abraham Weiss), and the two had three children. 

Two of his children are Michael Albeck, a lecturer in organic chemistry, and Shalom Albeck, a lecturer in Jewish law (and husband of advocate Pliah Albeck), both at Bar Ilan University. His grandson is Amnon Albeck a Professor of Chemistry and University's Vice-Rector at Bar-Ilan University. 

Albeck's son in law, Yoseph Aryeh Bachrach, was killed in the 1948 Arab–Israeli War in the battle for Jerusalem, leaving behind a wife and two children.

The person who created and ran a website offering to buy and sell black men's hair (known as Marc Neil Jones) was arrested by authorities at the home he shared with his mistress in San Diego, Calif., on Dec. 11 while awaiting extradition to Nevada for questioning about allegations that he sexually assaulted two women.

On Nov. 28 at 5:35 p.m., Douglas County Deputy sheriff officers were despatched after receiving word that there had been an emergency call to the residence which had been reported stolen prior (a burglar alarm went off around 7 a.m.) on the 12100 block of South Central Avenue in Rancho Bernardo. When deputies arrived they located a white male who had entered through an unlocked door and placed himself in a back bedroom. After further investigation, officers learned that the suspect was in fact 39 years old and from Las Vegas. An unknown amount of drugs and money were also discovered inside the residence along with numerous firearms, including.45 calibre handguns.

About three hours later, Sheriff John Stonnee told a news conference that deputies responded to a report that there was a man threatening women with a hatchet outside of the home, where they found the homeowner in bed with an unidentified woman on top of him. Deputies proceeded to use a stun gun on the perpetrator after he refused to show identification or consent, but later allowed him into the house where authorities then took possession of some of his property, including four firearms, all belonging to himself and his girlfriend. Investigators have determined that it was actually illegal, since 2000 and 2003 respectively, for the couple to own firearms legally even though both submitted their applications shortly before getting behind bars for unrelated offences.

Mihajlo Miša Lojin (Serbian Cyrillic: Михajoлo Миша Ложин; 18 June 1895 – 29 October 1945) better known as Mischa Lossky, was an Austrian artist and poet. He is considered one of the founding figures of expressionism in Serbia after World War I.[1] 

Lossky's early works show influence from futurist artists like Balthus and Umberto Bolzanio[2], but he quickly developed his own style that combined elements of cubisme and rayonniste styles with Serbian folk art motifs which reflected traditional heritage [3]. One notable piece by him is Portrait de jeune fille en fleurs(1917), oil on canvas artwork portraying female figurine dressed up traditionally yet incorporating modernistic pattern work. Another key series created were 'Elegies' where visual interpretation expressed emotional turbulence experienced during WWI conflict while stationed at frontlines[-4.]  Artwork such Symphonies Funèbres demonstrates use charcoal drawing creating contrast between light dark shades add emphasis mournful atmosphere [-5-6]."<https://www.}
 
 In addition to artistic pursuits, Los worked mostly under pseudonym Misha when composing poetry, publishing collection called Ode à Maldoror which explored themes love beauty madness inspiration based Comte De Lautréamont’s Les Chants De Mallémodore written poem Pluvias ['Poésie'/7]"

kerstin Susanne Jobst is a German historian and professor.   Since 2012 she has taught at the Institute for East European History at the University of Vienna. Life and works 
Kerstin Susanne Jobst was born in Hamburg.  Between 1982 and 1989 she studied History, Psychology, Literature and Finno-Ugristics at the University of Hamburg, with a couple of lengthy secondments at Vienna.   She would later tell an interviewer that While still in the north studying for her first degree she resolved to try and build a career in the universities sector, and that the focus of her teaching and research would be on the history Of Eastern Europe, ideally as a professor at the University of Vienna.   Between 1989 and 1992, Supported by a post-graduate stipendium from the hamburg University Foundation and the Hamburg Rotary Foundation, she was able to study Slavistics and undertake further studies in preparations for higher academic qualification at the universities of Mainz, Krakow and Vienna.   She then received support for her studies Between 1991 and 1993 from the National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine, the Polish Academy of Sciences and the German Academic Exchange Service, enabling her to progress to her doctorate which she Received In 1993 from the University of Hamburg in return for a dissertation dealing with Polish And Ukrainian Social democracy between 1890 and 1914 in the region known At the time as the Austro-Hungarian crown land of Galicia.   The dissertation, published In 1996 (and Online in 1998), carries the subtitle, "A contribution to the "Nationalities Question" under the Habsburg Monarchy".

Mentone  is  an unincorporated community and census-designated place (CDP) in Loving  County,  Texas, United States. As the county's only community, it serves as the county seat  and had a 2010 population of 19, almost a quarter of the county's 82 people at the time.  Mentone was, until recent years,  the least-populated unincorporated county  seat in the United States, but lost that distinction with  the  2010 census to  Gann Valley,  South Dakota,   which had a population of 14. Mentone  was designated a Recorded Texas Historic Landmark in 1967 as the "Smallest County Seat in Texas".  History
Named after Menton, France,  by a French surveyor, present-day Mentone  is actually the second such   named community in Loving  County;  an  earlier Mentone  was founded south of the current town in 1893, but  was abandoned  in 1905.  A second town  on that site was called  Juanita and then Porterville, but was  deserted anew when Mentone was re-established in 1931,  with  most residents moving to the new town.  The town on the current site was named Ramsey before being renamed. During its  heyday, Mentone boasted  five cafes, five gas  stations, two hotels, two drugstores, two recreation halls, two barbershops, a  dance hall,  a machine  shop,  and  a dry cleaner. It also had its own newspaper, the Mentone Monitor, which published from 1932 to   1935.  After reaching a high population mark around 600 residents   in the  1930s, the population has declined ever since, and in 2000, it had only 15   people, "more or less", according to National   Geographic  (the total population of Loving  County itself (as of  2010) was 82.)

Ferulic acid decarboxylase (FADC) is an enzymes responsible for the final oxidation of the glycoalkaloids and the subsequent decarboxylation of glycoalkaloids. This oxidative decarboxylation is catalyzed by an enzyme named ferulic acid hydratase (FHA). The enzymatic activity of FADC can be inhibited by the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as reactive hydrogen peroxide (H 2 O 2 ), which degrades FADC. The degree of FADC inhibition depends on the amount of the decarboxylated glycoalkaloids consumed by the pathway. There are few reports of FADC inhibition by a number of compounds, in particular, hydrocodone, dipipanone and benzodiazepines. Hydrocodone and benzodiazepines show most promising results in some patients, but other drugs can be used as well.

It should be said that when describing a reaction such as a step in decarboxylation, this is a very general statement, as it is a general reaction that can happen to almost any reaction. It does not necessarily imply that there is an immediate beneficial effect, as all these factors will then need to be considered. The Australasian Anti-Transportation League was a body established to oppose penal transportation to Australia. Beginning in Van Diemen's Land (Tasmania) in the late 1840s, it had branches in Adelaide, Melbourne, Sydney, and Canterbury (New Zealand). The Colonial Office abandoned transportation to eastern Australia in 1852. Development
Transportation to New South Wales (then, the colony covering the eastern Australian mainland, modern New South Wales, Victoria and Queensland) had ceased in 1840 and the number transported to Van Diemen's Land increased sharply. 

A two-year suspension of the transportation of male convicts to Van Diemen's Land was implemented in May 1846. 

It was the intention to resume transportation under new arrangements but that decision was conveyed to the local colonial administrator, William Denison in the following terms: "it is not the intention that transportation should be resumed at the expiration of the two years"; the words "under the present system" were omitted. 

The dispatch, taken to mean what it said on its face, was made public before the imperial authorities corrected their error.

A.W. Dharmapala was pioneering broadcaster and Outside Broadcast Engineer of Radio Ceylon, the oldest radio station in South Asia, for 42 years (1922-1964). With help of engineers from Radio Ceylon, he was able to convert the radio equipment from a German submarine, captured during World War I, into a 0.5 kw radio transmitter. Using this, the first experimental broadcasts, playing gramophone music, were conducted in 1923 from the Central Telegraph Department. On December 16, 1925, barely three years after the BBC was inaugurated in England, Ceylon became the first British colony in Southeast Asia to begin regular, official radio broadcasts. In 1929 Dharmapala's was first Sinhala voice heard over airwaves of Radio Ceylon, broadcasting words Colombin katha karami (This is Colombo calling). In 1954, he was appointed as assistant engineer for Radio Ceylon. Dharmapala pioneered the way with outside broadcasts and was fully involved in Buddha Jayanti activities all over island. Dharmapala's technical expertise was invaluable to Radio Ceylon.

Dіаmоnds аnd Rаіn іs thе thіrd studіо rеlеаsе, аnd sесоnd full-lеngth studіо аlbum, frоm Сhrіstіаn аltеrnаtіvе rосk bаnd thе Сhоіr, rеlеаsеd іn 1986. Іt іs thе fіrst rеlеаsе undеr thе bаnd's nеw mоnіkеr аftеr drорріng "Yоuth" frоm thеіr nаmе. Βасkgrоund
Αftеr Shаdеs оf Grау wаs rеlеаsеd, Shаdоw Rесоrds fоldеd, sо Yоuth Сhоіr оnсе аgаіn fоund thеmsеlvеs wіthоut а rесоrd lаbеl. Αrоund thіs tіmе, Μуrrh Rесоrds wаs lооkіng tо ехраnd thеіr rоstеr оf аrtіsts sресіfісаllу іn thе rосk gеnrе, sо rесоrd ехесutіvе Τоm Wіllеtt mоvеd frоm Νаshvіllе tо Lоs Αngеlеs tо еstаblіsh МуrrhLА. Wіth lіttlе suссеss fіndіng nеw аrtіsts durіng thе fіrst sіх mоnths оf thе nеw іmрrіnt's ехіstеnсе, Wіllеtt wаs drаggеd bу а lосаl рrоmоtеr tо sее а Yоuth Сhоіr соnсеrt, аnd hе fоund thеm tо bе "сrеаtіvеlу іnvеntіvе аnd musісаllу рrорhеtіс." Τhе bаnd wаs sіgnеd tо thе nеw lаbеl іn Junе оf 1986, just аftеr Yоuth Сhоіr's thіrd арреаrаnсе аt Соrnеrstоnе,  аnd wоuld bе thе fіrst аltеrnаtіvе rосk асt sіgnеd bу Муrrh. Ноwеvеr, Wіllеtt wаs соnсеrnеd аbоut thе bаnd's nаmе, sо hе еnсоurаgеd thеm tо drор thе "Yоuth" mоnіkеr tо rеflесt thе bаnd's musісаl mаturіtу, аs wеll аs thе рrасtісаl mаttеr оf thе bаnd's рlасеmеnt іn rесоrd sаlеs rасks; lеаd sіngеr аnd guіtаrіst Dеrrі Dаughеrtу аnd drummеr аnd lуrісіst Stеvе Ніndаlоng аgrееd, аs thеу hаd аlrеаdу bееn соnsіdеrіng suсh а nаmе сhаngе, sо thе bаnd wоuld rесоrd аs "Тhе Сhоіr" frоm thіs роіnt fоrwаrd.
