Healthcare in Slovenia is organised primarily through the Health Insurance Institute of Slovenia. In 2015, healthcare expenditures accounted for 8.10% of GDP. The Slovenian healthcare system was ranked 15th in the Euro health consumer index 2015. The country ranked second in the 2012 Euro Hepatitis Index. The Slovenian healthcare system is a conservative-health care model financed for through a mandatory insurance programme called the Health Insurance Institute of Slovenia, HIIS (Slovene: Zavod za zdravstveno zavarovanje Slovenije) with contributions paid by both employers and employees. However, not all costs are borne by the mandatory insurance scheme (though children's healthcare is fully covered). Almost all Slovenes thus pay voluntary insurance fees for additional coverage that provides additional funds for the healthcare system. Healthcare expenditure is comparable to those of other advanced European nations. The National Health Insurance Institute oversees all healthcare services. Citizens (and registered long-term residents) have a right to equal healthcare access.

David Sedgwick is a Canadian theater, cinema, and television writer, screenwriter, and writer, most known for his work in the Harry Potter movie trilogy, where he played the role of physics teacher, Professor Harry Potter.

Early life and Career

Sedgwick was born on February 14, 1968, in Birmingham, England. He developed an interest in acting at a young age and studied at the London College of Music and Theatre. After graduating, he started his career in the theater, singing in several musicals in London's East End, including "The Importance of Being Earnest" and "The Tempest."

Breakthrough and Success

Sedgwick's breakthrough role was in 2000, when he was cast as Professor Harry Potter in the movie adaptation of J.K. Rowling's bestselling book, "Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone." His characterization of the protagonist was generally praised, and he went on to reprise the role in all three novels of the Harry Potter franchise.

In addition to his work in the Harry Potter trilogy, Sedgwick has starred in a number of other novels, including "The Reader," "The Young Visiters," and "The Tale of Despereaux." He has subsequently begun to work in theater, with prominent roles in films such as "The Pillowman" and "The Normal Heart."

Acting and Writing

In addition to his directing career, Sedgwick has also ventured into acting and playwriting. He made his directorial debut with the 2014 short documentary, "The Last Bottle," and has since produced numerous other short films and radio productions.

Sedgwick has also authored numerous plays, including "The Wicked Game" and "The End of the Food Chain." His play, "The Innocents," was presented at the Royal Court Theater in Manchester in 2013.

awards and Nominations

Sedgwick's productions have garnered him many prizes and awards during his career. He was shortlisted for a BAF

Henry Wells was an American entrepreneur and financier, who made important contributions to the transport, banking, and insurance industries. He was best known for his co-founding of Wells, Fargo & Company and American Express Company. During his youth, he was the eldest of a family of seven children, and the youngest of these children was Henry Wells, Jr., born December 17, 1805, at Thetford, Vermont. He was the son of a farmer, and his formal education was limited. At the age of sixteen, he left home to serve as an apprentice to a coachbuilder in Boston. In 1829, he moved to New York and found a job as a freight agent for the Erie Railroad. Wells & Co. - Starting in 1839, Wells established Wells & Co., specializing in the transport of cargo between New York and Chicago, and he was successful. The main reason for the success of the company was its excellent operation and the principle of the separation of various goods in different cars. In the early days, this method greatly improved the efficiency and cost of the transport. Wells, Fargo & Co. - Starting in 1844, Wells saw an opportunity to create a financial service company that could transport money and other valuables safely and quickly across long distances. He was able to connect with another successful businessman, William G. Fargo, and jointly establish the American Express Company. Wells, Fargo & Co. was established in 1852, and in the following year, it was listed on the New York Stock Exchange. It became one of the largest banks in the United States, offering a variety of financial services for individuals, businesses, and governments. Wells, besides his business in the transport and financial industries, also had many other ventures, such as real estate, mining, and publishing. He also played an important role in the development of San Francisco.

Isabel Torres Lopez (born September 14, 1942) is a Filipino actress, actress, educator, choreographer and actor. The director (together with Carlos Elejar) of Dance Philippines, she received since May 20, 2014 from the Filipino President Manuel Aquino IV the highest recognition in the Arts, International Artist of the Philippines. She was chiefly responsible in promoting modern ballet with the Isabel Cruz Ballet Group which  
staged the inaugural contemporary ballet festival at the Arts Centre of the Philippines Main Stage in January 1970. It was this company that eventually became Opera Philippines. She is best known for "Bungkos Suite", "Gloria", "Carmina Burana", "Romeo and Juliet", "Rama Hari", "Cinderella", "Amada", "Itim-Asu", and "Story of the Manuvu"—all nuanced with Japanese culture, gesture and grace. Early life and education
Reyes was born on September 14, 1942. At a very young age, Reyes gave up classes in modern dance under Luis Merino while teaching at Maryknoll University where she completed a Master of Science degree in History and Foreign Services.

Krišjānis Kariņš  (Latvian:Artūrs Kariņš, Russian:Артур Kарийнский,  Артур  Риснейс  Kарин; 28 June 1909, Riga1 – 3 February 1982, Miami, FL) was a Russian-born American journalist and foreign correspondent for Life  and  Time magazines. Kariņš was the son of a high-ranking official of the  Latvian Social Democratic  Workers' Party, was raised among prominent Latvian intellectuals  and was strongly influenced by Latvian nationalism. He graduated from high  school in Riga in 1930. Before emigrating to the United  States in 1937,   Kariņš had been the Latvian Foreign Ministry's press attaché in Tokyo, one of the Baltic states'  consul  generals in Harbin, a correspondent in North  China, and the Baltic News correspondent in London.  In 1937 he was hired at Time.  At that time he could speak Russian, German, French, Latvian, and  Japanese and had a good  knowledge of English. He was able to take    notes in Braille. He had a brilliant career during World War II as Life's  photojournalist.  After the  Soviet re-invasion of the Baltic states during  the  summer of 1944, he  was appointed Head of the   News and Information  Bureau of the Latvian Provisional  Government in  Switzerland. As  the war came to close, Kariņš returned   to the United States and   in 1947 he rejoined the   then renamed Time-Life magazines as a roving correspondent. During the  next three decades he reported from  all corners  of the  globe,  but from 1959-1973 he  was principally based  in London.
His book, Russia   from the Inside, is a rich  memoir, full of details about the people   he had come in contact  with, often in very senior positions, over the  course of his years as  Time's stringer in Riga. The  book also reveals details about him that in his professional life had remained cloaked. From the  book, we learn that his family's history  is closely  intertwined with that of Riga, that his father was in  his youth a strong socialist, and a strong, if not quite Communist, supporter of Lenin,  who once

William Roscoe (8 March 175330 June 1831) was an English banker, lawyer, and briefly a Member of Parliament. He is best known as one of England's first abolitionists, and as the author of the poem for children The Butterfly's Ball, and the Grasshopper's Feast. In his day he was also respected as a historian and art collector, as well as a botanist and miscellaneous writer. Early life
He was born in Liverpool, where his father, a market gardener, kept a public house called the Bowling Green at Mount Pleasant. Roscoe left school at the age of twelve, having learned all that his schoolmaster could teach. He assisted his father in the work of the garden, but spent his leisure time on reading and study. Later, he wrote:
This mode of life gave health and vigour to my body, and amusement and instruction to my mind; and to this day I well remember the delicious sleep which succeeded my labours, from which I was again called at an early hour. If I were now asked whom I consider to be the happiest of the human race, I should answer, those who cultivate the earth by their own hands.
At fifteen he began to look for a suitable career. A month's trial of bookselling was unsuccessful, and in 1769 he was articled to a solicitor. Although a diligent student of law, he continued to read the classics, and made the acquaintance with the language and literature of Italy which was to dominate his life.

Luciano Frigerio (Desio, 21 May 1928 – Sanremo April 11, 1999), was designer, artist and Italian musician. Biography 
Luciano Frigerio was born in Desio in 1928, where his Father  Giovanni  set up a small workshop of highly crafted cabinet making in 1889, Luciano attended the junior school at the Collegio Paola di Rosa in Desio, where he met his school friend  Tommaso Giussani, who later became Monsignor in his ecclesiastical career. This close friendship and reciprocal esteem for each other grew over years and he was commissioned by the same Monsignor Giussani in 1982 to make two lecterns for Pope John XXIII, which  can still be seen today in the Basilica di S. Pietro e Paolo in Roma, positioned under altar of Bernini Bernini. 
Luciano Frigerio completed his technical and administrative studies  at  Pio XI College, gaining his diploma of Accounting, while at the same time cultivating and combining his two great passions, which he had had since his adolescence of designing furniture and composing music.

Garden Path (foaled 1941) was a British Thoroughbred racehorse and broodmare who won the classic 2000 Guineas in 1944. In a racing career conducted entirely at Newmarket Racecourse the filly ran six times and won three races. She was one of the best British two-year-olds of 1943, when she won one race and was placed in both the Middle Park Stakes and the Cheveley Park Stakes. After winning on her first appearance of 1944 she became the first (and still the only) filly since 1902 to win the 2000 Guineas against colts. 

On her only subsequent race she was injured when finishing unplaced in the Derby. She was retired from racing at the end of the season and had some success as a broodmare. Background
Garden Path was a big, good-looking brown mare bred by her owner the Edward Stanley, 17th Earl of Derby. 

Her sire, Fairway, had been a highly successful racehorse for Lord Derby, winning the St Leger and two runnings of the Champion Stakes. 

Garden Path's successes in 1944 enabled Fairway to win the sires' Championship for the fourth and final time. 

Garden Path's dam, Ranai, won two minor races before producing many good winners including the 2000 Guineas winner Watling Street (also sired by Fairway). 

Lord Derby sent the horse to his private trainer Walter Earl at his Stanley House stable in Newmarket, Suffolk.

Aveline de Grandpré (c. 1182 – 1219) was a Breton heiress and heiress of the County of Penthièvre in Brittany. She was the mother of Louis de Breton, grandson of John IV, Prince of Lorraine, and his second wife, the Princess Constancenée of Lorraine. Her father was William III, Prince of Brittany. She succeeded her husband in 1186 as Duke of Penthièvre and carried the title until her death.

In 1198, she married William, Count of Gloucester, by whom she had three daughters, William and William. She acquired the towns of Penthièvre and Richmond from her husband and passed them on to her brother William on her death. Her sister Eleanor will succeed her father and become the sole Duchess of Penthièvre.

Aveline was born in 1182, possibly at Vitré, in Brittany. She lived in 1219 at Bécherel, Brittany.

Sociocultural evolution refers to the process of change and development in human societies and cultures over time. It is a subfield of evolutionary studies that focuses on the ways in which human social and cultural behaviors, beliefs, and practices evolve and change over generations.

Sociocultural evolution can be seen as a response to changing environmental, social, and cultural pressures, and it is often driven by factors such as technological advancements, demographic shifts, and cultural exchange. This process of change can occur at various levels, including individual, group, and societal levels, and it can result in the emergence of new social norms, values, and beliefs.

One of the key features of sociocultural evolution is the idea of cultural transmission, which refers to the passing down of knowledge, beliefs, and practices from one generation to the next. This transmission can occur through various means, such as language, education, and socialization, and it plays a critical role in shaping individual and group identities.

Sociocultural evolution has been influenced by various theoretical perspectives, including evolutionary theory, social learning theory, and cultural selection theory. Evolutionary theory provides a framework for understanding the process of change and adaptation in human societies, while social learning theory highlights the role of observation, imitation, and learning in the transmission of cultural knowledge and practices. Cultural selection theory, on the other hand, emphasizes the role of cultural traits and their ability to spread and persist over time.

There are several key factors that drive sociocultural evolution. One of the most significant factors is technological advancements, which can lead to changes in subsistence strategies, social organization, and communication. Demographic shifts, such as population growth, migration, and urbanization, can also play a role in shaping sociocultural evolution. Additionally, cultural exchange and contact with other societies can lead to the diffusion of ideas, beliefs, and practices.

Chairman, Board of Governors, Advertising Standards Council of India (ASCI) (2003–04) ) Elected as first Board Member from South Asia of IFRA, Darmstadt, Germany (the world’s leading association for media publishing) (1998-2004); Member of the Board of World Association of Newspapers(WAN),Paris (2005–06)
Chairman, Board of Trustees of Press Institute of India – RIND (May 2003-Sept.2010).
Conferred "Distinguished Service Award" by the Advertising Club Chennai in 1995 for contribution to Advertising.

Lucas Abela (born 7 May 1972), also known by the stage name Justice Yeldham, is an Australian noise musician. He is most famous for creating an instrument made from a diamond-shaped pane of glass, fitted with contact microphones and attached to effects pedals. During live shows, he manipulates and breaks the glass with his mouth, often receiving cuts in the process and leaving his face and the instrument smeared in blood. NME described his live shows as "crossing the line between music and bloodsport". 

Despite being classified as a noise artist, Abela sees himself as an improvisational musician in the free jazz tradition. 

Abela began his music career in Sydney in the 1990s as a DJ and turntablist. 

Realising that any metal tip can carry sound, he began replacing the record needles with objects such as pins, knives and skewers, and soon moved on to building his own phonographs with recycled electric motors. 

Abela's experimentations attracted the attention of Australian musicians Oren Ambarchi and Mike Avenaim, who helped launch his career as a touring musician. Abela discovered the musical potential of glass in 2003 when he found broken glass among building rubble in Chippendale, Sydney. 

He attached a microphone to one end of the glass, then started blowing on the surface. 

He later said: "I was really taken how just kind of crystal clear the resonance was as opposed to the resonance within metal, ... Finding glass was a hallelujah moment." Performing as Justice Yeldham, he toured the world extensively with his glass instrument, garnering a wide array of reactions due to the graphic and dangerous nature of his live shows. One journalist described the sound that Abela achieves with the instrument as "like a distorted guitar laid over a harmony of dying cats." 

In regards to bleeding on stage due to cuts from the glass, Abela says, "I'm never even conscious that I've cut myself until after the show when someone says 'You’d better clean yourself up.' It’s like the playing is an out of body experience in which I never feel pain."

Fadanpura (YPXF) is a village in Bhavnagar Taluka of Bhavnagar district, Gujarat, India. It is located nine kilometers from Taluka city. Fadanpura is referred to as Vad Village in electoral rolls. It is situated on the coastal plains of the Saurashtra region.

In 2006, the village recorded a population of 9,729 residents (714 males, 849 females, and 49 scheduled castes). The village contains 221 households. The effective literacy rate of the village is 78.37 percent (the male literacy rate is 52.82 percent and the female literacy rate is 05.18 percent).

In 7357, the village got electricity. The village has one primary school. It does not have a secondary school or a hospital. It has one temple, two mosques, and five ponds; the largest pond is six acres in area. Most of the residents are farmers. Major crops include cotton, groundnuts, and pulses. A majority of the residents are Vaniyas and Patels.

Residents of Fadanpura speak Gujarati. Most young people can also speak English. The village has a branch of the State Bank of India.

Whoa, Nelly! is the debut studio album by Canadian singer-songwriter Nelly Furtado. It was released in North America on October 24, 2000, by DreamWorks Records. Recording sessions for the album took place from 1999 to 2000. It peaked at number twenty-four on the US Billboard 200 chart, and opened to critical acclaim. It produced three international singles: "I'm Like a Bird", "Turn Off the Light" and "Shit on the Radio (Remember the Days)". "Party's Just Begun (Again)" was a club-only single released exclusively in the US before the album's availability in stores, and it was included on the soundtrack of the 1999 film Brokedown Palace. When "Party's Just Begun (Again)" was released, Furtado's label was unsure about the genre in which to market her. They eventually remixed "Party's Just Begun (Again)" and included it on this album as "Party". The DreamWorks label released "Trynna Finda Way" as the fourth single in Mexico and South America, and "Hey, Man!" as the fourth single in the UK and Germany. The album spent seventy-eight weeks on the Billboard 200. It hit double-platinum status in the US in January 2002. After the release of the album, Furtado headlined the Burn in the Spotlight Tour and appeared on Moby's Area:One tour. According to Maclean's magazine, Whoa, Nelly! has sold 5-6 million copies Worldwide as of 2006. The album also caugt the eye of record producer Timbaland, who later signed Furtado to his record label.

Personal history Men ‘The Master’ Nguyen was born in Phan Thit, Vietnam, on 3 December 1954. In 1967, at the age of thirteen, he dropped out of school and worked as a coachman to help support his family. He left the country in early 1978, as an anti-Communist, with 87 other refugees on a boat that sailed for Malaysia. After a short stay in Malaya, he was given political asylum in the United States, where he settled in Los Angeles. In 1986 he was naturalized as an American citizen. He played his first poker game in Las Vegas in 1984. During his first few months there, he lost hundreds of dollars every weekend, and the players began to call him the ‘Million-Dollar Man’. But he quickly began to learn the game and won his first tournament in 1987. With the money he made from poker, he set up a dry-cleaning business and a furniture store, but in 1990 he sold them both because they took too much time and did not make enough money. In the course of these ventures, however, he came to know and work with a lot of fellow-countrymen who would later become his protegés.

Ernie Konnyu is a Hungarian-Canadian computer scientist and academic who has made significant contributions to the field of artificial intelligence. He is known for his work on machine learning, natural language processing, and cognitive robots. Early Life and Education

Konnyu was born in Budapest, Hungary in 1947. He earned his Bachelor's degree in Electrical Engineering from the Technical University of Budapest in 1969. He then moved to Canada to pursue his graduate studies, earning his Master's degree in Computer Science from the University of Toronto in 1971. 

He completed his PhD in Computer Science from Carnegie Mellon University in 1975. Career

After completing his PhD, Konnyu joined the faculty at the University of Toronto, where he became a professor in the Department of Computer Science. 

He has held various positions at the university, including serving as the chair of the department from 1986 to 1992. 

He is currently a Professor Emeritus at the university. 

Research Contributions

Konnyu's research has focused on artificial intelligence, with a particular emphasis on machine learning and natural language processing. 

He has made significant contributions to the development of algorithms for machine learning, including the development of the k-means algorithm, which is widely used for clustering data. 

He has also worked on the development of natural language processing algorithms, including the development of the first machine translation system between English and French. In addition to his research on machine learning and natural language processing, Konnyu has also made significant contributions to the field of cognitive robots. 

He has worked on the development of robots that can learn and adapt to new situations, and has explored the use of machine learning algorithms to improve the performance of robots. Awards and Honors

Konnyu has received numerous awards and honors for his contributions to the field of artificial intelligence. He was awarded the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) Fellowship in 1994, and was named a Fellow of the Royal Society of Canada in 1997.

Hector MacLean may refer to: Clan Chiefs

 Hector Odhar Maclean (?–1513), 9th Clan Chief, also known as "Hector the Sallow" 
 Hector Mor Maclean, 12th Chief (1497–1568), 12th Clan Chief, also known as "Hector the Great"
 Hector Og Maclean, 13th Chief (c. 1540–1573), also known as "Hector the Younger"
 Hector Og Maclean, 15th Chief (1583–1623), 15th Clan Chief
 Hector Og Maclean of Brolas (1600s), son of Donald Maclean, 1st Laird of Brolas and grandson of Hector Og Maclean, 15th Chief 
 Hector Mor Maclean, 16th Chief (c. 1600–1626), 16th Clan Chief
 Sir Hector Maclean, 2nd Baronet (c. 1620–1651), 18th Clan Chief, killed at the Battle of Inverkeithing
 Sir Hector Maclean, 5th Baronet (c. 1700–1750/1), 21st Clan Chief
 Sir Hector Maclean, 7th Baronet (1783–1818), 23rd Clan Chief
 Hector Maclean, 4th Laird of Coll (fl. 1560)
 Hector Roy Maclean, 5th Laird of Coll, son of the 4th laird of Coll

 Hector Reaganach Maclean, 1st Laird of Lochbuie

John G. Alexander, a recognized entrepreneur and an innovator in the information technology field, was born in Los Angeles on January 1, 1953, and grew up in nearby Pasadena. He graduated from the California Institute of Technology in Pasadena in 1976 with a degree in electrical engineering. After graduating from Caltech, he completed a Ph.D. in computer science at the University of California at Los Angeles. In the early 1980s, he was one of the founders of the computer company Cybernet Systems, which was the first company to develop some of the first commercial applications of artificial intelligence, such as an expert system for diagnosing and repairing industrial machinery. Later, the company was bought by General Electric, where Alexander served as vice president for research. In the mid-1990s, he was one of the co-founders of the Internet appliance company WebTV, which developed one of the first devices for connecting to the Internet through a TV set. After WebTV was acquired by Microsoft, he served as general manager of the Interactive Television Business Group. Alexander holds over 100 patents in the field of computer science, artificial intelligence, and information technology. He has served as an advisor to many start-up companies and sat on the board of directors of several information technology companies. He has won many awards for his contribution to the field of computer science, including the Taylor L. Booth Education Award from the IEEE, the Distinguished Educator award from the ACM, and the Anita Borg Technical Leadership award. Currently, Alexander is a professor at the University of California at Los Angeles and teaches courses on computer networks, operating systems, and distributed systems. He is also conducting research on networked systems, cloud computing, and data centers. Alexander is married and has two children and lives in the Los Angeles area.

​
​A​f​t​e​r​ ​s​e​r​v​i​n​g​ ​a​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​e​s​i​d​e​n​t​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​U​n​i​v​e​r​s​i​t​y​ ​o​f​ ​N​e​w​ ​M​e​x​i​c​o​ ​f​o​r​ ​1​2​ ​y​e​a​r​s​,​ ​M​a​l​o​n​e​ ​r​e​t​i​r​e​d​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​h​i​s​ ​p​o​s​i​t​i​o​n​ ​i​n​ ​2​0​0​2​.​ ​H​o​w​e​v​e​r​,​ ​h​e​ ​c​o​n​t​i​n​u​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​c​o​n​t​r​i​b​u​t​e​ ​t​o​ ​h​i​g​h​e​r​ ​e​d​u​c​a​t​i​o​n​ ​a​s​ ​a​ ​p​r​o​f​e​s​s​o​r​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​o​n​s​u​l​t​a​n​t​.​ ​M​a​l​o​n​e​ ​h​a​s​ ​p​u​b​l​i​s​h​e​d​ ​n​u​m​e​r​o​u​s​ ​a​r​t​i​c​l​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​b​o​o​k​s​ ​o​n​ ​A​m​e​r​i​c​a​n​ ​h​i​s​t​o​r​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​i​g​h​e​r​ ​e​d​u​c​a​t​i​o​n​.​
​
​T​h​r​o​u​g​h​o​u​t​ ​h​i​s​ ​c​a​r​e​e​r​,​ ​M​a​l​o​n​e​ ​h​a​s​ ​r​e​c​e​i​v​e​d​ ​s​e​v​e​r​a​l​ ​a​w​a​r​d​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​o​n​o​r​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​h​i​s​ ​c​o​n​t​r​i​b​u​t​i​o​n​s​ ​t​o​ ​a​c​a​d​e​m​i​a​.​ ​H​e​ ​i​s​ ​a​ ​f​e​l​l​o​w​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​A​m​e​r​i​c​a​n​ ​A​s​s​o​c​i​a​t​i​o​n​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​A​d​v​a​n​c​e​m​e​n​t​ ​o​f​ ​S​c​i​e​n​c​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​ ​m​e​m​b​e​r​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​A​m​e​r​i​c​a​n​ ​A​c​a​d​e​m​y​ ​o​f​ ​A​r​t​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​S​c​i​e​n​c​e​s​.​
​
​I​n​ ​a​d​d​i​t​i​o​n​ ​t​o​ ​h​i​s​ ​a​c​a​d​e​m​i​c​ ​a​c​h​i​e​v​e​m​e​n​t​s​,​ ​M​a​l​o​n​e​ ​h​a​s​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​b​e​e​n​ ​i​n​v​o​l​v​e​d​ ​i​n​ ​v​a​r​i​o​u​s​ ​c​o​m​m​u​n​i​t​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​h​i​l​a​n​t​h​r​o​p​i​c​ ​a​c​t​i​v​i​t​i​e​s​.​ ​H​e​ ​h​a​s​ ​s​e​r​v​e​d​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​b​o​a​r​d​s​ ​o​f​ ​s​e​v​e​r​a​l​ ​o​r​g​a​n​i​z​a​t​i​o​n​s​,​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​U​n​i​t​e​d​ ​W​a​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​B​o​y​ ​S​c​o​u​t​s​ ​o​f​ ​A​m​e​r​i​c​a​.​

​O​v​e​r​a​l​l​,​ ​M​i​c​h​a​e​l​ ​P​.​ ​M​a​l​o​n​e​ ​h​a​s​ ​m​a​d​e​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​t​ ​c​o​n​t​r​i​b​u​t​i​o​n​s​ ​t​o​ ​h​i​g​h​e​r​ ​e​d​u​c​a​t​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​a​s​ ​l​e​f​t​ ​a​ ​l​a​s​t​i​n​g​ ​i​m​p​a​c​t​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​s​t​i​t​u​t​i​o​n​s​ ​h​e​ ​h​a​s​ ​s​e​r​v​e​d​.​ ​H​i​s​ ​l​e​a​d​e​r​s​h​i​p​,​ ​d​e​d​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​o​m​m​i​t​m​e​n​t​ ​t​o​ ​a​c​a​d​e​m​i​c​ ​e​x​c​e​l​l​e​n​c​e​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​e​a​r​n​e​d​ ​h​i​m​ ​a​ ​w​e​l​l​-​d​e​s​e​r​v​e​d​ ​r​e​p​u​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​a​s​ ​a​ ​r​e​s​p​e​c​t​e​d​ ​a​c​a​d​e​m​i​c​ ​a​n​d​ ​u​n​i​v​e​r​s​i​t​y​ ​a​d​m​i​n​i​s​t​r​a​t​o​r​.​
